title: Optimization of Constrained Quasiconformal Mapping for Origami Design
summary: Origami structures, particularly Miura-ori patterns, offer unique capabilities for surface approximation and deployable designs. In this study, a constrained mapping optimization algorithm is designed for designing surface-aligned Miura-ori via a narrow band approximation of the input surface. The Miura-fold, embedded in the narrow band, is parameterized to a planar domain, and a mapping is computed on the parameter pattern by optimizing certain energy terms and constraints. Extensive experiments are conducted, showing the significance and flexibility of our methods.

AI.tldr: A constrained optimization approach to design surface-aligned Miura-ori via a narrow band approximation of a target surface, enabling a planar-domain parameterization and energy-based mapping with constraints, validated by extensive experiments.
AI.motivation: To achieve accurate, flexible surface approximation and deployable design using Miura-ori by enabling precise alignment to arbitrary surfaces through a mapping-based design workflow.
AI.method: Introduce a constrained mapping optimization algorithm that embeds a Miura-fold within a narrow surface band. The Miura-ori is parameterized in a planar domain, and a mapping on the parameter pattern is computed by optimizing energy terms (e.g., distortion, curvature, and boundary constraints) under relevant constraints to ensure foldability and manufacturability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the approach’s significance and flexibility, showing improved alignment to target surfaces and robust design outcomes across varied geometries.
AI.conclusion: The proposed constrained mapping optimization framework provides an effective and flexible solution for surface-aligned Miura-ori design via narrow-band projections, enabling accurate surface approximation and deployable structures.

title: SL(C)AMma: Simultaneous Localisation, (Calibration) and Mapping With a Magnetometer Array
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures, 1 table, python implementation available at https://github.com/Tedridge/SL-C-AMma, experimental data available at https://hendeby.gitlab-pages.liu.se/research/magdata/
summary: Indoor localisation techniques suffer from attenuated Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signals and from the accumulation of unbounded drift by integration of proprioceptive sensors. Magnetic field-based Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping (SLAM) reduces drift through loop closures by revisiting previously seen locations, but extended exploration of unseen areas remains challenging. Recently, magnetometer arrays have demonstrated significant benefits over single magnetometers, as they can directly estimate the odometry. However, inconsistencies between magnetometer measurements negatively affect odometry estimates and complicate loop closure detection. We propose two filtering algorithms: The first focuses on magnetic field-based SLAM using a magnetometer array (SLAMma). The second extends this to jointly estimate the magnetometer calibration parameters (SLCAMma). We demonstrate, using Monte Carlo simulations, that the calibration parameters can be accurately estimated when there is sufficient orientation excitation, and that magnetometers achieve inter-sensor measurement consistency regardless of the type of motion. Experimental validation on ten datasets confirms these results, and we demonstrate that in cases where single magnetometer SLAM fails, SLAMma and SLCAMma provide good trajectory estimates with, more than 80% drift reduction compared to integration of proprioceptive sensors.

AI.tldr: Two filtering algorithms for magnetometer-array indoor SLAM: SLAMma (magnetometer array SLAM) and SLCAMma (joint magnetometer calibration and SLAM). They achieve improved trajectory estimates and significant drift reduction, validated by Monte Carlo simulations and 10 experimental datasets.
AI.motivation: GNSS-denied indoor navigation suffers from attenuation and proprioceptive drift; magnetometer arrays offer direct odometry but suffer from inter-sensor inconsistencies and weak loop closure; need calibration and filtering to exploit array gains for robust SLAM.
AI.method: Develop two filters: SLAMma uses a magnetometer array for SLAM; SLCAMma extends SE to jointly estimate magnetometer calibration parameters. Evaluate via Monte Carlo simulations to assess calibration identifiability under sufficient orientation excitation and to confirm inter-sensor consistency across motions; validate experimentally on ten datasets, comparing against single-magnetometer SLAM and proprioceptive integration.
AI.result: Calibration parameters can be accurately estimated given adequate orientation excitation; inter-sensor measurements become consistent regardless of motion; SLAMma and SLCAMma yield good trajectory estimates where single magnetometer SLAM fails, achieving >80% drift reduction relative to proprioceptive-accelerometer integration.
AI.conclusion: Magnetometer-array SLAM with joint calibration robustly reduces drift and improves loop closures in GNSS-denied indoor environments; orientation excitation enables reliable magnetometer calibration, making magnetometer arrays a viable solution for accurate indoor localization.

title: Rabies diagnosis in low-data settings: A comparative study on the impact of data augmentation and transfer learning
comment: This work has been accepted for publication in ICMI IEEE Conference (04/2026)
summary: Rabies remains a major public health concern across many African and Asian countries, where accurate diagnosis is critical for effective epidemiological surveillance. The gold standard diagnostic methods rely heavily on fluorescence microscopy, necessitating skilled laboratory personnel for the accurate interpretation of results. Such expertise is often scarce, particularly in regions with low annual sample volumes. This paper presents an automated, AI-driven diagnostic system designed to address these challenges. We developed a robust pipeline utilizing fluorescent image analysis through transfer learning with four deep learning architectures: EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB2, VGG16, and Vision Transformer (ViTB16). Three distinct data augmentation strategies were evaluated to enhance model generalization on a dataset of 155 microscopic images (123 positive and 32 negative). Our results demonstrate that TrivialAugmentWide was the most effective augmentation technique, as it preserved critical fluorescent patterns while improving model robustness. The EfficientNetB0 model, utilizing Geometric & Color augmentation and selected through stratified 3fold cross-validation, achieved optimal classification performance on cropped images. Despite constraints posed by class imbalance and a limited dataset size, this work confirms the viability of deep learning for automating rabies diagnosis. The proposed method enables fast and reliable detection with significant potential for further optimization. An online tool was deployed to facilitate practical access, establishing a framework for future medical imaging applications. This research underscores the potential of optimized deep learning models to transform rabies diagnostics and improve public health outcomes.

AI.tldr: A small AI-driven pipeline using transfer learning on fluorescence images for automated rabies diagnosis; EfficientNetB0 with Geometric & Color augmentation and TrivialAugmentWide achieved best performance on a dataset of 155 images; an online tool was deployed.
AI.motivation: Address shortage of skilled personnel for fluorescence-based rabies diagnosis and improve surveillance in low-volume settings by automating image interpretation.
AI.method: Train four CNNs (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB2, VGG16) and a Vision Transformer on cropped fluorescent images; evaluate three data augmentation strategies including TrivialAugmentWide; use stratified 3-fold cross-validation; dataset: 123 positive, 32 negative; deploy an online tool.
AI.result: TrivialAugmentWide outperformed other augmentations; EfficientNetB0 with Geometric & Color augmentation achieved best performance on cropped images; despite class imbalance and small dataset, deep learning can automate detection; tool deployed for practical access.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning-based rabies diagnosis from fluorescence images is viable and scalable with optimization; future work should expand data, address imbalance, and validate across settings; potential for rapid, accessible diagnostics in public health.

title: WorkflowGen:an adaptive workflow generation mechanism driven by trajectory experience
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents often suffer from high reasoning overhead, excessive token consumption, unstable execution, and inability to reuse past experiences in complex tasks like business queries, tool use, and workflow orchestration. Traditional methods generate workflows from scratch for every query, leading to high cost, slow response, and poor robustness. We propose WorkflowGen, an adaptive, trajectory experience-driven framework for automatic workflow generation that reduces token usage and improves efficiency and success rate. Early in execution, WorkflowGen captures full trajectories and extracts reusable knowledge at both node and workflow levels, including error fingerprints, optimal tool mappings, parameter schemas, execution paths, and exception-avoidance strategies. It then employs a closed-loop mechanism that performs lightweight generation only on variable nodes via trajectory rewriting, experience updating, and template induction. A three-tier adaptive routing strategy dynamically selects among direct reuse, rewriting-based generation, and full initialization based on semantic similarity to historical queries. Without large annotated datasets, we qualitatively compare WorkflowGen against real-time planning, static single trajectory, and basic in-context learning baselines. Our method reduces token consumption by over 40 percent compared to real-time planning, improves success rate by 20 percent on medium-similarity queries through proactive error avoidance and adaptive fallback, and enhances deployability via modular, traceable experiences and cross-scenario adaptability. WorkflowGen achieves a practical balance of efficiency, robustness, and interpretability, addressing key limitations of existing approaches.

AI.tldr: WorkflowGen is an adaptive framework that learns from early trajectories to generate reusable, modular workflows for LLM agents, reducing token usage while increasing robustness and success rate through a three-tier routing strategy (direct reuse, rewriting-based generation, full initialization).
AI.motivation: LLM agents incur high reasoning overhead, token costs, instability, and lack of experience reuse in complex tasks; existing approaches generate workflows anew for each query, causing inefficiency and fragility.
AI.method: Capture full execution trajectories early to extract reusable node- and workflow-level knowledge (error fingerprints, tool mappings, parameter schemas, execution paths, exception-avoidance strategies). Use a closed-loop mechanism to perform lightweight generation only on variable nodes via trajectory rewriting, experience updating, and template induction. Apply a three-tier adaptive routing strategy that chooses among direct reuse, rewriting-based generation, and full initialization based on semantic similarity to historical queries. Qualitatively compare against baselines without large annotated datasets.
AI.result: Token consumption reduced by over 40% compared to real-time planning; success rate increased by ~20% on medium-similarity queries due to proactive error avoidance and adaptive fallback; improved deployability via modular, traceable experiences and cross-scenario adaptability.
AI.conclusion: WorkflowGen offers a practical balance of efficiency, robustness, and interpretability, addressing limitations of existing real-time planning and single-trajectory methods and enhancing deployability across scenarios.

title: The Tool-Overuse Illusion: Why Does LLM Prefer External Tools over Internal Knowledge?
summary: Equipping LLMs with external tools effectively addresses internal reasoning limitations. However, it introduces a critical yet under-explored phenomenon: tool overuse, the unnecessary tool-use during reasoning. In this paper, we first reveal this phenomenon is pervasive across diverse LLMs. We then experimentally elucidate its underlying mechanisms through two key lenses: (1) First, by analyzing tool-use behavior across different internal knowledge availability regions, we identify a \textit{knowledge epistemic illusion}: models misjudge internal knowledge boundaries and fail to accurately perceive their actual knowledge availability. To mitigate this, we propose a knowledge-aware epistemic boundary alignment strategy based on direct preference optimization, which reduces tool usage in by 82.8\% while yielding an accuracy improvement. (2) Second, we establish a causal link between reward structures and tool-use behavior by visualizing the tool-augmented training process. It reveals that \textit{outcome-only rewards} inadvertently encourage tool overuse by rewarding only final correctness, regardless of tool efficiency. To verify this, we balance reward signals during training rather than relying on outcome-only rewards, cutting unnecessary tool calls by 66.7\% (7B) and 60.7\% (32B) without sacrificing accuracy. Finally, we provide theoretical justification in this two lenses to understand tool overuse.

AI.tldr: The abstract studies tool overuse in LLMs and proposes two approaches to mitigate it: knowledge-aware epistemic boundary alignment to reduce unnecessary tool use with accuracy gains; and reward-structure balancing during training to curb tool overuse without sacrificing accuracy, supported by empirical results on 7B and 32B models.
AI.motivation: Tool augmentation can improve reasoning but causes overuse; understanding underlying causes and providing practical remedies is essential for efficient and reliable LLM operation.
AI.method: Two experimental lenses: (1) analyze tool-use across internal knowledge availability regions to identify a 'knowledge epistemic illusion'; develop a direct preference optimization-based boundary-alignment strategy; evaluate tool usage and accuracy; (2) analyze training rewards by visualizing tool-augmented training and comparing outcome-only rewards vs balanced rewards; measure reductions in tool calls on models and accuracy.
AI.result: Knowledge-aware boundary alignment reduces tool usage by 82.8% and improves accuracy; balanced reward signals reduce tool calls by 66.7% on 7B and 60.7% on 32B without accuracy loss; both approaches come with theoretical justification.
AI.conclusion: Tool overuse is pervasive; addressing epistemic limits and reward shaping provides effective, scalable remedies, with solid theoretical grounding.

title: Radar Odometry Subject to High Tilt Dynamics of Subarctic Environments
summary: Rotating FMCW radar odometry methods often assume flat ground conditions. While this assumption is sufficient in many scenarios, including urban environments or flat mining setups, the highly dynamic terrain of subarctic environments poses a challenge to standard feature extraction and state estimation techniques. This paper benchmarks three existing radar odometry methods under demanding conditions, exhibiting up to 13° in pitch and 4° in roll difference between consecutive scans, with absolute pitch and roll reaching 30° and 8°, respectively. Furthermore, we propose a novel radar-inertial odometry method utilizing tilt-proximity submap search and a hard threshold for vertical displacement between scan points and the estimated axis of rotation. Experimental results demonstrate a state-of-the-art performance of our method on an urban baseline and a 0.3% improvement over the second-best comparative method on a 2-kilometer-long dynamic trajectory. Finally, we analyze the performance of the four evaluated methods on a complex radar sequence characterized by high lateral slip and a steep ditch traversal.

AI.tldr: Benchmarks three radar odometry methods under demanding subarctic terrain with large attitude differences and proposes a radar-inertial odometry using tilt-proximity submap search and a vertical-displacement threshold; achieves state-of-the-art on urban data and marginal improvement on a dynamic 2 km trajectory; also analyzes performance on a challenging sequence with high lateral slip and ditch traversal.
AI.motivation: Rotating FMCW radar odometry typically assumes flat ground, which fails in dynamic, non-flat subarctic environments. There is a need to evaluate existing methods under extreme attitude changes and develop more robust fusion-based odometry.
AI.method: Evaluate three existing radar odometry methods on sequences with up to 13° pitch difference, 4° roll difference between scans, and absolute pitch/roll up to 30°/8°. Propose a new radar-inertial odometry that uses tilt-proximity submap search and a hard threshold on vertical displacement between scan points and the estimated rotation axis.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on an urban baseline and shows a 0.3% improvement over the second-best method on a 2-km dynamic trajectory. The study also reports comparative performance across four methods on a complex radar sequence with high lateral slip and ditch traversal.
AI.conclusion: The radar-inertial approach with tilt-aware submap matching and vertical-displacement threshold enhances robustness in non-flat, dynamic environments and provides measurable gains over existing methods in urban and dynamic scenarios; further evaluation on varied terrains and longer dynamic sequences would strengthen generalization.

title: TactileEval: A Step Towards Automated Fine-Grained Evaluation and Editing of Tactile Graphics
comment: Code, data, and models are available at https://TactileEval.github.io/
summary: Tactile graphics require careful expert validation before reaching blind and visually impaired (BVI) learners, yet existing datasets provide only coarse holistic quality ratings that offer no actionable repair signal. We present TactileEval, a three-stage pipeline that takes a first step toward automating this process. Drawing on expert free-text comments from the TactileNet dataset, we establish a five-category quality taxonomy; encompassing view angle, part completeness, background clutter, texture separation, and line quality aligned with BANA standards. We subsequently gathered 14,095 structured annotations via Amazon Mechanical Turk, spanning 66 object classes organized into six distinct families. A reproducible ViT-L/14 feature probe trained on this data achieves 85.70% overall test accuracy across 30 different tasks, with consistent difficulty ordering suggesting the taxonomy suggesting the taxonomy captures meaningful perceptual structure. Building on these evaluations, we present a ViT-guided automated editing pipeline that routes classifier scores through family-specific prompt templates to produce targeted corrections via gpt-image-1 image editing. Code, data, and models are available at https://TactileEval.github.io/

AI.tldr: Three-stage TactileEval pipeline derives a five-category tactile-graphics quality taxonomy from expert comments, scales with 14,095 MTurk annotations over 66 classes in six families, achieves 85.70% accuracy with a ViT-L/14 probe across 30 tasks, and enables a ViT-guided GPT-based editing workflow; code and data released.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of actionable, repair-oriented feedback for tactile graphics quality used by BVI learners, moving beyond coarse holistic ratings to enable automated, domain-aligned quality assessment.
AI.method: (1) Derive a five-category quality taxonomy from expert free-text comments in the TactileNet dataset (categories: view angle, part completeness, background clutter, texture separation, line quality aligned with BANA). (2) Collect 14,095 structured annotations via Amazon Mechanical Turk across 66 object classes in six families. (3) Train a ViT-L/14 feature probe on this data; report 85.70% overall test accuracy across 30 tasks, with consistent difficulty ordering. (4) Develop a ViT-guided automated editing pipeline that routes classifier scores through family-specific prompt templates to generate targeted corrections via gpt-image-1 editing.
AI.result: Achieves 85.70% accuracy on 30 tasks; demonstrates that the taxonomy aligns with perceptual structure; scales annotations to 66 classes in six families; enables actionable editing via GPT-based image edits; code/data/models are released.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a practical, reproducible pathway to automate tactile-graphic quality assessment and repair, yielding actionable feedback and an end-to-end editing pipeline; further work could enhance taxonomy granularity, robustness, and expansion to more classes and edit capabilities.

title: Transparent Screening for LLM Inference and Training Impacts
summary: This paper presents a transparent screening framework for estimating inference and training impacts of current large language models under limited observability. The framework converts natural-language application descriptions into bounded environmental estimates and supports a comparative online observatory of current market models. Rather than claiming direct measurement for opaque proprietary services, it provides an auditable, source-linked proxy methodology designed to improve comparability, transparency, and reproducibility.

AI.tldr: A transparent screening framework to estimate LLM inference and training impacts under limited observability, using proxy estimates from natural-language descriptions and an online observatory for model comparison.
AI.motivation: Addresses the need for transparent, comparable evaluation of current LLMs when direct measurement of proprietary services is not possible, aiming to improve reproducibility and auditability.
AI.method: Transforms natural-language application descriptions into bounded environmental estimates; provides an auditable, source-linked proxy methodology; supports a comparative online observatory of current market models.
AI.result: Proposes a framework and proxy methodology that enables comparability and transparency across models without claiming direct measurement of opaque services; emphasizes auditable provenance and reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Offers a transparent, auditable proxy framework and ongoing observatory to facilitate cross-model comparison and accountability in the deployment and evaluation of large language models.

title: AI to Learn 2.0: A Deliverable-Oriented Governance Framework and Maturity Rubric for Opaque AI in Learning-Intensive Domains
summary: Generative AI is entering research, education, and professional work faster than current governance frameworks can specify how AI-assisted outputs should be judged in learning-intensive settings. The central problem is proxy failure: a polished artifact can be useful while no longer serving as credible evidence of the human understanding, judgment, or transfer ability that the work is supposed to cultivate or certify. This paper proposes AI to Learn 2.0, a deliverable-oriented governance framework for AI-assisted work. Rather than claiming element-wise novelty, it reorganizes adjacent ideas around the final deliverable package, distinguishes artifact residual from capability residual, and operationalizes the result through a five-part package, a seven-dimension maturity rubric, gate thresholds on critical dimensions, and a companion capability-evidence ladder. AI to Learn 2.0 allows opaque AI during exploration, drafting, hypothesis generation, and workflow design, but requires that the released deliverable be usable, auditable, transferable, and justifiable without the original large language model or cloud API. In learning-intensive contexts, it additionally requires context-appropriate human-attributable evidence of explanation or transfer. Worked scoring across contrastive cases, including coursework substitution, a symbolic-regression governance contrast, teacher-audited national-exam practice forms, and a self-hosted lecture-to-quiz pipeline with deterministic quality control, shows how the framework separates polished substitution workflows from bounded, auditable, and handoff-ready AI-assisted workflows. AI to Learn 2.0 is proposed as a governance instrument for structured third-party review where capability preservation, accountability, and validity boundaries matter.

AI.tldr: Proposes AI to Learn 2.0, a deliverable-oriented governance framework to curb proxy failure in AI-assisted work, balancing exploration with auditable, transferable deliverables and a multi-part assessment structure.
AI.motivation: Proxy failure in learning-intensive settings: polished AI artifacts can misrepresent human understanding or transfer ability; governance is needed to ensure credibility of learning outcomes and accountability.
AI.method: Deliverable-centered framework: reorganizes ideas around the final deliverable package; distinguishes artifact residual from capability residual; specifies a five-part deliverable package, a seven-dimension maturity rubric, gate thresholds on critical dimensions, and a capability-evidence ladder; permits opaque AI during exploration and drafting but requires the released deliverable to be usable, auditable, transferable, and justifiable without the original LLM or cloud API; in learning contexts, requires context-appropriate human-attributable evidence of explanation/transfer; supports contrastive worked scoring across cases (coursework substitution, symbolic-regression governance, teacher-audited national-exam practice forms, self-hosted lecture-to-quiz with deterministic QC).
AI.result: Illustrates that the framework can separate polished substitution workflows from bounded, auditable, handoff-ready AI-assisted workflows; provides a structured approach for governance in third-party review emphasizing capability preservation, accountability, and validity boundaries.
AI.conclusion: AI to Learn 2.0 is proposed as a governance instrument for structured third-party review where preserving capabilities, ensuring accountability, and maintaining validity boundaries matter in AI-assisted learning and assessment.

title: Efficient Reinforcement Learning using Linear Koopman Dynamics for Nonlinear Robotic Systems
summary: This paper presents a model-based reinforcement learning (RL) framework for optimal closed-loop control of nonlinear robotic systems. The proposed approach learns linear lifted dynamics through Koopman operator theory and integrates the resulting model into an actor-critic architecture for policy optimization, where the policy represents a parameterized closed-loop controller. To reduce computational cost and mitigate model rollout errors, policy gradients are estimated using one-step predictions of the learned dynamics rather than multi-step propagation. This leads to an online mini-batch policy gradient framework that enables policy improvement from streamed interaction data. The proposed framework is evaluated on several simulated nonlinear control benchmarks and two real-world hardware platforms, including a Kinova Gen3 robotic arm and a Unitree Go1 quadruped. Experimental results demonstrate improved sample efficiency over model-free RL baselines, superior control performance relative to model-based RL baselines, and control performance comparable to classical model-based methods that rely on exact system dynamics.

AI.tldr: A model-based reinforcement learning framework that learns linear lifted dynamics via Koopman operator theory, integrated into an actor-critic policy for a parameterized closed-loop controller. Policy gradients are estimated from one-step predictions to reduce rollout error and computation, enabling online mini-batch updates from streamed data. Demonstrated on simulated nonlinear benchmarks and two real robots (Kinova Gen3 & Unitree Go1), achieving higher sample efficiency than model-free baselines, better performance than other model-based RL baselines, and competitive results with classical model-based methods using exact dynamics.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear robotic systems typically require data-efficient control methods. While model-free RL struggles with sample inefficiency and model-based approaches depend on accurate dynamics, lifting nonlinear dynamics to a linear representation via Koopman theory can enable tractable planning and control with data-efficient learning.
AI.method: Learn linear lifted dynamics using Koopman operator theory and embed this model into an actor-critic framework where the policy represents a parameterized closed-loop controller. To reduce computational cost and rollout errors, estimate policy gradients using one-step predictions from the learned dynamics rather than multi-step rollouts, forming an online mini-batch policy gradient routine that updates from streamed interaction data.
AI.result: The approach yields improved sample efficiency compared to model-free RL baselines, and superior control performance relative to other model-based RL baselines. Performance is comparable to classical model-based methods that rely on exact system dynamics. Demonstrated on several simulated nonlinear benchmarks and real hardware platforms (Kinova Gen3 robotic arm and Unitree Go1 quadruped).
AI.conclusion: A data-efficient model-based RL framework that leverages Koopman-based linearization within an actor-critic policy to enable one-step rollout-based gradient estimation and online learning, achieving strong performance on nonlinear robotic control tasks and real hardware with reduced reliance on accurate full dynamics.

title: KD-Judge: A Knowledge-Driven Automated Judge Framework for Functional Fitness Movements on Edge Devices
summary: Functional fitness movements are widely used in training, competition, and health-oriented exercise programs, yet consistently enforcing repetition (rep) standards remains challenging due to subjective human judgment, time constraints, and evolving rules. Existing AI-based approaches mainly rely on learned scoring or reference-based comparisons and lack explicit rule-based, limiting transparency and deterministic rep-level validation. To address these limitations, we propose KD-Judge, a novel knowledge-driven automated judging framework for functional fitness movements. It converts unstructured rulebook standards into executable, machine-readable representations using an LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation and chain-of-thought rule-structuring pipeline. The structured rules are then incorporated by a deterministic rule-based judging system with pose-guided kinematic reasoning to assess rep validity and temporal boundaries. To improve efficiency on edge devices, including a high-performance desktop and the resource-constrained Jetson AGX Xavier, we introduce a dual strategy caching mechanism that can be selectively applied to reduce redundant and unnecessary computation. Experiments demonstrate reliable rule-structuring performance and accurate rep-level assessment, with judgment evaluation conducted on the CFRep dataset, achieving faster-than-real-time execution (real-time factor (RTF) < 1). When the proposed caching strategy is enabled, the system achieves up to 3.36x and 15.91x speedups on resource-constrained edge device compared to the non-caching baseline for pre-recorded and live-streaming scenarios, respectively. These results show that KD-Judge enables transparent, efficient, and scalable rule-grounded rep-level analysis that can complement human judging in practice.

AI.tldr: KD-Judge introduces a knowledge-driven, rule-based automated judging framework for functional fitness movements, converting rulebooks into executable rules via LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation and chain-of-thought structuring, then applies a deterministic pose-based judging system with caching for edge devices, achieving real-time or faster-than-real-time rep-level assessment and significant speedups on Jetson devices, evaluated on CFRep.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of transparency and determinism in existing AI-based judging approaches that rely on learned scoring or reference-based comparisons; enforce consistent rep standards amid subjective judgments and varying rules.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) rule-structuring where unstructured rulebooks are converted into machine-readable representations using LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and chain-of-thought (CoT) rule structuring; (2) a deterministic rule-based judging system with pose-guided kinematic reasoning to validate reps and timing. A dual-strategy caching mechanism optimizes computation on edge devices (desktop and Jetson AGX Xavier). Evaluation on CFRep with real-time factor < 1 and notable speedups.
AI.result: Demonstrates reliable rule-structuring and accurate rep-level assessment; achieves real-time execution; caching yields up to 3.36x (pre-recorded) and 15.91x (live) speedups on Jetson AGX Xavier, enabling efficient edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: KD-Judge offers transparent, efficient, and scalable rule-grounded rep-level analysis that can complement human judging in practice, with strong potential for deployment in training and competition contexts; future work could explore generalization to more movements, robustness to rule changes, and broader evaluation across datasets.

title: Accelerating PayPal's Commerce Agent with Speculative Decoding: An Empirical Study on EAGLE3 with Fine-Tuned Nemotron Models
summary: We evaluate speculative decoding with EAGLE3 as an inference-time optimization for PayPal's Commerce Agent, powered by a fine-tuned llama3.1-nemotron-nano-8B-v1 model. Building on prior work (NEMO-4-PAYPAL) that reduced latency and cost through domain-specific fine-tuning, we benchmark EAGLE3 via vLLM against NVIDIA NIM on identical 2xH100 hardware across 40 configurations spanning speculative token counts (gamma=3, gamma=5), concurrency levels (1-32), and sampling temperatures (0, 0.5). Key findings: (1) gamma=3 achieves 22-49% throughput improvement and 18-33% latency reduction at zero additional hardware cost; (2) acceptance rates remain stable at approximately 35.5% for gamma=3 across all conditions; (3) gamma=5 yields diminishing returns (approximately 25% acceptance rate); (4) LLM-as-Judge evaluation confirms fully preserved output quality; and (5) speculative decoding on a single H100 matches or exceeds NIM on two H100s, enabling 50% GPU cost reduction.

AI.tldr: EAGLE3 speculative decoding with a domain-tuned Llama 3.1 model for PayPal shows substantial speedups and latency reductions without extra hardware; gamma=3 is optimal, gamma=5 less beneficial; single-GPU decoding can rival or beat multi-GPU setups with ~50% GPU cost savings; output quality preserved per LLM-as-Judge.
AI.motivation: Reduce latency and cost for PayPal's Commerce Agent inference by combining speculative decoding (EAGLE3) with domain-specific fine-tuning of a Llama 3.1 model.
AI.method: Benchmark EAGLE3 (via vLLM) against NVIDIA NIM on identical 2x H100 hardware across 40 configurations: gamma values {3,5}, concurrency {1–32}, temperatures {0,0.5}; measure throughput, latency, and acceptance rate; assess output quality with LLM-as-Judge; compare against NIM.
AI.result: gamma=3 yields 22–49% throughput improvement and 18–33% latency reduction without extra hardware cost; acceptance rate ~35.5% for gamma=3 across configurations; gamma=5 shows diminished returns with ~25% acceptance; LLM-as-Judge confirms fully preserved output quality; speculative decoding on a single H100 matches or exceeds NIM on two H100s, implying ~50% GPU cost reduction.
AI.conclusion: EAGLE3 with gamma=3 provides meaningful performance and cost benefits for PayPal's Commerce Agent with preserved output quality; gamma-5 offers limited gains; results support deploying speculative decoding in a hardware-efficient manner and warrant further exploration of gamma selection and generalization to other tasks.

title: Algorithm Selection with Zero Domain Knowledge via Text Embeddings
summary: We propose a feature-free approach to algorithm selection that replaces hand-crafted instance features with pretrained text embeddings. Our method, ZeroFolio, proceeds in three steps: it reads the raw instance file as plain text, embeds it with a pretrained embedding model, and selects an algorithm via weighted k-nearest neighbors. The key to our approach is the observation that pretrained embeddings produce representations that distinguish problem instances without any domain knowledge or task-specific training. This allows us to apply the same three-step pipeline (serialize, embed, select) across diverse problem domains with text-based instance formats. We evaluate our approach on 11 ASlib scenarios spanning 7 domains (SAT, MaxSAT, QBF, ASP, CSP, MIP, and graph problems). Our experiments show that this approach outperforms a random forest trained on hand-crafted features in 10 of 11 scenarios with a single fixed configuration, and in all 11 with two-seed voting; the margin is often substantial. Our ablation study shows that inverse-distance weighting, line shuffling, and Manhattan distance are the key design choices. On scenarios where both selectors are competitive, combining embeddings with hand-crafted features via soft voting yields further improvements.

AI.tldr: ZeroFolio: a feature-free algorithm selector using pretrained text embeddings with a weighted k-NN, achieving strong cross-domain performance across 11 ASlib scenarios; ablation highlights inverse-distance weighting, line shuffling, and Manhattan distance; combining embeddings with hand-crafted features via soft voting can further boost results.
AI.motivation: Circumvent reliance on hand-crafted instance features and task-specific training by leveraging generic pretrained text embeddings to represent problem instances for algorithm selection across diverse domains.
AI.method: Serialize problem instances as raw text, embed with a pretrained embedding model, then perform weighted k-nearest neighbors (with inverse-distance weighting) to select the algorithm. Evaluate with a single fixed configuration across 11 ASlib scenarios in 7 domains, and with a two-seed voting ensemble. Conduct ablations on weighting, data ordering (line shuffling), and distance metric (Manhattan). Explore combining embeddings with hand-crafted features via soft voting.
AI.result: Outperforms a random forest trained on hand-crafted features in 10 of 11 scenarios with a single fixed configuration, and in all 11 scenarios with two-seed voting; margins are often substantial. Ablation shows inverse-distance weighting, line shuffling, and Manhattan distance are key design choices. In scenarios where both selectors are competitive, combining embeddings with hand-crafted features via soft voting yields additional improvements.
AI.conclusion: A simple, feature-free embedding-based representation coupled with a weighted KNN selector can achieve strong cross-domain algorithm selection performance, obviating the need for domain-specific features. The approach is extendable, and combining embeddings with traditional features can provide further gains in some cases.

title: Strain in Sound: Soft Corrugated Tube for Local Strain Sensing with Acoustic Resonance
comment: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft). IEEE, 2025
summary: We present a soft corrugated tube sensor designed to estimate strain in each half segment. When air flows through the tube, the internal corrugated cavities induce pressure oscillations that excite the tube's standing wave resonance mode, generating an acoustic tone. Stretching the tube affects both the resonance mode frequency, due to changes in overall length, and the frequency-flow speed relationship, due to variations in cavity width, which is particularly useful for local strain estimation. By sweeping flow rates in a controlled manner, we collected resonance frequency data across flow speeds under various local stretch conditions, enabling a machine learning algorithm (gradient boosting regressor) to estimate segmental strain with high accuracy. The dual-period tube design (3.1 mm and 4.18 mm corrugation periods) achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.8 mm, while the single-period tube (3.1 mm) provided a satisfactory MAE of 1 mm. Testing on a mannequin finger demonstrated the sensor's capability to differentiate multi-joint configurations, showing its potential for estimating non-uniform deformations in soft bodies.

AI.tldr: A soft corrugated-tube acoustic sensor estimates segmental strain by flow-induced resonance; a dual-period design improves MAE to 0.8 mm (vs 1.0 mm for a single-period) and the approach discriminates multi-joint configurations on a mannequin finger.
AI.motivation: To achieve local, non-contact or non-invasive strain sensing in soft structures with non-uniform deformation by leveraging flow-driven acoustic resonance coupled to geometry changes.
AI.method: Internal corrugated cavities in a soft tube generate pressure oscillations when air flows through, exciting standing-wave resonances. Stretching alters both resonance frequency (via length) and the frequency–flow relation (via cavity width). Data are collected across multiple flow rates and local stretch states, and a gradient-boosting regressor maps resonance features to segmental strain. Two designs are tested: dual-period tube (3.1 mm and 4.18 mm periods) and single-period tube (3.1 mm).
AI.result: Dual-period design yields MAE of 0.8 mm; single-period yields MAE of 1.0 mm. On a mannequin finger, the sensor can differentiate multi-joint configurations, indicating capability for estimating non-uniform deformations in soft bodies.
AI.conclusion: Flow-induced acoustic-resonance sensing in soft corrugated tubes can provide accurate local strain estimates, with the dual-period geometry offering improved accuracy; feasible for soft robotics and complex deformation sensing, though calibration over flow and stretch ranges is required.

title: Environmental Understanding Vision-Language Model for Embodied Agent
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown strong perception and reasoning abilities for instruction-following embodied agents. However, despite these abilities and their generalization performance, they still face limitations in environmental understanding, often failing on interactions or relying on environment metadata during execution. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework named Environmental Understanding Embodied Agent (EUEA), which fine-tunes four core skills: 1) object perception for identifying relevant objects, 2) task planning for generating interaction subgoals, 3) action understanding for judging success likelihood, and 4) goal recognition for determining goal completion. By fine-tuning VLMs with EUEA skills, our framework enables more reliable task execution for instruction-following. We further introduce a recovery step that leverages these core skills and a group relative policy optimization (GRPO) stage that refines inconsistent skill predictions. The recovery step samples alternative actions to correct failure cases, and the GRPO stage refines inconsistent skill predictions. Across ALFRED tasks, our VLM significantly outperforms a behavior-cloning baseline, achieving an 8.86% improvement in average success rate. The recovery and GRPO stages provide an additional 3.03% gain, further enhancing overall performance. Finally, our skill-level analyses reveal key limitations in the environmental understanding of closed- and open-source VLMs and identify the capabilities necessary for effective agent-environment interaction.

AI.tldr: Proposes Environmental Understanding Embodied Agent (EUEA) to fine-tune vision-language models on four core skills—object perception, task planning, action understanding, and goal recognition—plus recovery and group relative policy optimization (GRPO) to enhance instruction-following in embodied agents. Demonstrates improvements on ALFRED tasks, with 8.86% gain in average success rate over a behavior-cloning baseline and an additional 3.03% from recovery+GRPO, and analyzes environmental understanding limitations of VLMs.
AI.motivation: VLMs show strong perception and reasoning but struggle with environmental understanding in embodied tasks, often failing or relying on environment metadata. There is a need to explicitly train and integrate core environmental understanding skills to improve robust, autonomous interaction with real environments.
AI.method: Fine-tune VLMs on four core skills (object perception, task planning, action understanding, goal recognition). Introduce a recovery step that samples alternative actions to overcome failures, and a GRPO stage to refine inconsistent skill predictions and improve policy reliability in execution.
AI.result: On ALFRED tasks, the approach outperforms a behavior-cloning baseline by 8.86% in average success rate. The recovery step and GRPO contribute an additional 3.03% gain, yielding further performance improvements. Skill-level analyses reveal current limitations in environmental understanding and identify necessary capabilities for effective agent-environment interaction.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained environmental understanding skills, coupled with recovery and GRPO mechanisms, enable more reliable instruction-following in embodied agents and highlight the key capabilities required for robust agent–environment interaction.

title: On-Meter Graph Machine Learning: A Case Study of PV Power Forecasting for Grid Edge Intelligence
comment: This paper has been accepted for presentation at the 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE 2026) in Nanjing, China, April 17-19, 2026
summary: This paper presents a detailed study of how graph neural networks can be used on edge intelligent meters in a microgrid to forecast photovoltaic power generation. The problem background and the adopted technologies are introduced, including ONNX and ONNX Runtime. The hardware and software specifications of the smart meter are also briefly described. Then, the paper focuses on the training and deployment of two graph machine learning models, GCN and GraphSAGE, with particular emphasis on developing and deploying a customized ONNX operator for GCN. Finally, a case study is conducted using real datasets from a village microgrid. The performance of the two models is compared on both the PC and the smart meter, exhibiting successful deployments and executions on the smart meter.

AI.tldr: Graph neural networks (GCN, GraphSAGE) are trained and deployed on edge meters in a village microgrid to forecast PV power generation, featuring a customized ONNX operator for GCN and evaluation on PC and smart-meter hardware.
AI.motivation: To improve short-term PV generation forecasting in microgrids by leveraging spatial relationships captured by GNNs and to enable on-device inference through ONNX/ONNX Runtime, facilitating deployment on resource-constrained smart meters.
AI.method: Train two GNN models (GCN and GraphSAGE) on real village microgrid data; develop and integrate a custom ONNX operator for GCN; deploy models using ONNX Runtime; perform a case study comparing PC and smart-meter performance and feasibility of deployment.
AI.result: Both models were successfully deployed and executed on the smart meter; performance comparisons between PC and edge hardware demonstrated feasibility of edge GNN deployment with a customized ONNX operator for GCN.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates feasibility of end-to-end GNN forecasting for microgrid PV generation on edge devices, showcases interoperability via ONNX and a tailored GCN operator, and provides a practical deployment pathway from PC to field hardware.

title: JoyAI-RA 0.1: A Foundation Model for Robotic Autonomy
summary: Robotic autonomy in open-world environments is fundamentally limited by insufficient data diversity and poor cross-embodiment generalization. Existing robotic datasets are often limited in scale and task coverage, while relatively large differences across robot embodiments impede effective behavior knowledge transfer. To address these challenges, we propose JoyAI-RA, a vision-language-action (VLA) embodied foundation model tailored for generalizable robotic manipulation. JoyAI-RA presents a multi-source multi-level pretraining framework that integrates web data, large-scale egocentric human manipulation videos, simulation-generated trajectories, and real-robot data. Through training on heterogeneous multi-source data with explicit action-space unification, JoyAI-RA effectively bridges embodiment gaps, particularly between human manipulation and robotic control, thereby enhancing cross-embodiment behavior learning. JoyAI-RA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both simulation and real-world benchmarks, especially on diverse tasks with generalization demands.

AI.tldr: JoyAI-RA is a vision-language-action embodied foundation model for generalizable robotic manipulation, trained on diverse multi-source data with action-space unification to bridge embodiment gaps and enhance cross-embodiment generalization.
AI.motivation: Robotic autonomy in open-world settings is constrained by insufficient data diversity and poor cross-embodiment generalization. Existing datasets lack scale and task coverage, and differences across robot embodiments hinder knowledge transfer.
AI.method: A multi-source, multi-level pretraining framework that fuses web data, large-scale egocentric human manipulation videos, simulation-generated trajectories, and real-robot data, with explicit action-space unification to bridge embodiment gaps, especially between human manipulation and robotic control.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both simulation and real-world benchmarks, with pronounced gains on tasks requiring broad generalization across varied tasks and embodiments.
AI.conclusion: JoyAI-RA demonstrates that cross-embodiment generalization in robotic manipulation can be substantially improved by unified action spaces and diverse, multi-source pretraining, enabling more general and transferable manipulation policies.

title: If you're waiting for a sign... that might not be it! Mitigating Trust Boundary Confusion from Visual Injections on Vision-Language Agentic Systems
summary: Recent advances in embodied Vision-Language Agentic Systems (VLAS), powered by large vision-language models (LVLMs), enable AI systems to perceive and reason over real-world scenes. Within this context, environmental signals such as traffic lights are essential in-band signals that can and should influence agent behavior. However, similar signals could also be crafted to operate as misleading visual injections, overriding user intent and posing security risks. This duality creates a fundamental challenge: agents must respond to legitimate environmental cues while remaining robust to misleading ones. We refer to this tension as trust boundary confusion. To study this behavior, we design a dual-intent dataset and evaluation framework, through which we show that current LVLM-based agents fail to reliably balance this trade-off, either ignoring useful signals or following harmful ones. We systematically evaluate 7 LVLM agents across multiple embodied settings under both structure-based and noise-based visual injections. To address these vulnerabilities, we propose a multi-agent defense framework that separates perception from decision-making to dynamically assess the reliability of visual inputs. Our approach significantly reduces misleading behaviors while preserving correct responses and provides robustness guarantees under adversarial perturbations. The code of the evaluation framework and artifacts are made available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Visual-Prompt-Inject.

AI.tldr: Study trust boundary confusion in LVLM-based embodied agents; introduces a dual-intent dataset and evaluation framework; evaluates 7 LVLM agents under structure- and noise-based visual injections; proposes a defense framework that separates perception from decision-making to assess input reliability; reports robustness improvements and releases code.
AI.motivation: Environmental signals (e.g., traffic lights) are essential cues for agent behavior but can be crafted as misleading visual injections; agents must leverage legitimate cues while remaining robust to adversarial cues.
AI.method: Design a dual-intent dataset and evaluation framework; evaluate 7 LVLM-based agents across multiple embodied scenarios under both structure-based and noise-based visual injections; propose a multi-agent defense framework that decouples perception from decision-making and dynamically assesses input reliability; provide robustness guarantees under adversarial perturbations.
AI.result: The defense framework significantly reduces misleading behaviors while preserving correct responses and shows robustness under adversarial perturbations; 7 agents evaluated; code and artifacts released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical approach to mitigate trust boundary confusion in embodied VLAS by separating perception from decision-making, establishing a dataset and evaluation framework, and offering reproducible defenses with robustness guarantees.

title: Expert Upcycling: Shifting the Compute-Efficient Frontier of Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has become the dominant architecture for scaling large language models: frontier models routinely decouple total parameters from per-token computation through sparse expert routing. Scaling laws show that under fixed active computation, model quality scales predictably with total parameters, and MoEs realize this by increasing expert count. However, training large MoEs is expensive, as memory requirements and inter-device communication both scale with total parameter count. We propose expert upcycling, a method for progressively expanding MoE capacity by increasing the number of experts during continued pre-training (CPT). Given a trained E-expert model, the upcycling operator constructs an mE-expert model through expert duplication and router extension while holding top-K routing fixed, preserving per-token inference cost. Duplication provides a warm initialization: the expanded model inherits the source checkpoint's learned representations, starting from a substantially lower loss than random initialization. Subsequent CPT then breaks the symmetry among duplicated experts to drive specialization. We formalize the upcycling operator and develop a theoretical framework decomposing the quality gap into a capacity term and an initialization term. We further introduce utility-based expert selection, which uses gradient-based importance scores to guide non-uniform duplication, more than tripling gap closure when CPT is limited. In our 7B-13B total parameter experiments, the upcycled model matches the fixed-size baseline on validation loss while saving 32% of GPU hours. Comprehensive ablations across model scales, activation ratios, MoE architectures, and training budgets yield a practical recipe for deploying expert upcycling, establishing it as a principled, compute-efficient alternative to training large MoE models from scratch.

AI.tldr: Proposes expert upcycling for MoEs: progressively expand capacity by duplicating experts and extending routers during continued pre-training, preserving per-token cost and warm initializing, enabling compute-efficient scaling; shows 32% GPU-hour savings while matching loss in 7B-13B models.
AI.motivation: MoEs scale quality with total parameters under fixed compute, but training large MoEs is costly due to memory and communication; need efficient growth strategies.
AI.method: Define upcycling operator: duplicate E-expert model to mE-expert by duplicating experts and extending router while keeping top-K routing fixed; copying provides warm initialization; then continue CPT to specialize; introduce utility-based expert selection via gradient-based importance scores; decompose quality gap into capacity term and initialization term; provide theory and ablations.
AI.result: In 7B-13B totals, upcycled model matches fixed-size baseline loss while saving 32% GPU hours; comprehensive ablations identify scalable recipe; demonstrates compute-efficient alternative to training from scratch.
AI.conclusion: Expert upcycling is a principled, practical method to scale MoEs efficiently, enabling performance parity with reduced training cost and offering guidance on deployment across scales and budgets.

title: Explainable AML Triage with LLMs: Evidence Retrieval and Counterfactual Checks
summary: Anti-money laundering (AML) transaction monitoring generates large volumes of alerts that must be rapidly triaged by investigators under strict audit and governance constraints. While large language models (LLMs) can summarize heterogeneous evidence and draft rationales, unconstrained generation is risky in regulated workflows due to hallucinations, weak provenance, and explanations that are not faithful to the underlying decision. We propose an explainable AML triage framework that treats triage as an evidence-constrained decision process. Our method combines (i) retrieval-augmented evidence bundling from policy/typology guidance, customer context, alert triggers, and transaction subgraphs, (ii) a structured LLM output contract that requires explicit citations and separates supporting from contradicting or missing evidence, and (iii) counterfactual checks that validate whether minimal, plausible perturbations lead to coherent changes in both the triage recommendation and its rationale. We evaluate on public synthetic AML benchmarks and simulators and compare against rules, tabular and graph machine-learning baselines, and LLM-only/RAG-only variants. Results show that evidence grounding substantially improves auditability and reduces numerical and policy hallucination errors, while counterfactual validation further increases decision-linked explainability and robustness, yielding the best overall triage performance (PR-AUC 0.75; Escalate F1 0.62) and strong provenance and faithfulness metrics (citation validity 0.98; evidence support 0.88; counterfactual faithfulness 0.76). These findings indicate that governed, verifiable LLM systems can provide practical decision support for AML triage without sacrificing compliance requirements for traceability and defensibility.

AI.tldr: An explainable AML triage framework using evidence-grounded LLMs with retrieval-augmented evidence bundles, a structured output contract with explicit citations, and counterfactual checks; demonstrates improved auditability and robustness on synthetic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: AML alert triage requires rapid, compliant decision-making; LLMs offer summaries but risk hallucinations and non-faithful explanations; need governance-friendly, verifiable AI that preserves traceability.
AI.method: Three components: (i) retrieve-and-bundle evidence from policy/typology guidance, customer context, alert triggers, transaction subgraphs; (ii) structured LLM output contract enforcing explicit citations and separating supporting/contradictory/missing evidence; (iii) counterfactual checks to ensure perturbations cause coherent changes in triage and rationale; evaluated against baselines on synthetic AML benchmarks and simulators.
AI.result: Evidence grounding reduces numerical and policy hallucinations; counterfactual checks boost explainability and robustness; best overall triage performance with PR-AUC 0.75 and Escalate F1 0.62; provenance metrics high: citation validity 0.98; evidence support 0.88; counterfactual faithfulness 0.76.
AI.conclusion: Governed, verifiable LLM systems can support AML triage without compromising traceability and defensibility; approach provides practical decision support with improved auditability and compliance.

title: Toward Safe Autonomous Robotic Endovascular Interventions using World Models
comment: This manuscript is a preprint and has been submitted to the IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) 2026
summary: Autonomous mechanical thrombectomy (MT) presents substantial challenges due to highly variable vascular geometries and the requirements for accurate, real-time control. While reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for the automation of endovascular navigation, existing approaches often show limited robustness when faced with diverse patient anatomies or extended navigation horizons. In this work, we investigate a world-model-based framework for autonomous endovascular navigation built on TD-MPC2, a model-based RL method that integrates planning and learned dynamics. We evaluate a TD-MPC2 agent trained on multiple navigation tasks across hold out patient-specific vasculatures and benchmark its performance against the state-of-the-art Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm agent. Both approaches are further validated in vitro using patient-specific vascular phantoms under fluoroscopic guidance. In simulation, TD-MPC2 demonstrates a significantly higher mean success rate than SAC (58% vs. 36%, p < 0.001), and mean tip contact forces of 0.15 N, well below the proposed 1.5 N vessel rupture threshold. Mean success rates for TD-MPC2 (68%) were comparable to SAC (60%) in vitro, but TD-MPC2 achieved superior path ratios (p = 0.017) at the cost of longer procedure times (p < 0.001). Together, these results provide the first demonstration of autonomous MT navigation validated across both hold out in silico data and fluoroscopy-guided in vitro experiments, highlighting the promise of world models for safe and generalizable AI-assisted endovascular interventions.

AI.tldr: A world-model-based RL framework (TD-MPC2) for autonomous endovascular navigation improves robustness and safety over SAC, validated in silico and with patient-specific phantoms; shows higher success and lower contact forces, with trade-off in procedure time.
AI.motivation: Address robustness and generalization of autonomous MT navigation across diverse anatomies and long horizons via model-based RL integrating planning and learned dynamics.
AI.method: Train TD-MPC2 on multiple navigation tasks; evaluate on hold-out vasculatures; compare with SAC; validate in vitro on fluoroscopy-guided patient-specific phantoms.
AI.result: Simulation: mean success 58% vs 36% (p<0.001); mean tip forces 0.15 N (below 1.5 N rupture threshold). In vitro: success 68% vs 60%; path ratios higher for TD-MPC2 (p=0.017); longer procedure times (p<0.001).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates first autonomous MT navigation validated across hold-out in silico and fluoroscopy-guided in vitro; world-models offer safe, generalizable AI-assisted endovascular interventions.

title: Wan-Image: Pushing the Boundaries of Generative Visual Intelligence
summary: We present Wan-Image, a unified visual generation system explicitly engineered to paradigm-shift image generation models from casual synthesizers into professional-grade productivity tools. While contemporary diffusion models excel at aesthetic generation, they frequently encounter critical bottlenecks in rigorous design workflows that demand absolute controllability, complex typography rendering, and strict identity preservation. To address these challenges, Wan-Image features a natively unified multi-modal architecture by synergizing the cognitive capabilities of large language models with the high-fidelity pixel synthesis of diffusion transformers, which seamlessly translates highly nuanced user intents into precise visual outputs. It is fundamentally powered by large-scale multi-modal data scaling, a systematic fine-grained annotation engine, and curated reinforcement learning data to surpass basic instruction following and unlock expert-level professional capabilities. These include ultra-long complex text rendering, hyper-diverse portrait generation, palette-guided generation, multi-subject identity preservation, coherent sequential visual generation, precise multi-modal interactive editing, native alpha-channel generation, and high-efficiency 4K synthesis. Across diverse human evaluations, Wan-Image exceeds Seedream 5.0 Lite and GPT Image 1.5 in overall performance, reaching parity with Nano Banana Pro in challenging tasks. Ultimately, Wan-Image revolutionizes visual content creation across e-commerce, entertainment, education, and personal productivity, redefining the boundaries of professional visual synthesis.

AI.tldr: Wan-Image is a unified multi-modal system that combines large language models with diffusion transformers to deliver professional-grade, controllable visual synthesis (long text rendering, identity-preserving portrait generation, palette-guided outputs, multi-subject editing, alpha-channel and 4K output). It claims to outperform several baselines and rival top systems on challenging tasks.
AI.motivation: Address bottlenecks in diffusion-model-driven design workflows requiring strict controllability, complex typography, and identity preservation; deliver expert-level productivity tools bridging language-based intent with high-fidelity synthesis.
AI.method: A unified architecture that fuses LLM capabilities with diffusion-transformer-based pixel synthesis, supported by large-scale multi-modal data, fine-grained annotation, and curated reinforcement-learning data; enables ultra-long text rendering, portrait diversity, palette-guided generation, multi-subject identity preservation, sequential visual generation, interactive editing, native alpha-channel generation, and 4K synthesis.
AI.result: Human evaluations indicate Wan-Image surpasses Seedream 5.0 Lite and GPT Image 1.5 in overall performance and matches Nano Banana Pro on challenging tasks.
AI.conclusion: Wan-Image aspires to redefine professional visual synthesis across industries by enabling precise controllability and production-grade outputs, potentially altering e-commerce, entertainment, education, and personal productivity workflows.

title: Graph-Theoretic Models for the Prediction of Molecular Measurements
summary: Graph-theoretic approaches offer simplicity, interpretability, and low computational cost for molecular property prediction. Among these, the model proposed by Mukwembi and Nyabadza, based on the external activity $D(G)$ and internal activity $ζ(G)$ indices, achieved strong results on a small flavonoid dataset. However, its ability to generalize to larger and chemically diverse datasets has not been tested. This study evaluates the baseline $D(G)$-$ζ(G)$ polynomial model on five benchmark datasets from MoleculeNet, covering biological activity (BACE, 1,513 molecules), lipophilicity (LogP synthetic, 14,610 molecules; LogP experimental, 753 molecules), aqueous solubility (ESOL, 1,128 molecules), and hydration free energy (SAMPL, 642 molecules). The baseline model achieves an average $R^2 = 0.24$, confirming limited transferability. To address this, a systematic enhancement framework is proposed, progressively incorporating Ridge regularization, additional graph descriptors, physicochemical properties, ensemble learning with Gradient Boosting, Lasso feature selection, and a hybrid approach combining topological indices with Morgan fingerprints. The enhanced models raise the average best $R^2$ to 0.79, with individual improvements ranging from 165\% to 274\%. All improvements are statistically significant ($p < 0.001$). A direct comparison with a Graph Convolutional Network under identical experimental conditions shows that the enhanced classical models match or outperform deep learning on all five datasets. Comparison with the recent GNN+PGM hybrid of Djagba et al.\ further confirms competitiveness, with the enhanced models achieving the best results on two datasets and tying on one. The entire framework requires no GPU, trains in under five minutes, and uses only open-source tools, making it accessible for researchers in resource-limited settings.

AI.tldr: Baseline D(G)-ζ(G) model shows limited transferability; a staged enhancement framework yields competitive results on MoleculeNet datasets, matching or surpassing some GNNs; no GPU required and training is fast; robust across five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Evaluate generalizability of a simple graph-theoretic index model versus deep learning approaches on diverse molecular property datasets; provide an accessible, resource-efficient modeling framework.
AI.method: Assess the baseline D(G)-ζ(G) polynomial model on five MoleculeNet datasets (BACE; LogP synthetic; LogP experimental; ESOL; SAMPL). Implement a progressive enhancement framework: Ridge regularization, additional graph descriptors, physicochemical properties, ensemble learning with Gradient Boosting, Lasso feature selection, and a hybrid approach combining topological indices with Morgan fingerprints. Compare against Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) and a GNN+PGM hybrid under identical conditions. Use R^2 as the primary metric and verify statistical significance (p<0.001). Report training time and resource usage.
AI.result: Baseline average R^2 = 0.24. Enhanced models raise average best R^2 to 0.79, with improvements ranging 165%–274%. All improvements statistically significant (p<0.001). Under identical conditions, enhanced classical models match or outperform the GCN across all five datasets; they also outperform or tie with the GNN+PGM hybrid (best on two datasets, tie on one). The framework requires no GPU, trains in under five minutes, and uses open-source tools.
AI.conclusion: Enhanced classical graph-index models can be competitive with modern GNNs on diverse molecular benchmarks, offering strong transferability, efficiency, and accessibility for researchers in resource-limited settings.

title: ThermoQA: A Three-Tier Benchmark for Evaluating Thermodynamic Reasoning in Large Language Models
comment: 17 pages, 8 figures, open-source dataset and code
summary: We present ThermoQA, a benchmark of 293 open-ended engineering thermodynamics problems in three tiers: property lookups (110 Q), component analysis (101 Q), and full cycle analysis (82 Q). Ground truth is computed programmatically from CoolProp 7.2.0, covering water, R-134a, and variable-cp air. Six frontier LLMs are evaluated across three independent runs each. The composite leaderboard is led by Claude Opus 4.6 (94.1%), GPT-5.4 (93.1%), and Gemini 3.1 Pro (92.5%). Cross-tier degradation ranges from 2.8 pp (Opus) to 32.5 pp (MiniMax), confirming that property memorization does not imply thermodynamic reasoning. Supercritical water, R-134a refrigerant, and combined-cycle gas turbine analysis serve as natural discriminators with 40-60 pp performance spreads. Multi-run sigma ranges from +/-0.1% to +/-2.5%, quantifying reasoning consistency as a distinct evaluation axis. Dataset and code are open-source at https://huggingface.co/datasets/olivenet/thermoqa

AI.tldr: ThermoQA benchmarks 293 open-ended engineering thermodynamics questions across three tiers, ground-truth via CoolProp; six frontier LLMs evaluated; leading models achieve ~94% accuracy; cross-tier degradation indicates true reasoning rather than memorization; discriminators show large performance spreads; dataset open-source.
AI.motivation: Assess LLMs' thermodynamic reasoning capabilities and distinguish memorization from reasoning; provide a reproducible, physics-grounded benchmark for engineering thermodynamics.
AI.method: Construct questions from properties, components, and full-cycle analyses; compute ground truth via CoolProp 7.2.0; evaluate six frontier LLMs with three independent runs; measure per-tier performance, cross-tier degradation (difference between tiers), and inter-run sigma; test on water, R-134a, and variable-cp air; include discriminators (supercritical water, R-134a refrigerant, combined-cycle gas turbine) to probe boundary conditions; dataset and code open-source.
AI.result: Leaderboard: Claude Opus 4.6 94.1%, GPT-5.4 93.1%, Gemini 3.1 Pro 92.5%; cross-tier degradation ranges 2.8 pp (Opus) to 32.5 pp (MiniMax); discriminators show 40–60 pp performance spreads; multi-run sigma ranges +/-0.1% to +/-2.5%; dataset available at HuggingFace.
AI.conclusion: ThermoQA offers a challenging, reproducible physics-grounded benchmark for evaluating thermodynamic reasoning in LLMs, revealing that high overall performance does not guarantee robust cross-tier thermodynamic reasoning and providing a resource for future improvements and comparability.

title: LLM-Guided Safety Agent for Edge Robotics with an ISO-Compliant Perception-Compute-Control Architecture
summary: Ensuring functional safety in human-robot interaction is challenging because AI perception is inherently probabilistic, whereas industrial standards require deterministic behavior. We present an LLM-guided safety agent for edge robotics, built on an ISO-compliant low-latency perception-compute-control architecture. Our method translates natural-language safety regulations into executable predicates and deploys them through a redundant heterogeneous edge runtime. For fault-tolerant closed-loop execution under edge constraints, we adopt a symmetric dual-modular redundancy design with parallel independent execution for low-latency perception, computation, and control. We prototype the system on a dual-RK3588 platform and evaluate it in representative human-robot interaction scenarios. The results demonstrate a practical edge implementation path toward ISO 13849 Category 3 and PL d using cost-effective hardware, supporting practical deployment of safety-critical embodied AI.

AI.tldr: LLM-guided, ISO-compliant edge safety architecture for human-robot interaction using dual-modular redundancy; demonstrates feasibility toward ISO 13849 Cat 3/PL d on cost-effective RK3588 hardware.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between probabilistic AI perception and deterministic safety standards in safety-critical HRI, enabling low-latency, edge-based compliance.
AI.method: LLM-guided safety agent translates natural-language safety regulations into executable predicates; deploys on a redundant heterogeneous edge runtime. Uses symmetric dual-modular redundancy for parallel, independent execution across perception, compute, and control. Prototype on dual-RK3588 hardware and evaluate in HRI scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrates a practical edge implementation path toward ISO 13849 Category 3 and PL d with cost-effective hardware, enabling deployment of safety-critical embodied AI at the edge.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a feasible, scalable path to ISO-compliant safety in embodied AI at the edge, balancing probabilistic AI perception with deterministic safety through redundancy and predicate-based execution.

title: SGAP-Gaze: Scene Grid Attention Based Point-of-Gaze Estimation Network for Driver Gaze
summary: Driver gaze estimation is essential for understanding the driver's situational awareness of surrounding traffic. Existing gaze estimation models use driver facial information to predict the Point-of-Gaze (PoG) or the 3D gaze direction vector. We propose a benchmark dataset, Urban Driving-Face Scene Gaze (UD-FSG), comprising synchronized driver-face and traffic-scene images. The scene images provide cues about surrounding traffic, which can help improve the gaze estimation model, along with the face images. We propose SGAP-Gaze, Scene-Grid Attention based Point-of-Gaze estimation network, trained and tested on our UD-FSG dataset, which explicitly incorporates the scene images into the gaze estimation modelling. The gaze estimation network integrates driver face, eye, iris, and scene contextual information. First, the extracted features from facial modalities are fused to form a gaze intent vector. Then, attention scores are computed over the spatial scene grid using a Transformer-based attention mechanism fusing face and scene image features to obtain the PoG. The proposed SGAP-Gaze model achieves a mean pixel error of 104.73 on the UD-FSG dataset and 63.48 on LBW dataset, achieving a 23.5% reduction in mean pixel error compared to state-of-the-art driver gaze estimation models. The spatial pixel distribution analysis shows that SGAP-Gaze consistently achieves lower mean pixel error than existing methods across all spatial ranges, including the outer regions of the scene, which are rare but critical for understanding driver attention. These results highlight the effectiveness of integrating multi-modal gaze cues with scene-aware attention for a robust driver PoG estimation model in real-world driving environments.

AI.tldr: Multimodal driver gaze estimation using scene-aware attention with a new UD-FSG dataset; SGAP-Gaze improves PoG accuracy by leveraging face and scene context with Transformer-based attention.
AI.motivation: Enhance driver gaze estimation by incorporating scene context to resolve ambiguities when facial cues alone are insufficient for precise PoG estimation.
AI.method: Introduce UD-FSG dataset (synchronized driver-face and traffic-scene images). Propose SGAP-Gaze: a multi-modal network that fuses facial features (face, eyes, iris) into a gaze intent vector and applies Transformer-based attention over a scene grid to fuse with scene features, yielding PoG estimates.
AI.result: Mean pixel error: 104.73 on UD-FSG and 63.48 on LBW; 23.5% error reduction over state-of-the-art. SGAP-Gaze achieves lower pixel errors across spatial ranges, including outer scene regions.
AI.conclusion: Scene-aware, multi-modal fusion with attention improves robustness and accuracy of PoG estimation in real-world driving; validates the benefit of integrating scene context for driver gaze estimation.

title: Rethinking Reinforcement Fine-Tuning in LVLM: Convergence, Reward Decomposition, and Generalization
summary: Reinforcement fine-tuning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for equipping large vision-language models (LVLMs) with agentic capabilities such as tool use and multi-step reasoning. Despite striking empirical successes, most notably Visual Agentic Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (Visual-ARFT), the theoretical underpinnings of this paradigm remain poorly understood. In particular, two critical questions lack rigorous answers: (i)~how does the composite structure of verifiable rewards (format compliance, answer accuracy, tool executability) affect the convergence of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), and (ii)~why does training on a small set of tool-augmented tasks transfer to out-of-distribution domains? We address these gaps by introducing the \emph{Tool-Augmented Markov Decision Process} (TA-MDP), a formal framework that models multimodal agentic decision-making with bounded-depth tool calls. Within this framework, we establish three main results. First, we prove that GRPO under composite verifiable rewards converges to a first-order stationary point at rate $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ with explicit dependence on the number of reward components and group size (\textbf{Theorem~1}). Second, we derive a \emph{Reward Decomposition Theorem} that bounds the sub-optimality gap between decomposed per-component optimization and joint optimization, providing a precise characterization of when reward decomposition is beneficial (\textbf{Theorem~2}).
