Third, we establish a PAC-Bayes generalization bound for tool-augmented policies that explains the strong out-of-distribution transfer observed in Visual-ARFT (\textbf{Theorem~3}).

AI.tldr: TA-MDP framework for GRPO with composite verifiable rewards; establishes three main results: (1) convergence of GRPO to a first-order stationary point at rate O(1/√T) with explicit dependence on reward components and group size; (2) Reward Decomposition Theorem bounding sub-optimality when optimizing per-component rewards versus joint optimization; (3) PAC-Bayes generalization bound for tool-augmented policies explaining strong OOD transfer observed in Visual-ARFT.
AI.motivation: Explain theoretical underpinnings of reinforcement fine-tuning with verifiable rewards for vision-language agentic systems, addressing two gaps: (i) how composite reward structure affects convergence, and (ii) why training on a small set of tool-augmented tasks transfers to new domains.
AI.method: Introduce Tool-Augmented Markov Decision Process (TA-MDP) modeling multimodal agentic decision-making with bounded-depth tool calls. Analyze Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) under composite rewards and derive three formal results: convergence rate, reward decomposition bound, and PAC-Bayes generalization bound.
AI.result: Theorems 1–3: (1) GRPO with composite verifiable rewards converges to a first-order stationary point at rate O(1/√T), depending on the number of reward components and group size; (2) Reward Decomposition Theorem quantifies when decomposing rewards per component is beneficial by bounding the sub-optimality gap relative to joint optimization; (3) PAC-Bayes generalization bound for tool-augmented policies accounts for OOD transfer observed in Visual-ARFT.
AI.conclusion: Provides rigorous guarantees for RLVR with tool use, clarifies conditions under which reward decomposition improves optimization, and offers theoretical justification for robust OOD generalization in tool-augmented LVLM fine-tuning.

title: Automated Detection of Dosing Errors in Clinical Trial Narratives: A Multi-Modal Feature Engineering Approach with LightGBM
summary: Clinical trials require strict adherence to medication protocols, yet dosing errors remain a persistent challenge affecting patient safety and trial integrity. We present an automated system for detecting dosing errors in unstructured clinical trial narratives using gradient boosting with comprehensive multi-modal feature engineering. Our approach combines 3,451 features spanning traditional NLP (TF-IDF, character n-grams), dense semantic embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6v2), domain-specific medical patterns, and transformer-based scores (BiomedBERT, DeBERTa-v3), used to train a LightGBM model. Features are extracted from nine complementary text fields (median 5,400 characters per sample) ensuring complete coverage across all 42,112 clinical trial narratives. On the CT-DEB benchmark dataset with severe class imbalance (4.9% positive rate), we achieve 0.8725 test ROC-AUC through 5-fold ensemble averaging (cross-validation: 0.8833 + 0.0091 AUC). Systematic ablation studies reveal that removing sentence embeddings causes the largest performance degradation (2.39%), demonstrating their critical role despite contributing only 37.07% of total feature importance. Feature efficiency analysis demonstrates that selecting the top 500-1000 features yields optimal performance (0.886-0.887 AUC), outperforming the full 3,451-feature set (0.879 AUC) through effective noise reduction. Our findings highlight the importance of feature selection as a regularization technique and demonstrate that sparse lexical features remain complementary to dense representations for specialized clinical text classification under severe class imbalance.

AI.tldr: A gradient-boosted, multi-modal feature ensemble (3,451 features) detects dosing errors in unstructured clinical trial narratives, achieving strong ROC-AUC on CT-DEB with notable ablations highlighting sentence embeddings and feature selection.
AI.motivation: To improve patient safety and trial integrity by automatically identifying dosing errors in free-text trial narratives, addressing the challenge of errors in unstructured data with severe class imbalance.
AI.method: Developed a LightGBM model trained on 3,451 features extracted from nine text fields, combining traditional NLP (TF-IDF, char n-grams), dense embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6v2), domain-specific patterns, and transformer-based scores (BiomedBERT, DeBERTa-v3). Used 5-fold ensemble averaging on CT-DEB with strong class imbalance. Performed ablation to assess feature importance and found sentence embeddings most critical; conducted feature selection to identify optimal 500–1000 features for improved performance.
AI.result: On CT-DEB with 4.9% positives, achieved 0.8725 test ROC-AUC (5-fold ensemble; cross-validation 0.8833 ± 0.0091). Ablation: removing sentence embeddings reduces performance by 2.39%. Feature selection: top 500–1000 features yield AUC 0.886–0.887, surpassing full 3,451-feature set (0.879).
AI.conclusion: Dense and sparse features are complementary; sentence embeddings are crucial despite not being the largest feature contributor; effective feature selection acts as regularization under severe class imbalance in specialized clinical text classification.

title: Stochastic Barrier Certificates in the Presence of Dynamic Obstacles
summary: Safety of stochastic dynamic systems in environments with dynamic obstacles is studied in this paper through the lens of stochastic barrier functions. We introduce both time-invariant and time-varying barrier certificates for discrete-time, continuous-space systems subject to uncertainty, which provide certified lower bounds on the probability of remaining within a safe set over a finite horizon. These certificates explicitly account for time-varying unsafe regions induced by obstacle dynamics. By leveraging Bellman's optimality perspective, the time-varying formulation directly captures temporal structure and yields less conservative bounds than state-of-the-art approaches. By restricting certificates to polynomial functions, we show that time-varying barrier synthesis can be formulated as a convex sum-of-squares program, enabling tractable optimization. Empirical evaluations on nonlinear systems with dynamic obstacles show that time-varying certificates consistently achieve tight guarantees, demonstrating improved accuracy and scalability over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: Time-varying barrier certificates provide less conservative finite-horizon probabilistic safety guarantees for stochastic discrete-time systems with dynamic obstacles, using SOS optimization over polynomial barriers.
AI.motivation: To obtain certified lower bounds on the probability of remaining in a safe set when unsafe regions evolve over time due to obstacle dynamics, addressing conservatism and time-variability.
AI.method: Introduce both time-invariant and time-varying barrier certificates for discrete-time, continuous-space systems with uncertainty. Restrict barriers to polynomials and formulate the synthesis as a convex sum-of-squares program, leveraging Bellman's optimality to capture temporal structure of dynamic obstacles.
AI.result: Time-varying certificates yield tighter, scalable probabilistic safety guarantees compared to state-of-the-art methods; empirical evaluations on nonlinear systems with dynamic obstacles demonstrate improved accuracy and computational tractability.
AI.conclusion: Time-varying barrier synthesis via polynomial SOS is a tractable approach that delivers tighter finite-horizon safety guarantees in environments with dynamic obstacles, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and scalability.

title: MMCORE: MultiModal COnnection with Representation Aligned Latent Embeddings
summary:
We present MMCORE, a unified framework designed for multimodal image generation and editing. MMCORE leverages a pre-trained Vision-Language Model (VLM) to predict semantic visual embeddings via learnable query tokens, which subsequently serve as conditioning signals for a diffusion model. This streamlined design effectively transfers the rich understanding and reasoning capabilities of VLMs into the visual generation process. By obviating the need for deep fusion between autoregressive and diffusion models or training from scratch, MMCORE significantly reduces computational overhead while maintaining high-fidelity synthesis.
  MMCORE seamlessly integrates text-to-image synthesis with interleaved image generation, demonstrating robust multimodal comprehension in complex scenarios such as spatial reasoning and visual grounding. Comprehensive evaluations indicate that MMCORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across a broad spectrum of text-to-image and single/multi-image editing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MMCORE is a unified multimodal image generation/editing framework that uses a pre-trained Vision-Language Model to generate semantic embeddings via learnable query tokens, conditioning a diffusion model, enabling efficient, high-fidelity generation and editing without training from scratch.
AI.motivation: To overcome computational overhead and integration challenges of combining autoregressive and diffusion models, and to leverage the rich reasoning of VLMs for multimodal understanding, enabling robust spatial reasoning and grounding in generation.
AI.method: Utilize a pre-trained Vision-Language Model to produce semantic visual embeddings through learnable query tokens; these embeddings condition a diffusion-based generator; support text-to-image and interleaved image generation; avoid training from scratch and deep fusion to reduce compute.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across broad text-to-image and single/multi-image editing benchmarks; demonstrates robust multimodal comprehension, including spatial reasoning and visual grounding.
AI.conclusion: MMCORE effectively transfers VLM reasoning into visual generation, offering a streamlined, computation-efficient design with high-fidelity synthesis and a unified framework for multimodal image generation and editing.

title: DR-Venus: Towards Frontier Edge-Scale Deep Research Agents with Only 10K Open Data
summary: Edge-scale deep research agents based on small language models are attractive for real-world deployment due to their advantages in cost, latency, and privacy. In this work, we study how to train a strong small deep research agent under limited open-data by improving both data quality and data utilization. We present DR-Venus, a frontier 4B deep research agent for edge-scale deployment, built entirely on open data. Our training recipe consists of two stages. In the first stage, we use agentic supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to establish basic agentic capability, combining strict data cleaning with resampling of long-horizon trajectories to improve data quality and utilization. In the second stage, we apply agentic reinforcement learning (RL) to further improve execution reliability on long-horizon deep research tasks. To make RL effective for small agents in this setting, we build on IGPO and design turn-level rewards based on information gain and format-aware regularization, thereby enhancing supervision density and turn-level credit assignment. Built entirely on roughly 10K open-data, DR-Venus-4B significantly outperforms prior agentic models under 9B parameters on multiple deep research benchmarks, while also narrowing the gap to much larger 30B-class systems. Our further analysis shows that 4B agents already possess surprisingly strong performance potential, highlighting both the deployment promise of small models and the value of test-time scaling in this setting. We release our models, code, and key recipes to support reproducible research on edge-scale deep research agents.

AI.tldr: A 4B open-data trained agent (DR-Venus) uses two-stage training (SFT then RL) to match or approach 9B and 30B-model performance on deep research benchmarks, highlighting edge-scale viability and data-quality/utilization gains.
AI.motivation: Enable cost-efficient, low-latency, private-edge deployment of deep research agents by leveraging limited open data through improved data quality and utilization.
AI.method: Stage 1: agentic supervised fine-tuning with strict data cleaning and resampling of long-horizon trajectories. Stage 2: agentic reinforcement learning using IGPO with turn-level rewards based on information gain and format-aware regularization, improving supervision density and turn-level credit assignment.
AI.result: DR-Venus-4B, trained on ~10K open data, significantly outperforms prior agentic models at 9B and narrows the gap to 30B-class systems on multiple deep research benchmarks; highlights strong performance potential of small models and benefits of test-time scaling.
AI.conclusion: Small-edge models can achieve strong agentic capabilities with carefully designed data and training recipes; the work supports deploying 4B agents in edge settings and provides reproducible resources (models, code, recipes).

title: Inference Headroom Ratio: A Diagnostic and Control Framework for Inference Stability Under Constraint
comment: Resubmission with revisions addressing moderator concerns regarding distinction from signal-to-noise metrics and structural dependence in simulation design. See updated Section 4.4 for clarification
summary: We present a simulation-based evaluation of the Inference Headroom Ratio (IHR), a dimensionless diagnostic quantity for characterizing inference stability in constrained decision systems. IHR formalizes the relationship between a system's effective inferential capacity C and the combined uncertainty and constraint load U + K imposed by its operating environment, and is intended to capture proximity to an inference stability boundary rather than output-level performance. Across three controlled experiments, we show that IHR functions as: (1) a quantifiable risk indicator whose relationship to collapse probability follows a well-fitted logistic curve with estimated critical threshold IHR* approx. 1.19, (2) a sensitive indicator of proximity to the inference stability boundary under environmental noise, and (3) a viable control variable whose active regulation reduces system collapse rate from 79.4% to 58.7% and IHR variance by 70.4% across 300 Monte Carlo runs. These results position IHR as a prospective, system-level complement to standard performance, drift, and uncertainty metrics, enabling estimation of remaining inferential margin before overt failure in AI systems operating under distributional shift and constraint.

AI.tldr: IHR is a dimensionless diagnostic of inference stability. Simulations show a logistic link to collapse probability with a critical threshold near 1.19; regulated IHR reduces collapses and variance, suggesting it complements traditional metrics for stability under shifts.
AI.motivation: To characterize stability of inference in constrained decision systems by formalizing how effective inferential capacity competes with environmental uncertainty and constraint load, capturing proximity to an inference stability boundary rather than raw output performance.
AI.method: Simulation-based evaluation across three controlled experiments. Compute IHR from C and U+K, fit a logistic model to collapse probability, test environmental noise effects, and apply active regulation; 300 Monte Carlo runs.
AI.result: Collapse probability follows a logistic curve with IHR* ≈ 1.19. IHR is sensitive to proximity to instability under noise. Active regulation lowers collapse rate from 79.4% to 58.7% and reduces IHR variance by 70.4% over 300 runs.
AI.conclusion: IHR offers a viable system-level metric to estimate remaining inferential margin before failure, complementing performance, drift, and uncertainty metrics, especially under distributional shift and constraint.

title: Toward Cooperative Driving in Mixed Traffic: An Adaptive Potential Game-Based Approach with Field Test Verification
summary: Connected autonomous vehicles (CAVs), which represent a significant advancement in autonomous driving technology, have the potential to greatly increase traffic safety and efficiency through cooperative decision-making. However, existing methods often overlook the individual needs and heterogeneity of cooperative participants, making it difficult to transfer them to environments where they coexist with human-driven vehicles (HDVs).To address this challenge, this paper proposes an adaptive potential game (APG) cooperative driving framework. First, the system utility function is established on the basis of a general form of individual utility and its monotonic relationship, allowing for the simultaneous optimization of both individual and system objectives. Second, the Shapley value is introduced to compute each vehicle's marginal utility within the system, allowing its varying impact to be quantified. Finally, the HDV preference estimation is dynamically refined by continuously comparing the observed HDV behavior with the APG's estimated actions, leading to improvements in overall system safety and efficiency. Ablation studies demonstrate that adaptively updating Shapley values and HDV preference estimation significantly improve cooperation success rates in mixed traffic. Comparative experiments further highlight the APG's advantages in terms of safety and efficiency over other cooperative methods. Moreover, the applicability of the approach to real-world scenarios was validated through field tests.

AI.tldr: Adaptive potential game (APG) framework for mixed-traffic cooperative driving that uses Shapley values to quantify each vehicle's marginal utility and dynamically refines HDV preferences, improving safety and efficiency; validated via simulations and field tests.
AI.motivation: Address heterogeneity among cooperative participants (CAVs and HDVs) and transferability to mixed traffic environments; align individual objectives with system-wide safety and efficiency.
AI.method: Define a general, monotone utility-based system; compute Shapley values for marginal contributions; dynamically refine HDV preference estimation by comparing observed HDV behavior to APG actions; ablation studies and comparisons; field tests.
AI.result: Ablation studies show significant improvements in cooperation success rates; APG outperforms other cooperative methods in safety and efficiency; field tests confirm applicability to real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: APG provides a viable framework for heterogeneous mixed-traffic cooperative driving, with adaptive Shapley values and HDV preference estimation enhancing performance and safety; suitable for real-world deployment.

title: SceneOrchestra: Efficient Agentic 3D Scene Synthesis via Full Tool-Call Trajectory Generation
summary: Recent agentic frameworks for 3D scene synthesis have advanced realism and diversity by integrating heterogeneous generation and editing tools. These tools are organized into workflows orchestrated by an off-the-shelf LLM. Current approaches typically adopt an execute-review-reflect loop: at each step, the orchestrator executes a tool, renders intermediate results for review, and then decides on the tool and its parameters for the next step. However, this design has two key limitations. First, next-step tool selection and parameter configuration are driven by heuristic rules, which can lead to suboptimal execution flows, unnecessary tool invocations, degraded output quality, and increased runtime. Second, rendering and reviewing intermediate results after each step introduces additional latency. To address these issues, we propose SceneOrchestra, a trainable orchestration framework that optimizes the tool-call execution flow and eliminates the step-by-step review loop, improving both efficiency and output quality. SceneOrchestra consists of an orchestrator and a discriminator, which we fine-tune with a two-phase training strategy. In the first phase, the orchestrator learns context-aware tool selection and complete tool-call trajectory generation, while the discriminator is trained to assess the quality of full trajectories, enabling it to select the best trajectory from multiple candidates. In the second phase, we perform interleaved training, where the discriminator adapts to the orchestrator's evolving trajectory distribution and distills its discriminative capability back into the orchestrator. At inference, we only use the orchestrator to generate and execute full tool-call trajectories from instructions, without requiring the discriminator. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art scene quality while reducing runtime compared to previous work.

AI.tldr: SceneOrchestra trains a learnable orchestrator (with a discriminator during training) to optimize full tool-call trajectories for 3D scene synthesis, removing the per-step review loop and improving quality and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Conventional execute-review-reflect loops rely on heuristic next-step tool choices and incur latency due to rendering/intermediate reviews, limiting quality and speed.
AI.method: Two-phase training: Phase 1 jointly trains the orchestrator to generate context-aware tool-call trajectories and trains a discriminator to score trajectory quality, enabling selection of the best trajectory among candidates. Phase 2 interleaved training where the discriminator adapts to the evolving trajectory distribution and distills its knowledge back into the orchestrator. Inference uses only the trained orchestrator to produce and execute full trajectories without the discriminator.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art scene quality and reduced runtime compared to prior methods, validating improved efficiency and output fidelity.
AI.conclusion: A trainable orchestrator, aided by a discriminative evaluator during training, can outperform heuristic, stepwise pipelines by optimizing end-to-end tool trajectories and removing the review loop.

title: Super Apriel: One Checkpoint, Many Speeds
comment: Models: https://huggingface.co/ServiceNow-AI/SuperApriel-15B-Base and https://huggingface.co/ServiceNow-AI/SuperApriel-15B-Instruct . Dev model: https://huggingface.co/ServiceNow-AI/SuperApriel-0.5B-Base . Training code: https://github.com/ServiceNow/Fast-LLM . Async RL: https://github.com/ServiceNow/pipeline-rl . Training logs: https://wandb.ai/servicenow-team/Super_Apriel
summary: We release Super Apriel, a 15B-parameter supernet in which every decoder layer provides four trained mixer choices -- Full Attention (FA), Sliding Window Attention (SWA), Kimi Delta Attention (KDA), and Gated DeltaNet (GDN). A placement selects one mixer per layer; placements can be switched between requests at serving time without reloading weights, enabling multiple speed presets from a single checkpoint. The shared checkpoint also enables speculative decoding without a separate draft model. The all-FA preset matches the Apriel 1.6 teacher on all reported benchmarks; recommended hybrid presets span $2.9\times$ to $10.7\times$ decode throughput at 96% to 77% quality retention, with throughput advantages that compound at longer context lengths. With four mixer types across 48 layers, the configuration space is vast. A surrogate that predicts placement quality from the per-layer mixer assignment makes the speed-quality landscape tractable and identifies the best tradeoffs at each speed level. We investigate whether the best configurations at each speed level can be identified early in training or only after convergence. Rankings stabilize quickly at 0.5B scale, but the most efficient configurations exhibit higher instability at 15B, cautioning against extrapolation from smaller models. Super Apriel is trained by stochastic distillation from a frozen Apriel 1.6 teacher, followed by supervised fine-tuning. We release the supernet weights, Fast-LLM training code, vLLM serving code, and a placement optimization toolkit.

AI.tldr: A 15B supernet with per-layer mixer choices (FA, SWA, KDA, GDN) enables serving-time speed presets from a single checkpoint; speculative decoding and a placement surrogate enable efficient exploration; all-FA matches a teacher; significant speedups (2.9x–10.7x) with modest quality loss; trained via stochastic distillation; release of weights and toolkits.
AI.motivation: Enable flexible, multi-speed deployment from a single model checkpoint, reducing the need for multiple draft models and enabling speculative decoding.
AI.method: Train a 15B supernet where each of 48 decoder layers has four mixers; use a placement that selects one mixer per layer; allow dynamic switching of placements at serving time; train via stochastic distillation from a frozen Apriel 1.6 teacher, followed by supervised fine-tuning; develop a surrogate to predict per-layer placement quality to map speed-quality tradeoffs; evaluate stability of configurations at different scales; release code and weights and tooling.
AI.result: An all-FA preset matches the teacher on reported benchmarks; hybrid presets yield 2.9x–10.7x decode throughput with 77%–96% quality retention; throughput advantages compound at longer context; the surrogate identifies high-quality configurations; stability of configurations depends on model size; 0.5B shows stable rankings, 15B shows higher instability; weights and tooling released.
AI.conclusion: Multi-mixer supernets can efficiently navigate speed-quality tradeoffs from a single checkpoint, enabling flexible deployment and speculative decoding; cautions include instability at large scales and limited extrapolation from smaller models; released resources to reproduce and apply the approach.

title: EvoForest: A Novel Machine-Learning Paradigm via Open-Ended Evolution of Computational Graphs
summary: Modern machine learning is still largely organized around a single recipe: choose a parameterized model family and optimize its weights. Although highly successful, this paradigm is too narrow for many structured prediction problems, where the main bottleneck is not parameter fitting but discovering what should be computed from the data. Success often depends on identifying the right transformations, statistics, invariances, interaction structures, temporal summaries, gates, or nonlinear compositions, especially when objectives are non-differentiable, evaluation is cross-validation-based, interpretability matters, or continual adaptation is required. We present EvoForest, a hybrid neuro-symbolic system for end-to-end open-ended evolution of computation. Rather than merely generating features, EvoForest jointly evolves reusable computational structure, callable function families, and trainable low-dimensional continuous components inside a shared directed acyclic graph. Intermediate nodes store alternative implementations, callable nodes encode reusable transformation families such as projections, gates, and activations, output nodes define candidate predictive computations, and persistent global parameters can be refined by gradient descent. For each graph configuration, EvoForest evaluates the discovered computation and uses a lightweight Ridge-based readout to score the resulting representation against a non-differentiable cross-validation target. The evaluator also produces structured feedback that guides future LLM-driven mutations. In the 2025 ADIA Lab Structural Break Challenge, EvoForest reached 94.13% ROC-AUC after 600 evolution steps, exceeding the publicly reported winning score of 90.14% under the same evaluation protocol.

AI.tldr: EvoForest is a hybrid neuro-symbolic system that evolves computation graphs and trainable components via open-ended evolution, guided by LLM-driven mutations and non-differentiable cross-validation targets, achieving state-of-the-art ROC-AUC.
AI.motivation: Structured prediction tasks often require discovering what to compute (transformations, invariances, interactions, temporal summaries) rather than just fitting parameters. Open-ended evolution and neuro-symbolic schemas help explore this design space, especially with non-differentiable objectives and interpretability needs, using feedback to guide mutations.
AI.method: EvoForest forms a directed acyclic graph where intermediate nodes store alternative implementations, callable nodes encode reusable transformation families (projections, gates, activations), and output nodes define predictive computations. Global parameters are refined by gradient descent. For each graph, a lightweight Ridge readout evaluates the representation against a non-differentiable cross-validation target. The evaluator provides structured feedback guiding future LLM-driven mutations. The system ran 600 evolution steps.
AI.result: Reached 94.13% ROC-AUC after 600 steps, surpassing the publicly reported winning score of 90.14% under the same evaluation protocol in the 2025 ADIA Lab Structural Break Challenge.
AI.conclusion: The results suggest that open-ended evolution of computation graphs with reusable symbolic components, combined with gradient-based refinement and LLM-guided mutation, can surpass traditional single-parameter optimization methods on structured prediction tasks, enabling automatic discovery of effective computation pipelines.

title: Cortex 2.0: Grounding World Models in Real-World Industrial Deployment
summary: Industrial robotic manipulation demands reliable long-horizon execution across embodiments, tasks, and changing object distributions. While Vision-Language-Action models have demonstrated strong generalization, they remain fundamentally reactive. By optimizing the next action given the current observation without evaluating potential futures, they are brittle to the compounding failure modes of long-horizon tasks. Cortex 2.0 shifts from reactive control to plan-and-act by generating candidate future trajectories in visual latent space, scoring them for expected success and efficiency, then committing only to the highest-scoring candidate. We evaluate Cortex 2.0 on a single-arm and dual-arm manipulation platform across four tasks of increasing complexity: pick and place, item and trash sorting, screw sorting, and shoebox unpacking. Cortex 2.0 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action baselines, achieving the best results across all tasks. The system remains reliable in unstructured environments characterized by heavy clutter, frequent occlusions, and contact-rich manipulation, where reactive policies fail. These results demonstrate that world-model-based planning can operate reliably in complex industrial environments.

AI.tldr: Cortex 2.0 uses world-model-based planning in visual latent space to generate and evaluate candidate future trajectories, selecting the best plan for reliable long-horizon robotic manipulation, outperforming Vision-Language-Action baselines across four tasks.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language-Action models are reactive and struggle with long-horizon tasks, leading to brittle behavior and error accumulation in cluttered, contact-rich industrial settings.
AI.method: Plan-and-act pipeline: generate multiple candidate trajectories in a learned visual latent space, score by predicted success and efficiency, commit to the highest-scoring plan; evaluated on single-arm and dual-arm platforms across four tasks (pick/place, item/trash sorting, screw sorting, shoebox unpacking).
AI.result: Cortex 2.0 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action baselines; achieves best results across all tasks; robust in unstructured environments with clutter, occlusions, and contact-rich manipulation where reactive policies fail.
AI.conclusion: World-model-based planning can enable reliable long-horizon manipulation in complex industrial environments.

title: UniCon3R: Contact-aware 3D Human-Scene Reconstruction from Monocular Video
summary: We introduce UniCon3R (Unified Contact-aware 3D Reconstruction), a unified feed-forward framework for online human-scene 4D reconstruction from monocular videos. Recent feed-forward methods enable real-time world-coordinate human motion and scene reconstruction, but they often produce physically implausible artifacts such as bodies floating above the ground or penetrating parts of the scene. The key reason is that existing approaches fail to model physical interactions between the human and the environment. A natural next step is to predict human-scene contact as an auxiliary output -- yet we find this alone is not sufficient: contact must actively correct the reconstruction. To address this, we explicitly model interaction by inferring 3D contact from the human pose and scene geometry and use the contact as a corrective cue for generating the final pose. This enables UniCon3R to jointly recover high-fidelity scene geometry and spatially aligned 3D humans within the scene. Experiments on standard human-centric video benchmarks such as RICH, EMDB, 3DPW and SLOPER4D show that UniCon3R outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on physical plausibility and global human motion estimation while achieving real-time online inference. We experimentally demonstrate that contact serves as a powerful internal prior rather than just an external metric, thus establishing a new paradigm for physically grounded joint human-scene reconstruction. Project page is available at https://surtantheta.github.io/UniCon3R .

AI.tldr: Unified contact-aware 3D reconstruction framework (UniCon3R) for online monocular 4D human-scene reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Address physically implausible artifacts in real-time monocular human-scene reconstruction by modeling human-scene interaction through contact.
AI.method: Infer 3D contact from human pose and scene geometry and use it as a corrective cue for final pose; jointly estimate high-fidelity scene geometry and spatially aligned 3D humans in the scene; real-time feed-forward pipeline.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on physical plausibility and global motion estimation on datasets RICH, EMDB, 3DPW, and SLOPER4D; demonstrates that contact serves as a powerful internal prior rather than external metric; enables online inference.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling contact enables physically grounded, unified 3D reconstruction of humans and scenes; establishes a paradigm where contact informs pose correction and scene recovery in real time.

title: A Multi-Plant Machine Learning Framework for Emission Prediction, Forecasting, and Control in Cement Manufacturing
summary: Cement production is among the largest contributors to industrial air pollution, emitting ~3 Mt NOx/year. The industry-standard mitigation approach, selective non-catalytic reduction (SNCR), exhibits low NH3 utilization efficiency, resulting in operational inefficiencies and increased reagent costs. Here, we develop a data-driven framework for emission control using large-scale operational data from four cement plants worldwide. Benchmarking nine machine learning architectures, we observe that prediction error varies ~3-5x across plants due to variation in data richness. Incorporating short-term process history nearly triples NOx prediction accuracy, revealing that NOx formation carries substantial process memory, a timescale dependence that is absent in CO and CO2. Further, we develop models that forecast NOx overshoots as early as nine minutes, providing a buffer for operational adjustments. The developed framework controls NOx formation at the source, reducing NH3 consumption in downstream SNCR. Surrogate model projections estimate a ~34-64% reduction in NOx while preserving clinker quality, corresponding to a reduction of ~290 t NOx/year and ~58,000 USD/year in NH3 savings. This work establishes a generalizable framework for data-driven emission control, offering a pathway toward low-emission operation without structural modifications or additional hardware, with potential applicability to other hard-to-abate industries such as steel, glass, and lime.

AI.tldr: A data-driven emission-control framework for cement plants that predicts NOx with ML, leverages short-term process history, forecasts NOx overshoots up to 9 minutes, and reduces NH3 usage in SNCR, achieving 34-64% NOx reductions (~290 t/year) while preserving clinker quality, suggesting broad applicability to other hard-to-abate industries.
AI.motivation: Cement production is a major NOx source with inefficient SNCR; reducing NOx and NH3 costs without hardware changes addresses emissions, operating expenses, and process efficiency.
AI.method: Benchmark nine ML architectures on large-scale, multi-plant data (four plants), analyze data richness across plants, incorporate short-term process history to capture memory effects in NOx formation, develop nine-minute overshoot forecasts, and construct surrogate control models that curb NOx at source and reduce downstream NH3 usage.
AI.result: Inter-plant NOx prediction error varies 3–5x due to data richness; including short-term history nearly triples NOx prediction accuracy; nine-minute overshoot forecasts enable proactive control; estimated NOx reductions of 34–64% (~290 t/year) and NH3 savings of ~58,000 USD/year, with preserved clinker quality.
AI.conclusion: A generalizable, data-driven emission-control framework that reduces emissions and reagent use without structural changes, with potential applicability to steel, glass, and lime industries.

title: From Actions to Understanding: Conformal Interpretability of Temporal Concepts in LLM Agents
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents capable of reasoning, planning, and acting within interactive environments. Despite their growing capability to perform multi-step reasoning and decision-making tasks, internal mechanisms guiding their sequential behavior remain opaque. This paper presents a framework for interpreting the temporal evolution of concepts in LLM agents through a step-wise conformal lens. We introduce the conformal interpretability framework for temporal tasks, which combines step-wise reward modeling with conformal prediction to statistically label model's internal representation at each step as successful or failing. Linear probes are then trained on these representations to identify directions of temporal concepts - latent directions in the model's activation space that correspond to consistent notions of success, failure or reasoning drift. Experimental results on two simulated interactive environments, namely ScienceWorld and AlfWorld, demonstrate that these temporal concepts are linearly separable, revealing interpretable structures aligned with task success. We further show preliminary results on improving an LLM agent's performance by leveraging the proposed framework for steering the identified successful directions inside the model. The proposed approach, thus, offers a principled method for early failure detection as well as intervention in LLM-based agents, paving the path towards trustworthy autonomous language models in complex interactive settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces a step-wise conformal interpretability framework for temporal tasks in LLM agents that labels each action step as success/failure via conformal prediction, learns linear probes to identify temporal concept directions, and demonstrates linear separability and potential interventions in ScienceWorld and AlfWorld.
AI.motivation: Address the opacity of sequential internal mechanisms in LLM agents by providing a principled, statistically grounded method to interpret temporal evolution of concepts and enable early failure detection and intervention.
AI.method: Combine step-wise reward modeling with conformal prediction to label the model's internal representations at each step as successful or failing. Train linear probes on these representations to identify latent temporal directions corresponding to success, failure, or reasoning drift. Validate in ScienceWorld and AlfWorld; show linear separability of temporal concepts and explore steering successful directions to improve performance.
AI.result: Temporal concepts are linearly separable in the studied environments; latent directions corresponding to success, failure, and reasoning drift are identifiable via linear probes; preliminary improvements in agent performance achieved by steering identified successful directions.
AI.conclusion: Presents a principled framework for interpretable temporal reasoning in LLM-based agents, enabling early failure detection and intervention, and contributing to trustworthy autonomous language models in interactive settings.

title: Onboard Wind Estimation for Small UAVs Equipped with Low-Cost Sensors: An Aerodynamic Model-Integrated Filtering Approach
summary: To enable autonomous wind estimation for energy-efficient flight in small unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), this study proposes a method that estimates flight states and wind using only the low-cost essential onboard sensors required for autonomous flight, without relying on additional wind measurement devices. The core of the method includes an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) integrated with the aerodynamic model and an Adaptive Moving Average Estimation (AMAE) technique, which improves the accuracy and smoothness of the wind estimation. Simulation results show that the approach efficiently estimates both steady and time-varying 3D wind vectors without requiring flow angle measurements. The impact of aerodynamic model accuracy on wind estimation errors is also analyzed to assess practical applicability. Flight tests validate the effectiveness of the method and its feasibility for real-time onboard computation. Additionally, uncertainties and error sources encountered during testing are systematically examined, providing a foundation for further refinement.

AI.tldr: An EKF-based wind estimation method for small UAVs using only onboard sensors, enhanced by Adaptive Moving Average Estimation (AMAE) to estimate steady/time-varying 3D wind vectors in real time; validated via simulations and flight tests with analysis of model accuracy and uncertainties.
AI.motivation: Provide autonomous wind estimation for energy-efficient UAV flight without extra wind sensors.
AI.method: Integrates Extended Kalman Filter with an aerodynamic model and Adaptive Moving Average Estimation to estimate 3D wind using low-cost onboard sensors; does not require flow angle measurements.
AI.result: Accurately estimates steady and time-varying 3D winds; capable of real-time onboard computation; validated in simulation and flight tests; uncertainty sources analyzed.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of onboard real-time wind estimation; provides pathways for refinement considering model accuracy and uncertainties.

title: Infection-Reasoner: A Compact Vision-Language Model for Wound Infection Classification with Evidence-Grounded Clinical Reasoning
summary: Assessing chronic wound infection from photographs is challenging because visual appearance varies across wound etiologies, anatomical locations, and imaging conditions. Prior image-based deep learning methods have mainly focused on classification with limited interpretability, despite the need for evidence-grounded explanations to support point-of-care decision making. We present Infection-Reasoner, a compact 4B-parameter reasoning vision-language model for chronic wound infection classification and rationale generation. To address the scarcity of expert-labeled wound images with reasoning annotations, Infection-Reasoner is trained using a two-stage pipeline: (1) reasoning distillation, in which GPT-5.1 generates chain-of-thought rationales for unlabeled wound images to initialize wound-specific reasoning in a smaller student model (Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking), and (2) reinforcement learning post-training with Group Relative Policy Optimization on a small labeled infection dataset to refine classification reasoning. On a held-out heterogeneous wound dataset, Infection-Reasoner achieved 86.8\% accuracy, 86.4\% sensitivity, and 87.1\% specificity, outperforming several strong baselines, including GPT-5.1. Rationale quality was further evaluated using both multimodal large language model (MLLM) judges and wound expert review. Across four MLLM judges, visual-support agreement scores ranged from 0.722 to 0.903, while expert review rated 61.8\% of rationales as Correct and 32.4\% as Partially Correct.

AI.tldr: Infection-Reasoner: a compact 4B-parameter vision-language model for chronic wound infection classification with rationale generation using a two-stage distillation and RL approach, achieving strong accuracy and plausible explanations on diverse wounds.
AI.motivation: Need for accurate, interpretable infection assessment from wound photographs across etiologies, locations, and imaging conditions; prior methods lack interpretability and evidence-grounded explanations.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) reasoning distillation where GPT-5.1 generates chain-of-thought rationales for unlabeled images to initialize a smaller student model (Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking); (2) reinforcement learning post-training with Group Relative Policy Optimization on a small labeled infection dataset to refine reasoning and classification.
AI.result: On a held-out heterogeneous wound dataset: 86.8% accuracy, 86.4% sensitivity, 87.1% specificity; outperforms baselines including GPT-5.1. Rationale quality: visual-support agreement 0.722–0.903 across four MLLM judges; expert review: 61.8% Correct, 32.4% Partially Correct.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a compact reasoning VLM can achieve competitive infection detection and generate evidence-grounded rationales, with two-stage distillation and RL enabling data-efficient training and interpretability in wound care imaging.

title: Physics-Guided Dimension Reduction for Simulation-Free Operator Learning of Stiff Differential--Algebraic Systems
summary: Neural surrogates for stiff differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) face two key challenges: soft-constraint methods leave algebraic residuals that stiffness amplifies into large errors, while hard-constraint methods require trajectory data from computationally expensive stiff integrators. We introduce an extended Newton implicit layer that enforces algebraic consistency and quasi-steady-state reduction within a single differentiable solve. Given slow-state predictions from a physics-informed DeepONet, the proposed layer recovers fast and algebraic states, eliminates the stiffness-amplification pathway within each time window, and reduces the output dimension to the slow states alone. Gradients derived via the implicit function theorem capture a stiffness-scaled coupling term that is absent in penalty-based approaches. Cascaded implicit layers further extend the framework to multi-component systems with provable convergence. On a grid-forming inverter DAE (21 states), the proposed method (7 outputs, 1.42 percent error) significantly outperforms penalty methods (39.3 percent), standard Newton approaches (57.0 percent), and augmented Lagrangian or feedback linearization baselines, which fail to converge. Two independently trained models compose into a 44-state system without retraining, achieving 0.72 to 1.16 percent error with zero algebraic residual. Conformal prediction further provides 90 percent coverage in-distribution and enables automatic out-of-distribution detection.

AI.tldr: Extended Newton implicit layer for neural surrogates of stiff DAEs; enforces algebraic consistency and quasi-steady-state reduction within a differentiable solve; yields accurate slow-state surrogates, reduces dimensionality, and scales via cascaded layers; includes conformal prediction for uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Soft-constraint methods leave algebraic residuals that stiffness amplifies into large errors, while hard-constraint methods require computationally expensive stiff integrators. A differentiable solver that enforces algebraic constraints and quasi-steady-state reduction is needed.
AI.method: Propose an extended Newton implicit layer that uses slow-state predictions from a physics-informed DeepONet to recover fast and algebraic states within a single differentiable solve. Derivatives via the implicit-function theorem capture a stiffness-scaled coupling term. Cascaded implicit layers extend to multi-component systems; conformal prediction provides uncertainty quantification.
AI.result: On a grid-forming inverter DAE with 21 states, the method achieves 7 outputs with 1.42% error, outperforming penalty methods (39.3%), standard Newton approaches (57.0%), and baselines (augmented Lagrangian/feedback linearization) that fail to converge. Two independently trained models compose into a 44-state system with 0.72–1.16% error and zero algebraic residual. Conformal prediction yields 90% coverage in-distribution and enables automatic out-of-distribution detection.
AI.conclusion: The extended Newton implicit layer enables accurate, convergent neural surrogates for stiff DAEs, with algebraic consistency enforced in a differentiable solve and potential for provable convergence when cascading layers. It also provides reliable uncertainty quantification via conformal prediction.

title: Using Learning Theories to Evolve Human-Centered XAI: Future Perspectives and Challenges
comment: Accepted at the CHI 2023 Human-Centered XAI workshop
summary: As Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems continue to grow in size and complexity, so does the difficulty of the quest for AI transparency. In a world of large models and complex AI systems, why do we explain AI and what should we explain? While explanations serve multiple functions, in the face of complexity humans have used and continue to use explanations to foster learning. In this position paper, we discuss how learning theories can be infused in the XAI lifecycle, as well as the key opportunities and challenges when adopting a learner-centered approach to assess, design and evaluate AI explanations. Building on past work, we argue that a learner-centered approach to Explainable AI (XAI) can enhance human agency and ease XAI risks mitigation, helping evolve the practice of human-centered XAI.

AI.tldr: The authors argue for embedding learning theories into the XAI lifecycle and adopting a learner-centered approach to explanations to boost human agency and mitigate XAI risks.
AI.motivation: As AI systems grow in size and complexity, transparency becomes harder; explanations carry learning value, and a learner-centered perspective may improve how explanations are assessed, designed, and used.
AI.method: A conceptual position paper that synthesizes prior work and proposes a framework for infusing learning theories into the XAI lifecycle, detailing opportunities and challenges.
AI.result: Proposes a learner-centered XAI framework; identifies opportunities and challenges for designing, evaluating, and mitigating risks in AI explanations; emphasizes enhancement of learning and human agency.
AI.conclusion: A learner-centered XAI approach can advance human-centered practices, elevate user agency, and strengthen risk mitigation, calling for integrating learning theories into XAI practice.

title: ETac: A Lightweight and Efficient Tactile Simulation Framework for Learning Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Tactile sensors are increasingly integrated into dexterous robotic manipulators to enhance contact perception. However, learning manipulation policies that rely on tactile sensing remains challenging, primarily due to the trade-off between fidelity and computational cost of soft-body simulations. To address this, we present ETac, a tactile simulation framework that models elastomeric soft-body interactions with both high fidelity and efficiency. ETac employs a lightweight data-driven deformation propagation model to capture soft-body contact dynamics, achieving high simulation quality and boosting efficiency that enables large-scale policy training. When serving as the simulation backend, ETac produces surface deformation estimates comparable to FEM and demonstrates applicability for modeling real tactile sensors. Then, we showcase its capability in training a blind grasping policy that leverages large-area tactile feedback to manipulate diverse objects. Running on a single RTX 4090 GPU, ETac supports reinforcement learning across 4,096 parallel environments, achieving a total throughput of 869 FPS. The resulting policy reaches an average success rate of 84.45% across four object types, underscoring ETac's potential to make tactile-based skill learning both efficient and scalable.

AI.tldr: ETac is a high-fidelity yet efficient tactile simulation framework for elastomeric soft-body interactions, enabling large-scale policy learning with tactile sensing and achieving 84.5% average success and 869 FPS on 4096 parallel environments.
AI.motivation: To overcome the fidelity–computational-cost trade-off in soft-body tactile simulation and enable scalable reinforcement learning for tactile-based manipulation.
AI.method: ETac uses a lightweight data-driven deformation propagation model as the simulation backend, providing surface deformation estimates comparable to FEM; GPU-accelerated implementation that supports modeling real tactile sensors and training across 4,096 parallel environments on an RTX 4090, achieving 869 FPS.
AI.result: High-fidelity deformation estimates close to FEM; scalable RL with 4,096 parallel environments at 869 FPS; policy attains 84.45% average success across four object types.
AI.conclusion: ETac offers a practical, scalable solution for tactile-based skill learning, balancing fidelity and efficiency and supporting realistic tactile sensor modeling.

title: CrackForward: Context-Aware Severity Stage Crack Synthesis for Data Augmentation
summary: Reliable crack detection and segmentation are vital for structural health monitoring, yet the scarcity of well-annotated data constitutes a major challenge. To address this limitation, we propose a novel context-aware generative framework designed to synthesize realistic crack growth patterns for data augmentation. Unlike existing methods that primarily manipulate textures or background content, CrackForward explicitly models crack morphology by combining directional crack elongation with learned thickening and branching. Our framework integrates two key innovations: (i) a contextually guided crack expansion module, which uses local directional cues and adaptive random walk to simulate realistic propagation paths; and (ii) a two-stage U-Net-style generator that learns to reproduce spatially varying crack characteristics such as thickness, branching, and growth. Experimental results show that the generated samples preserve target-stage saturation and thickness characteristics and improve the performance of several crack segmentation architectures. These results indicate that structure-aware synthetic crack generation can provide more informative training data than conventional augmentation alone.

AI.tldr: CrackForward: a context-aware generative framework that synthesizes realistic crack growth for data augmentation by modeling crack morphology (directional elongation, thickening, branching) using a context-guided expansion module and a two-stage U-Net, improving crack segmentation performance.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of well-annotated crack data for structural health monitoring; propose morphology-aware synthetic data to enhance segmentation models.
AI.method: 1) Contextually guided crack expansion: uses local directional cues and adaptive random walk to simulate realistic crack propagation paths. 2) Two-stage U-Net generator: learns spatially varying crack characteristics (thickness, branching, growth) to produce crack representations with target-stage saturation.
AI.result: Generated samples preserve target-stage saturation and thickness and improve performance across several crack segmentation architectures; demonstrates structure-aware synthesis yields more informative data than conventional augmentation.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware synthetic crack generation can significantly enhance data augmentation for crack segmentation by explicitly modeling crack morphology rather than relying on texture/background manipulation.

title: Generalization and Membership Inference Attack a Practical Perspective
summary: With the emergence of new evaluation metrics and attack methodologies for Membership Inference Attacks (MIA), it becomes essential to reevaluate previously accepted assumptions. In this paper, we revisit the longstanding debate regarding the correlation between MIA success rates and model generalization using an empirical approach. We focused on employing augmentation techniques and early stopping to enhance model generalization and examined their impact on MIA success rates. We found that utilizing advanced generalization techniques can significantly decrease attack performance, potentially by up to 100 times. Moreover, combining these methods not only improves model generalization but also reduces attack effectiveness by introducing randomness during training. Additionally, our study confirmed the direct impact of generalization on MIA performance through an analysis of over 1K models in a controlled environment.

AI.tldr: Advanced generalization techniques reduce MIA success; the presumed simple link between generalization and privacy risk is challenged by more nuanced interactions.
AI.motivation: Reevaluate the claimed correlation between model generalization and membership inference risk in the presence of modern augmentation and training randomness.
AI.method: Empirical study across 1k+ models in a controlled environment, applying augmentation strategies and early stopping to improve generalization; introduce randomness during training; assess MIA performance under these settings.
AI.result: Generalization techniques can drastically lower MIA success (up to ~100x); combining augmentation with training randomness reduces attack effectiveness; large-scale analysis corroborates a direct, but complex, impact of generalization on MIA.
AI.conclusion: The relationship between generalization and MIA is nuanced; strong generalization can mitigate privacy risk, but simple correlations may be overstated. Further broad evaluation across datasets, attack types, and training regimes is warranted.

title: From Data to Theory: Autonomous Large Language Model Agents for Materials Science
summary: We present an autonomous large language model (LLM) agent for end-to-end, data-driven materials theory development. The model can choose an equation form, generate and run its own code, and test how well the theory matches the data without human intervention. The framework combines step-by-step reasoning with expert-supplied tools, allowing the agent to adjust its approach as needed while keeping a clear record of its decisions. For well-established materials relationships such as the Hall-Petch equation and Paris law, the agent correctly identifies the governing equation and makes reliable predictions on new datasets. For more specialized relationships, such as Kuhn's equation for the HOMO-LUMO gap of conjugated molecules as a function of length, performance depends more strongly on the underlying model, with GPT-5 showing better recovery of the correct equation. Beyond known theories, the agent can also suggest new predictive relationships, illustrated here by a strain-dependent law for changes in the HOMO-LUMO gap. At the same time, the results show that careful validation remains essential, because the agent can still return incorrect, incomplete, or inconsistent equations even when the numerical fit appears strong. Overall, these results highlight both the promise and the current limitations of autonomous LLM agents for AI-assisted scientific modeling and discovery.

AI.tldr: An autonomous LLM agent performs end-to-end, data-driven materials theory development: selecting equation forms, generating/running code, and testing against data without human input; it succeeds on known relationships, shows variable performance on specialized ones, can propose new predictive laws, but requires careful validation due to potential inconsistencies.
AI.motivation: Enable AI-driven, human-in-the-loop-free discovery and validation of material theories, reducing manual derivation and coding burden while enabling rapid exploration of both established and novel relationships.
AI.method: An autonomous agent that combines step-by-step reasoning with expert-supplied tools to choose equation forms, generate and execute code, and evaluate fit to data. It tests known relationships (Hall-Petch, Paris law) and specialized ones (Kuhnans Kuhn's equation for HOMO-LUMO gap) and can propose new strain-dependent predictive laws. The approach leverages large language models (including GPT-5) to recover governing equations and validate results, emphasizing transparent decision trails.
AI.result: The agent correctly identifies governing equations for well-established relationships and makes reliable predictions on new datasets. For specialized relationships, performance depends on the underlying model, with GPT-5 showing better recovery of the correct equation. It can suggest new predictive relationships beyond established theories. However, validation remains essential as the agent can yield incorrect, incomplete, or inconsistent equations despite strong numerical fits.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous LLM agents hold promise for AI-assisted scientific modeling and discovery in materials science, but current limitations—especially around validation and potential equation quality—necessitate rigorous checks and human oversight.

title: AdaTracker: Learning Adaptive In-Context Policy for Cross-Embodiment Active Visual Tracking
summary: Realizing active visual tracking with a single unified model across diverse robots is challenging, as the physical constraints and motion dynamics vary drastically from one platform to another. Existing approaches typically train separate models for each embodiment, leading to poor scalability and limited generalization. To address this, we propose AdaTracker, an adaptive in-context policy learning framework that robustly tracks targets on diverse robot morphologies. Our key insight is to explicitly model embodiment-specific constraints through an Embodiment Context Encoder, which infers embodiment-specific constraints from history. This contextual representation dynamically modulates a Context-Aware Policy, enabling it to infer optimal control actions for unseen embodiments in a zero-shot manner. To enhance robustness, we introduce two auxiliary objectives to ensure accurate context identification and temporal consistency. Experiments in both simulation and the real world demonstrate that AdaTracker significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in cross-embodiment generalization, sample efficiency, and zero-shot adaptation.

AI.tldr: AdaTracker learns an embodiment-aware tracker with a single model by encoding embodiment constraints from history and conditioning policy, enabling zero-shot adaptation across unseen robot morphologies.
AI.motivation: Existing cross-embodiment tracking methods require training separate models per embodiment, causing poor scalability and limited generalization. A unified policy capable of adapting to diverse morphologies is highly desirable.
AI.method: Introduce Embodiment Context Encoder to infer embodiment-specific constraints from tracking history. This context modulates a Context-Aware Policy to produce control actions for unseen embodiments in a zero-shot manner. Add two auxiliary objectives to improve context identification and temporal consistency.
AI.result: Empirical results in simulation and real-world experiments show AdaTracker outperforms state-of-the-art methods in cross-embodiment generalization, sample efficiency, and zero-shot adaptation.
AI.conclusion: A single unified model with embodiment-aware conditioning enables robust active visual tracking across diverse robots, improving scalability and generalization.

title: Visual Reasoning through Tool-supervised Reinforcement Learning
summary: In this paper, we investigate the problem of how to effectively master tool-use to solve complex visual reasoning tasks for Multimodal Large Language Models. To achieve that, we propose a novel Tool-supervised Reinforcement Learning (ToolsRL) framework, with direct tool supervision for more effective tool-use learning. We focus on a series of simple, native, and interpretable visual tools, including zoom-in, rotate, flip, and draw point/line, whose tool supervision is easy to collect. A reinforcement learning curriculum is developed, where the first stage is solely optimized by a set of well motivated tool-specific rewards, and the second stage is trained with the accuracy targeted rewards while allowing calling tools. In this way, tool calling capability is mastered before using tools to complete visual reasoning tasks, avoiding the potential optimization conflict among those heterogeneous tasks. Our experiments have shown that the tool-supervised curriculum training is efficient and ToolsRL can achieve strong tool-use capabilities for complex visual reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: A tool-supervised reinforcement learning framework (ToolsRL) enables multimodal LLMs to master simple visual tools via a two-stage curriculum, prioritizing tool calling before tool-based reasoning; claims efficient training and strong tool-use on complex visual tasks.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal models struggle to learn effective and controllable tool usage. Direct tool supervision combined with curriculum RL aims to align tool-use behavior with visual reasoning objectives and reduce optimization conflicts between tool calling and reasoning.
AI.method: Propose ToolsRL with native visual tools (zoom-in, rotate, flip, draw point/line) and direct tool supervision. Implement a two-stage curriculum: stage 1 optimizes tool-specific rewards; stage 2 optimizes accuracy-based rewards while allowing tool calls.
AI.result: Experiments show efficient training and that ToolsRL achieves strong tool-use capabilities for complex visual reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Tool-supervised curriculum learning can effectively instill controllable tool use in multimodal LLMs, with potential applicability to broader tool-enabled reasoning tasks.

title: Are LLM Uncertainty and Correctness Encoded by the Same Features? A Functional Dissociation via Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Large language models can be uncertain yet correct, or confident yet wrong, raising the question of whether their output-level uncertainty and their actual correctness are driven by the same internal mechanisms or by distinct feature populations. We introduce a 2x2 framework that partitions model predictions along correctness and confidence axes, and uses sparse autoencoders to identify features associated with each dimension independently. Applying this to Llama-3.1-8B and Gemma-2-9B, we identify three feature populations that play fundamentally different functional roles. Pure uncertainty features are functionally essential: suppressing them severely degrades accuracy. Pure incorrectness features are functionally inert: despite showing statistically significant activation differences between correct and incorrect predictions, the majority produce near-zero change in accuracy when suppressed. Confounded features that encode both signals are detrimental to output quality, and targeted suppression of them yields a 1.1% accuracy improvement and a 75% entropy reduction, with effects transferring across the ARC-Challenge and RACE benchmarks. The feature categories are also informationally distinct: the activations of just 3 confounded features from a single mid-network layer predict model correctness (AUROC ~0.79), enabling selective abstention that raises accuracy from 62% to 81% at 53% coverage. The results demonstrate that uncertainty and correctness are distinct internal phenomena, with implications for interpretability and targeted inference-time intervention.

AI.tldr: A 2x2 framework separates LLM output into correctness and confidence, identifying three distinct feature populations via sparse autoencoders. Pure uncertainty features are essential for accuracy; pure incorrectness features are inert; confounded features hurt output quality. Suppressing confounded features improves accuracy and reduces entropy, with cross-benchmark transfer and potential abstention-based accuracy gains.
AI.motivation: To determine whether uncertainty and correctness in LLM outputs arise from the same internal representations or separate feature populations, enabling targeted inference-time interventions.
AI.method: Propose a 2x2 partitioning of predictions along correctness (correct/incorrect) and confidence (uncertainty/confidence). Use sparse autoencoders to extract feature populations associated with each dimension from mid-to-late network layers. Apply the framework to two models, Llama-3.1-8B and Gemma-2-9B, and assess the functional role of each feature population via targeted suppression experiments and cross-benchmark transfer (ARC-Challenge, RACE).
AI.result: Identified three feature populations with distinct roles. Pure uncertainty features are functionally essential (suppressing them degrades accuracy). Pure incorrectness features are functionally inert (statistical differences but little impact when suppressed). Confounded features (encoding both signals) are detrimental; suppressing them yields a 1.1% accuracy improvement and 75% entropy reduction, with transfer across ARC-Challenge and RACE. Three confounded features from a mid-network layer can predict model correctness (AUROC ~0.79), enabling selective abstention that raises accuracy from 62% to 81% at 53% coverage.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty and correctness are separable internal phenomena, suggesting distinct feature populations that have implications for interpretability and inference-time intervention in LLMs.

title: Hidden Reliability Risks in Large Language Models: Systematic Identification of Precision-Induced Output Disagreements
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed under diverse numerical precision configurations, including standard floating-point formats (e.g., bfloat16 and float16) and quantized integer formats (e.g., int16 and int8), to meet efficiency and resource constraints. However, minor inconsistencies between LLMs of different precisions are difficult to detect and are often overlooked by existing evaluation methods. In this paper, we present PrecisionDiff, an automated differential testing framework for systematically detecting precision-induced behavioral disagreements in LLMs. PrecisionDiff generates precision-sensitive test inputs and performs cross-precision comparative analysis to uncover subtle divergences that remain hidden under conventional testing strategies. To demonstrate its practical significance, we instantiate PrecisionDiff on the alignment verification task, where precision-induced disagreements manifest as jailbreak divergence-inputs that are rejected under one precision may produce harmful responses under another. Experimental results show that such behavioral disagreements are widespread across multiple open-source aligned LLMs and precision settings, and that PrecisionDiff significantly outperforms vanilla testing methods in detecting these issues. Our work enables automated precision-sensitive test generation, facilitating effective pre-deployment evaluation and improving precision robustness during training.

AI.tldr: PrecisionDiff is an automated differential testing framework that detects precision-induced behavioral disagreements in LLMs across numeric precisions by generating precision-sensitive inputs and cross-precision comparisons, demonstrated on alignment verification where jailbreak divergences occur; it outperforms vanilla testing methods and aids pre-deployment robustness.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are deployed with diverse precision formats to optimize efficiency, small inconsistencies across precisions can go unnoticed; existing tests miss subtle, precision-sensitive behavior differences that can affect safety and reliability.
AI.method: Generate precision-sensitive test inputs; perform cross-precision comparative analysis to reveal divergences; instantiate on alignment verification to identify jailbreak divergence-inputs; evaluate across open-source aligned LLMs and precision settings.
AI.result: PrecisionDiff uncovers widespread precision-induced disagreements across multiple open-source aligned LLMs and settings and significantly outperforms vanilla testing methods in detection.
AI.conclusion: Automated precision-sensitive test generation enables effective pre-deployment evaluation and improves precision robustness during training.

title: A Vision-Language-Action Model for Adaptive Ultrasound-Guided Needle Insertion and Needle Tracking
summary: Ultrasound (US)-guided needle insertion is a critical yet challenging procedure due to dynamic imaging conditions and difficulties in needle visualization. Many methods have been proposed for automated needle insertion, but they often rely on hand-crafted pipelines with modular controllers, whose performance degrades in challenging cases. In this paper, a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model is proposed for adaptive and automated US-guided needle insertion and tracking on a robotic ultrasound (RUS) system. This framework provides a unified approach to needle tracking and needle insertion control, enabling real-time, dynamically adaptive adjustment of insertion based on the obtained needle position and environment awareness. To achieve real-time and end-to-end tracking, a Cross-Depth Fusion (CDF) tracking head is proposed, integrating shallow positional and deep semantic features from the large-scale vision backbone. To adapt the pretrained vision backbone for tracking tasks, a Tracking-Conditioning (TraCon) register is introduced for parameter-efficient feature conditioning. After needle tracking, an uncertainty-aware control policy and an asynchronous VLA pipeline are presented for adaptive needle insertion control, ensuring timely decision-making for improved safety and outcomes. Extensive experiments on both needle tracking and insertion show that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art trackers and manual operation, achieving higher tracking accuracy, improved insertion success rates, and reduced procedure time, highlighting promising directions for RUS-based intelligent intervention.

AI.tldr: Presents a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework for real-time, adaptive US-guided needle insertion on a robotic ultrasound system, featuring a Cross-Depth Fusion (CDF) tracking head and Tracking-Conditioning (TraCon) for efficient feature conditioning, along with an uncertainty-aware control policy and asynchronous VLA pipeline to improve tracking and insertion performance.
AI.motivation: Automated US-guided needle insertion is hindered by dynamic imaging conditions and poor needle visualization. Traditional hand-crafted pipelines with modular controllers struggle in challenging scenarios; a unified, end-to-end approach could enhance robustness and safety.
AI.method: Introduces a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model for adaptive needle tracking and insertion control on a robotic ultrasound (RUS) system. Key components include a Cross-Depth Fusion (CDF) tracking head that fuses shallow positional and deep semantic features from a large vision backbone, a Tracking-Conditioning (TraCon) register for parameter-efficient feature conditioning of the backbone, and an uncertainty-aware control policy with an asynchronous VLA pipeline for real-time decision-making.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art trackers and manual operation, achieving higher tracking accuracy, improved insertion success rates, and reduced procedure time.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unified, real-time approach for adaptive needle tracking and insertion on RUS, suggesting promising directions for intelligent, safe, and efficient ultrasound-guided interventions.

title: Camera Control for Text-to-Image Generation via Learning Viewpoint Tokens
summary: Current text-to-image models struggle to provide precise camera control using natural language alone. In this work, we present a framework for precise camera control with global scene understanding in text-to-image generation by learning parametric camera tokens. We fine-tune image generation models for viewpoint-conditioned text-to-image generation on a curated dataset that combines 3D-rendered images for geometric supervision and photorealistic augmentations for appearance and background diversity. Qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while preserving image quality and prompt fidelity. Unlike prior methods that overfit to object-specific appearance correlations, our viewpoint tokens learn factorized geometric representations that transfer to unseen object categories. Our work shows that text-vision latent spaces can be endowed with explicit 3D camera structure, offering a pathway toward geometrically-aware prompts for text-to-image generation. Project page: https://randdl.github.io/viewtoken_control/

AI.tldr: A framework for precise, viewpoint-controlled text-to-image generation using parametric camera tokens learned during fine-tuning, combining 3D geometric supervision with photorealistic augmentations to achieve state-of-the-art viewpoint accuracy and generalize to unseen objects by factorizing geometry into transferable representations.
AI.motivation: Current T2I models lack precise, controllable camera manipulation via natural language. Introducing explicit 3D camera structure into the text-vision latent space enables geometrically-aware prompts and more reliable viewpoint control.
AI.method: Fine-tune image generation models with viewpoint-conditioned prompts using parametric camera tokens. Build a dataset that blends 3D-rendered images (for geometric supervision) with photorealistic augmentations (for appearance/background diversity). Train to learn factorized geometric representations that map to camera parameters and transfer across unseen object categories.
AI.result: Qualitative and quantitative experiments show state-of-the-art accuracy in controlling viewpoint while preserving image quality and prompt fidelity. Viewpoint tokens generalize to unseen objects by disentangling geometry from object appearance, enabling transfer across categories.
AI.conclusion: Text-vision latent spaces can be endowed with explicit 3D camera structure throughLearned viewpoint tokens, paving the way for geometrically-aware prompts in T2I generation and broader controllability across scenes and objects.

title: Fast Amortized Fitting of Scientific Signals Across Time and Ensembles via Transferable Neural Fields
summary: Neural fields, also known as implicit neural representations (INRs), offer a powerful framework for modeling continuous geometry, but their effectiveness in high-dimensional scientific settings is limited by slow convergence and scaling challenges. In this study, we extend INR models to handle spatiotemporal and multivariate signals and show how INR features can be transferred across scientific signals to enable efficient and scalable representation across time and ensemble runs in an amortized fashion.
