Across controlled transformation regimes (e.g., geometric transformations and localized perturbations of synthetic fields) and high-fidelity scientific domains-including turbulent flows, fluid-material impact dynamics, and astrophysical systems-we show that transferable features improve not only signal fidelity but also the accuracy of derived geometric and physical quantities, including density gradients and vorticity. In particular, transferable features reduce iterations to reach target reconstruction quality by up to an order of magnitude, increase early-stage reconstruction quality by multiple dB (with gains exceeding 10 dB in some cases), and consistently improve gradient-based physical accuracy.

AI.tldr: Extends implicit neural representations (INRs) to spatiotemporal and multivariate signals, enabling transferable features across time and ensembles for efficient, scalable scientific modeling with improved fidelity and physics accuracy.
AI.motivation: INRs are powerful for continuous geometry but struggle with high-dimensional scientific data due to slow convergence and poor scaling. Transferring features across signals and amortized representations can address these bottlenecks, enabling rapid, accurate reconstructions and reliable physics quantities.
AI.method: Extend INR architectures to handle spatiotemporal and multivariate signals; learn transferable feature representations that can be amortized across time and ensemble runs. Evaluate under controlled transformations (geometric changes, localized perturbations) and real scientific domains (turbulent flows, fluid-material interactions, astrophysical systems). Compare reconstruction quality, iteration counts, and physics-derived metrics such as density gradients and vorticity.
AI.result: Transferable INR features yield faster convergence (up to an order of magnitude fewer iterations to target quality), higher early-stage reconstruction quality (multi-dB gains, sometimes >10 dB), and more accurate gradient-based physical quantities (e.g., density gradients, vorticity). These features generalize across time and ensembles, improving both signal fidelity and derived physics metrics.
AI.conclusion: Transferable, amortized INR features enable scalable, accurate representations of high-dimensional scientific signals across time and ensembles, reducing optimization burden and enhancing physics fidelity, with broad applicability to turbulent, fluid-material, and astrophysical systems.

title: Stabilising Generative Models of Attitude Change
summary: Attitude change - the process by which individuals revise their evaluative stances - has been explained by a set of influential but competing verbal theories. These accounts often function as mechanism sketches: rich in conceptual detail, yet lacking the technical specifications and operational constraints required to run as executable systems. We present a generative actor-based modelling workflow for "rendering" these sketches as runnable actor - environment simulations using the Concordia simulation library. In Concordia, actors operate by predictive pattern completion: an operation on natural language strings that generates a suffix which describes the actor's intended action from a prefix containing memories of their past and observations of the present. We render the theories of cognitive dissonance (Festinger 1957), self-consistency (Aronson 1969), and self-perception (Bem 1972) as distinct decision logics that populate and process the prefix through theory-specific sequences of reasoning steps. We evaluate these implementations across classic psychological experiments. Our implementations generate behavioural patterns consistent with known results from the original empirical literature. However, we find that achieving stable reproduction requires resolving the inherent underdetermination of the verbal accounts and the conflicts between modern linguistic priors and historical experimental assumptions. And, we document how this manual process of iterative model "stabilisation" surfaces specific operational and socio-ecological dependencies that were largely undocumented in the original verbal accounts. Ultimately, we argue that the manual stabilisation process itself should be regarded as a core part of the methodology functioning to clarify situational and representational commitments needed to generate characteristic effects.

AI.tldr: A study that translates verbal attitude-change theories into runnable agent-based models using Concordia; compares cognitive dissonance, self-consistency, and self-perception; simulations reproduce classic effects but require manual stabilisation, revealing hidden dependencies; proposes stabilisation as integral to the methodology.
AI.motivation: Bridge rich verbal mechanism sketches of attitude change to executable, testable models; address the lack of formal specifications and operational constraints; investigate underdetermination and dependencies between priors and historical data.
AI.method: Use the Concordia agent-based framework; predictive pattern completion where agents generate action suffixes from memories/present prefixes; implement cognitive dissonance, self-consistency, and self-perception as distinct decision logics with theory-specific reasoning sequences; run simulations on classic experiments; iteratively stabilise models.
AI.result: Implementations produce behavioral patterns consistent with original literature; stable reproduction requires addressing underdetermination and reconciling linguistic priors with historical experimental assumptions; the stabilisation process reveals operational and socio-ecological dependencies not documented in the verbal accounts.
AI.conclusion: Manual stabilisation is a core methodological component that clarifies commitments needed to generate characteristic effects and informs cross-theory comparison; supports a path toward more transparent, reproducible computational investigations of attitude change.

title: Bimanual Robot Manipulation via Multi-Agent In-Context Learning
summary: Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful reasoning engines for embodied control. In particular, In-Context Learning (ICL) enables off-the-shelf, text-only LLMs to predict robot actions without any task-specific training while preserving their generalization capabilities. Applying ICL to bimanual manipulation remains challenging, as the high-dimensional joint action space and tight inter-arm coordination constraints rapidly overwhelm standard context windows. To address this, we introduce BiCICLe (Bimanual Coordinated In-Context Learning), the first framework that enables standard LLMs to perform few-shot bimanual manipulation without fine-tuning. BiCICLe frames bimanual control as a multi-agent leader-follower problem, decoupling the action space into sequential, conditioned single-arm predictions. This naturally extends to Arms' Debate, an iterative refinement process, and to the introduction of a third LLM-as-Judge to evaluate and select the most plausible coordinated trajectories. Evaluated on 13 tasks from the TWIN benchmark, BiCICLe achieves up to 71.1% average success rate, outperforming the best training-free baseline by 6.7 percentage points and surpassing most supervised methods. We further demonstrate strong few-shot generalization on novel tasks.

AI.tldr: A few-shot, training-free bimanual manipulation framework BiCICLe uses in-context learning with LLMs, decoupling actions into sequential arm predictions, plus Debate and a Judge, achieving up to 71.1% success on 13 TWIN tasks and showing strong generalization.
AI.motivation: ICL with LLMs is powerful for closed-loop control but struggles in high-dimensional, tightly coordinated bimanual tasks due to large joint action spaces and context window limits; a training-free solution is highly desirable.
AI.method: Frame bimanual control as a multi-agent leader-follower problem and decompose the joint action space into sequential, conditioned single-arm predictions. Introduce Arms' Debate for iterative refinement and employ a third LLM-as-Judge to select the most plausible coordinated trajectories.
AI.result: Evaluated on 13 tasks from the TWIN benchmark; BiCICLe achieves up to 71.1% average success, outperforming the best training-free baseline by 6.7 percentage points and surpassing most supervised methods. Demonstrates strong few-shot generalization to novel tasks.
AI.conclusion: BiCICLe shows that standard LLMs can perform few-shot bimanual manipulation without fine-tuning by leveraging action-space decomposition, iterative refinement, and external judgment to coordinate arms effectively.

title: DistortBench: Benchmarking Vision Language Models on Image Distortion Identification
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used in settings where sensitivity to low-level image degradations matters, including content moderation, image restoration, and quality monitoring. Yet their ability to recognize distortion type and severity remains poorly understood. We present DistortBench, a diagnostic benchmark for no-reference distortion perception in VLMs. DistortBench contains 13,500 four-choice questions covering 27 distortion types, six perceptual categories, and five severity levels: 25 distortions inherit KADID-10k calibrations, while two added rotation distortions use monotonic angle-based levels. We evaluate 18 VLMs, including 17 open-weight models from five families and one proprietary model. Despite strong performance on high-level vision-language tasks, the best model reaches only 61.9% accuracy, just below the human majority-vote baseline of 65.7% (average individual: 60.2%), indicating that low-level perceptual understanding remains a major weakness of current VLMs. Our analysis further reveals weak and non-monotonic scaling with model size, performance drops in most base--thinking pairs, and distinct severity-response patterns across model families. We hope DistortBench will serve as a useful benchmark for measuring and improving low-level visual perception in VLMs.

AI.tldr: DistortBench is a no-reference distortion-perception benchmark for vision-language models (VLMs). It contains 13,500 four-choice questions across 27 distortion types, 6 perceptual categories, and 5 severity levels (25 distortions calibrated from KADID-10k; 2 rotation distortions with monotonic angle levels). Eighteen VLMs from five families plus one proprietary model were evaluated. The best model achieves 61.9% accuracy, below the human majority baseline of 65.7% (average human: 60.2%), indicating low-level perceptual understanding remains a weakness of current VLMs. The study finds weak and non-monotonic scaling with model size, performance drops in most base–thinking pairs, and distinct severity-response patterns across model families. DistortBench is proposed as a useful benchmark to measure and improve low-level visual perception in VLMs.
AI.motivation: To quantify and diagnose VLMs' ability to recognize distortion type and severity in images, addressing a gap where models excel at high-level tasks but struggle with low-level perceptual judgments.
AI.method: Construct DistortBench with 13,500 four-choice questions spanning 27 distortion types, 6 perceptual categories, and 5 severity levels. Calibrate 25 distortions using KADID-10k parameters and add two rotation distortions with monotonic angle-based levels. Evaluate 18 VLMs (17 open-weight from five families plus one proprietary) on the benchmark; analyze accuracy, scaling with model size, base–thinking pair performance, and severity-response patterns.
AI.result: Best-performing model: 61.9% accuracy; human majority baseline: 65.7%; average human: 60.2%. Indicates weak low-level perceptual understanding in current VLMs. Observed non-monotonic scaling with model size, drops in most base–thinking pairs, and distinct severity-response patterns across model families.
AI.conclusion: DistortBench provides a practical benchmark to measure and drive improvements in low-level visual perception for VLMs; current models lag behind humans on distortion perception, highlighting a clear research direction for enhancing fine-grained perceptual capabilities.

title: Benefits of Low-Cost Bio-Inspiration in the Age of Overparametrization
summary: While Central Pattern Generators (CPGs) and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP) are widely used paradigms in robot control, few systematic studies have been performed on the relative merits of large parameter spaces. In contexts where input and output spaces are small and performance is bounded, having more parameters to optimize may actively hinder the learning process instead of empowering it. To empirically measure this, we submit a given robot morphology, with limited proprioceptive capabilities, to controller optimization under two bio-inspired paradigms (CPGs and MLPs) with evolutionary- and reinforcement- trainer protocols. By varying parameter spaces across multiple reward functions, we observe that shallow MLPs and densely connected CPGs result in better performance when compared to deeper MLPs or Actor-Critic architectures. To account for the relationship between said performance and the number of parameters, we introduce a Parameter Impact metric which demonstrates that the additional parameters required by the reinforcement technique do not translate into better performance, thus favouring evolutionary strategies.

AI.tldr: Parameter-efficient controllers with evolutionary training outperform larger networks and Actor-Critic methods for a fixed robot morphology with limited sensing.
AI.motivation: To systematically assess how large parameter spaces affect learning in robot control when input/output spaces are small and performance is bounded, by comparing CPGs vs MLPs under evolutionary vs reinforcement-based training.
AI.method: Fix robot morphology with limited proprioception. Optimize controllers under two paradigms (CPGs and MLPs) and two trainer protocols (evolutionary and reinforcement). Vary parameter spaces across multiple reward functions. Introduce a Parameter Impact metric to relate parameter count to performance.
AI.result: Shallow MLPs and densely connected CPGs achieve better performance than deeper MLPs or Actor-Critic architectures. The reinforcement-based parameter growth does not translate to better performance; Parameter Impact demonstrates that extra parameters from reinforcement do not improve outcomes. Evolutionary strategies are favored.
AI.conclusion: For small, bounded control problems with limited sensing, simpler architectures and evolutionary training are advantageous. Adding parameters via reinforcement learning may hinder learning. The Parameter Impact metric provides a quantitative tool to assess parameter efficiency.

title: Lucky High Dynamic Range Smartphone Imaging
summary: While the human eye can perceive an impressive twenty stops of dynamic range, smartphone camera sensors remain limited to about twelve stops despite decades of research. A variety of high dynamic range (HDR) image capture and processing techniques have been proposed, and, in practice, they can extend the dynamic range by 3-5 stops for handheld photography. This paper proposes an approach that robustly captures dynamic range using a handheld smartphone camera and lightweight networks suitable for running on mobile devices. Our method operates indirectly on linear raw pixels in bracketed exposures. Every pixel in the final HDR image is a convex combination of input pixels in the neighborhood, adjusted for exposure, and thus avoids hallucination artifacts typical of recent deep image synthesis networks. We validate our system on both synthetic imagery and unseen real bracketed images -- we confirm zero-shot generalization of the method to smartphone camera captures. Our iterative inference architecture is capable of processing an arbitrary number of bracketed input photos, and we show examples from capture stacks containing 3--9 images. Our training process relies only on synthetic captures yet generalizes to unseen real photos from several cameras. Moreover, we show that this training scheme improves other SOTA methods over their pretrained counterparts.

AI.tldr: A lightweight HDR method for handheld smartphones uses iterative convex combinations of raw pixels from bracketed exposures to extend dynamic range, trained on synthetic data with zero-shot generalization to real cameras, avoiding hallucinations and supporting variable numbers of inputs (3–9).
AI.motivation: Close the HDR capability gap between human vision and mobile sensors by robust, artifact-free HDR capture on devices with limited computation.
AI.method: Operate in linear RAW space; final HDR pixel is a convex combination of neighboring input pixels adjusted for exposure. An iterative inference network handles a variable number of bracketed inputs (3–9). Training uses synthetic captures but generalizes to unseen real bracketed images from multiple cameras.
AI.result: Zero-shot generalization to smartphone captures demonstrated; the approach can handle 3–9 bracketed images; training on synthetic data improves state-of-the-art methods when transferred.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a stable, non-synthesizing HDR fusion framework suitable for mobile devices that generalizes across cameras and bracket counts, offering artifact safety and compatibility with limited hardware, though reliant on synthetic training data and neighborhood-based fusion.

title: Continuous Semantic Caching for Low-Cost LLM Serving
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) become increasingly popular, caching responses so that they can be reused by users with semantically similar queries has become a vital strategy for reducing inference costs and latency. Existing caching frameworks have proposed to decide which query responses to cache by assuming a finite, known universe of discrete queries and learning their serving costs and arrival probabilities. As LLMs' pool of users and queries expands, however, such an assumption becomes increasingly untenable: real-world LLM queries reside in an infinite, continuous embedding space. In this paper, we establish the first rigorous theoretical framework for semantic LLM response caching in continuous query space under uncertainty. To bridge the gap between discrete optimization and continuous representation spaces, we introduce dynamic $ε$-net discretization coupled with Kernel Ridge Regression. This design enables the system to formally quantify estimation uncertainty and generalize partial feedback on LLM query costs across continuous semantic query neighborhoods. We develop both offline learning and online adaptive algorithms optimized to reduce switching costs incurred by changing the cached responses. We prove that our online algorithm achieves a sublinear regret bound against an optimal continuous oracle, which reduces to existing bounds for discrete query models. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that our framework approximates the continuous optimal cache well while also reducing computational and switching overhead compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for semantic LLM response caching in continuous query space using dynamic ε-net discretization and Kernel Ridge Regression; online/offline learning with sublinear regret and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: As LLM queries form a continuous embedding space rather than a finite discrete set, existing discrete-cache models are inadequate. A rigorous framework under uncertainty is needed to exploit semantic similarity and partial feedback to reduce inference costs and latency.
AI.method: Introduce dynamic ε-net discretization to bridge continuous query space with discrete optimization, coupled with Kernel Ridge Regression to quantify estimation uncertainty and propagate feedback across neighborhoods. Develop offline learning and online adaptive caching algorithms that minimize switching costs. Establish a sublinear regret bound against a continuous oracle, recovering discrete bounds as a special case.
AI.result: The online algorithm achieves sublinear regret with respect to the optimal continuous oracle; empirical evaluations show the approach closely approximates the continuous optimum and reduces computational and switching overhead compared with existing methods.
AI.conclusion: First rigorous theoretical framework for semantic LLM response caching in continuous query space under uncertainty; demonstrates practical efficacy and efficiency, with potential for scalable deployment and extensions.

title: SkillGraph: Graph Foundation Priors for LLM Agent Tool Sequence Recommendation
summary: LLM agents must select tools from large API libraries and order them correctly. Existing methods use semantic similarity for both retrieval and ordering, but ordering depends on inter-tool data dependencies that are absent from tool descriptions. As a result, semantic-only methods can produce negative Kendall-$τ$ in structured workflow domains. We introduce SkillGraph, a directed weighted execution-transition graph mined from 49,831 successful LLM agent trajectories, which encodes workflow-precedence regularities as a reusable graph foundation prior. Building on this graph foundation prior, we propose a two-stage decoupled framework: GS-Hybrid retrieval for candidate selection and a learned pairwise reranker for ordering. On ToolBench (9,965 test instances; ~16,000 tools), the method reaches Set-F1 = 0.271 and Kendall-$τ$ = 0.096; on API-Bank, Kendall-$τ$ improves from -0.433 to +0.613. Under identical Stage-1 inputs, the learned reranker also outperforms LLaMA-3.1-8B Stage-2 rerankers.

AI.tldr: SkillGraph introduces a reusable execution-transition graph mined from thousands of LLM agent trajectories to capture workflow precedence for tool usage, enabling a decoupled two-stage framework that improves tool selection and ordering over semantic-only methods.
AI.motivation: Existing semantic-only retrieval for LLM tool usage fails to capture inter-tool data dependencies and workflow precedence, leading to negative Kendall-tau ordering in structured domains. A graph-based prior can encode recurring workflow regularities to guide both candidate retrieval and tool ordering.
AI.method: Mine an execution-transition graph (SkillGraph) from 49,831 successful LLM trajectories; use it as a reusable prior in a two-stage framework: (Stage 1) GS-Hybrid retrieval for candidate tool selection; (Stage 2) a learned pairwise reranker for ordering. Evaluation on ToolBench and API-Bank with metrics Set-F1 and Kendall-tau.
AI.result: On ToolBench (9,965 tests; ~16k tools): Set-F1=0.271; Kendall-tau=0.096. On API-Bank: Kendall-tau improves from -0.433 to +0.613. Under the same Stage-1 input, the learned reranker outperforms LLaMA-3.1-8B Stage-2 rerankers.
AI.conclusion: The SkillGraph-based prior and decoupled GS-Hybrid + learned reranker yield improved tool selection ordering over semantic-only baselines, demonstrating the value of workflow-precedence information for LLM-based tool usage and superior performance on multiple benchmarks.

title: OVPD: A Virtual-Physical Fusion Testing Dataset of OnSite Auton-omous Driving Challenge
summary: The rapid iteration of autonomous driving algorithms has created a growing demand for high-fidelity, replayable, and diagnosable testing data. However, many public datasets lack real vehicle dynamics feedback and closed-loop interaction with surrounding traffic and road infrastructure, limiting their ability to reflect deployment readiness. To address this gap, we present OVPD (OnSite Virtual-Physical Dataset), a virtual-physical fusion testing dataset released from the 2025 OnSite Autonomous Driving Challenge. Centered on real-vehicle-in-the-loop testing, OVPD integrates virtual background traffic with vehicle-infrastructure perception to build controllable and interactive closed-loop test environments on a proving ground. The dataset contains 20 testing clips from 20 teams over a scenario chain of 15 atomic scenarios, totaling nearly 3 hours of multi-modal data, including vehicle trajectories and states, control commands, and digital-twin-rendered surround-view observations. OVPD supports long-tail planning and decision-making validation, open-loop or platform-enabled closed-loop evaluation, and comprehensive assessment across safety, efficiency, comfort, rule compliance, and traffic impact, providing actionable evidence for failure diagnosis and iterative improvement. The dataset is available via: https://huggingface.co/datasets/Yuhang253820/Onsite_OPVD

AI.tldr: OVPD is a virtual-physical fusion dataset for real-vehicle-in-the-loop testing in autonomous driving, enabling controllable closed-loop evaluation with multi-modal data across 3 hours, 20 teams, and 15 atomic scenario chains.
AI.motivation: Public autonomous-driving datasets lack real vehicle dynamics feedback and closed-loop interaction with traffic and infrastructure, hindering deployment-readiness and robust planning/diagnosis.
AI.method: Developed OVPD by fusing virtual background traffic with vehicle‑infrastructure perception to create controllable, interactive closed-loop test environments on a proving ground. Collected 20 clips from 20 teams across 15 atomic scenarios (≈3 hours) with multi-modal data (trajectories, states, commands, digital-twin surround views). Released as part of the 2025 OnSite Autonomous Driving Challenge.
AI.result: OVPD enables long-tail planning/decision validation, supports open-loop and platform-enabled closed-loop evaluation, and allows comprehensive assessment of safety, efficiency, comfort, rule compliance, and traffic impact, providing actionable evidence for failure diagnosis and iterative improvement.
AI.conclusion: OVPD fills a critical gap in testing data for deployment readiness by offering controllable, diagnostically rich, real-vehicle-in-the-loop datasets and enabling robust evaluation and failure diagnosis across diverse scenarios.

title: Online CS-based SAR Edge-Mapping
comment: SPIE Defense and Commercial Sensing 2026, Algorithms for Synthetic Aperture Radar Imagery XXXIII
summary: With modern defense applications increasingly relying on inexpensive, small Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), a major challenge lies in designing intelligent and computationally efficient onboard Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) algorithms to carry out operational objectives. This is especially critical in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), where processing techniques such as ATR are often carried out post data collection, requiring onboard systems to bear the memory burden of storing the back-scattered signals. To alleviate this high cost, we propose an online, direct, edge-mapping technique which bypasses the image reconstruction step to classify scenes and targets. Furthermore, by reconstructing the scene as an edge-map we inherently promote sparsity, requiring fewer measurements and computational power than classic SAR reconstruction algorithms such as backprojection.

AI.tldr: Proposes online edge-mapping for SAR ATR that bypasses full image reconstruction, enabling on-board, memory-efficient scene/target classification via sparse edge representations, reducing measurements and computation compared to backprojection.
AI.motivation: Urgent need for low-cost, onboard ATR in small UAVs with strict memory and power limits; traditional SAR ATR relies on post-collection reconstruction and heavy storage of back-scattered signals, hindering real-time operation.
AI.method: Develops an online, direct edge-mapping pipeline that bypasses image reconstruction and classifies scenes/targets from edge-map representations; leverages sparsity to reduce required measurements and computation relative to classic SAR reconstruction (e.g., backprojection).
AI.result: Anticipated improvements include reduced memory/storage requirements and computation for onboard processing, with maintained or improved classification performance due to sparse edge representations.
AI.conclusion: Edge-mapping SAR ATR provides a viable, efficient alternative for real-time onboard target recognition in UAVs by eliminating full reconstruction and exploiting sparsity through edge-based scene representations.

title: Statistics, Not Scale: Modular Medical Dialogue with Bayesian Belief Engine
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous diagnostic agents, yet they conflate two fundamentally different capabilities: natural-language communication and probabilistic reasoning. We argue that this conflation is an architectural flaw, not an engineering shortcoming. We introduce BMBE (Bayesian Medical Belief Engine), a modular diagnostic dialogue framework that enforces a strict separation between language and reasoning: an LLM serves only as a sensor, parsing patient utterances into structured evidence and verbalising questions, while all diagnostic inference resides in a deterministic, auditable Bayesian engine. Because patient data never enters the LLM, the architecture is private by construction; because the statistical backend is a standalone module, it can be replaced per target population without retraining. This separation yields three properties no autonomous LLM can offer: calibrated selective diagnosis with a continuously adjustable accuracy-coverage tradeoff, a statistical separation gap where even a cheap sensor paired with the engine outperforms a frontier standalone model from the same family at a fraction of the cost, and robustness to adversarial patient communication styles that cause standalone doctors to collapse. We validate across empirical and LLM-generated knowledge bases against frontier LLMs, confirming the advantage is architectural, not informational.

AI.tldr: Separates language from reasoning in medical diagnostics via BMBE: a modular architecture where the LLM is only a sensor that parses patient utterances into structured evidence, while a deterministic Bayesian engine performs inference. This private, replaceable backend enables calibrated diagnosis, cost efficiency, and robustness, with empirical validation against frontier LLMs highlighting architectural advantages.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous LLMs conflate natural-language communication with probabilistic reasoning, leading to opaque, uncalibrated inference and privacy concerns. A clean architectural separation aims to enable auditable, target-population–specific inference and privacy guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce BMBE (Bayesian Medical Belief Engine): a modular diagnostic dialogue framework that uses an LLM as a sensor to extract structured evidence and generate questions, while a separate, deterministic Bayesian engine performs diagnostic inference. Patient data never enters the LLM; backend can be swapped per population without retraining; enables adjustable accuracy-coverage via the sensor–engine tradeoff.
AI.result: Three properties emerge: (1) calibrated selective diagnosis with a tunable accuracy–coverage tradeoff; (2) a separation gap where a cheap sensor plus engine outperforms a frontier standalone model at lower cost; (3) robustness to adversarial patient communication styles that challenge standalone clinicians. Empirical and LLM-generated knowledge-base validation against frontier LLMs confirms the advantage is architectural, not informational.
AI.conclusion: Architectural separation of language and reasoning in medical diagnostics yields privacy-by-design, replaceable backends, calibrated performance, and robustness, with improvements demonstrated against frontier LLMs indicating the benefit is structural rather than data-driven.

title: Handbook of Rough Set Extensions and Uncertainty Models
comment: 159 pages. Peer-Reviewed Book. ISBN: 978-1-59973-867-3. Publisher: Neutrosophic Science International Association (NSIA) Publishing House
summary: Rough set theory models uncertainty by approximating target concepts through lower and upper sets induced by indiscernibility, or more generally, by granulation relations in data tables. This perspective captures vagueness caused by limited observational resolution and supports set-theoretic reasoning about what can be determined with certainty and what remains only possible. This book is written as a map of models. Rather than developing a single algorithmic pipeline in depth, it provides a systematic survey of the main rough set paradigms and their extension routes. More specifically, representative variants are organized according to (i) the underlying granulation mechanism, such as equivalence-based, tolerance-based, covering-based, neighborhood-based, and probabilistic approximations, and (ii) the uncertainty semantics attached to data and relations, such as crisp, fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, and plithogenic settings. The book also explains how each choice changes the form of approximations and the interpretation of boundary regions. Throughout the book, small illustrative examples are used to clarify modeling intent and typical use cases in classification and decision support. Finally, an important clarification of scope should be noted. Since the main purpose of this book is to provide a map of models, the Abstract and Introduction should not lead readers to expect that feature reduction and rule induction are primary objectives. Although these topics are central in the rough set literature, they are treated here mainly as motivating applications and as entry points to the broader research landscape. The principal aim of the book is to survey and position rough set models and their extensions in a systematic and coherent manner.

AI.tldr: Systematic survey mapping the rough set literature: taxonomy by granulation (equivalence, tolerance, covering, neighborhood, probabilistic) and by uncertainty semantics (crisp, fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, plithogenic), with illustrative examples and a clarified scope that feature reduction/rule induction are not the primary focus.
AI.motivation: To provide a coherent map of rough set models and their extensions, enabling researchers to navigate diverse paradigms and interpret how granulation and uncertainty semantics shape approximations and boundary regions.
AI.method: Conceptual survey and taxonomic organization of main rough set paradigms. The work analyzes how different granulation mechanisms and uncertainty semantics affect approximations, with illustrative examples and discussions of interpretation and scope.
AI.result: A comprehensive taxonomy and cross-cutting analysis of rough set models and extensions, linking granulation choices to approximation forms and boundary semantics; clarifies that feature reduction and rule induction are motivating applications rather than central objectives; offers guidance for classification and decision support.
AI.conclusion: The principal aim is to survey and position rough set models and their extensions in a systematic and coherent manner.

title: Lexicographic Minimum-Violation Motion Planning using Signal Temporal Logic
comment: Submitted to the IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems (under review)
summary: Motion planning for autonomous vehicles often requires satisfying multiple conditionally conflicting specifications. In situations where not all specifications can be met simultaneously, minimum-violation motion planning maintains system operation by minimizing violations of specifications in accordance with their priorities. Signal temporal logic (STL) provides a formal language for rigorously defining these specifications and enables the quantitative evaluation of their violations. However, a total ordering of specifications yields a lexicographic optimization problem, which is typically computationally expensive to solve using standard methods. We address this problem by transforming the multi-objective lexicographic optimization problem into a single-objective scalar optimization problem using non-uniform quantization and bit-shifting. Specifically, we extend a deterministic model predictive path integral (MPPI) solver to efficiently solve optimization problems without quadratic input cost. Additionally, a novel predicate-robustness measure that combines spatial and temporal violations is introduced. Our results show that the proposed method offers an interpretable and scalable solution for lexicographic STL minimum-violation motion planning within a single-objective solver framework.

AI.tldr: Converts lexicographic STL-based minimum-violation planning into a single-objective scalar problem using non-uniform quantization and bit-shifting; extends a deterministic MPPI solver to avoid quadratic input costs; introduces a predicate-robustness metric that fuses spatial and temporal violations, yielding an interpretable, scalable solution within a single-solver framework.
AI.motivation: Autonomous vehicles face conflicting high-level specifications that cannot all be satisfied simultaneously. Formal tools like STL enable rigorous violation assessment, but lexicographic optimization is computationally expensive. A scalable, interpretable method is needed to perform minimum-violation planning under multiple priorities.
AI.method: Transform the multi-objective lexicographic optimization into a scalar objective via non-uniform quantization of STL violations and bit-shifting to encode priority order. Extend a deterministic model predictive path integral (MPPI) solver to handle optimization without quadratic input costs. Propose a predicate-robustness measure that aggregates spatial and temporal violations to quantify violations.
AI.result: The proposed approach yields an interpretable and scalable solution for lexicographic STL minimum-violation motion planning within a single-objective solver, reducing computational burden and maintaining prioritization of specifications.
AI.conclusion: A single-objective solver framework with non-uniform quantization and predicate-robustness can efficiently perform lexicographic minimum-violation motion planning under STL, offering interpretability and scalability.

title: A Computational Model of Message Sensation Value in Short Video Multimodal Features that Predicts Sensory and Behavioral Engagement
summary: The contemporary media landscape is characterized by sensational short videos. While prior research examines the effects of individual multimodal features, the collective impact of multimodal features on viewer engagement with short videos remains unknown. Grounded in the theoretical framework of Message Sensation Value (MSV), this study develops and tests a computational model of MSV with multimodal feature analysis and human evaluation of 1,200 short videos. This model that predicts sensory and behavioral engagement was further validated across two unseen datasets from three short video platforms (combined N = 14,492). While MSV is positively associated with sensory engagement, it shows an inverted U-shaped relationship with behavioral engagement: Higher MSV elicits stronger sensory stimulation, but moderate MSV optimizes behavioral engagement. This research advances the theoretical understanding of short video engagement and introduces a robust computational tool for short video research.

AI.tldr: A study develops a computational model of Message Sensation Value (MSV) integrating multimodal features to predict sensory and behavioral engagement in short videos. Using 1,200 videos and human evaluation, the model is validated on two unseen datasets (n=14,492). Findings: MSV positively predicts sensory engagement but has an inverted-U relation with behavioral engagement, where moderate MSV maximizes behavioral engagement. The work advances theory and provides a new tool for short-video research.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in understanding the collective impact of multimodal features (not just individual features) on viewer engagement in short videos, grounded in MSV theory. It aims to quantify and predict how sensory stimuli translate into engagement, and to test generalizability across platforms.
AI.method: Computational modeling of MSV with multimodal feature analysis, combined with human evaluation of 1,200 short videos. Model predictions of sensory and behavioral engagement are validated on two unseen datasets from three short-video platforms (total N=14,492).
AI.result: MSV is positively associated with sensory engagement. It exhibits an inverted U-shaped relationship with behavioral engagement: increasing MSV boosts behavioral engagement up to a point, after which further MSV dampens it. The model generalizes across platforms, supporting robustness of the findings.
AI.conclusion: The study advances theoretical understanding of short-video engagement, showing distinct effects of MSV on sensory vs. behavioral engagement and providing a robust computational tool for future short-video research.

title: Replicable Bandits with UCB based Exploration
summary: We study replicable algorithms for stochastic multi-armed bandits (MAB) and linear bandits with UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) based exploration. A bandit algorithm is $ρ$-replicable if two executions using shared internal randomness but independent reward realizations, produce the same action sequence with probability at least $1-ρ$. Prior work is primarily elimination-based and, in linear bandits with infinitely many actions, relies on discretization, leading to suboptimal dependence on the dimension $d$ and $ρ$. We develop optimistic alternatives for both settings. For stochastic multi-armed bandits, we propose RepUCB, a replicable batched UCB algorithm and show that it attains a regret $O\!\left(\frac{K^2\log^2 T}{ρ^2}\sum_{a:Δ_a>0}\left(Δ_a+\frac{\log(KT\log T)}{Δ_a}\right)\right)$. For stochastic linear bandits, we first introduce RepRidge, a replicable ridge regression estimator that satisfies both a confidence guarantee and a $ρ$-replicability guarantee. Beyond its role in our bandit algorithm, this estimator and its guarantees may also be of independent interest in other statistical estimation settings. We then use RepRidge to design RepLinUCB, a replicable optimistic algorithm for stochastic linear bandits, and show that its regret is bounded by $\widetilde{O}\!\big(\big(d+\frac{d^3}ρ\big)\sqrt{T}\big)$. This improves the best prior regret guarantee by a factor of $O(d/ρ)$, showing that our optimistic algorithm can substantially reduce the price of replicability.

AI.tldr: RepUCB, RepRidge, and RepLinUCB deliver replicable optimism-based algorithms for stochastic MAB and linear bandits, achieving regret bounds that depend on 1/ρ and surpass discretization-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Address replicability in bandit learning; reduce the price of replicability; improve over elimination/discretization methods in infinite-action spaces.
AI.method: Introduce RepUCB (batched UCB) for MAB; RepRidge estimator with confidence and ρ-replicability; RepLinUCB using RepRidge; theoretical analyses yielding regret bounds.
AI.result: MAB regret: O((K^2 log^2 T)/ρ^2 * sum_{a: Δ_a>0}(Δ_a + log(KT log T)/Δ_a)). Linear bandits: 	ilde{O}((d + d^3/ρ) sqrt(T)). Gains factor ~ O(d/ρ) over previous best.
AI.conclusion: Optimistic replicable algorithms can substantially mitigate the cost of requiring replicability; RepRidge could be of independent interest; potential extensions include lower bounds, experiments, non-stochastic settings.

title: VTouch++: A Multimodal Dataset with Vision-Based Tactile Enhancement for Bimanual Manipulation
summary: Embodied intelligence has advanced rapidly in recent years; however, bimanual manipulation-especially in contact-rich tasks remains challenging. This is largely due to the lack of datasets with rich physical interaction signals, systematic task organization, and sufficient scale. To address these limitations, we introduce the VTOUCH dataset. It leverages vision based tactile sensing to provide high-fidelity physical interaction signals, adopts a matrix-style task design to enable systematic learning, and employs automated data collection pipelines covering real-world, demand-driven scenarios to ensure scalability. To further validate the effectiveness of the dataset, we conduct extensive quantitative experiments on cross-modal retrieval as well as real-robot evaluation. Finally, we demonstrate real-world performance through generalizable inference across multiple robots, policies, and tasks.

AI.tldr: VTOUCH: a vision-based tactile dataset for bimanual manipulation with a matrix-style task design, scalable data collection, and cross-robot evaluation.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of datasets providing rich physical interaction signals, systematic task organization, and scale for contact-rich, bimanual manipulation.
AI.method: Introduce the VTOUCH dataset using vision-based tactile sensing; adopt a matrix-style task design for systematic learning; implement automated data collection pipelines covering real-world, demand-driven scenarios to ensure scalability.
AI.result: Extensive quantitative experiments on cross-modal retrieval and real-robot evaluation; demonstration of real-world performance via generalizable inference across multiple robots, policies, and tasks.
AI.conclusion: The dataset enables scalable, signal-rich data collection that supports robust learning and cross-robot generalization for contact-rich, bimanual manipulation.

title: Optimizing Data Augmentation for Real-Time Small UAV Detection: A Lightweight Context-Aware Approach
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 34th International Conference on Electrical Engineering (ICEE 2026)
summary: Visual detection of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) is a critical task in surveillance systems due to their small physical size and environmental challenges. Although deep learning models have achieved significant progress, deploying them on edge devices necessitates the use of lightweight models, such as YOLOv11 Nano, which possess limited learning capacity. In this research, an efficient and context-aware data augmentation pipeline, combining Mosaic strategies and HSV color-space adaptation, is proposed to enhance the performance of these models. Experimental results on four standard datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach, compared to heavy and instance-level methods like Copy-Paste, not only prevents the generation of synthetic artifacts and overfitting but also significantly improves mean Average Precision (mAP) across all scenarios. Furthermore, the evaluation of generalization capability under foggy conditions revealed that the proposed method offers the optimal balance between Precision and stability for real-time systems, whereas alternative methods, such as MixUp, are effective only in specific applications.

AI.tldr: Efficient context-aware augmentation (Mosaic + HSV adaptation) improves detection of small UAVs on ultra-lightweight edge models, surpassing heavy/Copy-Paste baselines in mAP and in foggy/generalization scenarios.
AI.motivation: Enable robust UAV detection on resource-constrained edge devices by boosting learning capacity through augmented data, reducing artifacts and overfitting while maintaining real-time performance.
AI.method: An augmentation pipeline combining Mosaic strategies with HSV color-space adaptation, designed to be context-aware and to minimize synthetic artifacts; evaluated on four standard UAV datasets against heavy and instance-level methods like Copy-Paste.
AI.result: Significant mAP improvements across all datasets; better generalization under fog, achieving an optimal precision-stability balance for real-time operation; MixUp performs well only in particular contexts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Mosaic+HSV augmentation provides an effective, lightweight enhancement for edge-deployed UAV detectors, offering robustness to challenging conditions without introducing artifacts or excessive training burden.

title: Federated Learning over Blockchain-Enabled Cloud Infrastructure
summary: The rise of IoT devices and the uptake of cloud computing have informed a new era of data-driven intelligence. Traditional centralized machine learning models that require a large volume of data to be stored in a single location have therefore become more susceptible to data breaches, privacy violations, and regulatory non-compliance. This report presents a thorough examination of the merging of Federated Learning (FL) and blockchain technology in a cloud-edge setting, demonstrating it as an effective solution to the stated concerns. We are proposing a detailed four-dimensional architectural categorization that meticulously assesses coordination frameworks, consensus algorithms, data storage practices, and trust models that are significant to these integrated systems. The manuscript presents a comprehensive comparative examination of two cutting-edge frameworks: the Multi-Objectives Reinforcement Federated Learning Blockchain (MORFLB), which is designed for intelligent transportation systems, and the Federated Blockchain-IoT Framework for Sustainable Healthcare Systems (FBCI-SHS), elucidating their distinctive contributions and inherent limitations. Lastly, we engage in a thorough evaluation of the literature that integrates a comparative perspective on current frameworks to discern the singular nature of this research within existing knowledge systems. The manuscript culminates in delineating the principal challenges and offering a strategic framework for prospective research trajectories, emphasizing the advancement of adaptive, resilient, and standardized BCFL systems across diverse application domains.

AI.tldr: A survey on integrating Federated Learning and blockchain (BCFL) in cloud-edge IoT, proposing a four-dimensional architectural taxonomy (coordination, consensus, data storage, trust), with comparative studies of MORFLB and FBCI-SHS, literature synthesis, and a roadmap for adaptive, resilient BCFL systems.
AI.motivation: Mitigate data breaches, privacy violations, and regulatory non-compliance associated with centralized ML by combining FL with blockchain in cloud-edge IoT environments.
AI.method: Develop a four-dimensional architectural categorization; perform a comparative examination of two frameworks MORFLB (for ITS) and FBCI-SHS (for healthcare); survey existing literature from a BCFL perspective; identify challenges; propose a strategic research framework for future BCFL developments.
AI.result: Proposes a structured architectural taxonomy for BCFL systems; contrasts MORFLB and FBCI-SHS, highlighting contributions and limitations; provides literature-based insights and a roadmap of challenges and future research directions toward standardized, adaptive BCFL solutions.
AI.conclusion: BCFL in cloud-edge IoT shows promise for privacy-preserving, compliant AI; success hinges on robust coordination, scalable consensus, secure data storage, and trust mechanisms; future work should focus on adaptive, resilient, and standardizable BCFL frameworks across domains.

title: The AI Telco Engineer: Toward Autonomous Discovery of Wireless Communications Algorithms
summary: Agentic AI is rapidly transforming the way research is conducted, from prototyping ideas to reproducing results found in the literature. In this paper, we explore the ability of agentic AI to autonomously design wireless communication algorithms. To that end, we implement a dedicated framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to iteratively generate, evaluate, and refine candidate algorithms. We evaluate the framework on three tasks spanning the physical (PHY) and medium access control (MAC) layers: statistics-agnostic channel estimation, channel estimation with known covariance, and link adaptation. Our results show that, in a matter of hours, the framework produces algorithms that are competitive with and, in some cases, outperforming conventional baselines. Moreover, unlike neural network-based approaches, the generated algorithms are fully explainable and extensible. This work represents a first step toward the autonomous discovery of novel wireless communication algorithms, and we look forward to the progress our community makes in this direction.

AI.tldr: An LLM-driven autonomous framework designs wireless PHY/MAC algorithms in hours, achieving competitive performance with explainable, extensible solutions.
AI.motivation: To explore whether agentic AI can autonomously discover effective wireless algorithms, improving speed, reproducibility, and transparency compared with neural-network-only approaches.
AI.method: Implement a loop where LLMs generate candidate algorithms for PHY/MAC tasks (statistics-agnostic channel estimation, estimation with known covariance, link adaptation), evaluate them, and refine iteratively; assess on three tasks across PHY and MAC layers.
AI.result: The framework yields algorithms that are competitive with, and sometimes outperform, conventional baselines. Results are produced quickly (hours) and the artifacts are explainable and extensible.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates a first step toward autonomous discovery of wireless algorithms and paves the way for broader use of agentic AI in protocol/design research.

title: MOMO: A framework for seamless physical, verbal, and graphical robot skill learning and adaptation
summary: Industrial robot applications require increasingly flexible systems that non-expert users can easily adapt for varying tasks and environments. However, different adaptations benefit from different interaction modalities. We present an interactive framework that enables robot skill adaptation through three complementary modalities: kinesthetic touch for precise spatial corrections, natural language for high-level semantic modifications, and a graphical web interface for visualizing geometric relations and trajectories, inspecting and adjusting parameters, and editing via-points by drag-and-drop. The framework integrates five components: energy-based human-intention detection, a tool-based LLM architecture (where the LLM selects and parameterizes predefined functions rather than generating code) for safe natural language adaptation, Kernelized Movement Primitives (KMPs) for motion encoding, probabilistic Virtual Fixtures for guided demonstration recording, and ergodic control for surface finishing. We demonstrate that this tool-based LLM architecture generalizes skill adaptation from KMPs to ergodic control, enabling voice-commanded surface finishing. Validation on a 7-DoF torque-controlled robot at the Automatica 2025 trade fair demonstrates the practical applicability of our approach in industrial settings.

AI.tldr: A multi-modal framework for industrial robot skill adaptation enabling non-expert users to adjust tasks via kinesthetic touch, natural language, and a graphical interface, underpinned by five components (energy-based intention detection, tool-based LLM for safe function parameterization, Kernelized Movement Primitives, probabilistic Virtual Fixtures, and ergodic control); demonstrates generalization from KMPs to ergodic control and enables voice-controlled surface finishing, validated on a 7-DoF torque-controlled robot at Automatica 2025.
AI.motivation: Industrial robots need greater flexibility to adapt to varied tasks and environments. Different adaptations benefit from different interaction modalities, and enabling non-expert users to safely and effectively modify skills reduces programming burden and improves practicality in industrial settings.
AI.method: An interactive framework with three modalities (kinesthetic corrections, natural language for high-level modifications, graphical web interface) integrated with five components: (1) energy-based human-intention detection, (2) a tool-based LLM architecture that parameterizes predefined functions for safe NL adaptation, (3) Kernelized Movement Primitives for motion encoding, (4) probabilistic Virtual Fixtures for guided demonstration recording, and (5) ergodic control for surface finishing. Validation via demonstration and a real-world trial on a 7-DoF torque-controlled robot at Automatica 2025.
AI.result: The tool-based LLM architecture generalizes skill adaptation from KMPs to ergodic control, enabling voice-commanded surface finishing, with practical applicability demonstrated at a leading industrial trade fair on a 7-DoF robot.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a safe, modular, and practical multi-modal approach for rapid and intuitive robot skill adaptation in industrial settings, bridging high-level NL guidance with low-level motion controllers and enabling non-expert users to perform tasks such as surface finishing.

title: RareSpot+: A Benchmark, Model, and Active Learning Framework for Small and Rare Wildlife in Aerial Imagery
summary: Automated wildlife monitoring from aerial imagery is vital for conservation but remains limited by two persistent challenges: the difficulty of detecting small, rare species and the high cost of large-scale expert annotation. Prairie dogs exemplify this problem -- they are ecologically important yet appear tiny, sparsely distributed, and visually indistinct from their surroundings, posing a severe challenge for conventional detection models. To overcome these limitations, we present RareSpot+, a detection framework that integrates multi-scale consistency learning, context-aware augmentation, and geospatially guided active learning to address these issues. A novel multi-scale consistency loss aligns intermediate feature maps across detection heads, enhancing localization of small (approx. 30 pixels wide) objects without architectural changes, while context-aware augmentation improves robustness by synthesizing hard, ecologically plausible examples. A geospatial active learning module exploits domain-specific spatial priors linking prairie dogs and burrows, together with test-time augmentation and a meta-uncertainty model, to reduce redundant labeling. On a 2 km^2 aerial dataset, RareSpot+ improves detection over the baseline mAP@50 by +35.2% (absolute +0.13). Cross-dataset tests on HerdNet, AED, and several other wildlife benchmarks demonstrate robust detector-level transferability. The active learning module further boosts prairie dog AP by 14.5% using an annotation budget of just 1.7% of the unlabeled tiles. Beyond detection, RareSpot+ enables spatial ecological analyses such as clustering and co-occurrence, linking vision-based detection with quantitative ecology.

AI.tldr: RareSpot+ delivers robust detection of tiny, rare wildlife from aerial imagery by combining multi-scale consistency, context-aware augmentation, and geospatial active learning; achieves large gains on prairie dogs and shows cross-dataset transferability with efficient annotation budgets; enables ecological analyses.
AI.motivation: Detect small, sparsely distributed species in large-scale remote sensing data while minimizing labeling costs; leverage geospatial priors to guide learning.
AI.method: (1) Multi-scale consistency loss aligning intermediate features across detection heads to improve localization of ~30-pixel-wide objects without changing architecture. (2) Context-aware augmentation to synthesize hard, ecologically plausible examples. (3) Geospatial active learning leveraging spatial priors linking species and burrows, augmented with test-time augmentation and a meta-uncertainty model to reduce redundant labeling.
AI.result: On a 2 km^2 aerial dataset, mAP@50 improves by 35.2 percentage points (absolute, ~+0.13 AP). Cross-dataset tests show robust detector transferability to HerdNet, AED, and other wildlife benchmarks. Active learning raises prairie dog AP by 14.5% with only 1.7% of unlabeled tiles annotated. RareSpot+ enables spatial ecological analyses (clustering, co-occurrence) linking vision with ecology.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively addresses small-object detection and annotation cost in wildlife monitoring, with strong transferability and added ecological analytic capabilities.

title: Maximum Entropy Semi-Supervised Inverse Reinforcement Learning
comment: In Proceedings of the 24th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI 2015)
summary: A popular approach to apprenticeship learning (AL) is to formulate it as an inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) problem. The MaxEnt-IRL algorithm successfully integrates the maximum entropy principle into IRL and unlike its predecessors, it resolves the ambiguity arising from the fact that a possibly large number of policies could match the expert's behavior. In this paper, we study an AL setting in which in addition to the expert's trajectories, a number of unsupervised trajectories is available. We introduce MESSI, a novel algorithm that combines MaxEnt-IRL with principles coming from semi-supervised learning. In particular, MESSI integrates the unsupervised data into the MaxEnt-IRL framework using a pairwise penalty on trajectories. Empirical results in a highway driving and grid-world problems indicate that MESSI is able to take advantage of the unsupervised trajectories and improve the performance of MaxEnt-IRL.

AI.tldr: MESSI: a semi-supervised MaxEnt-IRL algorithm that uses pairwise penalties to incorporate unsupervised trajectories into MaxEnt-IRL, improving apprenticeship learning performance.
AI.motivation: IRL-based apprenticeship learning often suffers from ambiguity where many policies mimic expert behavior. Access to unsupervised trajectories provides additional structure; integrating them can regularize learning and improve policy estimation.
AI.method: Introduce MESSI, which extends MaxEnt-IRL with semi-supervised principles. Unsupervised trajectories are integrated via a pairwise trajectory penalty, aligning the learned policy with both expert and unlabelled data within the MaxEnt-IRL framework.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on highway driving and grid-world domains show that MESSI leverages unsupervised trajectories to improve over standard MaxEnt-IRL.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating unsupervised trajectory data through a pairwise penalty within MaxEnt-IRL improves apprenticeship learning performance in the tested domains, validating a semi-supervised IRL approach.

title: Skyline-First Traversal as a Control Mechanism for Multi-Criteria Graph Search
summary:
In multi-criteria graph traversal, paths are compared via Pareto dominance, an ordering that identifies which paths are non-dominated, but says nothing about which path to expand next or when the search may stop. As a result, existing approaches rely on external mechanisms-heuristics, scalarization, or population-based exploration while Pareto dominance remains confined to passive roles such as pruning or ranking.
  This paper shows that, under constrained cost models, finite cost grids, Markovian transitions, and a nonzero progress measure, Pareto geometry alone is sufficient to drive both scheduling and termination. We show that extracting exclusively from the first Pareto layer, the skyline, induces a deterministic descent in a discrete completion potential, ensuring monotone progress toward solution completion. In parallel, a vector lower-bound certificate provides a stopping condition that guarantees dominance coverage of all remaining traversals without requiring a predefined number of solutions.
  Our analysis establishes deterministic potential descent, certified termination via dominance coverage, a uniform bound on layer width induced by cost-grid geometry, and greedy cost-space dispersion within the skyline. The resulting framework operates without scalarization, heuristic guidance, or probabilistic models, and repositions Pareto dominance from a passive filter to a deterministic driver of search.

AI.tldr: Pareto dominance can deterministically drive multi-criteria graph search and stopping under constrained cost models by exploiting the first Pareto layer (skyline) and a vector lower-bound certificate, removing the need for scalarization or heuristics.
AI.motivation: Pareto dominance has been a passive filter; this work investigates whether it can actively guide scheduling and termination in a discrete, cost-bounded setting.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis under finite cost grids, Markovian transitions, and a nonzero progress measure. It shows monotone descent of a completion potential when expanding the skyline, and introduces a vector lower-bound certificate that guarantees dominance coverage of all remaining traversals without a preset number of solutions. It also derives a uniform bound on layer width and demonstrates greedy cost-space dispersion within the skyline.
AI.result: Demonstrates deterministic potential descent, certified termination via dominance coverage, width bounds on Pareto layers due to cost-grid geometry, and effective dispersion of costs within the skyline. The framework operates without scalarization, heuristics, or probabilistic models.
AI.conclusion: Pareto dominance can function as a deterministic driver of search and termination in constrained, discrete settings, removing reliance on external heuristics and leading to a principled, termination-guaranteed multi-criteria search paradigm.

title: Temporal Difference Calibration in Sequential Tasks: Application to Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Recent advances in vision-language-action (VLA) models for robotics have highlighted the importance of reliable uncertainty quantification in sequential tasks. However, assessing and improving calibration in such settings remains mostly unexplored, especially when only partial trajectories are observed. In this work, we formulate sequential calibration for episodic tasks, where task-success confidence is produced along an episode, while success is determined at the end of it. We introduce a sequential extension of the Brier score and show that, for binary outcomes, its risk minimizer coincides with the VLA policy's value function. This connection bridges uncertainty calibration and reinforcement learning, enabling the use of temporal-difference (TD) value estimation as a principled calibration mechanism over time. We empirically show that TD calibration improves performance relative to the state-of-the-art on simulated and real-robot data. Interestingly, we show that when calibrated using TD, the VLA's single-step action probabilities can yield competitive uncertainty estimates, in contrast to recent findings that employed different calibration techniques.

AI.tldr: Sequentially calibrate uncertainty in episodic vision-language-action (VLA) robotics tasks by extending the Brier score to trajectories; show that TD-based value estimation aligns with risk minimization for binary outcomes, enabling principled online calibration and improving empirical calibration performance.
AI.motivation: Calibration of uncertainty in sequential, partially observed episodic tasks is underexplored in VLA robotics; reliable confidence estimates are crucial for decision-making and safety.
AI.method: Define a sequential Brier score for episodic binary outcomes; prove its risk-minimizer coincides with the VLA policy value function; propose using temporal-difference (TD) value estimation as a calibration mechanism over time; evaluate on simulated and real-robot data, comparing to state-of-the-art calibration methods.
AI.result: TD-based calibration improves calibration performance over state-of-the-art on both simulated and real-robot datasets; when calibrated with TD, the VLA's single-step action probabilities provide competitive uncertainty estimates, contrasting with prior work using different calibration techniques.
AI.conclusion: Links uncertainty calibration with reinforcement learning by using TD value estimation as a principled, time-aware calibration mechanism for sequential tasks; TD calibration can yield better uncertainty estimates and can make single-step probabilities robust for online calibration in VLA robotics.

title: EmbodiedMidtrain: Bridging the Gap between Vision-Language Models and Vision-Language-Action Models via Mid-training
summary: Vision-Language-Action Models (VLAs) inherit their visual and linguistic capabilities from Vision-Language Models (VLMs), yet most VLAs are built from off-the-shelf VLMs that are not adapted to the embodied domain, limiting their downstream performance. In this work, we propose EmbodiedMidtrain to bridge the gap between VLMs and VLAs. We first characterize the data distribution gap between them, showing that VLA data occupy compact regions that are largely separated from the broader VLM distribution, while the degree of alignment varies substantially both across and within VLM data sources. Then, we build a mid-training data engine that leverages a lightweight learnable proximity estimator to select the most VLA-aligned candidates from a large VLM pool, and mid-trains the VLM on this curated mixture before downstream VLA fine-tuning. Experiments on three robot manipulation benchmarks show that mid-training consistently improves performance across different VLM backbones, achieving results competitive with expert VLAs and off-the-shelf VLMs trained with larger model scale and training budgets. Further analysis reveals that mid-training provides a stronger initialization for VLA fine-tuning, with gains emerging from the earliest steps and widening throughout training. Moreover, the data engine captures both dataset-level and sample-level alignment signals, favoring spatial reasoning over text-centric tasks while preserving the diversity of the VLM data. We will release all code, data and models for future research.

AI.tldr: EmbodiedMidtrain proposes a mid-training data engine that curates VLA-aligned samples from a large VLM pool to better align Vision-Language Models with embodied Vision-Language-Action tasks, yielding improved downstream VLA performance across backbones.
AI.motivation: VLA data occupy compact regions largely separate from the broader VLM distribution, and alignment varies across data sources. This distribution gap limits VLA performance when using off-the-shelf VLMs not adapted to embodied tasks.
AI.method: Train a lightweight learnable proximity estimator to select VLA-aligned candidates from a large VLM pool. Mid-train the VLM on this curated mixture before downstream VLA fine-tuning. Evaluate on three robot manipulation benchmarks with multiple VLM backbones. Analyze dataset-level and sample-level alignment signals, noting a preference for spatial reasoning tasks while preserving data diversity.
AI.result: Mid-training consistently improves VLA performance across different VLM backbones, achieving results competitive with expert VLAs and larger VLMs trained with larger budgets. Gains emerge from the earliest training steps and widen over time. The data engine captures alignment signals at both dataset and sample levels and emphasizes spatial reasoning while maintaining data diversity.
AI.conclusion: Mid-training with a proximity-based data engine bridges the gap between VLMs and VLAs, providing a stronger initialization for VLA fine-tuning and enabling robust improvements across backbones. The approach is data-efficient and generalizes to embodied tasks; code, data, and models will be released for future research.

title: Analysis of Nystrom method with sequential ridge leverage scores
summary: Large-scale kernel ridge regression (KRR) is limited by the need to store a large kernel matrix K_t. To avoid storing the entire matrix K_t, Nystrom methods subsample a subset of columns of the kernel matrix, and efficiently find an approximate KRR solution on the reconstructed matrix. The chosen subsampling distribution in turn affects the statistical and computational tradeoffs. For KRR problems, recent works show that a sampling distribution proportional to the ridge leverage scores (RLSs) provides strong reconstruction guarantees for the approximation. While exact RLSs are as difficult to compute as a KRR solution, we may be able to approximate them well enough. In this paper, we study KRR problems in a sequential setting and introduce the INK-ESTIMATE algorithm, that incrementally computes the RLSs estimates. INK-ESTIMATE maintains a small sketch of K_t, that at each step is used to compute an intermediate estimate of the RLSs. First, our sketch update does not require access to previously seen columns, and therefore a single pass over the kernel matrix is sufficient. Second, the algorithm requires a fixed, small space budget to run dependent only on the effective dimension of the kernel matrix. Finally, our sketch provides strong approximation guarantees on the distance between the true kernel matrix and its approximation, and on the statistical risk of the approximate KRR solution at any time, because all our guarantees hold at any intermediate step.

AI.tldr: INK-ESTIMATE provides an online, space-efficient method to approximate ridge leverage scores (RLSs) for kernel ridge regression (KRR) via a single-pass kernel sketch, enabling adaptive Nyström sampling with guarantees on kernel approximation and KRR risk.
AI.motivation: Large-scale KRR requires storing a large kernel matrix. Nyström methods reduce cost by sampling columns, but sampling quality hinges on RLSs, which are hard to compute exactly. A sequential setting benefits from incremental RLS estimates with fixed memory.
AI.method: Maintain a small, kernel sketch of K_t and incrementally compute RLS estimates from this sketch. The sketch update does not require access to previously seen columns, enabling a single-pass algorithm. The space budget is fixed and depends on the kernel's effective dimension. Guarantees are provided for the distance between the true kernel and its approximation and for the statistical risk of the approximate KRR solution at any intermediate step.
AI.result: INK-ESTIMATE yields a single-pass, memory-bounded procedure for estimating RLSs online, enabling Nyström-based KRR with theoretical guarantees on kernel approximation error and the risk of the resulting KRR predictor at all times.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables online, resource-efficient KRR with principled sampling via approximate RLSs, offering strong intermediate guarantees and practical applicability to streaming/kernel data where full kernel storage is infeasible.

title: MIRROR: A Hierarchical Benchmark for Metacognitive Calibration in Large Language Models
summary: We introduce MIRROR, a benchmark comprising eight experiments across four metacognitive levels that evaluates whether large language models can use self-knowledge to make better decisions. We evaluate 16 models from 8 labs across approximately 250,000 evaluation instances using five independent behavioral measurement channels. Core experiments are run across the full model roster; experiments with specialized infrastructure requirements report explicitly marked model subsets. We find two phenomena with direct implications for agentic deployment: (1) compositional self-prediction fails universally -- the Compositional Calibration Error ranges from 0.500 to 0.943 on the original 15-model Exp3-v1 set (and 0.434 to 0.758 on the balanced 16-model Exp3-v2 expansion), indicating that models cannot predict their own performance on multi-domain tasks, and (2) models exhibit above-chance but imperfect domain-specific self-knowledge yet systematically fail to translate even this partial awareness into appropriate agentic action-selection -- external metacognitive control reduces the Confident Failure Rate from 0.600 to 0.143 (76% reduction at temperature 0; mean 70% at temperature 0.7 across 5 models from 4 labs). Providing models with their own calibration scores produces no significant improvement (p > 0.05); only architectural constraint is effective. This suggests that external metacognitive scaffolding -- not improved self-knowledge -- is the path to safer autonomous AI systems. Code, data, and Croissant metadata will be released publicly with the benchmark.

AI.tldr: MIRROR is a large benchmark across four metacognitive levels to test if LLMs can use self-knowledge to make better decisions. It finds universal failure of compositional self-prediction, partial domain-specific self-knowledge that does not translate into action, and that external metacognitive scaffolding markedly improves safe action selection, while giving models their own calibration scores does not help.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether LLMs can leverage self-knowledge at multiple metacognitive levels for decision-making, and determine whether enhancing self-knowledge suffices for safe autonomous behavior or whether external scaffolding is necessary.
AI.method: Eight experiments across four metacognitive levels, evaluating 16 models from 8 labs (~250k evaluations) using five behavioral channels. Core experiments span the full roster; specialized experiments report subsets. Key metrics include Compositional Calibration Error (self-prediction across domains) and Confident Failure Rate under external metacognitive control; tests include providing calibration scores and varying temperature; data/code will be released.
AI.result: Compositional self-prediction fails universally: Compositional Calibration Error ranges ~0.50–0.94 (Exp3-v1) and ~0.43–0.76 (Exp3-v2). Models show above-chance but imperfect domain-self-knowledge and fail to translate it into appropriate action. External metacognitive control reduces Confident Failure Rate from ~0.60 to ~0.143 (up to 76% reduction at temp 0; ~70% at 0.7 across 5 models). Providing calibration scores yields no significant improvement (p>0.05); only architectural constraint is effective.
AI.conclusion: External metacognitive scaffolding, not enhanced self-knowledge, is key for safer autonomous AI. The authors will release code, data, and metadata; further work should explore architectural constraints to enable safe agentic control.

title: Passive Variable Impedance For Shared Control
comment: submitted for publication at the IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: Shared Control methods often use impedance control to track target poses in a robotic manipulator. The guidance behavior of such controllers is shaped by the used stiffness gains, which can be varying over time to achieve an adaptive guiding. When multiple target poses are tracked at the same time with varying importance, the corresponding output wrenches have to be arbitrated with weightings changing over time. In this work, we study the stabilization of both variable stiffness in impedance control as well as the arbitration of different controllers through a scaled addition of their output wrenches, reformulating both into a holistic framework. We identify passivity violations in the closed loop system and provide methods to passivate the system. The resulting approach can be used to stabilize standard impedance controllers, allowing for the development of novel and flexible shared control methods. We do not constrain the design of stiffness matrices or arbitration factors; both can be matrix-valued including off-diagonal elements and change arbitrarily over time. The proposed methods are furthermore validated in simulation as well as in real robot experiments on different systems, proving their effectiveness and showcasing different behaviors which can be utilized depending on the requirements of the shared control approach.

AI.tldr: A holistic, passivity-aware framework for variable-stiffness impedance control and target arbitration in shared control, via a scaled addition of output wrenches with fully general, time-varying, matrix-valued gains; validated in simulation and on real robots.
