AI.motivation: Ensure stability and passivity in shared-control systems when stiffness gains vary over time and multiple controllers with different target priorities must arbitrate outputs.
AI.method: Reformulate variable-stiffness impedance control and arbitration as a single scaled sum of output wrenches. Analyze passivity of the closed-loop, introduce passivation techniques, and allow fully general, time-varying matrix-valued stiffness and arbitration gains (including off-diagonal terms). Validate with simulations and real-robot experiments across different systems.
AI.result: Identified passivity violations in the original closed-loop; proposed passivation methods to restore stability; demonstrated that standard impedance controllers can be stabilized within the holistic framework; showcased diverse behaviors by adjusting gains, with successful validation on multiple systems in both simulation and hardware.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables stable, flexible shared control with time-varying stiffness and arbitration without constraining gain structures, enabling novel shared-control methods and broad applicability across different robotic platforms.

title: Investigation of cardinality classification for bacterial colony counting using explainable artificial intelligence
summary: Automatic bacterial colony counting is a highly sought-after technology in modern biological laboratories because it eliminates manual counting effort. Previous work has observed that MicrobiaNet, currently the best-performing cardinality classification model for colony counting, has difficulty distinguishing colonies of three or more individuals. However, it is unclear if this is due to properties of the data together with inherent characteristics of the MicrobiaNet model. By analysing MicrobiaNet with explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), we demonstrate that XAI can provide insights into how data properties constrain cardinality classification performance in colony counting. Our results show that high visual similarity across classes is the key issue hindering further performance improvement, revising prior assertions about MicrobiaNet. These findings suggest future work should focus on models that explicitly incorporate visual similarity or explore density estimation approaches, with broader implications for neural network classifiers trained on imbalanced datasets.

AI.tldr: XAI analysis shows that high visual similarity across colony-count classes constrains MicrobiaNet more than model capacity; density estimation or similarity-aware models are likely needed, with implications for imbalanced data classifiers.
AI.motivation: Determine whether MicrobiaNet's difficulty counting three or more colonies arises from data properties or model limitations, to improve colony counting performance.
AI.method: Apply explainable AI techniques to MicrobiaNet's predictions on colony images to assess feature attributions, class separability, and the role of data properties (visual similarity) in counting accuracy.
AI.result: Findings indicate that intra-/inter-class visual similarity is the primary bottleneck, challenging previous claims that model limitations are the main issue; data properties constrain performance.
AI.conclusion: Future work should explore models that explicitly account for visual similarity or adopt density estimation approaches, with broader implications for neural classifiers trained on imbalanced datasets.

title: Improved large-scale graph learning through ridge spectral sparsification
summary: Graph-based techniques and spectral graph theory have enriched the field of machine learning with a variety of critical advances. A central object in the analysis is the graph Laplacian L, which encodes the structure of the graph. We consider the problem of learning over this Laplacian in a distributed streaming setting, where new edges of the graph are observed in real time by a network of workers. In this setting, it is hard to learn quickly or approximately while keeping a distributed representation of L. To address this challenge, we present a novel algorithm, GSQUEAK, which efficiently sparsifies the Laplacian by maintaining a small subset of effective resistances. We show that our algorithm produces sparsifiers with strong spectral approximation guarantees, all while processing edges in a single pass and in a distributed fashion.

AI.tldr: GSQUEAK is a distributed, single-pass Laplacian sparsification algorithm that maintains a small set of effective resistances to produce spectral sparsifiers with provable guarantees in a streaming graph setting.
AI.motivation: Learning over graph Laplacians in a distributed streaming setting is challenging due to the need for fast updates and a compact, distributed representation of the Laplacian.
AI.method: Introduce GSQUEAK, which maintains a compact subset of effective resistances and updates the Laplacian sparsifier in a single pass across streaming edges in a distributed network.
AI.result: GSQUEAK achieves strong spectral approximation guarantees for the graph Laplacian sparsifier while operating in one pass and in a distributed fashion.
AI.conclusion: This approach enables scalable, provably accurate spectral learning on large streaming graphs by compactly representing the Laplacian without sacrificing spectral properties.

title: The Existential Theory of Research: Why Discovery Is Hard
summary: Can scientific discovery be made arbitrarily easy by choosing the right representation, collecting enough data, and deploying sufficiently powerful algorithms? This paper argues that the answer is fundamentally negative. We introduce the Existential Theory of Research (ETR), a formal framework that models discovery as the recovery of structured explanations under constraints of representation, observation, and computation. Within this framework, we show that these three components cannot be simultaneously optimized: no method can guarantee universally simple explanations, arbitrarily compressed observations, and efficient exact inference. This limitation is not model-specific, but arises from a synthesis of uncertainty principles in sparse representation, sample complexity bounds in high-dimensional recovery, and the computational hardness of exact inference. We further show that representation mismatch alone can inflate intrinsic simplicity into apparent complexity, rendering otherwise tractable problems observationally and computationally prohibitive. To quantify these effects, we introduce an uncertainty functional that captures the joint difficulty of discovery. The results suggest that scientific difficulty is not accidental, but a structural consequence of the geometry and complexity of inference.

AI.tldr: ETR formalizes discovery as recovering structured explanations under representation, observation, and computation constraints, showing trade-offs prevent universally simple explanations, arbitrary compression, and efficient exact inference.
AI.motivation: Explain why scientific discovery cannot be universally easy and quantify the inherent difficulty arising from the interplay of representation, data, and computation.
AI.method: Introduce the Existential Theory of Research (ETR) as a formal framework; derive impossibility results from (i) uncertainty principles in sparse representations, (ii) sample complexity in high-dimensional recovery, and (iii) computational hardness of exact inference; define an uncertainty functional; discuss representation mismatch.
AI.result: No method can simultaneously guarantee simple explanations, arbitrarily compressed observations, and efficient exact inference; difficulty is structural and model-agnostic; representation mismatch can inflate intrinsic simplicity into apparent complexity; an uncertainty functional quantifies joint discovery difficulty.
AI.conclusion: Scientific difficulty is a structural consequence of the geometry and complexity of inference; ETR provides a baseline for assessing discovery tasks and explains why trade-offs are unavoidable.

title: Kinematic Optimization of Phalanx Length Ratios in Robotic Hands Using Potential Dexterity
comment: This manuscript has been submitted for possible publication
summary: In the design stage of robotic hands, it is not straightforward to quantitatively evaluate the effect of phalanx length ratios on dexterity without defining specific objects or manipulation tasks. Therefore, this study presents a framework for optimizing the phalanx length ratios of a five-finger robotic hand based on potential dexterity within a kinematic structure. The proposed method employs global manipulability, workspace volume, overlap workspace volume, and fingertip sensitivity as evaluation metrics, and identifies optimal design configurations using a weighted objective function under given constraints. The reachable workspace is discretized using a voxel-based representation, and joint motions are discretized at uniform intervals for evaluation. The optimization is performed over design sets for both the thumb and the other fingers, and design combinations that do not generate overlap workspace are excluded. The results show that each phalanx does not contribute equally to the overall dexterity, and the factors influencing each phalanx are identified. In addition, it is observed that the selection of weighting coefficients does not necessarily lead to the direct maximization of individual performance metrics, due to the non-uniform distribution of evaluation measures within the design space. The proposed framework provides a systematic approach to analyze the trade-offs among reachability, dexterity, and controllability, and can serve as a practical guideline for the kinematic design of multi-fingered robotic hands.

AI.tldr: Framework for optimizing phalanx length ratios of a five-finger robotic hand using global manipulability, workspace metrics, and fingertip sensitivity; voxel-based discretization and non-overlap constraints; reveals non-uniform phalanx contributions and non-monotonic effects of weightings; guides trade-offs in kinematic hand design.
AI.motivation: Quantitative evaluation of phalanx length ratios is challenging without task-specific objects; a systematic methodology is needed to assess dexterity across diverse kinematic configurations.
AI.method: Compute global manipulability, reachable/workspace volumes, overlap workspace volumes, and fingertip sensitivity; discretize workspace with voxels; discretize joint motions; optimize design sets for the thumb and other fingers under non-overlap constraints using a weighted objective function.
AI.result: Phalanges contribute unequally to dexterity; weighting coefficients do not necessarily maximize individual metrics due to non-uniform metric distribution in design space; framework enables systematic trade-off analysis between reachability, dexterity, and controllability.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical framework to analyze and balance competing design criteria in multi-fingered hand kinematics and offers a guideline for practical robotic hand design.

title: Cognitive Alignment At No Cost: Inducing Human Attention Biases For Interpretable Vision Transformers
summary: For state-of-the-art image understanding, Vision Transformers (ViTs) have become the standard architecture but their processing diverges substantially from human attentional characteristics. We investigate whether this cognitive gap can be shrunk by fine-tuning the self-attention weights of Google's ViT-B/16 on human saliency fixation maps. To isolate the effects of semantically relevant signals from generic human supervision, the tuned model is compared against a shuffled control. Fine-tuning significantly improved alignment across five saliency metrics and induced three hallmark human-like biases: tuning reversed the baseline's anti-human large-object bias toward small-objects, amplified the animacy preference and diminished extreme attention entropy. Bayesian parity analysis provides decisive to very-strong evidence that this cognitive alignment comes at no cost to the model's original classification performance on in- (ImageNet), corrupted (ImageNet-C) and out-of-distribution (ObjectNet) benchmarks. An equivalent procedure applied to a ResNet-50 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) instead degraded both alignment and accuracy, suggesting that the ViT's modular self-attention mechanism is uniquely suited for dissociating spatial priority from representational logic. These findings demonstrate that biologically grounded priors can be instilled as a free emergent property of human-aligned attention, to improve transformer interpretability.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning ViT self-attention on human saliency maps improves human-alignment and reveals human-like biases without hurting classification, unlike CNNs.
AI.motivation: Bridge the cognitive gap between human visual attention and Vision Transformer processing; investigate whether biologically grounded priors can be embedded into Transformer attention to improve interpretability and possibly robustness.
AI.method: Fine-tune the ViT-B/16 self-attention weights on human saliency fixation maps, with a shuffled-control comparison; evaluate alignment with five saliency metrics; identify three human-like biases (bias shift toward small objects, enhanced animacy preference, reduced attention entropy); perform Bayesian parity analysis; assess in-domain (ImageNet) and OOD (ImageNet-C, ObjectNet) performance; compare against a ResNet-50 CNN.
AI.result: Significant improvement in alignment across five saliency metrics; emergence of three human-like biases: reversed large-object bias toward small objects, stronger animacy preference, lower extreme attention entropy; Bayesian parity analysis shows decisive to very-strong evidence that alignment does not harm classification on ImageNet, ImageNet-C, or ObjectNet; CNN baseline (ResNet-50) degrades both alignment and accuracy under similar procedure; suggests ViT's self-attention is uniquely suited to dissociate spatial priority from representation; supports the view that biologically grounded priors can be learned as emergent properties of human-aligned attention, enhancing transformer interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Biologically grounded priors can be embedded via human-aligned attention to yield more human-like attention without compromising performance, highlighting ViTs’ modular attention as particularly receptive to such priors and enhancing interpretability.

title: On the Quantization Robustness of Diffusion Language Models in Coding Benchmarks
summary: Auto-regressive Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on coding tasks, but incur high memory and inference costs. Diffusion-based language models (d-LLMs) offer bounded inference cost via iterative denoising, but their behavior under post-training quantization (PTQ) has been sparsely explored. We investigate the application and robustness of PTQ techniques, specifically GPTQ and a modified Hessian-Aware Quantization (HAWQ) algorithm, on a diffusion-based coding LLM (CoDA) and observe that these methods applied to CoDA exhibit greater robustness at low bitwidths compared to Qwen3-1.7B, its auto-regressive counterpart, under a standardized evaluation pipeline. We find that in our setup, CoDA exhibits greater robustness at low bitwidths (2-4 bits), with smaller accuracy degradation across HumanEval and MBPP benchmarks. Additionally, mixed-precision configurations derived from HAWQ provide smooth trade-offs across accuracy, latency, and memory. The results suggest that diffusion LLMs may offer advantages for efficient deployment due to more quantization-resilience.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based LLMs (CoDA) show improved robustness to post-training quantization (PTQ) at low bitwidths (2–4 bits) compared with an autoregressive baseline (Qwen3-1.7B). GPTQ and a modified HAWQ enable effective mixed-precision deployment, balancing accuracy, latency, and memory. This suggests diffusion LLMs may be more amenable to efficient deployment via quantization.
AI.motivation: Assess PTQ robustness and deployment efficiency for diffusion-based LLMs (CoDA) using GPTQ and HAWQ, and compare against autoregressive models under standardized benchmarks and bitwidth constraints.
AI.method: Apply GPTQ and a modified Hessian-Aware Quantization (HAWQ) to the diffusion LLM CoDA; compare against Qwen3-1.7B (autoregressive) using a standardized evaluation pipeline. Evaluate at low bitwidths (2–4 bits) on programming benchmarks HumanEval and MBPP. Explore mixed-precision configurations derived from HAWQ to study trade-offs in accuracy, latency, and memory.
AI.result: CoDA demonstrates greater robustness to low-bit quantization than Qwen3-1.7B, with smaller accuracy degradation at 2–4 bits on HumanEval and MBPP. HAWQ-derived mixed-precision configurations provide smooth transitions in accuracy, latency, and memory consumption across settings.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based LLMs may offer practical advantages for efficient deployment due to inherent quantization resilience, enabling more favorable trade-offs between accuracy and resource usage compared with autoregressive LLMs.

title: Large Language Models Meet Biomedical Knowledge Graphs for Mechanistically Grounded Therapeutic Prioritization
summary: Drug repurposing is often framed as a candidate identification task, but existing approaches provide limited guidance for distinguishing biologically plausible candidates from historically well-connected ones. Here we introduce DrugKLM, a hybrid framework that integrates biomedical knowledge graph structure with large language model-based mechanistic reasoning to enable mechanistically grounded therapeutic prioritization. Across benchmark datasets, DrugKLM outperforms knowledge graph-only and language model-only baselines, including TxGNN. Beyond improved recall, DrugKLM confidence scores exhibit functional alignment with molecular phenotypes: higher scores are associated with transcriptional signatures linked to improved survival across 12 TCGA cancers. The scoring framework preferentially captures biologically perturbational signals rather than historical indication patterns. Expert curation across five cancers further reveals systematic differences in prioritization behavior, with DrugKLM elevating candidates supported by coherent mechanistic rationale and disease-specific clinical context. Together, these results establish DrugKLM as an evidence-integrative framework that translates heterogeneous biomedical data into mechanistically interpretable and clinically grounded therapeutic hypotheses.

AI.tldr: DrugKLM is a hybrid framework that combines biomedical knowledge graph structure with LLM-based mechanistic reasoning to prioritize therapeutically plausible drug repurposing candidates, outperforming baselines and aligning with molecular phenotypes.
AI.motivation: There is a need to distinguish biologically plausible drug repurposing candidates from historically well-connected ones and to provide mechanistic explanations, not just historical associations.
AI.method: DrugKLM integrates knowledge graph structure with large language model–based mechanistic reasoning to produce mechanistically grounded therapeutic priors; it uses a confidence scoring framework evaluated on benchmark datasets against baselines (including TxGNN) and includes expert curation across multiple cancers.
AI.result: DrugKLM outperforms KG-only and LLM-only baselines, with confidence scores functionally aligned to transcriptional signatures associated with improved survival across 12 TCGA cancers; it preferentially captures perturbational signals over historical indications; expert curation shows increased prioritization of candidates with coherent mechanistic rationale and disease-specific clinical context.
AI.conclusion: DrugKLM is an evidence-integrative framework that translates heterogeneous biomedical data into mechanistically interpretable and clinically grounded therapeutic hypotheses.

title: FingerEye: Continuous and Unified Vision-Tactile Sensing for Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Dexterous robotic manipulation requires comprehensive perception across all phases of interaction: pre-contact, contact initiation, and post-contact. Such continuous feedback allows a robot to adapt its actions throughout interaction. However, many existing tactile sensors, such as GelSight and its variants, only provide feedback after contact is established, limiting a robot's ability to precisely initiate contact. We introduce FingerEye, a compact and cost-effective sensor that provides continuous vision-tactile feedback throughout the interaction process. FingerEye integrates binocular RGB cameras to provide close-range visual perception with implicit stereo depth. Upon contact, external forces and torques deform a compliant ring structure; these deformations are captured via marker-based pose estimation and serve as a proxy for contact wrench sensing. This design enables a perception stream that smoothly transitions from pre-contact visual cues to post-contact tactile feedback. Building on this sensing capability, we develop a vision-tactile imitation learning policy that fuses signals from multiple FingerEye sensors to learn dexterous manipulation behaviors from limited real-world data. We further develop a digital twin of our sensor and robot platform to improve policy generalization. By combining real demonstrations with visually augmented simulated observations for representation learning, the learned policies become more robust to object appearance variations. Together, these design aspects enable dexterous manipulation across diverse object properties and interaction regimes, including coin standing, chip picking, letter retrieving, and syringe manipulation. The hardware design, code, appendix, and videos are available on our project website: https://nus-lins-lab.github.io/FingerEyeWeb/

AI.tldr: FingerEye provides continuous vision-tactile feedback for dexterous manipulation, bridging pre-contact visuals to post-contact tactile sensing, with a digital twin and multi-sensor imitation learning for better generalization.
AI.motivation: Current tactile sensors (e.g., GelSight) typically offer feedback only after contact, hindering precise contact initiation and adaptive interaction; there is a need for continuous perception across pre-contact, contact, and post-contact phases.
AI.method: Hardware: FingerEye sensor with binocular RGB cameras for close-range vision and implicit stereo depth; a compliant ring deforms under contact, with marker-based pose estimation to infer contact wrench. Learning: vision-tactile imitation learning policy fuses signals from multiple FingerEye sensors; digital twin of sensor/robot platform to improve generalization; training uses real demonstrations plus visually augmented simulated observations to enhance robustness to appearance variations.
AI.result: Demonstrated manipulation capabilities across diverse tasks (coin standing, chip picking, letter retrieving, syringe manipulation) and robustness to object appearance variations; policy generalization improved via digital twin and simulated data integration.
AI.conclusion: The integrated vision-tactile sensing and imitation learning framework enables continuous perception throughout interaction and robust dexterous manipulation; resources (hardware, code, appends) are made available on the project site.

title: Learning to count small and clustered objects with application to bacterial colonies
summary: Automated bacterial colony counting from images is an important technique to obtain data required for the development of vaccines and antibiotics. However, bacterial colonies present unique machine vision challenges that affect counting, including (1) small physical size, (2) object clustering, (3) high data annotation cost, and (4) limited cross-species generalisation. While FamNet is an established object counting technique effective for clustered objects and costly data annotation, its effectiveness for small colony sizes and cross-species generalisation remains unknown. To address the first three challenges, we propose ACFamNet, an extension of FamNet that handles small and clustered objects using a novel region of interest pooling with alignment and optimised feature engineering. To address all four challenges above, we introduce ACFamNet Pro, which augments ACFamNet with multi-head attention and residual connections, enabling dynamic weighting of objects and improved gradient flow. Experiments show that ACFamNet Pro achieves a mean normalised absolute error (MNAE) of 9.64% under 5-fold cross-validation, outperforming ACFamNet and FamNet by 2.23% and 12.71%, respectively.

AI.tldr: ACFamNet and ACFamNet Pro extend FamNet for automated bacterial colony counting, addressing small/clustered colonies, annotation cost, and cross-species generalization. ACFamNet uses ROI pooling with alignment and feature engineering; ACFamNet Pro adds multi-head attention and residuals for dynamic weighting and better gradient flow. Result: MNAE 9.64% (5-fold CV), outperforming ACFamNet by 2.23% and FamNet by 12.71%.
AI.motivation: Bacterial colony counting from imaging faces challenges: small colony size, clustering, high annotation costs, and limited cross-species generalization. Existing FamNet handles clustered objects but struggles with small sizes and cross-species generalization. The work aims to improve accuracy and generalization for automated colony counting, enabling reliable data for vaccines and antibiotics.
AI.method: Propose AC FamNet, an extension of FamNet with a novel ROI pooling module that aligns regions and applies optimized feature engineering to handle small and clustered objects. Further augment with AC FamNet Pro, incorporating multi-head attention and residual connections to weight objects dynamically and improve gradient flow.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows AC FamNet Pro achieves mean normalized absolute error (MNAE) of 9.64% under 5-fold cross-validation, outperforming AC FamNet by 2.23% and FamNet by 12.71%.
AI.conclusion: ACFamNet Pro provides improved accuracy and robustness for automated bacterial colony counting across small sizes and clustered instances, with enhanced generalization potential across species and conditions, validating the proposed architectural innovations.

title: Concept Graph Convolutions: Message Passing in the Concept Space
summary: The trust in the predictions of Graph Neural Networks is limited by their opaque reasoning process. Prior methods have tried to explain graph networks via concept-based explanations extracted from the latent representations obtained after message passing. However, these explanations fall short of explaining the message passing process itself. To this aim, we propose the Concept Graph Convolution, the first graph convolution designed to operate on node-level concepts for improved interpretability. The proposed convolutional layer performs message passing on a combination of raw and concept representations using structural and attention-based edge weights. We also propose a pure variant of the convolution, only operating in the concept space. Our results show that the Concept Graph Convolution allows to obtain competitive task accuracy, while enabling an increased insight into the evolution of concepts across convolutional steps.

AI.tldr: Introduces Concept Graph Convolution to operate on node-level concepts for improved interpretability of GNNs; includes mixed raw/concept and pure-concept variants; achieves competitive accuracy and clearer tracking of concept evolution across layers.
AI.motivation: Address the opacity of GNNs by explaining not just latent representations but the message passing process itself; provide interpretable insights into how concepts evolve during graph convolution.
AI.method: A graph convolution designed to pass messages on a combination of raw node features and concept representations, using both structural (graph topology) and attention-based edge weights; also proposes a pure variant that operates exclusively in the concept space.
AI.result: Empirical results show competitive task accuracy while enabling increased insight into the evolution of concepts across convolutional steps.
AI.conclusion: Concept Graph Convolution can enhance interpretability of GNNs without sacrificing performance, enabling analysis of concept dynamics over layers and potentially guiding concept discovery and refinement.

title: Emergence Transformer: Dynamical Temporal Attention Matters
summary: The Transformer, a breakthrough architecture in artificial intelligence, owes its success to the attention mechanism, which utilizes long-range interactions in sequential data, enabling the emergent coherence between large language models (LLMs) and data distributions. However, temporal attention, that is, different forms of long-range interactions in temporal sequences, has rarely been explored in emergence phenomenon of complex systems including oscillatory coherence in quantum, biophysical, or climate systems. Here, by designing dynamical temporal attention (DTA) with time-varying query, key, and value matrices, we propose an Emergence Transformer. This architecture allows each component to interact with its own or its neighbors' past states through dynamical attention kernels, thereby enabling the promotion and/or suppression of the emergent coherence of components. Interestingly, we uncover that neighbor-DTA consistently promotes oscillatory coherence, whereas self-DTA exhibits an optimal attention weight for coherence enhancement, owing to its non-monotonic dependence on network structure. Practically, we demonstrate how DTA reshapes social coherence, suggesting strategies to either enhance agreement or preserve plurality. We further apply DTA to the paradigmatic Hopfield neural network, achieving emergent continual learning without catastrophic forgetting. Together, these results lay a foundation and provide an immediate paradigm for modulating emergence phenomenon in networked dynamics only using DTA.

AI.tldr: Emergence Transformer with dynamical temporal attention (DTA) enables control over emergent coherence in networked dynamics. Neighbor-DTA promotes oscillatory coherence; self-DTA has a non-monotonic optimal weight for coherence. Demonstrated in social coherence shaping and Hopfield continual learning, suggesting a general paradigm for modulating emergence using temporal attention.
AI.motivation: Explore temporal attention in emergence phenomena across complex systems where long-range interactions shape coherence and learning, extending beyond static attention to time-varying interactions.
AI.method: Introduce DTA with time-varying query, key, and value matrices; compare neighbor-based and self-based attention kernels; apply to social coherence scenarios and Hopfield networks; evaluate impact on coherence patterns and continual learning capability.
AI.result: Neighbor-DTA consistently enhances oscillatory coherence; self-DTA exhibits an optimal attention weight for coherence enhancement due to non-monotonic dependence on network structure; DTA can reshape social coherence toward agreement or plurality; enables emergent continual learning in Hopfield networks without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: DTA provides a foundational paradigm to modulate emergence in networked dynamics through temporal attention, with broad applicability to complex systems and learning tasks.

title: A Kinematic Framework for Evaluating Pinch Configurations in Robotic Hand Design without Object or Contact Models
comment: This manuscript has been submitted for possible publication
summary: Evaluating the pinch capability of a robotic hand is important for understanding its functional dexterity. However, many existing grasp evaluation methods rely on object geometry or contact force models, which limits their applicability during the early stages of robotic hand design. This study proposes a kinematic evaluation method for analyzing pinch configurations of robotic hands based on interactions between fingertip workspaces. First, the reachable workspace of each fingertip is computed from the joint configurations of the fingers. Then, feasible pinch configurations are detected by evaluating the relationships between fingertip pairs. Since the proposed method does not require information about object geometry or contact force models, the pinch capability of a robotic hand can be evaluated solely based on its kinematic structure. In addition, analyses are performed on four different kinematic structures of the hand to investigate their impact on the pinch configurations. The proposed evaluation framework can serve as a useful tool for comparing different robotic hand designs and analyzing pinch capability during the design stage.

AI.tldr: A purely kinematic framework evaluates pinch capability of robotic hands by analyzing fingertip workspaces, independent of object geometry or contact-force models; it compares four hand kinematic structures to guide design.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation methods depend on object geometry or contact models, limiting early-stage hand design. A geometry-free, kinematic metric enables comparison of hand designs before prototyping.
AI.method: Compute each fingertip reachable workspace from finger joint configurations; detect feasible pinch configurations by evaluating relationships between fingertip pairs; compare four different hand kinematic structures.
AI.result: A geometry-free framework for evaluating pinch capability; enables design comparisons and analysis of how kinematic structure affects pinch capabilities across four hand designs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed kinematic evaluation offers a useful tool for early design-stage comparison of robotic hands and their pinch ability without relying on object geometry or contact models.

title: FluSplat: Sparse-View 3D Editing without Test-Time Optimization
summary:
Recent advances in text-guided image editing and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled high-quality 3D scene manipulation. However, existing pipelines rely on iterative edit-and-fit optimization at test time, alternating between 2D diffusion editing and 3D reconstruction. This process is computationally expensive, scene-specific, and prone to cross-view inconsistencies.
  We propose a feed-forward framework for cross-view consistent 3D scene editing from sparse views. Instead of enforcing consistency through iterative 3D refinement, we introduce a cross-view regularization scheme in the image domain during training. By jointly supervising multi-view edits with geometric alignment constraints, our model produces view-consistent results without per-scene optimization at inference. The edited views are then lifted into 3D via a feedforward 3DGS model, yielding a coherent 3DGS representation in a single forward pass.
  Experiments demonstrate competitive editing fidelity and substantially improved cross-view consistency compared to optimization-based methods, while reducing inference time by orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: A training-time cross-view regularization enables instant, view-consistent 3D scene edits from sparse views via a feed-forward 3DGS model.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational cost, scene-specificity, and cross-view inconsistencies of existing iterative text-guided 3D editing pipelines that alternate 2D diffusion edits and 3D reconstruction.
AI.method: Introduce cross-view regularization in the image domain during training; jointly supervise multi-view edits with geometric alignment constraints; produce view-consistent edits without per-scene optimization; lift edited views into 3D via a feed-forward 3DGS model in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Competitive editing fidelity; substantially improved cross-view consistency vs optimization-based methods; inference time reduced by orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables efficient, consistent 3D scene editing from sparse views in a single forward pass, effectively bridging 2D edit guidance and 3D reconstruction.

title: Energy-Based Open-Set Active Learning for Object Classification
comment: To be published in the 2026 International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR)
summary: Active learning (AL) has emerged as a crucial methodology for minimizing labeling costs in deep learning by selecting the most valuable samples from a pool of unlabeled data for annotation. Traditional AL operates under a closed-set assumption, where all classes in the dataset are known and consistent. However, real-world scenarios often present open-set conditions in which unlabeled data contains both known and unknown classes. In such environments, standard AL techniques struggle. They can mistakenly query samples from unknown categories, leading to inefficient use of annotation budgets. In this paper, we propose a novel dual-stage energy-based framework for open-set AL. Our method employs two specialized energy-based models (EBMs). The first, an energy-based known/unknown separator, filters out samples likely to belong to unknown classes. The second, an energy-based sample scorer, assesses the informativeness of the filtered known samples. Using the energy landscape, our models distinguish between data points from known and unknown classes in the unlabeled pool by assigning lower energy to known samples and higher energy to unknown samples, ensuring that only samples from classes of interest are selected for labeling. By integrating these components, our approach ensures efficient and targeted sample selection, maximizing learning impact in each iteration. Experiments on 2D (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet) and 3D (ModelNet40) object classification benchmarks demonstrates that our framework outperforms existing approaches, achieving superior annotation efficiency and classification performance in open-set environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes a dual-stage energy-based framework for open-set active learning using two EBMs: one to separate known vs unknown data, and another to score informativeness of known samples; shows improved annotation efficiency on CIFAR-10/100, TinyImageNet, and ModelNet40.
AI.motivation: Open-set active learning (AL) scenarios where unlabeled pools contain both known and unknown classes challenge traditional AL, which assumes a closed set; mislabeling or querying unknowns wastes annotation budgets. A mechanism to filter unknowns and selectively label informative known samples is needed for efficiency.
AI.method: Two EBMs operate in stages: (1) an energy-based known/unknown separator filters out potential unknown-class samples by assigning higher energy to unknowns; (2) an energy-based sample scorer evaluates the informativeness of the remaining known samples; the energy landscape guides selection to query labeled data from known classes only. Training relies on energy-based objectives to assign lower energy to known data and higher energy to unknown data, enabling stage-wise filtering and scoring in an integrated pipeline.
AI.result: Empirical results on 2D benchmarks (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet) and 3D ModelNet40 show the approach outperforms existing open-set AL methods in terms of annotation efficiency and final classification accuracy.
AI.conclusion: A dual-stage EBM framework effectively filters unknowns and prioritizes informative known samples, yielding improved annotation efficiency and accuracy in open-set AL across image and 3D shape datasets.

title: JTPRO: A Joint Tool-Prompt Reflective Optimization Framework for Language Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents augmented with external tools often struggle as number of tools grow large and become domain-specific. In such settings, ambiguous tool descriptions and under-specified agent instructions frequently lead to tool mis-selection and incorrect slot/value instantiation. We hypothesize that this is due to two root causes: generic, one-size-fits-all prompts that ignore tool-specific nuances, and underspecified tool schemas that lack clear guidance on when and how to use each tool and how to format its parameters. We introduce Joint Tool-Prompt Reflective Optimization (JTPRO), a framework for improving tool-calling reliability in trace-supervised settings by iteratively using rollout-driven reflection to co-optimize global instructions and per-tool schema/argument descriptions for accurate tool selection and argument instantiation in large tool inventories. JTPRO is designed to preserve only tool-local cues needed for correct disambiguation and slot filling. We evaluate JTPRO across multi-tool benchmarks, which account for different number of tools using three metrics: Tool Selection Accuracy (TSA), Slot Filling Accuracy(SFA), and Overall Success Rate(OSR) (correct tool + correct slots + correct values). JTPRO consistently outperforms strong baselines, including CoT-style agents, and reflective prompt optimizers such as GEPA by 5%-20% (relative) on OSR. Ablations show that joint optimization of instructions and tool schemas is more effective and robust than optimizing either component in isolation.

AI.tldr: JTPRO jointly optimizes tool prompts and tool schemas to improve tool selection and slot/value filling in large tool inventories, using rollout-driven reflection in trace-supervised settings.
AI.motivation: Ambiguity in tool descriptions and under-specified schemas cause mis-selection; generic one-size-fits-all prompts fail for many domain-specific tools.
AI.method: Iterative rollout-driven reflection to co-optimize global instructions and per-tool argument schemas, preserving tool-local cues for disambiguation and slot filling; evaluated on multi-tool benchmarks with TSA, SFA, and OSR; ablations.
AI.result: JTPRO outperforms baselines (including CoT and GEPA) by 5%-20% relative on OSR across benchmarks; joint optimization more effective and robust than optimizing instructions or schemas alone.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of tool prompts and per-tool schemas improves tool calling reliability in large inventories under trace supervision, with robust, cross-benchmark gains.

title: Visual-Tactile Peg-in-Hole Assembly Learning from Peg-out-of-Hole Disassembly
summary: Peg-in-hole (PiH) assembly is a fundamental yet challenging robotic manipulation task. While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in tackling such tasks, it requires extensive exploration. In this paper, we propose a novel visual-tactile skill learning framework for the PiH task that leverages its inverse task, i.e., peg-out-of-hole (PooH) disassembly, to facilitate PiH learning. Compared to PiH, PooH is inherently easier as it only needs to overcome existing friction without precise alignment, making data collection more efficient. To this end, we formulate both PooH and PiH as Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) in a unified environment with shared visual-tactile observation space. A visual-tactile PooH policy is first trained; its trajectories, containing kinematic, visual and tactile information, are temporally reversed and action-randomized to provide expert data for PiH. In the policy learning, visual sensing facilitates the peg-hole approach, while tactile measurements compensate for peg-hole misalignment. Experiments across diverse peg-hole geometries show that the visual-tactile policy attains 6.4% lower contact forces than its single-modality counterparts, and that our framework achieves average success rates of 87.5% on seen objects and 77.1% on unseen objects, outperforming direct RL methods that train PiH policies from scratch by 18.1% in success rate. Demos, code, and datasets are available at https://sites.google.com/view/pooh2pih.

AI.tldr: A visual-tactile framework for peg-in-hole learning uses the easier peg-out-of-hole task as an inverse policy source, leveraging POMDPs with shared visual-tactile observations. It trains a PooH policy, then reverses trajectories and randomizes actions to create PiH expert data, achieving lower contact forces and higher success rates than direct RL.
AI.motivation: PiH assembly is data-hungry and sensitive to contact dynamics. The authors exploit an easier inverse task (PooH) under a unified visual-tactile POMDP to generate high-quality expert data for PiH and reduce data collection burden.
AI.method: Formulate PiH and PooH as POMDPs in a shared observation space. Train a visual-tactile PooH policy. Use temporally reversed trajectories and action randomization to convert PooH data into expert demonstrations for PiH. In policy learning, use visual sensing to guide approach and tactile measurements to handle misalignment.
AI.result: The visual-tactile policy achieves 6.4% lower contact forces than single-modality baselines. Average success: 87.5% on seen objects, 77.1% on unseen objects. Outperforms direct RL policies trained from scratch by 18.1% in success rate. Demos, code, and datasets are released.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively leverages an inverse task and multimodal sensing to improve PiH learning, generalize to unseen objects, and reduce data requirements; it offers practical resources for replication.

title: Normalizing Flows with Iterative Denoising
summary:
Normalizing Flows (NFs) are a classical family of likelihood-based methods that have received revived attention. Recent efforts such as TARFlow have shown that
  NFs are capable of achieving promising performance on image modeling tasks, making them viable alternatives to other methods such as diffusion models.
  In this work, we further advance the state of Normalizing Flow generative models by introducing iterative TARFlow (iTARFlow). Unlike diffusion models, iTARFlow maintains a fully end-to-end, likelihood-based objective during training. During sampling, it performs autoregressive generation followed by an iterative denoising procedure inspired by diffusion-style methods. Through extensive experiments, we show that iTARFlow achieves competitive performance across ImageNet resolutions of 64, 128, and 256 pixels, demonstrating its potential as a strong generative model and advancing the frontier of Normalizing Flows. In addition, we analyze the characteristic artifacts produced by iTARFlow, offering insights that may shed light on future improvements. Code is available at https://github.com/apple/ml-itarflow.

AI.tldr: Iterative TARFlow (iTARFlow) improves likelihood-based normalizing flows by adding an autoregressive sampling stage followed by iterative denoising (diffusion-inspired). Trains end-to-end with likelihood objectives and achieves competitive results on ImageNet (64/128/256) with analyzed artifacts.
AI.motivation: Advance normalizing-flow generative models by combining end-to-end likelihood training with diffusion-like denoising during sampling, aiming for high-quality samples without abandoning likelihood-based training.
AI.method: Train iTARFlow end-to-end using a likelihood-based objective. At sampling time, perform autoregressive generation, then apply an iterative denoising procedure inspired by diffusion models. Evaluate on ImageNet resolutions 64, 128, 256 and analyze artifacts. Code released.
AI.result: iTARFlow achieves competitive performance across ImageNet resolutions, showing that fully likelihood-based NF models with iterative denoising can reach strong generative capabilities. The paper also provides an analysis of characteristic artifacts to inform future improvements.
AI.conclusion: iTARFlow demonstrates that normalizing flows can attain competitive image modeling performance while preserving a likelihood-based training objective, and that diffusion-inspired iterative denoising is a viable enhancement for NF-based generation.

title: Differentiable Conformal Training for LLM Reasoning Factuality
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently hallucinate, limiting their reliability in critical applications. Conformal Prediction (CP) addresses this by calibrating error rates on held-out data to provide statistically valid confidence guarantees. Recent work extends CP to LLM factuality to filter out risky claims, ensuring that hallucination rates remain below a user-specified level (e.g., 10%). While prior methods treat claims independently, Coherent Factuality extends to multi-step reasoning by representing outputs as dependency graphs and jointly validating claims with their logical ancestors. A key limitation is that Coherent Factuality is not differentiable, requiring hand-crafted scorers that at high reliability levels remove nearly 60% of true claims. We introduce Differentiable Coherent Factuality (DCF), a fully differentiable relaxation that enables learning improved scorers while provably recovering the original algorithm's guarantees. Experiments on two benchmark reasoning datasets demonstrate DCF achieves up to 141% improvement in claim retention while maintaining reliability guarantees, representing a significant step towards reliable conformal LLM systems.

AI.tldr: Differentiable Coherent Factuality (DCF) introduces a differentiable relaxation of Coherent Factuality to enable learning improved factuality scorers, while preserving the original conformal guarantees; it aims to reduce hallucinations in LLMs by jointly validating claims in a dependency graph with their ancestors.
AI.motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate, and while Conformal Prediction (CP) provides statistically valid confidence guarantees, extending CP to multi-step factual reasoning requires coherent validation of interdependent claims. Prior Coherent Factuality was not differentiable and relied on hand-crafted scorers, which at high reliability levels discarded a large fraction of true claims.
AI.method: Propose a fully differentiable relaxation of Coherent Factuality (DCF) that enables learning improved scorers within a conformal framework, preserving guaranteed error rates. Train and evaluate on two benchmark reasoning datasets to assess claim retention and reliability.
AI.result: DCF achieves up to 141% improvement in claim retention relative to the baseline while maintaining reliability guarantees, indicating a strong improvement in preserving true claims without sacrificing conformal validity.
AI.conclusion: DCF represents a significant advancement toward reliable, differentiable conformal LLM systems by combining differentiable scoring with conformal guarantees and improving multi-step factuality assessment.

title: Forage V2: Knowledge Evolution and Transfer in Autonomous Agent Organizations
summary:
Autonomous agents operating in open-world tasks -- where the completion boundary is not given in advance -- face denominator blindness: they systematically underestimate the scope of the target space. Forage V1 addressed this through co-evolving evaluation (an independent Evaluator discovers what "complete" means) and method isolation (Evaluator and Planner cannot see each other's code). V2 extends the architecture from a single expedition to a learning organization: experience accumulates across runs, transfers across model capabilities, and institutional safeguards prevent knowledge degradation.
  We demonstrate two claims across three task types (web scraping, API queries, mathematical reasoning). Knowledge accumulation: over six runs, knowledge entries grow from 0 to 54, and denominator estimates stabilize as domain understanding deepens. Knowledge transfer: a weaker agent (Sonnet) seeded with a stronger agent's (Opus) knowledge narrows a 6.6pp coverage gap to 1.1pp, halves cost (9.40 to 5.13 USD), converges in half the rounds (mean 4.5 vs. 7.0), and three independent seeded runs arrive at exactly the same denominator estimate (266), suggesting organizational knowledge calibrates evaluation itself.
  V2's contribution is architectural: it designs institutions -- audit separation, contract protocols, organizational memory -- that make any agent more reliable upon entry. The accumulated experience is organizational, model-agnostic, and transferable, stored as readable documents that any future agent inherits regardless of provider or capability level.

AI.tldr: Forage V2 introduces an organizational, model-agnostic architecture that accumulates and transfers experiential knowledge across runs to calibrate evaluation, reducing denominator blindness and improving reliability across tasks.
AI.motivation: Open-world autonomous agents face denominator blindness, underestimating the target space; a robust, transferable evaluation framework and organizational safeguards are needed to reliably define and reach 'complete' behavior.
AI.method: V1 uses co-evolving Evaluator and Planner with isolation; V2 expands to a learning organization where experience accumulates across runs, transfers across model capabilities, and is safeguarded by audit separation, contract protocols, and organizational memory. Experiments span web scraping, API queries, and mathematical reasoning; transfer is assessed by seeding weaker agents with stronger agents and measuring coverage, cost, rounds to convergence, and denominator consistency.
AI.result: Knowledge entries grow from 0 to 54 over six runs; denominator estimates stabilize with deeper domain understanding. Transfer from Opus to Sonnet reduces coverage gap from 6.6pp to 1.1pp, cuts cost from $9.40 to $5.13, halves convergence rounds (4.5 vs. 7.0), and three seeds converge on the same denominator (266).
AI.conclusion: Architectural contribution: establish audit separation, contract protocols, and organizational memory that render agents more reliable at entry. The accumulated knowledge is organizational, model-agnostic, and transferable, stored as readable documents that future agents inherit, calibrating evaluation itself.

title: ALAS: Adaptive Long-Horizon Action Synthesis via Async-pathway Stream Disentanglement
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2508.07842
summary: Long-Horizon (LH) tasks in Human-Scene Interaction (HSI) are complex multi-step tasks that require continuous planning, sequential decision-making, and extended execution across domains to achieve the final goal. However, existing methods heavily rely on skill chaining by concatenating pre-trained subtasks, with environment observations and self-state tightly coupled, lacking the ability to generalize to new combinations of environments and skills, failing to complete various LH tasks across domains. To solve this problem, this paper presents ALAS, a cross-domain learning framework for LH tasks via biologically inspired dual-stream disentanglement. Inspired by the brain's "where-what" dual pathway mechanism, ALAS comprises two core modules: i) an environment learning module for spatial understanding, which captures object functions, spatial relationships, and scene semantics, achieving cross-domain transfer through complete environment-self disentanglement; ii) a skill learning module for task execution, which processes self-state information including joint degrees of freedom and motor patterns, enabling cross-skill transfer through independent motor pattern encoding. We conducted extensive experiments on various LH tasks in HSI scenes. Compared with existing methods, ALAS can achieve an average subtasks success rate improvement of 23\% and average execution efficiency improvement of 29\%.

AI.tldr: ALAS is a cross-domain LH task learning framework for Human-Scene Interaction that uses a biologically inspired dual-stream disentanglement: an environment learning module for spatial understanding and cross-domain transfer, and a skill learning module for motor pattern encoding and cross-skill transfer. It reports 23% higher subtasks success rate and 29% faster execution on LH tasks.
AI.motivation: LH tasks in HSI require long-horizon planning and robust generalization across domains. Existing methods rely on concatenating pre-trained subtasks with tight coupling between observations and self-state, hindering cross-domain transfer and completion of diverse LH tasks.
AI.method: ALAS comprises two modules: (i) environment learning for spatial understanding, capturing object functions, spatial relations, and scene semantics with complete environment-self disentanglement to enable cross-domain transfer; (ii) skill learning for task execution, processing self-state info (joint DOFs, motor patterns) with independent motor pattern encoding to enable cross-skill transfer. Inspired by the brain's where-what pathway.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on multiple LH tasks in HSI scenes shows ALAS achieves average subtasks success rate improvements of 23% and average execution efficiency improvements of 29% over baselines.
AI.conclusion: ALAS demonstrates that cross-domain LH task learning is feasible via dual-stream disentanglement, improving generalization and efficiency in HSI tasks and suggesting a promising direction for cross-domain, long-horizon robotics.

title: Gaussians on a Diet: High-Quality Memory-Bounded 3D Gaussian Splatting Training
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has revolutionized novel view synthesis with high-quality rendering through continuous aggregations of millions of 3D Gaussian primitives. However, it suffers from a substantial memory footprint, particularly during training due to uncontrolled densification, posing a critical bottleneck for deployment on memory-constrained edge devices. While existing methods prune redundant Gaussians post-training, they fail to address the peak memory spikes caused by the abrupt growth of Gaussians early in the training process. To solve the training memory consumption problem, we propose a systematic memory-bounded training framework that dynamically optimizes Gaussians through iterative growth and pruning. In other words, the proposed framework alternates between incremental pruning of low-impact Gaussians and strategic growing of new primitives with an adaptive Gaussian compensation, maintaining a near-constant low memory usage while progressively refining rendering fidelity. We comprehensively evaluate the proposed training framework on various real-world datasets under strict memory constraints, showing significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods. Particularly, our proposed method practically enables memory-efficient 3DGS training on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier, achieving similar visual quality with up to 80% lower peak training memory consumption than the original 3DGS.

AI.tldr: A memory-bounded training framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting that alternates pruning and growth of Gaussians to maintain near-constant memory usage while progressively improving rendering quality; achieves up to 80% lower peak training memory on Jetson Xavier with similar visual quality to full-memory training.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) delivers high-quality novel view synthesis but has a large training memory footprint due to uncontrolled densification. Pruning after training reduces memory but does not prevent peak spikes during training; edge-device deployment requires memory-efficient training.
AI.method: An iterative, memory-bounded training framework that alternates incremental pruning of low-impact Gaussians with strategic growth of new primitives, coupled with adaptive Gaussian compensation to preserve rendering fidelity and keep memory usage near-constant during training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world datasets under tight memory constraints shows significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods; enables memory-efficient 3DGS training on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier with similar visual quality and up to 80% reduction in peak training memory.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates feasibility and effectiveness of memory-bounded 3DGS training, enabling edge-device deployment and paving the way for memory-aware training of primitive-based 3D representations; future work could explore further memory guarantees and extensions to related representations.

title: Learning to Solve the Quadratic Assignment Problem with Warm-Started MCMC Finetuning
summary: The quadratic assignment problem (QAP) is a fundamental NP-hard task that poses significant challenges for both traditional heuristics and modern learning-based solvers. Existing QAP solvers still struggle to achieve consistently competitive performance across structurally diverse real-world instances. To bridge this performance gap, we propose PLMA, an innovative permutation learning framework. PLMA features an efficient warm-started MCMC finetuning procedure to enhance deployment-time performance, leveraging short Markov chains to anchor the adaptation to the promising regions previously explored. For rapid exploration via MCMC over the permutation space, we design an additive energy-based model (EBM) that enables an $O(1)$-time 2-swap Metropolis-Hastings sampling step. Moreover, the neural network used to parameterize the EBM incorporates a scalable and flexible cross-graph attention mechanism to model interactions between facilities and locations in the QAP. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PLMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across various benchmarks. In particular, PLMA achieves a near-zero average optimality gap on QAPLIB, exhibits remarkably superior robustness on the notoriously difficult Taixxeyy instances, and also serves as an effective QAP solver in bandwidth minimization.

AI.tldr: PLMA: a permutation-learning QAP solver with warm-started MCMC finetuning and a scalable energy-based model enabling fast 2-swap MH sampling, achieving near-zero gaps and robust performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The quadratic assignment problem is NP-hard and current solvers fail to deliver consistent performance across diverse real-world instances. A robust, deployment-friendly learning-based approach is needed.
AI.method: PLMA uses an efficient warm-started MCMC finetuning protocol to anchor adaptation in promising regions. It introduces an additive energy-based model (EBM) parameterized by a scalable cross-graph attention network to model facility-location interactions, enabling an O(1)-time 2-swap Metropolis-Hastings sampling step for fast permutation-space exploration.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows PLMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across benchmarks, achieving near-zero average optimality gap on QAPLIB, exceptional robustness on the Taixxeyy instances, and effectiveness as a QAP solver for bandwidth minimization.
AI.conclusion: PLMA offers a scalable, robust learning-based framework for QAP that delivers deployment-ready performance gains through warm-started MCMC finetuning and fast, attention-driven EBM-based exploration.

title: Resolving space-sharing conflicts in road user interactions through uncertainty reduction: An active inference-based computational model
summary: Understanding how road users resolve space-sharing conflicts is important both for traffic safety and the safe deployment of autonomous vehicles. While existing models have captured specific aspects of such interactions (e.g., explicit communication), a theoretically-grounded computational framework has been lacking. In this paper, we extend a previously developed active inference-based driver behavior model to simulate interactive behavior of two agents. Our model captures three complementary mechanisms for uncertainty reduction in interaction: (i) implicit communication via direct behavioral coupling, (ii) reliance on normative expectations (stop signs, priority rules, etc.), and (iii) explicit communication. In a simplified intersection scenario, we show that normative and explicit communication cues can increase the likelihood of a successful conflict resolution. However, this relies on agents acting as expected. In situations where another agent (intentionally or unintentionally) violates normative expectations or communicates misleading information, reliance on these cues may induce collisions. These findings illustrate how active inference can provide a novel framework for modeling road user interactions which is also applicable in other fields.

AI.tldr: We extend an active inference driver model to two interacting agents at an intersection, incorporating implicit coupling, normative expectations, and explicit communication. The model suggests normative and explicit cues can improve conflict resolution when behavior aligns with expectations, but may cause collisions if norms are violated or cues are misleading; the framework is generalizable to other domains.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap in computationally grounded models of interactive road-user behavior by providing a principled, uncertainty-driven framework for two-agent space-sharing conflicts.
AI.method: Extend an existing active inference–based driver model to simulate two agents at a simplified intersection; implement three uncertainty-reduction mechanisms—implicit behavioral coupling, normative expectations (e.g., stop signs, priority rules), and explicit communication; run simulations to assess conflict-resolution outcomes under varying cues and potential norm violations/misinformation.
AI.result: Explicit and normative cues increase the likelihood of successful conflict resolution when agents comply with expectations; reliance on cues can lead to collisions if norms are violated or information is misleading.
AI.conclusion: Active inference offers a coherent framework for modeling road-user interactions and can be extended to other domains; ensuring cue reliability is crucial for safe autonomous-system deployment.

title: A Hough transform approach to safety-aware scalar field mapping using Gaussian Processes
summary: This paper presents a framework for mapping unknown scalar fields using a sensor-equipped autonomous robot operating in unsafe environments. The unsafe regions are defined as regions of high-intensity, where the field value exceeds a predefined safety threshold. For safe and efficient mapping of the scalar field, the sensor-equipped robot must avoid high-intensity regions during the measurement process. In this paper, the scalar field is modeled as a sample from a Gaussian process (GP), which enables Bayesian inference and provides closed-form expressions for both the predictive mean and the uncertainty. Concurrently, the spatial structure of the high-intensity regions is estimated in real-time using the Hough transform (HT), leveraging the evolving GP posterior. A safe sampling strategy is then employed to guide the robot towards safe measurement locations, using probabilistic safety guarantees on the evolving GP posterior. The estimated high-intensity regions also facilitate the design of safe motion plans for the robot. The effectiveness of the approach is verified through two numerical simulation studies and an indoor experiment for mapping a light-intensity field using a wheeled mobile robot.

AI.tldr: A GP-based Bayesian framework maps unknown scalar fields with safe exploration in unsafe regions, using GP posterior for field inference and Hough transform to identify high-intensity structures, guiding safe sampling and motion planning; validated on simulations and indoor light-field mapping with a wheeled robot.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous sensing in environments where high-intensity regions pose safety risks, requiring accurate field mapping while avoiding hazardous areas.
AI.method: Model the scalar field as a Gaussian process (GP) for Bayesian inference with closed-form predictive mean and variance; estimate the geometry of high-intensity regions in real-time via the Hough transform (HT) informed by the evolving GP posterior; employ a safe sampling strategy with probabilistic safety guarantees to select measurement locations and compute safe motion plans.
AI.result: Demonstrated via two numerical simulations and an indoor experiment mapping a light-intensity field with a wheeled robot, showing effective safe exploration and accurate field reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: The framework integrates GP-based scalar-field modeling with real-time HT-based high-intensity region estimation to enable safe exploration and planning in uncertain environments, with demonstrated effectiveness and potential for extension to other scalar fields and sensing modalities.

title: PASTA: A Patch-Agnostic Twofold-Stealthy Backdoor Attack on Vision Transformers
summary:
Vision Transformers (ViTs) have achieved remarkable success across vision tasks, yet recent studies show they remain vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing patch-wise attacks typically assume a single fixed trigger location during inference to maximize trigger attention. However, they overlook the self-attention mechanism in ViTs, which captures long-range dependencies across patches. In this work, we observe that a patch-wise trigger can achieve high attack effectiveness when activating backdoors across neighboring patches, a phenomenon we term the Trigger Radiating Effect (TRE). We further find that inter-patch trigger insertion during training can synergistically enhance TRE compared to single-patch insertion. Prior ViT-specific attacks that maximize trigger attention often sacrifice visual and attention stealthiness, making them detectable.
  Based on these insights, we propose PASTA, a twofold stealthy patch-wise backdoor attack in both pixel and attention domains. PASTA enables backdoor activation when the trigger is placed at arbitrary patches during inference. To achieve this, we introduce a multi-location trigger insertion strategy to enhance TRE. However, preserving stealthiness while maintaining strong TRE is challenging, as TRE is weakened under stealthy constraints. We therefore formulate a bi-level optimization problem and propose an adaptive backdoor learning framework, where the model and trigger iteratively adapt to each other to avoid local optima. Extensive experiments show that PASTA achieves 99.13% attack success rate across arbitrary patches on average, while significantly improving visual and attention stealthiness (144.43x and 18.68x) and robustness (2.79x) against state-of-the-art ViT defenses across four datasets, outperforming CNN- and ViT-based baselines.

AI.tldr: PASTA is a stealthy patch-wise backdoor attack on Vision Transformers that exploits the Trigger Radiating Effect (TRE) using multi-location trigger insertion and bi-level optimization to enable activation from arbitrary patches, achieving high attack success while improving visual/attention stealthiness and robustness.
AI.motivation: ViTs are vulnerable to backdoors, but existing patch-wise attacks assume a fixed trigger location and neglect ViT self-attention. TRE shows that triggers can radiate across neighboring patches via self-attention, and inter-patch insertion during training enhances this effect. There is a need for a stealthy, robust attack that works across arbitrary patches.
AI.method: Introduce PASTA, a twofold stealthy patch-wise backdoor attack in pixel and attention domains. Use a multi-location trigger insertion strategy to boost TRE, and formulate a bi-level optimization framework where the model and trigger adapt iteratively (adaptive backdoor learning) to avoid local optima and preserve stealthiness.
AI.result: PASTA achieves an average 99.13% attack success rate across arbitrary patches. It significantly improves visual stealthiness (≈144.43x) and attention stealthiness (≈18.68x), and robustness against state-of-the-art ViT defenses (≈2.79x) across four datasets, outperforming CNN- and ViT-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: PASTA provides a highly effective, stealthy, and robust patch-wise backdoor for ViTs by leveraging TRE and multi-location insertion, revealing an elevated threat posture of ViTs and underscoring the need for defenses that consider long-range cross-patch interactions.

title: Meta Additive Model: Interpretable Sparse Learning With Auto Weighting
summary: Sparse additive models have attracted much attention in high-dimensional data analysis due to their flexible representation and strong interpretability. However, most existing models are limited to single-level learning under the mean-squared error criterion, whose empirical performance can degrade significantly in the presence of complex noise, such as non-Gaussian perturbations, outliers, noisy labels, and imbalanced categories. The sample reweighting strategy is widely used to reduce the model's sensitivity to atypical data; however, it typically requires prespecifying the weighting functions and manually selecting additional hyperparameters. To address this issue, we propose a new meta additive model (MAM) based on the bilevel optimization framework, which learns data-driven weighting of individual losses by parameterizing the weighting function via an MLP trained on meta data. MAM is capable of a variety of learning tasks, including variable selection, robust regression estimation, and imbalanced classification. Theoretically, MAM provides guarantees on convergence in computation, algorithmic generalization, and variable selection consistency under mild conditions. Empirically, MAM outperforms several state-of-the-art additive models on both synthetic and real-world data under various data corruptions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a meta additive model (MAM) that uses bilevel optimization to learn data-driven instance weights for sparse additive models via an MLP trained on meta data, enabling robust regression, variable selection, and imbalanced classification with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Existing sparse additive models rely on a single-level mean-squared error objective and often need preset sample reweighting schemes; such approaches can be brittle under non-Gaussian noise, outliers, noisy labels, and class imbalance. A data-driven, automatic weighting mechanism aims to improve robustness and flexibility without manual hyperparameter tuning.
AI.method: Formulates a bilevel optimization: the inner problem fits the additive model with per-instance weighted losses; the outer problem learns the weighting function by an MLP using meta data, enabling data-driven reweighting. The framework supports variable selection, robust regression, and imbalanced classification.
AI.result: Theoretically, guarantees on computational convergence, algorithmic generalization, and variable selection consistency under mild conditions. Empirically, MAM outperforms state-of-the-art additive models on synthetic and real-world datasets across diverse data corruptions.
AI.conclusion: MAM provides a versatile, robust additive modeling framework with automatic data-driven weighting, improving resilience to noise and label issues while enabling common learning tasks, backed by theory and empirical validation.

title: Deconstructing Superintelligence: Identity, Self-Modification and Différance
summary: Self-modification is often taken as constitutive of artificial superintelligence (SI), yet modification is a relative action requiring a supplement outside the operation. When self-modification extends to this supplement, the classical self-referential structure collapses. We formalise this on an associative operator algebra $\mathcal{A}$ with update $\hat{U}$, discrimination $\hat{D}$, and self-representation $\hat{R}$, identifying the supplement with $\mathrm{Comm}(\hat{U})$; an expansion theorem shows that $[\hat{U},\hat{R}]$ decomposes through $[\hat{U},\hat{D}]$, so non-commutation generically propagates. The liar paradox appears as a commutator collapse $[\hat{T},Π_L]=0$, and class $\mathbf{A}$ self-modification realises the same collapse at system scale, yielding a structure coinciding with Priest's inclosure schema and Derrida's diffèrance.

AI.tldr: An algebraic framework for AI self-modification shows that external supplements to the self-referential loop induce non-commutativity that propagates through an update–discrimination–self-representation system; the liar paradox manifests as a commutator collapse, and a Class A self-modification yields the same collapse, aligning with Priest's inclosure and Derrida's différance.
AI.motivation: To formalize the limits of self-modification in autonomous systems by embedding update, discrimination, and self-representation in an associative operator algebra, and to connect the phenomenon to established philosophical notions.
AI.method: Model the system as an associative operator algebra A with operators: update Ũ, discrimination 𝐃̂, and self-representation Ŕ; identify the supplement with Comm(Ũ). Prove an expansion theorem: [Ũ, Ŕ] decomposes through [Ũ, 𝐃̂], implying non-commutation propagates. Interpret liar paradox as a commutator collapse [𝚃̂, Π_L] = 0; show Class A self-modification realizes the same collapse at the system scale, mapping to Priest’s inclosure schema and Derrida’s différance.
AI.result: An explicit decomposition showing how non-commutativity propagates from the update- or discrimination-driven parts to self-representation; a Lie-type commutator collapse representing the liar paradox; a system-scale realization of this collapse for Class A self-modification, with connections to inclosure and différance.
AI.conclusion: Self-modification inherently interfaces with external supplements, breaking pure self-reference and inducing a propagating non-commutativity. The framework links AI self-modification phenomena to established philosophical constructs (Priest, Derrida), offering a formal lens to study limits and paradoxes in self-modifying systems.

title: PokeVLA: Empowering Pocket-Sized Vision-Language-Action Model with Comprehensive World Knowledge Guidance
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have opened new avenues for robot manipulation, yet existing methods exhibit limited efficiency and a lack of high-level knowledge and spatial awareness. To address these challenges, we propose PokeVLA, a lightweight yet powerful foundation model for embodied manipulation that effectively infuses vision-language understanding into action learning. Our framework introduces a two-stage training paradigm: first, we pre-train a compact vision-language model (PokeVLM) on a curated multimodal dataset of 2.4M samples encompassing spatial grounding, affordance, and embodied reasoning tasks; second, we inject manipulation-relevant representations into the action space through multi-view goal-aware semantics learning, geometry alignment, and a novel action expert. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO-Plus benchmark and in real-world deployment, outperforming comparable baselines in success rate and robustness under diverse perturbations. To foster reproducibility and community progress, we will open-source our code, model weights, and the scripts for the curated pre-training dataset. Project page: https://getterupper.github.io/PokeVLA

AI.tldr: PokeVLA presents a lightweight Vision-Language-Action foundation model for embodied manipulation. It pre-trains a compact PokeVLM on 2.4M multimodal samples and then injects manipulation-relevant representations into the action space via multi-view semantics, geometry alignment, and an action expert, achieving state-of-the-art on LIBERO-Plus and robust real-world performance, with code and data to be released.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies and limited high-level knowledge and spatial awareness in existing Vision-Language-Action models for robot manipulation by developing a compact, expertise-enhanced foundation model capable of rich vision-language understanding integrated with action learning.
