AI.method: Two-stage training: (1) pre-train a compact vision-language model (PokeVLM) on a curated multimodal dataset (2.4M samples) focusing on spatial grounding, affordances, and embodied reasoning; (2) inject manipulation-relevant representations into the action space via multi-view goal-aware semantics learning, geometry alignment, and a novel action expert.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO-Plus benchmark and in real-world deployment, with higher success rates and robustness under perturbations compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of combining compact vision-language pretraining with structured action-space enhancements to advance embodied manipulation; plans to open-source code, weights, and dataset scripts to promote reproducibility.

title: FurnSet: Exploiting Repeats for 3D Scene Reconstruction
summary: Single-view 3D scene reconstruction involves inferring both object geometry and spatial layout. Existing methods typically reconstruct objects independently or rely on implicit scene context, failing to exploit the repeated instances commonly present in realworld scenes. We propose FurnSet, a framework that explicitly identifies and leverages repeated object instances to improve reconstruction. Our method introduces per-object CLS tokens and a set-aware self-attention mechanism that groups identical instances and aggregates complementary observations across them, enabling joint reconstruction. We further combine scene-level and object-level conditioning to guide object reconstruction, followed by layout optimization using object point clouds with 3D and 2D projection losses for scene alignment. Experiments on 3D-Future and 3D-Front demonstrate improved scene reconstruction quality, highlighting the effectiveness of exploiting repetition for robust 3D scene reconstruction.

AI.tldr: FurnSet exploits repeated object instances in a scene to improve single-view 3D reconstruction using per-object CLS tokens and set-aware self-attention, combined with scene- and object-level conditioning and layout optimization; validated on 3D-Future and 3D-Front with improved reconstruction quality.
AI.motivation: Repeated object instances are common in real-world scenes, but existing single-view reconstruction methods either reconstruct objects independently or rely on implicit global context, missing cross-instance cues that could improve accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: Introduce per-object CLS tokens and a set-aware self-attention mechanism that groups identical instances and aggregates complementary observations across them to enable joint reconstruction of repeated objects. Combine scene-level and object-level conditioning to guide object reconstruction, followed by layout optimization using object point clouds with 3D and 2D projection losses to align the scene.
AI.result: Experiments on 3D-Future and 3D-Front show improved scene reconstruction quality, demonstrating the effectiveness of leveraging repetition for robust 3D scene reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting repeated object instances is beneficial for single-view 3D scene reconstruction. FurnSet provides a practical framework that leverages repetition to achieve more robust and accurate scene reconstructions.

title: On the Stability and Generalization of First-order Bilevel Minimax Optimization
summary: Bilevel optimization and bilevel minimax optimization have recently emerged as unifying frameworks for a range of machine-learning tasks, including hyperparameter optimization and reinforcement learning. The existing literature focuses on empirical efficiency and convergence guarantees, leaving a critical theoretical gap in understanding how well these algorithms generalize. To bridge this gap, we provide the first systematic generalization analysis for first-order gradient-based bilevel minimax solvers with lower-level minimax problems. Specifically, by leveraging algorithmic stability arguments, we derive fine-grained generalization bounds for three representative algorithms, including single-timescale stochastic gradient descent-ascent, and two variants of two-timescale stochastic gradient descent-ascent. Our results reveal a precise trade-off among algorithmic stability, generalization gaps, and practical settings. Furthermore, extensive empirical evaluations corroborate our theoretical insights on realistic optimization tasks with bilevel minimax structures.

AI.tldr: The paper provides the first systematic generalization analysis for first-order gradient-based bilevel minimax solvers with lower-level minimax problems, deriving stability-based generalization bounds for three algorithms (including single-timescale SGD-ascent and two variants of two-timescale SGD-ascent) and validating theory with experiments.
AI.motivation: There is a theoretical gap in understanding how bilevel minimax optimization generalizes; existing work emphasizes empirical efficiency and convergence, but not generalization behavior.
AI.method: Employ algorithmic stability to derive fine-grained generalization bounds for three representative first-order algorithms: single-timescale SGD-ascent and two variants of two-timescale SGD-ascent, in the presence of lower-level minimax objectives.
AI.result: The bounds reveal a precise trade-off between algorithmic stability, generalization gaps, and practical settings; empirical experiments corroborate the theoretical insights on realistic bilevel minimax tasks.
AI.conclusion: First systematic generalization analysis for gradient-based bilevel minimax solvers with lower-level minimax problems; reveals stability-generalization trade-offs and provides guidance for algorithm design and selection in these structures.

title: Learning When Not to Decide: A Framework for Overcoming Factual Presumptuousness in AI Adjudication
summary: A well-known limitation of AI systems is presumptuousness: the tendency of AI systems to provide confident answers when information may be lacking. This challenge is particularly acute in legal applications, where a core task for attorneys, judges, and administrators is to determine whether evidence is sufficient to reach a conclusion. We study this problem in the important setting of unemployment insurance adjudication, which has seen rapid integration of AI systems and where the question of additional fact-finding poses the most significant bottleneck for a system that affects millions of applicants annually. First, through a collaboration with the Colorado Department of Labor and Employment, we secure rare access to official training materials and guidance to design a novel benchmark that systematically varies in information completeness. Second, we evaluate four leading AI platforms and show that standard RAG-based approaches achieve an average of only 15% accuracy when information is insufficient. Third, advanced prompting methods improve accuracy on inconclusive cases but over-correct, withholding decisions even on clear cases. Fourth, we introduce a structured framework requiring explicit identification of missing information before any determination (SPEC, Structured Prompting for Evidence Checklists). SPEC achieves 89% overall accuracy, while appropriately deferring when evidence is insufficient -- demonstrating that presumptuousness in legal AI is systematic but addressable, and that doing so is a necessary step towards systems that reliably support, rather than supplant, human judgment wherever decisions must await sufficient evidence.

AI.tldr: SPEC (Structured Prompting for Evidence Checklists) reduces presumptuous AI in unemployment insurance adjudication by explicitly eliciting missing information; achieves 89% accuracy vs 15% baseline and defers when evidence is insufficient.
AI.motivation: Address systematic overconfidence of AI in legal settings, particularly where evidence sufficiency is critical.
AI.method: Benchmark with official training materials; compare RAG-based baselines, advanced prompting, and SPEC framework; vary information completeness to simulate incomplete evidence.
AI.result: RAG-based approaches yield ~15% accuracy when information is insufficient; advanced prompting improves inconclusive cases but over-corrects and defers on clear cases; SPEC achieves 89% overall accuracy and appropriately defers when evidence is insufficient.
AI.conclusion: Presumptuousness in legal AI is a pervasive, addressable problem; structured prompting with explicit evidence-checklists aligns AI behavior with human judgment in high-stakes decisions.

title: Topology-Aware Skeleton Detection via Lighthouse-Guided Structured Inference
summary: In natural images, object skeletons are used to represent geometric shapes. However, even slight variations in pose or movement can cause noticeable changes in skeleton structure, increasing the difficulty of detecting the skeleton and often resulting in discontinuous skeletons. Existing methods primarily focus on point-level skeleton point detection and overlook the importance of structural continuity in recovering complete skeletons. To address this issue, we propose Lighthouse-Skel, a topology-aware skeleton detection method via lighthouse-guided structured inference. Specifically, we introduce a dual-branch collaborative detection framework that jointly learns skeleton confidence field and structural anchors, including endpoints and junction points. The spatial distributions learned by the point branch guide the network to focus on topologically vulnerable regions, which improves the accuracy of skeleton detection. Based on the learned skeleton confidence field, we further propose a lighthouse-guided topology completion strategy, which uses detected junction points and breakpoints as lighthouses to reconnect discontinuous skeleton segments along low-cost paths, thereby improving skeleton continuity and structural integrity. Experimental results on four public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive detection accuracy while substantially improving skeleton connectivity and structural integrity.

AI.tldr: A topology-aware skeleton detector with lighthouse-guided structured inference that improves skeleton connectivity while maintaining competitive detection accuracy.
AI.motivation: Skeletons in natural images are sensitive to pose/movement; small variations cause discontinuities, making skeleton detection challenging and often yielding broken skeletons. Existing methods focus on point-level detection and ignore topological continuity.
AI.method: A dual-branch collaborative framework learning a skeleton confidence field and structural anchors (endpoints and junctions). A lighthouse-guided topology completion uses detected lighthouses to reconnect segments along low-cost paths, guided by the confidence field.
AI.result: Competitive detection accuracy on four public datasets, with substantial improvements in skeleton connectivity and structural integrity.
AI.conclusion: Topology-aware skeleton detection with lighthouse-guided completion effectively enhances continuity and structural coherence without sacrificing detection performance.

title: Adaptive Conformal Anomaly Detection with Time Series Foundation Models for Signal Monitoring
summary: We propose a post-hoc adaptive conformal anomaly detection method for monitoring time series that leverages predictions from pre-trained foundation models without requiring additional fine-tuning. Our method yields an interpretable anomaly score directly interpretable as a false alarm rate (p-value), facilitating transparent and actionable decision-making. It employs weighted quantile conformal prediction bounds and adaptively learns optimal weighting parameters from past predictions, enabling calibration under distribution shifts and stable false alarm control, while preserving out-of-sample guarantees. As a model-agnostic solution, it integrates seamlessly with foundation models and supports rapid deployment in resource-constrained environments. This approach addresses key industrial challenges such as limited data availability, lack of training expertise, and the need for immediate inference, while taking advantage of the growing accessibility of time series foundation models. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets show that the proposed approach delivers strong performance, combining simplicity, interpretability, robustness, and adaptivity.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc adaptive conformal anomaly detection for time series using predictions from pre-trained foundation models, providing interpretable p-values as anomaly scores without fine-tuning. It learns adaptive weights for weighted quantile conformal bounds to maintain calibrated false alarm control under distribution shifts, model-agnostic and deployment-friendly.
AI.motivation: To enable reliable, interpretable anomaly detection in time series with limited data and expertise, leveraging large foundation models without fine-tuning, while ensuring short inference times and out-of-sample guarantees.
AI.method: Compute anomaly scores via weighted quantile conformal prediction bounds applied post-hoc to predictions from pre-trained foundation models. The method adaptively learns weighting parameters from past predictions to calibrate under distribution shifts, producing p-values that reflect false alarm rates and preserve guarantees.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows strong performance in terms of accuracy, robustness to distribution shifts, interpretability (p-values), and out-of-sample validity, with simplicity and rapid deployment favorable in resource-constrained settings.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a simple, robust, and interpretable model-agnostic solution for time-series anomaly detection that integrates foundation models without fine-tuning, maintaining calibration and fast deployment in industrial contexts.

title: CreativeGame:Toward Mechanic-Aware Creative Game Generation
summary:
Large language models can generate plausible game code, but turning this capability into \emph{iterative creative improvement} remains difficult. In practice, single-shot generation often produces brittle runtime behavior, weak accumulation of experience across versions, and creativity scores that are too subjective to serve as reliable optimization signals. A further limitation is that mechanics are frequently treated only as post-hoc descriptions, rather than as explicit objects that can be planned, tracked, preserved, and evaluated during generation.
  This report presents \textbf{CreativeGame}, a multi-agent system for iterative HTML5 game generation that addresses these issues through four coupled ideas: a proxy reward centered on programmatic signals rather than pure LLM judgment; lineage-scoped memory for cross-version experience accumulation; runtime validation integrated into both repair and reward; and a mechanic-guided planning loop in which retrieved mechanic knowledge is converted into an explicit mechanic plan before code generation begins. The goal is not merely to produce a playable artifact in one step, but to support interpretable version-to-version evolution.
  The current system contains 71 stored lineages, 88 saved nodes, and a 774-entry global mechanic archive, implemented in 6{,}181 lines of Python together with inspection and visualization tooling. The system is therefore substantial enough to support architectural analysis, reward inspection, and real lineage-level case studies rather than only prompt-level demos.
  A real 4-generation lineage shows that mechanic-level innovation can emerge in later versions and can be inspected directly through version-to-version records. The central contribution is therefore not only game generation, but a concrete pipeline for observing progressive evolution through explicit mechanic change.

AI.tldr: CreativeGame presents a multi-agent pipeline for iterative HTML5 game generation that foregrounds explicit mechanics, memory, and runtime validation to enable interpretable, version-to-version evolution rather than one-shot generation.
AI.motivation: LLMs often produce brittle, non-iterative results and treat game mechanics as post-hoc descriptions. The work seeks to shift generation toward interpretable, mechanic-aware progression with tangible version-to-version signals.
AI.method: Four coupled ideas: (1) proxy reward based on programmatic signals instead of solely LLM judgments, (2) lineage-scoped memory for cross-version experience accumulation, (3) runtime validation integrated into both repair and reward, and (4) a mechanic-guided planning loop that converts retrieved mechanic knowledge into an explicit mechanic plan before code generation. Implemented in Python (6,181 lines) with 71 lineages, 88 saved nodes, and a 774-entry global mechanic archive, plus tooling for inspection/visualization.
AI.result: A real 4-generation lineage shows late-emerging mechanic-level innovation inspectable through version-to-version records; demonstrates a concrete pipeline for observing progressive evolution via explicit mechanic changes, not just single demos.
AI.conclusion: The central contribution is a tangible pipeline for observing and analyzing iterative, mechanic-driven evolution in game-generation systems, enabling architecture analysis and lineage-level case studies.

title: Semi-Supervised Flow Matching for Mosaiced and Panchromatic Fusion Imaging
summary: Fusing a low resolution (LR) mosaiced hyperspectral image (HSI) with a high resolution (HR) panchromatic (PAN) image offers a promising avenue for video-rate HR-HSI imaging via single-shot acquisition, yet its severely ill-posed nature remains a significant challenge. In this work, we propose a novel semi-supervised flow matching framework for mosaiced and PAN image fusion. Unlike previous diffusion-based approaches constrained by specific protocols or handcrafted assumptions, our method seamlessly integrates an unsupervised scheme with flow matching, resulting in a generalizable and efficient generative framework. Specifically, our method follows a two-stage training pipeline. First, we pretrain an unsupervised prior network to produce an initial pseudo HR-HSI. Building on this, we then train a conditional flow matching model to generate the target HR-HSI, introducing a random voting mechanism that iteratively refines the initial HR-HSI estimate, enabling robust and effective fusion. During inference, we employ a conflict-free gradient guidance strategy that ensures spectrally and spatially consistent HR-HSI reconstruction. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior quantitative and qualitative performance by a significant margin compared to representative baselines. Beyond mosaiced and PAN fusion, our approach provides a flexible generative framework that can be readily extended to other image fusion tasks and integrated with unsupervised or blind image restoration algorithms.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised flow-matching framework fuses low-res mosaiced HSI with high-res PAN, using a two-stage training (unsupervised prior to generate pseudo HR-HSI, then conditional flow matching with random voting) and conflict-free gradient guidance for robust HR-HSI reconstruction, achieving state-of-the-art results and generalizing to other fusion tasks.
AI.motivation: Image fusion of mosaiced LR HSI and HR PAN is severely ill-posed and conventional diffusion-based methods can be protocol-restricted; there is a need for a generalizable, efficient framework that leverages unsupervised priors and flow-based generative modeling.
AI.method: Two-stage training: (1) pretrain an unsupervised prior network to produce an initial pseudo HR-HSI; (2) train a conditional flow-matching model to generate the target HR-HSI, incorporating a random voting mechanism to iteratively refine the initial estimate. Inference uses a conflict-free gradient guidance strategy to enforce spectral-spatial consistency. The approach integrates an unsupervised scheme with flow matching and is not tied to specific fusion protocols.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show significant quantitative and qualitative improvements over representative baselines, demonstrating robustness and generalizability. The method is described as flexible and extendable to other image fusion tasks and compatible with unsupervised or blind restoration algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The work presents a flexible semi-supervised generative framework for mosaiced and PAN fusion that outperforms baselines and can be extended to other fusion problems and integrated with unsupervised or blind restoration techniques, addressing the ill-posed nature of HR-HSI fusion.

title: Trajectory-Aware Reliability Modeling of Democratic Systems
summary: Failures in complex systems often emerge through gradual degradation and the propagation of stress across interacting components rather than through isolated shocks. Democratic systems exhibit similar dynamics, where weakening institutions can trigger cascading deterioration in related institutional structures. Traditional reliability and survival models typically estimate failure risk based on the current system state but do not explicitly capture how degradation propagates through institutional networks over time. This paper introduces a trajectory-aware reliability modeling framework based on Dynamic Causal Neural Autoregression (DCNAR). The framework first estimates a causal interaction structure among institutional indicators and then models their joint temporal evolution to generate forward trajectories of system states. Failure risk is defined as the probability that predicted trajectories cross predefined degradation thresholds within a fixed horizon. Using longitudinal institutional indicators, we compare DCNAR-based trajectory risk models with discrete-time hazard and Cox proportional hazards models. Results show that trajectory-aware modeling consistently outperforms Cox models and improves risk prediction for several propagation-driven institutional failures. These findings highlight the importance of modeling dynamic system interactions for reliability analysis and early detection of systemic degradation.

AI.tldr: Introduces a trajectory-aware reliability framework (DCNAR) to model propagation of degradation across institutional indicators; estimates causal interactions, forecasts joint trajectories, and predicts failure risk as threshold-crossing within a horizon; outperforms Cox models in risk prediction.
AI.motivation: Reliability analysis in complex institutions requires capturing how degradation propagates through networks over time rather than relying on current-state hazard models.
AI.method: 1) learn a causal interaction structure among indicators; 2) model joint temporal evolution with Dynamic Causal Neural Autoregression to generate forward trajectories; 3) define failure risk as probability trajectories cross degradation thresholds within a fixed horizon; 4) compare with discrete-time hazard and Cox models using longitudinal data.
AI.result: Trajectory-based DCNAR models outperform Cox models in predicting propagation-driven institutional failures.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic interaction-aware trajectory modeling enhances reliability analysis and enables earlier detection of systemic degradation in political/institutional networks.

title: What Makes a Good AI Review? Concern-Level Diagnostics for AI Peer Review
summary: Evaluating AI-generated reviews by verdict agreement is widely recognized as insufficient, yet current alternatives rarely audit which concerns a system identifies, how it prioritizes them, or whether those priorities align with the review rationale that shaped the final assessment. We propose concern alignment, a diagnostic framework that evaluates AI reviews at the concern level rather than only at the verdict level. The framework's core data structure is the match graph, a bipartite alignment between official and AI-generated concerns annotated with match type, severity, and post-rebuttal treatment. From this artifact we derive an evaluation ladder that moves from binary accuracy to concern detection, verdict-stratified behavior, decision-aware calibration, and rebuttal-aware decomposition. In a pilot study of four public AI review systems evaluated in six configurations, concern-level analysis suggests that detection alone does not determine review quality; calibration is often the binding constraint. Systems detect non-trivial fractions of official concerns yet most mark 25--55% of concerns on accepted papers as decisive, where, under our operationalization, no official concern on accepted papers was treated as a decisive blocker. Identical overall verdict accuracy can conceal reject-heavy behavior versus low-recall profiles, and low full-review false decisive rates can partly reflect concern dilution rather than calibrated prioritization. Most systems do not emit a native accept/reject, and inferring it from review tone is method-sensitive, reinforcing the need for concern-level diagnostics that remain stable across inference choices. The contribution is a reusable evaluation framework for auditing which concerns AI reviewers identify, how they weight them, and whether those priorities align with the review rationale that informed the paper's final assessment.

AI.tldr: Proposes a concern-alignment framework to audit AI-generated reviews at the level of concerns, using a match-graph to align AI and official concerns and an evaluation ladder that reveals calibration and prioritization issues not captured by verdicts.
AI.motivation: Verdict-level evaluation of AI reviews is known to be insufficient; there is a need to audit what concerns AI reviewers identify, how they weigh them, and whether these priorities align with the review rationale that informed the final decision.
AI.method: Introduce the match graph, a bipartite structure aligning official and AI concerns with attributes like match type, severity, and post-rebuttal treatment. Develop an evaluation ladder from binary accuracy to concern detection, verdict-stratified behavior, decision-aware calibration, and rebuttal-aware decomposition. Validate via a pilot study of four public AI review systems across six configurations.
AI.result: Concern-level analysis reveals detection alone does not determine review quality; calibration is often the binding constraint. Systems detect non-trivial fractions of official concerns, yet many mark 25–55% of concerns on accepted papers as decisive, while no official concern on accepted papers acts as a decisive blocker. Identical verdict accuracy can mask reject-heavy versus low-recall profiles; low full-review false-decisive rates may reflect concern dilution rather than calibrated prioritization. Most systems do not emit a native accept/reject, and inferring it from tone is method-sensitive. The analysis underscores the need for concern-level diagnostics that are robust across inference choices.
AI.conclusion: This work delivers a reusable evaluation framework for auditing which concerns AI reviewers identify, how they weight them, and whether these priorities align with the review rationale that informed the paper’s final assessment.

title: From Scene to Object: Text-Guided Dual-Gaze Prediction
summary: Interpretable driver attention prediction is crucial for human-like autonomous driving. However, existing datasets provide only scene-level global gaze rather than fine-grained object-level annotations, inherently failing to support text-grounded cognitive modeling. Consequently, while Vision-Language Models (VLMs) hold great potential for semantic reasoning, this critical data limitations leads to severe text-vision decoupling and visual-bias hallucinations. To break this bottleneck and achieve precise object-level attention prediction, this paper proposes a novel dual-branch gaze prediction framework, establishing a complete paradigm from data construction to model architecture. First, we construct G-W3DA, a object-level driver attention dataset. By integrating a multimodal large language model with the Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3), we decouple macroscopic heatmaps into object-level masks under rigorous cross-validation, fundamentally eliminating annotation hallucinations. Building upon this high-quality data foundation, we propose the DualGaze-VLM architecture. This architecture extracts the hidden states of semantic queries and dynamically modulates visual features via a Condition-Aware SE-Gate, achieving intent-driven precise spatial anchoring. Extensive experiments on the W3DA benchmark demonstrate that DualGaze-VLM consistently surpasses existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) models in spatial alignment metrics, notably achieving up to a 17.8% improvement in Similarity (SIM) under safety-critical scenarios. Furthermore, a visual Turing test reveals that the attention heatmaps generated by DualGaze-VLM are perceived as authentic by 88.22% of human evaluators, proving its capability to generate rational cognitive priors.

AI.tldr: DualGaze-VLM introduces an object-level gaze prediction framework built on the G-W3DA dataset, achieving state-of-the-art spatial alignment and highly authentic attention heatmaps through object-level masks generated via LLM+SAM3 integration and a Condition-Aware SE-Gate for intent-driven feature modulation.
AI.motivation: Existing driving gaze datasets are scene-level and fail to provide object-level, text-grounded cognitive data, causing text-vision decoupling and hallucinations in VLMs. The work aims to enable precise, interpretable, object-level attention in autonomous driving.
AI.method: Construct G-W3DA by integrating a multimodal large language model with Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) to derive object-level masks with cross-validation, eliminating annotation hallucinations. Propose DualGaze-VLM: a dual-branch architecture that extracts semantic query states and uses a Condition-Aware SE-Gate to dynamically modulate visual features for intent-driven spatial anchoring.
AI.result: On the W3DA benchmark, DualGaze-VLM surpasses SOTA in spatial alignment metrics, with up to 17.8% improvement in Similarity (SIM) under safety-critical scenarios. A visual Turing test reports 88.22% of human evaluators perceive the attention heatmaps as authentic, indicating plausible cognitive priors.
AI.conclusion: Data-quality enhancement via LLM+SAM3-based object masks reduces annotation hallucinations and enables accurate, interpretable object-level attention. The DualGaze-VLM framework delivers robust spatial alignment and human-perceived realism, though further validation across domains and assessment of computational cost and reliance on external models would be valuable.

title: IMPACT-CYCLE: A Contract-Based Multi-Agent System for Claim-Level Supervisory Correction of Long-Video Semantic Memory
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, code are available at https://github.com/MKong17/IMPACT_CYCLE
summary: Correcting errors in long-video understanding is disproportionately costly: existing multimodal pipelines produce opaque, end-to-end outputs that expose no intermediate state for inspection, forcing annotators to revisit raw video and reconstruct temporal logic from scratch. The core bottleneck is not generation quality alone, but the absence of a supervisory interface through which human effort can be proportional to the scope of each error. We present IMPACT-CYCLE, a supervisory multi-agent system that reformulates long-video understanding as iterative claim-level maintenance of a shared semantic memory -- a structured, versioned state encoding typed claims, a claim dependency graph, and a provenance log. Role-specialized agents operating under explicit authority contracts decompose verification into local object-relation correctness, cross-temporal consistency, and global semantic coherence, with corrections confined to structurally dependent claims. When automated evidence is insufficient, the system escalates to human arbitration as the supervisory authority with final override rights; dependency-closure re-verification then ensures correction cost remains proportional to error scope. Experiments on VidOR show substantially improved downstream reasoning (VQA: 0.71 to 0.79) and a 4.8x reduction in human arbitration cost, with workload significantly lower than manual annotation. Code will be released at https://github.com/MKong17/IMPACT_CYCLE.

AI.tldr: Supervisory multi-agent system IMPACT-CYCLE for long-video understanding that maintains a shared semantic memory and uses authority-driven agents to iteratively fix errors with human arbitration when needed, reducing annotation effort and improving reasoning on VidOR.
AI.motivation: Long-video understanding is costly due to opaque end-to-end outputs and lack of intermediate inspectable state. Correction cost should scale with error scope; need a supervisory interface.
AI.method: Shared semantic memory with typed claims, claim dependency graph, provenance log; role-specialized agents with authority contracts perform local object-relation checks, cross-temporal consistency, and global semantic coherence; corrections confined to dependent claims; escalate to human arbitration when automated evidence is insufficient; perform dependency-closure re-verification; iterative cycles.
AI.result: On VidOR: improved VQA reasoning (0.71 → 0.79) and 4.8x reduction in human arbitration cost; workload significantly lower than manual annotation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates cost proportionality to error scope and improved debuggability; code to be released; broader impact on scalable long-video understanding.

title: A Delta-Aware Orchestration Framework for Scalable Multi-Agent Edge Computing
summary: The Synergistic Collapse occurs when scaling beyond 100 agents causes superlinear performance degradation that individual optimizations cannot prevent. We observe this collapse with 150 cameras in Smart City deployment using MADDPG, where Deadline Satisfaction drops from 78% to 34%, producing approximately $180,000 in annual cost overruns. Prior work has addressed each contributing factor in isolation: exponential action-space growth, computational redundancy among spatially adjacent agents, and task-agnostic hardware scheduling. None has examined how these three factors interact and amplify each other. We present DAOEF (Delta-Aware Orchestration for Edge Federations), a framework that addresses all three simultaneously through: (1) Differential Neural Caching, which stores intermediate layer activations and computes only the input deltas, achieving 2.1x higher hit ratios (72% vs. 35%) than output-level caching while staying within 2% accuracy loss through empirically calibrated similarity thresholds; (2) Criticality-Based Action Space Pruning, which organizes agents into priority tiers and reduces coordination complexity from O(n2) to O(n log n) with less than 6% optimality loss; and (3) Learned Hardware Affinity Matching, which assigns tasks to their optimal accelerator (GPU, CPU, NPU, or FPGA) to prevent compounding mismatch penalties. Controlled factor-isolation experiments confirm that each mechanism is necessary but insufficient on its own: removing any single mechanism increases latency by more than 40%, validating that the gains are interdependent rather than additive. Across four datasets (100-250 agents) and a 20-device physical testbed, DAOEF achieves a 1.45x multiplicative gain over applying the three mechanisms independently. A 200-agent cloud deployment yields 62% latency reduction (280 ms vs. 735 ms), sub-linear latency growth up to 250 agents.

AI.tldr: DAOEF provides a synergistic orchestration for edge federations by combining differential neural caching, criticality-based action space pruning, and learned hardware affinity matching; the interactions are necessary to achieve gains and exhibit multiplicative improvements across multiple datasets and real hardware, mitigating the synergistic collapse beyond scale.
AI.motivation: to study and address interaction effects among three previously isolated factors driving superlinear performance degradation in large-scale multi-agent edge deployments.
AI.method: implement three mechanisms; conduct controlled factor-isolation experiments; evaluate on four datasets (100-250 agents), 20-device testbed, and a 200-agent cloud deployment; metrics include latency, deadline satisfaction, cache hit ratio, optimality loss, and coordinated complexity.
AI.result: nearly 2.1x cache hit ratio improvement; action-space pruning reduces coordination from O(n^2) to O(n log n) with <6% loss; 1.45x multiplicative gain when combining all mechanisms; 62% latency reduction (280 ms vs 735 ms) at 200 agents; sub-linear latency growth up to 250 agents.
AI.conclusion: the mechanisms interact synergistically and are collectively necessary; the approach generalizes to edge federations and mitigates the synergistic collapse; isolated optimizations are insufficient.

title: Separable Pathways for Causal Reasoning: How Architectural Scaffolding Enables Hypothesis-Space Restructuring in LLM Agents
summary: Causal discovery through experimentation and intervention is fundamental to robust problem solving. It requires not just updating beliefs within a fixed framework but revising the hypothesis space itself, a capacity current AI agents lack when evidence demands representations they have not previously constructed. We extend the blicket detector paradigm from developmental science to test this capacity in AI agents equipped with architectural scaffolding that targets hypothesis-space restructuring. Our compositional architecture has two discrete components: context graphs, which structure exploration as typed state machines, and dynamic behaviors, which monitor for evidence that the current hypothesis space is inadequate and expand it at runtime. Across 1,085 experimental trials, these components make orthogonal contributions: context graphs drive reasoning quality within the post-switch hypothesis space, accounting for 94\% of the accuracy gain, while dynamic behaviors drive reasoning eligibility by detecting regime changes and preventing premature commitment to outdated hypotheses.

AI.tldr: A compositional AI architecture enables hypothesis-space restructuring for causal discovery by combining context-guided exploration with dynamic, runtime expansion of hypothesis space; evaluated across 1,085 trials, where context graphs account for the majority of accuracy gains and dynamic behaviors prevent premature commitment.
AI.motivation: To overcome AI's limitation in revising representations when evidence requires new hypothesis spaces, drawing on the blicket detector paradigm from developmental science.
AI.method: Two-component architecture: context graphs (typed state-machine structures guiding exploration) and dynamic behaviors (monitoring for signs that the current hypothesis space is inadequate and expanding it at runtime); empirical evaluation across 1,085 trials.
AI.result: Context graphs explain ~94% of accuracy gain within the post-switch hypothesis space; dynamic behaviors detect regime changes and prevent premature commitment; both components contribute orthogonally to improved causal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Architectural scaffolding for hypothesis-space restructuring enables robust causal discovery and adaptation to new representations, addressing core limitations of current AI agents.

title: Distributional Value Estimation Without Target Networks for Robust Quality-Diversity
comment: Accepted as Full Paper at GECCO'26
summary: Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms excel at discovering diverse repertoires of skills, but are hindered by poor sample efficiency and often require tens of millions of environment steps to solve complex locomotion tasks. Recent advances in Reinforcement Learning (RL) have shown that high Update-to-Data (UTD) ratios accelerate Actor-Critic learning. While effective, standard high-UTD algorithms typically utilise target networks to stabilise training. This requirement introduces a significant computational bottleneck, rendering them impractical for resource-intensive Quality-Diversity (QD) tasks where sample efficiency and rapid population adaptation are critical. In this paper, we introduce QDHUAC, a sample-efficient, target-free and distributional QD-RL algorithm that provides dense and low-variance gradient signals, which enables high-UTD training for Dominated Novelty Search whilst requiring an order of magnitude fewer environment steps. We demonstrate that our method enables stable training at high UTD ratios, achieving competitive coverage and fitness on high-dimensional Brax environments with an order of magnitude fewer samples than baselines. Our results suggest that combining target-free distributional critics with dominance-based selection is a key enabler for the next generation of sample-efficient evolutionary RL algorithms.

AI.tldr: QDHUAC is a target-free distributional QD-RL algorithm enabling stable, high update-to-data training for Dominated Novelty Search, achieving competitive performance with an order of magnitude fewer environment samples.
AI.motivation: QD methods are sample-inefficient and slow to adapt; high UTD RL benefits stability but typically relies on target networks, which are computationally costly for QD tasks that require rapid population adaptation.
AI.method: Introduce QDHUAC, a target-free distributional critic with dominance-based (Dominated Novelty Search) selection, providing dense, low-variance gradients to support high UTD training without target networks, demonstrated on high-dimensional Brax environments.
AI.result: Stable training at high UTD ratios with competitive coverage and fitness, achieved with an order of magnitude fewer environment steps than baselines.
AI.conclusion: Combining target-free distributional critics with dominance-based selection is a key enabler for sample-efficient evolutionary RL and next-generation QD-RL algorithms.

title: GSCompleter: A Distillation-Free Plugin for Metric-Aware 3D Gaussian Splatting Completion in Seconds
summary: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has revolutionized real-time rendering, its performance degrades significantly under sparse-view extrapolation, manifesting as severe geometric voids and artifacts. Existing solutions primarily rely on an iterative "Repair-then-Distill" paradigm, which is inherently unstable and prone to overfitting. In this work, we propose GSCompleter, a distillation-free plugin that shifts scene completion to a stable "Generate-then-Register" workflow. Our approach first synthesizes plausible 2D reference images and explicitly lifts them into metric-scale 3D primitives via a robust Stereo-Anchor mechanism. These primitives are then seamlessly integrated into the global context through a novel Ray-Constrained Registration strategy. This shift to a rapid registration paradigm delivers superior 3DGS completion performance across three distinct benchmarks, enhancing the quality and efficiency of various baselines and achieving new SOTA results.

AI.tldr: Distillation-free 3DGS completion via a Generate-then-Register pipeline: synthesize 2D references, lift to metric 3D primitives with Stereo-Anchor, and align with Ray-Constrained Registration, achieving state-of-the-art results on sparse-view 3D Gaussian Splatting benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address severe geometric voids and artifacts in sparse-view extrapolation for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and the instability/overfitting of the traditional Repair-then-Distill approach.
AI.method: Generate-then-Register workflow: (1) synthesize plausible 2D reference images; (2) lift them into metric-scale 3D primitives via Stereo-Anchor; (3) integrate primitives into the global scene using Ray-Constrained Registration.
AI.result: Achieves superior 3DGS completion across three benchmarks, improves performance of baseline methods, and sets new state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: A distillation-free GSCompleter plugin enables robust, efficient 3DGS completion by decoupling completion from distillation and leveraging a stable generate-then-register pipeline.

title: Pairing Regularization for Mitigating Many-to-One Collapse in GANs
summary: Mode collapse remains a fundamental challenge in training generative adversarial networks (GANs). While existing works have primarily focused on inter-mode collapse, such as mode dropping, intra-mode collapse-where many latent variables map to the same or highly similar outputs-has received significantly less attention. In this work, we propose a pairing regularizer jointly optimized with the generator to mitigate the many-to-one collapse by enforcing local consistency between latent variables and generated samples. We show that the effect of pairing regularization depends on the dominant failure mode of training. In collapse-prone regimes with limited exploration, pairing encourages structured local exploration, leading to improved coverage and higher recall. In contrast, under stabilized training with sufficient exploration, pairing refines the generator's induced data density by discouraging redundant mappings, thereby improving precision without sacrificing recall. Extensive experiments on both toy distributions and real-image benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed regularizer effectively complements existing stabilization techniques by directly addressing intra-mode collapse.

AI.tldr: A pairing regularizer for GANs to mitigate intra-mode (many-to-one) collapse by enforcing local latent-to-sample consistency; effectiveness depends on training regime: improves coverage/recall in exploration-poor regimes and refines data density/precision in exploration-rich regimes; complements existing stabilization techniques.
AI.motivation: Intra-mode collapse (many latent codes map to similar outputs) is under-explored compared to inter-mode collapse; addressing it can improve coverage and the fidelity of generated samples across modes.
AI.method: Introduce a pairing regularizer that is jointly optimized with the generator to enforce local consistency between latent variables and generated samples. Analyze how the effect of regularization varies with the dominant failure mode. Validate on toy distributions and real-image benchmarks, demonstrating complementarity with stabilization techniques.
AI.result: The regularizer mitigates intra-mode collapse by encouraging structured local exploration in under-exploration regimes, increasing recall; in stabilized, well-explored training it refines the generator’s data density by discouraging redundant mappings, improving precision without reducing recall. Empirical results on toy and real-image datasets show improved coverage and reduced redundancy, complementing existing GAN stabilization methods.
AI.conclusion: A pairing regularizer offers a versatile, regime-adaptive solution to intra-mode collapse in GANs, working alongside stabilization techniques to improve both recall and precision depending on training dynamics.

title: From Fuzzy to Formal: Scaling Hospital Quality Improvement with AI
summary: Hospital Quality Improvement (QI) plays a critical role in optimizing healthcare delivery by translating high-level hospital goals into actionable solutions. A critical step of QI is to identify the key modifiable contributing factors, a process we call QI factor discovery, typically through expert-driven semi-structured qualitative tools like fishbone diagrams, chart reviews, and Lean Healthcare methods. AI has the potential to transform and accelerate QI factor discovery, which is traditionally time- and resource-intensive and limited in reproducibility and auditability. Nevertheless, current AI alignment methods assume the task is well-defined, whereas QI factor discovery is an exploratory, fuzzy, and iterative sense-making process that relies on complex implicit expert judgments. To design an AI pipeline that formalizes the QI process while preserving its exploratory components, we propose viewing the task as learning not only LLM prompts but also the overarching natural-language specifications. In particular, we map QI factor discovery to steps of the classical AI/ML development process (problem formalization, model learning, and model validation) where the specifications are tunable hyperparameters. Domain experts and AI agents iteratively refine both the overarching specifications and AI pipeline until AI extractions are concordant with expert annotations and aligned with clinical objectives. We applied this "Human-AI Spec-Solution Co-optimization" framework at an urban safety-net hospital to identify factors driving prolonged length of stay and unplanned 30-day readmissions. The resulting AI-for-QI pipelines achieved $\ge 70\%$ concordance with expert annotations. Compared to prior manual Lean analyses, the AI pipeline was substantially more efficient, recovered previous findings, surfaced new modifiable factors, and produced auditable reasoning traces.

AI.tldr: An AI-assisted QI factor discovery framework (“Human-AI Spec-Solution Co-optimization”) that formalizes qualitative hospital quality improvement tasks and accelerates identification of modifiable factors, achieving substantial concordance with expert annotations and improved efficiency.
AI.motivation: QI factor discovery is time-consuming, qualitative, and relies on expert interpretation; standard AI alignment assumes well-defined tasks, which is ill-suited for exploratory, sense-making processes typical of QI.
AI.method: Frame QI factor discovery as steps in the AI/ML lifecycle (problem formalization, model learning, validation) with tunable natural-language specifications and prompts; iteratively co-optimized by domain experts and AI agents until extractions align with expert annotations and clinical objectives; deployed at an urban safety-net hospital to identify factors affecting prolonged length of stay and unplanned 30-day readmissions.
AI.result: AI-for-QI pipelines achieved ≥70% concordance with expert annotations; more efficient than manual Lean analyses; recovered prior findings, surfaced new modifiable factors, and produced auditable reasoning traces.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable framework for integrating AI into QI while preserving exploratory, interpretive aspects; improves efficiency, reproducibility, and auditability, with potential applicability across hospital operations.

title: SpaCeFormer: Fast Proposal-Free Open-Vocabulary 3D Instance Segmentation
summary: Open-vocabulary 3D instance segmentation is a core capability for robotics and AR/VR, but prior methods trade one bottleneck for another: multi-stage 2D+3D pipelines aggregate foundation-model outputs at hundreds of seconds per scene, while pseudo-labeled end-to-end approaches rely on fragmented masks and external region proposals. We present SpaCeFormer, a proposal-free space-curve transformer that runs at 0.14 seconds per scene, 2-3 orders of magnitude faster than multi-stage 2D+3D pipelines. We pair it with SpaCeFormer-3M, the largest open-vocabulary 3D instance segmentation dataset (3.0M multi-view-consistent captions over 604K instances from 7.4K scenes) built through multi-view mask clustering and multi-view VLM captioning; it reaches 21x higher mask recall than prior single-view pipelines (54.3% vs 2.5% at IoU > 0.5). SpaCeFormer combines spatial window attention with Morton-curve serialization for spatially coherent features, and uses a RoPE-enhanced decoder to predict instance masks directly from learned queries without external proposals. On ScanNet200 we achieve 11.1 zero-shot mAP, a 2.8x improvement over the prior best proposal-free method; on ScanNet++ and Replica, we reach 22.9 and 24.1 mAP, surpassing all prior methods including those using multi-view 2D inputs.

AI.tldr: SpaCeFormer is a fast, proposal-free 3D instance segmentation method that leverages space-curve transformer with Morton-curve spatial encoding and RoPE-based decoder, achieving 0.14s per scene and strong zero-shot and multi-dataset performance; paired with SpaCeFormer-3M dataset built via multi-view clustering and captioning.
AI.motivation: Address the bottlenecks of existing 3D instance segmentation that rely on multi-stage 2D+3D pipelines or pseudo-labeled end-to-end methods with fragmented masks and external proposals; enable fast, accurate open-vocabulary 3D instance segmentation suitable for robotics and AR/VR.
AI.method: Proposes SpaCeFormer, a proposal-free space-curve transformer that uses spatial window attention and Morton-curve serialization to obtain spatially coherent features, with a RoPE-enhanced decoder to predict instance masks directly from learned queries without external proposals. Builds SpaCeFormer-3M via multi-view mask clustering and multi-view VLM captioning to create a large open-vocabulary 3D instance segmentation dataset.
AI.result: SpaCeFormer runs at 0.14 seconds per scene, 2-3 orders of magnitude faster than multi-stage 2D+3D pipelines. SpaCeFormer-3M yields 3.0M captions over 604K instances across 7.4K scenes, achieving 21x higher mask recall than prior single-view pipelines (54.3% vs 2.5% at IoU>0.5). On ScanNet200, zero-shot mAP is 11.1 (2.8x improvement over prior best), and on ScanNet++ and Replica, mAPs are 22.9 and 24.1, surpassing methods including those using multi-view 2D inputs.
AI.conclusion: Open-vocabulary 3D instance segmentation can be achieved with a fast, proposal-free architecture (SpaCeFormer) and a large, multi-view open-vocabulary dataset (SpaCeFormer-3M). The approach yields strong zero-shot generalization and competitive performance across standard benchmarks, surpassing both proposal-free and multi-view 2D-input methods.

title: HumanScore: Benchmarking Human Motions in Generated Videos
summary: Recent advances in model architectures, compute, and data scale have driven rapid progress in video generation, producing increasingly realistic content. Yet, no prior method systematically measures how faithfully these systems render human bodies and motion dynamics. In this paper, we present HumanScore, a systematic framework to evaluate the quality of human motions in AI-generated videos. HumanScore defines six interpretable metrics spanning kinematic plausibility, temporal stability, and biomechanical consistency, enabling fine-grained diagnosis beyond visual realism alone. Through carefully designed prompts, we elicit a diverse set of movements at varying intensities and evaluate videos generated by thirteen state-of-the-art models. Our analysis reveals consistent gaps between perceptual plausibility and motion biomechanical fidelity, identifies recurrent failure modes (e.g., temporal jitter, anatomically implausible poses, and motion drift), and produces robust model rankings from quantitative and physically meaningful criteria.

AI.tldr: HumanScore provides a six-metric framework to evaluate human motion fidelity in AI-generated videos, revealing gaps between perceptual plausibility and biomechanical correctness and offering robust model rankings across 13 models.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of systematic evaluation of motion dynamics in AI-generated human videos despite advances in generation, to diagnose and improve motion realism.
AI.method: Define six interpretable metrics covering kinematic plausibility, temporal stability, and biomechanical consistency; use carefully designed prompts to elicit diverse movements at different intensities; evaluate videos from 13 SOTA models; report quantitative and qualitative results and rankings.
AI.result: Identify gaps between perceptual plausibility and biomechanical fidelity; common failure modes include temporal jitter, anatomically implausible poses, motion drift; provide robust, physically-grounded model rankings.
AI.conclusion: HumanScore enables fine-grained diagnosis and benchmarking of human motion quality in AI-generated videos, guiding improvements and standardizing evaluation.

title: Fourier Weak SINDy: Spectral Test Function Selection for Robust Model Identification
comment: Accepted to the 8th Annual Learning for Dynamics & Control Conference (L4DC 2026)
summary: We introduce Fourier Weak SINDy, a minimal noise-robust and interpretable derivative-free equation learning method that combines weak-form sparse equation learning with spectral density estimation for data-driven test function selection. By using orthogonal sinusoidal test functions inspired by their prevalence in Modulating Function-based system identification, the weak-form sparse regression problem reduces to a regression over Fourier coefficients. Dominant frequencies are then selected via multitaper estimation of the frequency spectrum of the data. This formulation unifies weak-form learning and spectral estimation within a compact and flexible framework. We illustrate the effectiveness of this approach in numerical experiments across multiple chaotic and hyperchaotic ODE benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes Fourier Weak SINDy, a noise-robust, derivative-free equation learning method combining weak-form sparse learning with spectral density estimation, using orthogonal sinusoidal test functions and multitaper frequency selection; demonstrated on chaotic and hyperchaotic ODE benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address noise sensitivity in data-driven equation discovery and integrate spectral information for test function choice, unifying weak-form learning with spectral estimation to improve robustness and interpretability.
AI.method: Builds a weak-form sparse regression framework using orthogonal sinusoidal test functions (Fourier basis); reduces learning to regression over Fourier coefficients; dominant frequencies are identified via multitaper spectral estimation to select test functions.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on chaotic and hyperchaotic ODE benchmarks show robust performance, accurate term discovery, and clear frequency-structure identification, demonstrating the method's effectiveness and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The framework unifies weak-form learning with spectral estimation in a compact, interpretable model, offering noise robustness and applicability to complex dynamical systems.

title: EvoAgent: An Evolvable Agent Framework with Skill Learning and Multi-Agent Delegation
summary: This paper proposes EvoAgent - an evolvable large language model (LLM) agent framework that integrates structured skill learning with a hierarchical sub-agent delegation mechanism. EvoAgent models skills as multi-file structured capability units equipped with triggering mechanisms and evolutionary metadata, and enables continuous skill generation and optimization through a user-feedback-driven closed-loop process. In addition, by incorporating a three-stage skill matching strategy and a three-layer memory architecture, the framework supports dynamic task decomposition for complex problems and long-term capability accumulation. Experimental results based on real-world foreign trade scenarios demonstrate that, after integrating EvoAgent, GPT5.2 achieves significant improvements in professionalism, accuracy, and practical utility. Under a five-dimensional LLM-as-Judge evaluation protocol, the overall average score increases by approximately 28%. Further model transfer experiments indicate that the performance of an agent system depends not only on the intrinsic capabilities of the underlying model, but also on the degree of synergy between the model and the agent architecture.

AI.tldr: EvoAgent is an evolvable LLM agent framework that uses multi-file structured skill units with triggering mechanisms and evolutionary metadata, plus a three-stage skill matching and three-layer memory system, enabling dynamic task decomposition and long-term capability accumulation. Empirically improves GPT5.2 performance by ~28% on a five-dimensional LLM-as-Judge protocol in real-world foreign-trade scenarios; results highlight the importance of synergy between model and agent architecture.
AI.motivation: Address scalability and long-term adaptability in LLM agents by enabling evolvable, structured skills and hierarchical delegation with feedback-driven optimization, to support complex, long-horizon tasks and continuous capability accumulation.
AI.method: Design of skills as multi-file structured capability units with triggering mechanisms and evolutionary metadata. Incorporation of a three-stage skill matching strategy and a three-layer memory architecture. Closed-loop user-feedback to drive continuous skill generation and optimization. Evaluation in real-world foreign-trade scenarios using a five-dimensional LLM-as-Judge protocol, plus transfer experiments to assess model-architecture synergy.
AI.result: Significant improvements in professionalism, accuracy, and practical utility after integrating EvoAgent. Overall average LLM-as-Judge score increases by ~28%. Transfer experiments indicate agent performance depends on synergy between model and architecture, not solely intrinsic model capabilities.
AI.conclusion: EvoAgent demonstrates that an evolvable, skill-based agent framework with hierarchical delegation and memory can enhance LLM performance beyond raw model strength, enabling dynamic task decomposition, long-horizon capability accumulation, and meaningful model–architecture synergy in practical deployments.

title: Occupancy Reward Shaping: Improving Credit Assignment for Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
summary:
The temporal lag between actions and their long-term consequences makes credit assignment a challenge when learning goal-directed behaviors from data. Generative world models capture the distribution of future states an agent may visit, indicating that they have captured temporal information. How can that temporal information be extracted to perform credit assignment? In this paper, we formalize how the temporal information stored in world models encodes the underlying geometry of the world. Leveraging optimal transport, we extract this geometry from a learned model of the occupancy measure into a reward function that captures goal-reaching information. Our resulting method, Occupancy Reward Shaping, largely mitigates the problem of credit assignment in sparse reward settings. ORS provably does not alter the optimal policy, yet empirically improves performance by 2.2x across 13 diverse long-horizon locomotion and manipulation tasks. Moreover, we demonstrate the effectiveness of ORS in the real world for controlling nuclear fusion on 3 Tokamak control tasks.
  Code: https://github.com/aravindvenu7/occupancy_reward_shaping; Website: https://aravindvenu7.github.io/website/ors/

AI.tldr: Occupancy Reward Shaping (ORS) extracts temporal geometry from learned world models via optimal transport to produce a reward shaping term. It preserves the optimal policy (potential-based shaping) while improving learning in sparse-reward, long-horizon tasks, with ~2.2x performance gains across 13 tasks and validation on Tokamak fusion control.
AI.motivation: Credit assignment is hard in long-horizon, goal-directed RL with sparse rewards. World models encode temporal distributions; extracting their geometry could yield informative reward signals tied to goal-reaching without altering optimal policies.
AI.method: Train a world model to capture future state occupancy. Use optimal transport to map this occupancy measure into a geometry that informs a reward shaping term. The resulting ORS is a potential-based shaping signal that supplements the external reward without changing the optimal policy.
AI.result: Empirical performance improvement of about 2.2x across 13 diverse long-horizon locomotion/manipulation tasks. The method is claimed to be policy-preserving and is demonstrated in real-world Tokamak fusion control on 3 tasks.
AI.conclusion: Occupancy-based temporal geometry provides a principled source of shaping signals that mitigates credit assignment issues in sparse rewards. ORS leverages OT-derived geometry from a world model to yield substantial practical gains, including real-world applicability.

title: Semantic-Fast-SAM: Efficient Semantic Segmenter
summary: We propose Semantic-Fast-SAM (SFS), a semantic segmentation framework that combines the Fast Segment Anything model with a semantic labeling pipeline to achieve real-time performance without sacrificing accuracy. FastSAM is an efficient CNN-based re-implementation of the Segment Anything Model (SAM) that runs much faster than the original transformer-based SAM. Building upon FastSAM's rapid mask generation, we integrate a Semantic-Segment-Anything (SSA) labeling strategy to assign meaningful categories to each mask. The resulting SFS model produces high-quality semantic segmentation maps at a fraction of the computational cost and memory footprint of the original SAM-based approach. Experiments on Cityscapes and ADE20K benchmarks demonstrate that SFS matches the accuracy of prior SAM-based methods (mIoU ~ 70.33 on Cityscapes and 48.01 on ADE20K) while achieving approximately 20x faster inference than SSA in the closed-set setting. We also show that SFS effectively handles open-vocabulary segmentation by leveraging CLIP-based semantic heads, outperforming recent open-vocabulary models on broad class labeling. This work enables practical real-time semantic segmentation with the "segment-anything" capability, broadening the applicability of foundation segmentation models in robotics scenarios. The implementation is available at https://github.com/KBH00/Semantic-Fast-SAM.

AI.tldr: SFS combines FastSAM with a semantic labeling pipeline to deliver real-time semantic segmentation with competitive accuracy, including open-vocabulary capabilities via CLIP heads, achieving ~20x faster inference and enabling practical robotics applications.
AI.motivation: To achieve real-time, accurate semantic segmentation with segment-anything capabilities, addressing the computational burden of SAM while preserving accuracy and enabling open-vocabulary labeling.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) use FastSAM for fast mask generation; (2) apply a Semantic-Segment-Anything (SSA) labeling strategy to assign semantic categories to each mask, augmented with CLIP-based open-vocabulary heads for broad class labeling.
AI.result: SFS attains mIoU comparable to prior SAM-based methods (Cityscapes ~70.33, ADE20K ~48.01) with ~20x faster inference than SSA in the closed-set; it also demonstrates strong open-vocabulary segmentation by leveraging CLIP heads, outperforming recent open-vocabulary models.
AI.conclusion: SFS enables practical real-time semantic segmentation with segment-anything capabilities, expanding the applicability of foundation segmentation models in robotics; code is available at the project repository.

title: Temporally Extended Mixture-of-Experts Models
summary: Mixture-of-Experts models, now popular for scaling capacity at fixed inference speed, switch experts at nearly every token. Once a model outgrows available GPU memory, this churn can render optimizations like offloading and pre-fetching ineffective. We make the case that the options framework in reinforcement learning is a perfect match to tackle this problem, and argue for temporally extended mixture-of-experts layers. Building on the option-critic framework with deliberation costs, we add a controller to each layer that learns when to switch expert sets and which to load. By applying this to gpt-oss-20b with low-rank adapters and a self-distillation reward, our method reduces switch rates from over 50% to below 5% while retaining up to 90% of base-model accuracy on MATH, MMLU, and MMMLU. This shows that even existing pre-trained models can be converted to temporally extended MoEs with lightweight training, with the deliberation cost allowing model trainers to trade off switching rates against capability. We hope this opens a principled path, grounded in the options framework, for memory-efficient serving and continual learning in ever-growing MoE models.

AI.tldr: Temporally extended mixture-of-experts (MoEs) using the options framework with deliberation costs to reduce switching in MoE layers, enabling memory-efficient serving with minimal loss in accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard MoEs switch experts at high rates per token, causing memory and optimization inefficiencies as models scale. Need a principled mechanism to control when to switch and what to load to improve memory efficiency and enable continual learning.
AI.method: Extend MoEs with an option-critic controller per layer equipped with deliberation costs. Train lightweightly on gpt-oss-20b with low-rank adapters and a self-distillation reward to learn when to switch expert sets and which to load, reducing churn.
AI.result: Switch rates drop from over 50% to under 5%. Base-model accuracy is preserved up to about 90% on MATH, MMLU, and MMMLU benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that pre-trained models can be converted to temporally extended MoEs with modest training cost; the deliberation cost provides a tunable trade-off between switching and capability, offering a principled path for memory-efficient serving and continual learning in growing MoEs.

title: HiPO: Hierarchical Preference Optimization for Adaptive Reasoning in LLMs
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables. Includes ablation study across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on 5 math reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH500, Minerva, AIME24, Gaokao2023). GPT-4.1 used for structured evaluation of reasoning quality
summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is an effective framework for aligning large language models with human preferences, but it struggles with complex reasoning tasks. DPO optimizes for the likelihood of generating preferred over dispreferred responses in their entirety and lacks the granularity to provide feedback on subsections of many-step solutions typical of reasoning tasks. Existing methods excel at either stable preference learning (e.g., DPO variants like KTO and RSO) or structured reasoning (e.g., ReMA's multi-agent RL framework, Tree of Thoughts), but fail to merge these complementary strengths. We propose HiPO (Hierarchical Preference Optimization), an extension of DPO that separates responses into reasoning segments (query clarification and context, reasoning steps, and answer) and computes loss as a weighted sum of the DPO loss for each segment. Our approach enables segment-specific training while maintaining DPO's computational efficiency and training stability. We demonstrate that for multiple 7B LLMs fine-tuned using HiPO and DPO on the Math Stack Exchange preference dataset, the models trained with HiPO outperform the others on a variety of common math benchmarks and achieve greater organization, logical flow, and consistency as measured by GPT-4.1.

AI.tldr: HiPO extends Direct Preference Optimization by decomposing responses into reasoning segments (query context, reasoning steps, and final answer) and optimizing a weighted sum of segment-level DPO losses, enabling granular feedback while preserving DPO's stability. It outperforms DPO-based baselines on math benchmarks when trained on Math Stack Exchange data, with improved organization, logical flow, and consistency as judged by GPT-4.1.
AI.motivation: DPO optimizes the entire response for preferred vs dispreferred outputs but lacks granularity for multi-step reasoning tasks; a method that can provide segment-level feedback while retaining DPO's stability would better align LLMs on complex tasks.
AI.method: Partition each response into predefined segments: (1) query clarification/context, (2) reasoning steps, (3) final answer. Compute the DPO loss for each segment independently and form a weighted sum to train the model, allowing segment-specific training while preserving end-to-end efficiency.
AI.result: Models fine-tuned with HiPO on 7B LLMs using the Math Stack Exchange preference dataset outperform baselines on multiple math benchmarks and exhibit improved organization, logical flow, and consistency (as measured by GPT-4.1).
AI.conclusion: HiPO successfully integrates stable preference learning with structured reasoning by enabling segment-wise feedback and training, achieving better reasoning performance without compromising DPO stability and efficiency.

title: AAC: Admissible-by-Architecture Differentiable Landmark Compression for ALT
comment: 50 pages, 8 figures, 24 tables, submitted to Transactions on Machine Learning Research
summary:
We introduce \textbf{AAC} (Architecturally Admissible Compressor), a differentiable landmark-selection module for ALT (A*, Landmarks, and Triangle inequality) shortest-path heuristics whose outputs are admissible by construction: each forward pass is a row-stochastic mixture of triangle-inequality lower bounds, so the heuristic is admissible for \emph{every} parameter setting without requiring convergence, calibration, or projection. At deployment, the module reduces to classical ALT on a learned subset, composing end-to-end with neural encoders while preserving the classical toolchain. The construction is the first differentiable instance of the compress-while-preserving-admissibility tradition in classical heuristic search.
  Under a matched per-vertex memory protocol, we establish that ALT with farthest-point-sampling landmarks (FPS-ALT) has provably near-optimal coverage on metric graphs, leaving at most a few percentage points of headroom for \emph{any} selector. AAC operates near this ceiling: the gap is $0.9$--$3.9$ percentage points on 9 road networks and ${\leq}1.3$ percentage points on synthetic graphs, with zero admissibility violations across $1{,}500+$ queries and all logged runs. At matched memory, AAC is also $1.2$--$1.5{\times}$ faster than FPS-ALT at the median query on DIMACS road networks, amortizing its offline cost within $170$--$1{,}924$ queries. A controlled ablation isolates the binding constraint: training-objective drift under default initialization, not architectural capacity; identity-on-first-$m$ initialization closes the expansion-count gap entirely. We release the module, a reusable matched-memory benchmarking protocol with paired two-one-sided-test (TOST) equivalence and pre-registration, and a reference compressed-differential-heuristics baseline.

AI.tldr: AAC is a differentiable, admissible landmark-selection module for ALT that guarantees admissibility by construction, achieves near-optimal landmark coverage with significant speedups, and integrates end-to-end with neural encoders while preserving the classical ALT toolchain.
AI.motivation: Improve shortest-path search by combining neural encoders with classical, admissible heuristics, while enabling differentiability and end-to-end training without sacrificing admissibility.
AI.method: Introduce AAC, a row-stochastic mixture of triangle-inequality lower bounds that yields admissible heuristics by construction. It learns a subset of landmarks under a matched-memory protocol, and at deployment reduces to classical ALT on that learned subset. The approach preserves admissibility for all parameter settings and requires no convergence or projection. Training analyses highlight the influence of initialization and training-objective drift; identity-on-first-m initialization closes the expansion-gap. Release includes a reusable benchmarking protocol with TOST equivalence tests.
AI.result: AAC achieves near-optimal coverage on metric graphs with a small gap (0.9–3.9 percentage points; ≤1.3 on synthetic graphs) under matched memory, and zero admissibility violations across 1,500+ queries. It is 1.2–1.5× faster than FPS-ALT at the median query on DIMACS road networks, amortizing offline cost within 170–1,924 queries. Ablation shows training-objective drift, not architectural capacity, as the main binding constraint. Identity-on-first-m initialization fully closes the expansion-count gap.
AI.conclusion: AAC is the first differentiable instance of compress-while-preserving-admissibility in classical heuristic search, offering end-to-end differentiability with theoretical guarantees and practical speedups while preserving the classical ALT workflow. The authors provide released tooling for benchmarking and a reference baseline.

title: WildFireVQA: A Large-Scale Radiometric Thermal VQA Benchmark for Aerial Wildfire Monitoring
summary: Wildfire monitoring requires timely, actionable situational awareness from airborne platforms, yet existing aerial visual question answering (VQA) benchmarks do not evaluate wildfire-specific multimodal reasoning grounded in thermal measurements. We introduce WildFireVQA, a large-scale VQA benchmark for aerial wildfire monitoring that integrates RGB imagery with radiometric thermal data. WildFireVQA contains 6,097 RGB-thermal samples, where each sample includes an RGB image, a color-mapped thermal visualization, and a radiometric thermal TIFF, and is paired with 34 questions, yielding a total of 207,298 multiple-choice questions spanning presence and detection, classification, distribution and segmentation, localization and direction, cross-modal reasoning, and flight planning for operational wildfire intelligence. To improve annotation reliability, we combine multimodal large language model (MLLM)-based answer generation with sensor-driven deterministic labeling, manual verification, and intra-frame and inter-frame consistency checks. We further establish a comprehensive evaluation protocol for representative MLLMs under RGB, Thermal, and retrieval-augmented settings using radiometric thermal statistics. Experiments show that across task categories, RGB remains the strongest modality for current models, while retrieved thermal context yields gains for stronger MLLMs, highlighting both the value of temperature-grounded reasoning and the limitations of existing MLLMs in safety-critical wildfire scenarios. The dataset and benchmark code are open-source at https://github.com/mobiiin/WildFire_VQA.

AI.tldr: WildFireVQA is a large-scale RGB-thermal VQA benchmark for aerial wildfire monitoring, with 6,097 samples and 207,298 questions across multiple categories, evaluated under RGB, thermal, and retrieval-augmented settings; RGB dominates, but thermal context helps strong models.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap in wildfire-focused multimodal reasoning benchmarks by integrating radiometric thermal data with RGB imagery, enabling evaluation of models on presence/detection, classification, distribution/segmentation, localization, cross-modal reasoning, and flight planning.
AI.method: Assemble 6,097 RGB-thermal samples consisting of RGB images, color-mapped thermal visualizations, and radiometric thermal TIFFs; associate each sample with 34 MC questions (total 207,298). Employ MLLM-based answer generation combined with deterministic sensor-driven labeling, manual verification, and intra-frame/inter-frame consistency checks. Propose evaluation protocols for RGB, Thermal, and retrieval-augmented settings using radiometric statistics.
AI.result: Across task categories, RGB is the strongest modality for current models; retrieved thermal context provides gains for stronger MLLMs. Demonstrates the value of temperature-grounded reasoning and highlights limitations of existing MLLMs in safety-critical wildfire scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and benchmark code are open-source, enabling future research on multimodal wildfire VQA and encouraging development of models that effectively leverage thermal data for wildfire intelligence.

title: Stateless Decision Memory for Enterprise AI Agents
