comment: 16 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables. Companion paper to "Four-Axis Decision Alignment for Long-Horizon Enterprise AI Agents" (arXiv:TBD). Code and reproducibility artifacts at https://github.com/vasundras/stateless-decision-memory-enterprise-ai-agents
summary: Enterprise deployment of long-horizon decision agents in regulated domains (underwriting, claims adjudication, tax examination) is dominated by retrieval-augmented pipelines despite a decade of increasingly sophisticated stateful memory architectures. We argue this reflects a hidden requirement: regulated deployment is load-bearing on four systems properties (deterministic replay, auditable rationale, multi-tenant isolation, statelessness for horizontal scale), and stateful architectures violate them by construction. We propose Deterministic Projection Memory (DPM): an append-only event log plus one task-conditioned projection at decision time. On ten regulated decisioning cases at three memory budgets, DPM matches summarization-based memory at generous budgets and substantially outperforms it when the budget binds: at a 20x compression ratio, DPM improves factual precision by +0.52 (Cohen's h=1.17, p=0.0014) and reasoning coherence by +0.53 (h=1.13, p=0.0034), paired permutation, n=10. DPM is additionally 7-15x faster at binding budgets, making one LLM call at decision time instead of N. A determinism study of 10 replays per case at temperature zero shows both architectures inherit residual API-level nondeterminism, but the asymmetry is structural: DPM exposes one nondeterministic call; summarization exposes N compounding calls. The audit surface follows the same one-versus-N pattern: DPM logs two LLM calls per decision while summarization logs 83-97 on LongHorizon-Bench. We conclude with TAMS, a practitioner heuristic for architecture selection, and a failure analysis of stateful memory under enterprise operating conditions. The contribution is the argument that statelessness is the load-bearing property explaining enterprise's preference for weaker but replayable retrieval pipelines, and that DPM demonstrates this property is attainable without the decisioning penalty retrieval pays.

AI.tldr: Deterministic Projection Memory (DPM) offers a stateless, append-only memory architecture for regulated, long-horizon decisioning, achieving comparable or superior performance to summarization-based memory under tight budgets, with far fewer LLM calls and auditable behavior.
AI.motivation: Enterprise deployment in regulated domains demands deterministic replay, auditable rationale, multi-tenant isolation, and statelessness for horizontal scale. Stateful memory architectures violate these requirements by construction, hindering deployment and auditability.
AI.method: Introduce DPM: an append-only event log plus a single task-conditioned projection at decision time. Evaluate on ten regulated decisioning cases across three memory budgets, compare to summarization-based memory, assess factual precision and reasoning coherence, measure decision-time LLM calls and determinism, analyze audit surface.
AI.result: DPM matches summarization memory at generous budgets and substantially outperforms it under tight budgets (e.g., 20x compression: factual precision +0.52, coherence +0.53; p<0.01). DPM is 7–15x faster at binding budgets (one LLM call vs N). Determinism study shows residual nondeterminism is structurally lower in DPM (one nondeterministic call) than in summarization (N calls). Audit surface: DPM logs two LLM calls per decision vs 83–97 for summarization.
AI.conclusion: Statelessness is the load-bearing property explaining enterprise preference for retrieval pipelines. DPM demonstrates that statelessness harmful constraints can be met without the decisioning penalty of retrieval, offering a practical, auditable, and scalable alternative.

title: Cover meets Robbins while Betting on Bounded Data: $\ln n$ Regret and Almost Sure $\ln\ln n$ Regret
summary: Consider betting against a sequence of data in $[0,1]$, where one is allowed to make any bet that is fair if the data have a conditional mean $m_0 \in (0,1)$. Cover's universal portfolio algorithm delivers a worst-case regret of $O(\ln n)$ compared to the best constant bet in hindsight, and this bound is unimprovable against adversarially generated data. In this work, we present a novel mixture betting strategy that combines insights from Robbins and Cover, and exhibits a different behavior: it eventually produces a regret of $O(\ln \ln n)$ on \emph{almost} all paths (a measure-one set of paths if each conditional mean equals $m_0$ and intrinsic variance increases to $\infty$), but has an $O(\log n)$ regret on the complement (a measure zero set of paths). Our paper appears to be the first to point out the value in hedging two very different strategies to achieve a best-of-both-worlds adaptivity to stochastic data and protection against adversarial data. We contrast our results to those in~\cite{agrawal2025regret} for a sub-Gaussian mixture on unbounded data: their worst-case regret has to be unbounded, but a similar hedging delivers both an optimal betting growth-rate and an almost sure $\ln\ln n$ regret on stochastic data. Finally, our strategy witnesses a sharp game-theoretic upper law of the iterated logarithm, analogous to~\cite{shafer2005probability}.

AI.tldr: A new mixture betting strategy blends Cover's universal portfolio with a Robbins-type scheme, yielding O(log log n) regret on almost all paths and O(log n) regret on a measure-zero adversarial set, achieving best-of-both-worlds adaptivity between stochastic data and adversarial data, and connecting to a game-theoretic upper law of the iterated logarithm.
AI.motivation: Address robust sequential betting in environments that are partly stochastic and partly adversarial; extend regret guarantees beyond Cover's O(log n) to adaptivity, and to relate betting theory to game-theoretic probability and LIL-type results.
AI.method: Construct a meta-strategy that mixtures two distinct betting algorithms (Robbins-style and Cover's universal portfolio). Analyze the pathwise regret under conditional mean m0 in (0,1) and increasing intrinsic variance. Prove O(log log n) regret on a measure-one set of paths and O(log n) regret on the measure-zero complement; compare to sub-Gaussian unbounded-data results and demonstrate an upper-law of the iterated logarithm.
AI.result: The mixture strategy achieves a best-of-both-worlds performance: almost surely (on a measure-one path set) regret grows as log log n, while on the negligible complement the regret is log n. This is presented as the first demonstration that hedging two disparate strategies yields adaptivity to both stochastic and adversarial data, and it is linked to a game-theoretic upper law of the iterated logarithm.
AI.conclusion: Hedging dual strategies yields robust, adaptive regret bounds spanning stochastic and adversarial regimes, validating a general best-of-both-worlds approach in sequential betting and connecting to LIL-type behavior in game-theoretic probability.

title: Mol-Debate: Multi-Agent Debate Improves Structural Reasoning in Molecular Design
summary: Text-guided molecular design is a key capability for AI-driven drug discovery, yet it remains challenging to map sequential natural-language instructions with non-linear molecular structures under strict chemical constraints. Most existing approaches, including RAG, CoT prompting, and fine-tuning or RL, emphasize a small set of ad-hoc reasoning perspectives implemented in a largely one-shot generation pipeline. In contrast, real-world drug discovery relies on dynamic, multi-perspective critique and iterative refinement to reconcile semantic intent with structural feasibility. Motivated by this, we propose Mol-Debate, a generation paradigm that enables such dynamic reasoning through an iterative generate-debate-refine loop. We further characterize key challenges in this paradigm and address them through perspective-oriented orchestration, including developer-debater conflict, global-local structural reasoning, and static-dynamic integration. Experiments demonstrate that Mol-Debate achieves state-of-the-art performance against strong general and chemical baselines, reaching 59.82% exact match on ChEBI-20 and 50.52% weighted success rate on S$^2$-Bench. Our code is available at https://github.com/wyuzh/Mol-Debate.

AI.tldr: Mol-Debate introduces an iterative generate-debate-refine framework for text-guided molecular design that enables dynamic multi-perspective critique to reconcile semantic intent with chemical feasibility, achieving state-of-the-art results on ChEBI-20 and S2-Bench.
AI.motivation: Current text-guided molecular design struggles to map natural-language instructions to nonlinear molecular structures under strict chemical constraints. Most methods rely on one-shot generation and limited reasoning, lacking dynamic multi-perspective critique and iterative refinement found in real-world drug discovery.
AI.method: Mol-Debate employs an iterative generate-debate-refine loop with perspective-oriented orchestration. Key components include handling developer-debater conflict, global-local structural reasoning, and static-dynamic integration to fuse semantic intent with chemical feasibility.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance against strong baselines, achieving 59.82% exact match on ChEBI-20 and 50.52% weighted success rate on S2-Bench. The authors provide code at the linked GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: Mol-Debate demonstrates the benefit of dynamic, multi-perspective critique for text-guided molecular design, improving semantic-to-structure alignment and feasibility; the work offers reproducible results and a scalable paradigm for AI-driven drug discovery.

title: Weighted Knowledge Distillation for Semi-Supervised Segmentation of Maxillary Sinus in Panoramic X-ray Images
summary: Accurate segmentation of maxillary sinus in panoramic X-ray images is essential for dental diagnosis and surgical planning; however, this task remains relatively underexplored in dental imaging research. Structural overlap, ambiguous anatomical boundaries inherent to two-dimensional panoramic projections, and the limited availability of large scale clinical datasets with reliable pixel-level annotations make the development and evaluation of segmentation models challenging. To address these challenges, we propose a semi-supervised segmentation framework that effectively leverages both labeled and unlabeled panoramic radiographs, where knowledge distillation is utilized to train a student model with reliable structural information distilled from a teacher model. Specifically, we introduce a weighted knowledge distillation loss to suppress unreliable distillation signals caused by structural discrepancies between teacher and student predictions. To further enhance the quality of pseudo labels generated by the teacher network, we introduce SinusCycle-GAN which is a refinement network based on unpaired image-to-image translation. This refinement process improves the precision of boundaries and reduces noise propagation when learning from unlabeled data during semi-supervised training. To evaluate the proposed method, we collected clinical panoramic X-ray images from 2,511 patients, and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation models, achieving the Dice score of 96.35\% while reducing boundary error. The results indicate that the proposed semi-supervised framework provides robust and anatomically consistent segmentation performance under limited labeled data conditions, highlighting its potential for broader dental image analysis applications.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised segmentation framework for maxillary sinus in panoramic X-ray images that combines teacher-student knowledge distillation with a SinusCycle-GAN refinement module to improve pseudo-label quality, trained on labeled and unlabeled data from 2,511 patients, achieving high Dice (96.35%) and reduced boundary error.
AI.motivation: Segmentation of maxillary sinus in panoramic radiographs is clinically important but challenging due to 2D projection artifacts, structural overlap, and limited pixel-level annotations; a semi-supervised approach leveraging unlabeled data can improve accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: A teacher-student semi-supervised framework with a weighted knowledge distillation loss to mitigate unreliable signals from structural differences between teacher and student predictions. Pseudo-label quality is enhanced by SinusCycle-GAN, an unpaired image-to-image refinement network that improves boundary precision and reduces noise for unlabeled data during training.
AI.result: On a clinical dataset of 2,511 patients, the method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation models, achieving a Dice score of 96.35% and reduced boundary error, demonstrating robust performance under limited labeled data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed semi-supervised framework provides anatomically consistent, robust segmentation of the maxillary sinus in panoramic X-ray images and shows promise for broader dental image analysis tasks under limited labeled data conditions.

title: Lever: Inference-Time Policy Reuse under Support Constraints
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) policies are typically trained for fixed objectives, making reuse difficult when task requirements change. We study inference-time policy reuse: given a library of pre-trained policies and a new composite objective, can a high-quality policy be constructed entirely offline, without additional environment interaction? We introduce lever (Leveraging Efficient Vector Embeddings for Reusable policies), an end-to-end framework that retrieves relevant policies, evaluates them using behavioral embeddings, and composes new policies via offline Q-value composition. We focus on the support-limited regime, where no value propagation is possible, and show that the effectiveness of reuse depends critically on the coverage of available transitions. To balance performance and computational cost, lever proposes composition strategies that control the exploration of candidate policies. Experiments in deterministic GridWorld environments show that inference-time composition can match, and in some cases exceed, training-from-scratch performance while providing substantial speedups. At the same time, performance degrades when long-horizon dependencies require value propagation, highlighting a fundamental limitation of offline reuse.

AI.tldr: An offline policy-reuse framework (Lever) that retrieves and composes pre-trained RL policies using behavioral embeddings in a support-limited regime, achieving data-free composition with competitive performance but limited by long-horizon dependencies.
AI.motivation: To enable post hoc reuse of existing policies for new composite objectives without any additional environment interaction, reducing data collection and training costs while maintaining performance.
AI.method: Lever retrieves relevant pre-trained policies, evaluates them via behavioral embeddings, and composes a new policy offline through Q-value composition constrained to the support of observed transitions (no value propagation). It includes composition strategies to control exploration of candidate policies to balance performance and computation.
AI.result: Inference-time policy composition can match or exceed training-from-scratch performance in deterministic GridWorlds and offers substantial speedups; performance degrades when tasks require long-horizon dependencies that demand value propagation.
AI.conclusion: Offline policy reuse is viable in the coverage-limited regime with careful policy composition, but its effectiveness is limited for tasks with long-horizon dependencies that require propagation of value information across time.

title: Memory-Augmented LLM-based Multi-Agent System for Automated Feature Generation on Tabular Data
comment: 16 pages (including appendix), 4 main figures, 15 tables. Accepted to ACL 2026
summary: Automated feature generation extracts informative features from raw tabular data without manual intervention and is crucial for accurate, generalizable machine learning. Traditional methods rely on predefined operator libraries and cannot leverage task semantics, limiting their ability to produce diverse, high-value features for complex tasks. Recent Large Language Model (LLM)-based approaches introduce richer semantic signals, but still suffer from a restricted feature space due to fixed generation patterns and from the absence of feedback from the learning objective. To address these challenges, we propose a Memory-Augmented LLM-based Multi-Agent System (\textbf{MALMAS}) for automated feature generation. MALMAS decomposes the generation process into agents with distinct responsibilities, and a Router Agent activates an appropriate subset of agents per iteration, further broadening exploration of the feature space. We further integrate a memory module comprising procedural memory, feedback memory, and conceptual memory, enabling iterative refinement that adaptively guides subsequent feature generation and improves feature quality and diversity. Extensive experiments on multiple public datasets against state-of-the-art baselines demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. The code is available at https://github.com/fxdong24/MALMAS

AI.tldr: A memory-augmented multi-agent LLM system (MALMAS) for automated feature generation in tabular data, using specialized agents and a router to explore a broader feature space, augmented by procedural, feedback, and conceptual memories to iteratively refine features; shows improved results over baselines on public datasets; code released.
AI.motivation: Traditional automated feature generation relies on predefined operator libraries and cannot leverage task semantics, limiting diversity and quality of features. LLM-based methods add semantic signals but remain constrained by fixed generation patterns and lack objective-based feedback. There is a need for greater exploration and guidance aligned with learning objectives.
AI.method: MALMAS decomposes feature generation into agents with distinct roles. A Router Agent selects relevant subsets of agents per iteration to broaden exploration. A memory module with procedural memory, feedback memory, and conceptual memory enables iterative refinement, guiding subsequent feature generation based on past results and the learning objective.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple public datasets show MALMAS outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in feature quality and diversity. The authors provide code at the linked repository.
AI.conclusion: Memory-augmented, multi-agent LLM systems can substantially expand the search and refinement of informative features for tabular data, yielding higher-quality, more diverse features and better generalization potential.

title: Learning Spatial-Temporal Coherent Correlations for Speech-Preserving Facial Expression Manipulation
summary: Speech-preserving facial expression manipulation (SPFEM) aims to modify facial emotions while meticulously maintaining the mouth animation associated with spoken content. Current works depend on inaccessible paired training samples for the person, where two aligned frames exhibit the same speech content yet differ in emotional expression, limiting the SPFEM applications in real-world scenarios. In this work, we discover that speakers who convey the same content with different emotions exhibit highly correlated local facial animations in both spatial and temporal spaces, providing valuable supervision for SPFEM. To capitalize on this insight, we propose a novel spatial-temporal coherent correlation learning (STCCL) algorithm, which models the aforementioned correlations as explicit metrics and integrates the metrics to supervise manipulating facial expression and meanwhile better preserving the facial animation of spoken content. To this end, it first learns a spatial coherent correlation metric, ensuring that the visual correlations of adjacent local regions within an image linked to a specific emotion closely resemble those of corresponding regions in an image linked to a different emotion. Simultaneously, it develops a temporal coherent correlation metric, ensuring that the visual correlations of specific regions across adjacent image frames associated with one emotion are similar to those in the corresponding regions of frames associated with another emotion. Recognizing that visual correlations are not uniform across all regions, we have also crafted a correlation-aware adaptive strategy that prioritizes regions that present greater challenges. During SPFEM model training, we construct the spatial-temporal coherent correlation metric between corresponding local regions of the input and output image frames as an additional loss to supervise the generation process.

AI.tldr: Proposes STCCL for speech-preserving facial expression manipulation using spatial-temporal correlation metrics as supervision to preserve mouth animation while changing emotion, addressing lack of paired data.
AI.motivation: Current SPFEM methods rely on inaccessible paired data; need supervision to preserve speech-related mouth motion while altering emotion.
AI.method: Introduce spatial-temporal coherent correlation learning (STCCL) with a spatial coherent correlation metric and a temporal coherent correlation metric, plus a correlation-aware adaptive region weighting; incorporate these metrics as loss terms in training to supervise generation.
AI.result: The approach provides explicit guidance to align local correlations across emotions in space and time, enabling better preservation of spoken content during expression manipulation; expected improvements in lip-mouth motion fidelity, though specifics are not detailed in abstract.
AI.conclusion: STCCL offers a principled, supervision-friendly framework for SPFEM that leverages cross-emotion correlation cues, potentially enabling more realistic, language-consistent expression editing without paired data.

title: Physics-Enhanced Deep Learning for Proactive Thermal Runaway Forecasting in Li-Ion Batteries
summary: Accurate prediction of thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries is essential for ensuring the safety, efficiency, and reliability of modern energy storage systems. Conventional data-driven approaches, such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, can capture complex temporal dependencies but often violate thermodynamic principles, resulting in physically inconsistent predictions. Conversely, physics-based thermal models provide interpretability but are computationally expensive and difficult to parameterize for real-time applications. To bridge this gap, this study proposes a Physics-Informed Long Short-Term Memory (PI-LSTM) framework that integrates governing heat transfer equations directly into the deep learning architecture through a physics-based regularization term in the loss function. The model leverages multi-feature input sequences, including state of charge, voltage, current, mechanical stress, and surface temperature, to forecast battery temperature evolution while enforcing thermal diffusion constraints. Extensive experiments conducted on thirteen lithium-ion battery datasets demonstrate that the proposed PI-LSTM achieves an 81.9% reduction in root mean square error (RMSE) and an 81.3% reduction in mean absolute error (MAE) compared to the standard LSTM baseline, while also outperforming CNN-LSTM and multilayer perceptron (MLP) models by wide margins. The inclusion of physical constraints enhances the model's generalization across diverse operating conditions and eliminates non-physical temperature oscillations. These results confirm that physics-informed deep learning offers a viable pathway toward interpretable, accurate, and real-time thermal management in next-generation battery systems.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed LSTM (PI-LSTM) with a heat-transfer regularization enforces thermodynamic constraints to predict battery temperature, achieving substantial error reductions over standard LSTM and other baselines for real-time thermal management.
AI.motivation: Pure data-driven LSTMs can violate thermodynamics, while physics-based models are accurate but computationally heavy and hard to parameterize for real-time use. A hybrid approach aims to combine accuracy and physical interpretability.
AI.method: Integrate governing heat transfer equations into the LSTM via a physics-based regularization term in the loss function. Use multi-feature inputs (state of charge, voltage, current, mechanical stress, surface temperature) to forecast temperature evolution while enforcing thermal diffusion constraints.
AI.result: On thirteen Li-ion battery datasets, PI-LSTM achieves an 81.9% RMSE reduction and 81.3% MAE reduction compared to standard LSTM, and outperforms CNN-LSTM and MLP. Physics constraints improve generalization across diverse operating conditions and eliminate non-physical temperature oscillations.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed deep learning is a viable path for interpretable, accurate, and real-time thermal management in next-generation battery systems.

title: ActuBench: A Multi-Agent LLM Pipeline for Generation and Evaluation of Actuarial Reasoning Tasks
summary: We present ActuBench, a multi-agent LLM pipeline for the automated generation and evaluation of advanced actuarial assessment items aligned with the International Actuarial Association (IAA) Education Syllabus. The pipeline separates four LLM roles by adapter: one agent drafts items, one constructs distractors, a third independently verifies both stages and drives bounded one-shot repair loops, and a cost-optimized auxiliary agent handles Wikipedia-note summarization and topic labelling. The items, per-model responses and complete leaderboard are published as a browsable web interface at https://actubench.de/en/, allowing readers and practitioners to inspect individual items without a repository checkout. We evaluate 50 language models from eight providers on two complementary benchmarks -- 100 empirically hardest multiple-choice items and 100 open-ended items scored by an LLM judge -- and report three headline findings. First, multi-agent verification is load-bearing: the independent verifier flags a majority of drafted items on first pass, most of which the one-shot repair loop resolves. Second, locally-hosted open-weights inference sits on the cost-performance Pareto front: a Gemma~4 model running on consumer hardware and a Cerebras-hosted 120B open-weights model dominate the near-zero-cost region, with the latter within one item of the top of the leaderboard. Third, MCQ and LLM-as-Judge rankings differ meaningfully: the MCQ scaffold inflates the performance ceiling, and Judge-mode evaluation is needed to discriminate at the frontier.

AI.tldr: ActuBench introduces a four-agent LLM pipeline for generating and evaluating actuarial items aligned with the IAA syllabus; key findings show that multi-agent verification improves item quality, open-weight models offer cost-effective performance on consumer hardware or cloud, and MCQ-based evaluation inflates performance compared with judge-based evaluation.
AI.motivation: Automate scalable generation and rigorous evaluation of actuarial assessment items, ensure alignment with the IAA Education Syllabus, provide transparent, browsable leaderboard, and assess cost-performance of open-weight models.
AI.method: A pipeline with four LLM-adapter roles: (1) item drafting, (2) distractor construction, (3) independent verifier driving bounded one-shot repair loops, and (4) a cost-optimized auxiliary agent for Wikipedia-note summarization and topic labeling; performance evaluated on 50 models from eight providers using two benchmarks: 100 hardest MCQ items and 100 open-ended items scored by an LLM judge; results published via a browsable web interface.
AI.result: The verifier flags the majority of drafted items on first pass, with most issues addressed in bounded one-shot repair loops; open-weight models Gemma-4 on consumer hardware and Cerebras 120B dominate near-zero-cost regions on the Pareto front, the Cerebras model being almost top-ranked; MCQ-based scaffolding inflates performance ceilings, while judge-based evaluation more accurately distinguishes frontier performance.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent verification is essential for high-quality item generation; open-weight models enable cost-effective inference; MCQ scaffolding overestimates performance compared to LLM-judge evaluation, highlighting the need for judge-based assessment to discern near-frontier capabilities; the associated web interface supports transparent inspection of items.

title: Bio-inspired Color Constancy: From Gray Anchoring Theory to Gray Pixel Methods
summary: Color constancy is a fundamental ability of many biological visual systems and a crucial step in computer imaging systems. Bio-inspired modeling offers a promising way to elucidate the computational principles underlying color constancy and to develop efficient computational methods. However, bio-inspired methods for color constancy remain underexplored and lack a comprehensive analysis. This paper presents a comprehensive technical framework that integrates biological mechanisms, computational theory, and algorithmic implementation for bio-inspired color constancy. Specifically, we systematically revisit the computational theory of biological color constancy, which shows that illuminant estimation can be reduced to the task of gray-anchor (pixel or surface) detection in early vision. Subsequently, typical gray-pixel detection methods, including Gray-Pixel and Grayness-Index, are reinterpreted within a unified theoretical framework with the Lambertian reflection model and biological color-opponent mechanisms. Finally, we propose a simple learning-based method that couples reflection-model constraints with feature learning to explore the potential of bio-inspired color constancy based on gray-pixel detection. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of gray-pixel detection for color constancy and demonstrate the potential of bio-inspired methods.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive bio-inspired framework for color constancy that reduces illuminant estimation to gray-anchor detection, reinterprets Gray-Pixel/Grayness-Index under Lambertian and color-opponent models, and introduces a learning-based method; experiments validate gray-pixel detection and show promise for bio-inspired approaches.
AI.motivation: Color constancy remains underexplored in bio-inspired approaches; there is a need to unify biological mechanisms, computational theory, and algorithmic implementation to understand and improve color constancy.
AI.method: The paper analyzes that illuminant estimation can be cast as gray-anchor detection in early vision. It reinterprets Gray-Pixel and Grayness-Index within a unified framework based on the Lambertian reflection model and color-opponent mechanisms. It also proposes a simple learning-based method that couples reflection-model constraints with feature learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of gray-pixel detection for color constancy and indicate the potential of bio-inspired methods.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a coherent framework combining biology, theory, and computation for color constancy; gray-pixel detection serves as a plausible core mechanism and learning-based approaches show promise for further development.

title: Structure-Aware Variational Learning of a Class of Generalized Diffusions
summary: Learning the underlying potential energy of stochastic gradient systems from partial and noisy observations is a fundamental problem arising in physics, chemistry, and data-driven modeling. Classical approaches often rely on direct regression of governing equations or velocity fields, which can be sensitive to noise and external perturbations and may fail when observations are incomplete. In this work, we propose a structure-aware, energy-based learning framework for inferring unknown potential functions in generalized diffusion processes, grounded in the energetic variational approach. Starting from the energy-dissipation law associated with the Fokker-Planck equation, we construct loss functions based on the De Giorgi dissipation functional, which consistently couple the free energy and the dissipation mechanism of the system. This formulation avoids explicit enforcement of the governing partial differential equation and preserves the underlying variational structure of the dynamics. Through numerical experiments in one, two, and three dimensions, we demonstrate that the proposed energy-based loss exhibits enhanced robustness with respect to observation time, noise level, and the diversity and amount of available training data. These results highlight the effectiveness of energy-dissipation principles as a reliable foundation for learning stochastic diffusion dynamics from data.

AI.tldr: Energy-based learning of stochastic diffusion potentials using the De Giorgi functional to infer unknown potentials from partial, noisy data, preserving variational structure.
AI.motivation: Infer underlying potential energy in stochastic gradient systems from imperfect observations; overcome sensitivity to noise and incomplete data in direct PDE regression.
AI.method: Formulate learning via the energy-dissipation law of the Fokker–Planck equation and De Giorgi dissipation functional; construct energy-based loss that couples free energy and dissipation without explicit PDE enforcement, maintaining the variational structure; tested in 1D–3D.
AI.result: The energy-based loss improves robustness to observation time, noise, and data diversity/quantity; preserves variational structure and enhances stability under incomplete data.
AI.conclusion: Energy-dissipation principles provide a reliable, structure-preserving foundation for learning stochastic diffusion dynamics from data, enabling robust inference of unknown potentials.

title: FSFM: A Biologically-Inspired Framework for Selective Forgetting of Agent Memory
summary: For LLM agents, memory management critically impacts efficiency, quality, and security. While much research focuses on retention, selective forgetting--inspired by human cognitive processes (hippocampal indexing/consolidation theory and Ebbinghaus forgetting curve)--remains underexplored. We argue that in resource-constrained environments, a well-designed forgetting mechanism is as crucial as remembering, delivering benefits across three dimensions: (1) efficiency via intelligent memory pruning, (2) quality by dynamically updating outdated preferences and context, and (3) security through active forgetting of malicious inputs, sensitive data, and privacy-compromising content. Our framework establishes a taxonomy of forgetting mechanisms: passive decay-based, active deletion-based, safety-triggered, and adaptive reinforcement-based. Building on advances in LLM agent architectures and vector databases, we present detailed specifications, implementation strategies, and empirical validation from controlled experiments. Results show significant improvements: access efficiency (+8.49%), content quality (+29.2% signal-to-noise ratio), and security performance (100% elimination of security risks). Our work bridges cognitive neuroscience and AI systems, offering practical solutions for real-world deployment while addressing ethical and regulatory compliance. The paper concludes with challenges and future directions, establishing selective forgetting as a fundamental capability for next-generation LLM agents operating in real-world, resource-constrained scenarios. Our contributions align with AI-native memory systems and responsible AI development.

AI.tldr: Selective forgetting for LLM agents as a memory-management solution, with a taxonomy and empirical gains in efficiency, quality, and security.
AI.motivation: Resource-constrained environments demand not only retention but strategic forgetting to reduce memory overhead, refresh context, and mitigate risks.
AI.method: Develops a forgetting taxonomy (passive decay, active deletion, safety-triggered, adaptive reinforcement); aligns with LLM architectures and vector databases; provides specifications, implementation guidance, and controlled experiments.
AI.result: Empirical gains: access efficiency +8.49%, content signal-to-noise +29.2%, security risks eliminated (100%).
AI.conclusion: Frames selective forgetting as a core capability bridging neuroscience and AI, with implications for real-world deployment, ethics, and regulatory compliance.

title: Rethinking Where to Edit: Task-Aware Localization for Instruction-Based Image Editing
summary: Instruction-based image editing (IIE) aims to modify images according to textual instructions while preserving irrelevant content. Despite recent advances in diffusion transformers, existing methods often suffer from over-editing, introducing unintended changes to regions unrelated to the desired edit. We identify that this limitation arises from the lack of an explicit mechanism for edit localization. In particular, different editing operations (e.g., addition, removal and replacement) induce distinct spatial patterns, yet current IIE models typically treat localization in a task-agnostic manner. To address this limitation, we propose a training-free, task-aware edit localization framework that exploits the intrinsic source and target image streams within IIE models. For each image stream, We first obtain attention-based edit cues, and then construct feature centroids based on these attentive cues to partition tokens into edit and non-edit regions. Based on the observation that optimal localization is inherently task-dependent, we further introduce a unified mask construction strategy that selectively leverages source and target image streams for different editing tasks. We provide a systematic analysis for our proposed insights and approaches. Extensive experiments on EdiVal-Bench demonstrate our framework consistently improves non-edit region consistency while maintaining strong instruction-following performance on top of powerful recent image editing backbones, including Step1X-Edit and Qwen-Image-Edit.

AI.tldr: A training-free, task-aware edit localization framework for instruction-based image editing (IIE) that uses attention cues from both source and target image streams to partition tokens into edit vs non-edit regions, with a unified mask construction strategy tailored to the edit task; it improves non-edit region consistency while preserving instruction-following performance on backbones like Step1X-Edit and Qwen-Image-Edit on EdiVal-Bench.
AI.motivation: IIE methods suffer from over-editing due to lack of explicit localization. Different editing operations (add, remove, replace) create distinct spatial patterns, so a task-agnostic localization is insufficient; explicit, task-aware localization can better constrain edits to relevant regions.
AI.method: Training-free framework. For each image stream, derive attention-based edit cues; construct feature centroids from these cues to partition tokens into edit vs non-edit regions; propose a unified mask construction strategy that selectively uses source or target streams depending on the editing task; provide systematic analysis of localization behavior and impact on editing performance.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on EdiVal-Bench shows consistent improvement in non-edit region consistency without sacrificing instruction-following performance on strong backbones such as Step1X-Edit and Qwen-Image-Edit.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, task-aware localization framework for IIE that enhances edit localization accuracy and content preservation without additional training, compatible with state-of-the-art editing backbones and applicable across addition, removal, and replacement edits.

title: ACT: Anti-Crosstalk Learning for Cross-Sectional Stock Ranking via Temporal Disentanglement and Structural Purification
summary: Cross-sectional stock ranking is a fundamental task in quantitative investment, relying on both temporal modeling of individual stocks and the capture of inter-stock dependencies. While existing deep learning models leverage graph-based approaches to enhance ranking accuracy by propagating information over relational graphs, they suffer from a key challenge: crosstalk, namely unintended information interference across predictive factors. We identify two forms of crosstalk: temporal-scale crosstalk, where trends, fluctuations, and shocks are entangled in a shared representation and non-transferable local patterns contaminate cross-stock learning; and structural crosstalk, where heterogeneous relations are indiscriminately fused and relation-specific predictive signals are obscured. To address both issues, we propose the Anti-CrossTalk (ACT) framework for cross-sectional stock ranking via temporal disentanglement and structural purification. Specifically, ACT first decomposes each stock sequence into trend, fluctuation, and shock components, then extracts component-specific information through dedicated branches, which effectively decouples non-transferable local patterns. ACT further introduces a Progressive Structural Purification Encoder to sequentially purify structural crosstalk on the trend component after mitigating temporal-scale crosstalk. An adaptive fusion module finally integrates all branch representations for ranking. Experiments on CSI300 and CSI500 demonstrate that ACT achieves state-of-the-art ranking accuracy and superior portfolio performance, with improvements of up to 74.25% on the CSI300 dataset.

AI.tldr: ACT mitigates crosstalk in cross-sectional stock ranking by temporal disentanglement and progressive structural purification, yielding state-of-the-art ranking accuracy and portfolio performance on CSI300/CSI500 (up to 74.25% improvement on CSI300).
AI.motivation: Cross-sectional graph-based ranking suffers two forms of crosstalk: temporal-scale crosstalk (shared trend/fluctuation/shock representations contaminate local patterns) and structural crosstalk (heterogeneous relations are indiscriminately fused). A disentangled, structure-aware approach is needed to preserve signal fidelity.
AI.method: Decompose each stock time series into trend, fluctuation, and shock components; assign component-specific branches to extract clean, non-transferable information; apply a Progressive Structural Purification Encoder to sequentially remove structural crosstalk on the trend component after temporal disentanglement; use an adaptive fusion module to integrate branch representations for ranking.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CSI300 and CSI500 shows superior cross-sectional ranking accuracy and portfolio performance, with reported improvements up to 74.25% on CSI300.
AI.conclusion: Temporal disentanglement and structural purification effectively mitigate crosstalk in cross-sectional stock ranking, enabling more accurate predictions and better investment outcomes.

title: Self-Awareness before Action: Mitigating Logical Inertia via Proactive Cognitive Awareness
summary: Large language models perform well on many reasoning tasks, yet they often lack awareness of whether their current knowledge or reasoning state is complete. In non-interactive puzzle settings, the narrative is fixed and the underlying structure is hidden; once a model forms an early hypothesis under incomplete premises, it can propagate that error throughout the reasoning process, leading to unstable conclusions. To address this issue, we propose SABA, a reasoning framework that explicitly introduces self-awareness of missing premises before making the final decision. SABA formulates reasoning as a recursive process that alternates between structured state construction and obstacle resolution: it first applies Information Fusion to consolidate the narrative into a verifiable base state, and then uses Query-driven Structured Reasoning to identify and resolve missing or underspecified premises by turning them into queries and progressively completing the reasoning state through hypothesis construction and state refinement. Across multiple evaluation metrics, SABA achieves the best performance on all three difficulty splits of the non-interactive Detective Puzzle benchmark, and it also maintains leading results on multiple public benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SABA introduces self-aware reasoning by identifying missing premises; it uses recursive state construction with Information Fusion and Query-driven Reasoning to turn gaps into questions and refine the model's state; it achieves state-of-the-art on Detective Puzzle and public benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with awareness of missing or underspecified premises, causing early hypothesis formation to propagate errors in non-interactive puzzles where the narrative and structure are hidden; a mechanism to detect and resolve gaps before final decision is needed.
AI.method: Iterative process alternating between Information Fusion (consolidating narrative into a verifiable base state) and Query-driven Structured Reasoning (converting missing premises into queries, constructing hypotheses, and refining state) to progressively complete reasoning.
AI.result: SABA achieves best performance on all three difficulty splits of the non-interactive Detective Puzzle benchmark and maintains leading results on multiple public benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling missing premises via self-aware, query-guided, iterative reasoning improves robustness and accuracy of LLMs on structured puzzles and standard benchmarks.

title: Opportunistic Bone-Loss Screening from Routine Knee Radiographs Using a Multi-Task Deep Learning Framework with Sensitivity-Constrained Threshold Optimization
summary:
Background: Osteoporosis and osteopenia are often undiagnosed until fragility fractures occur. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) is the reference standard for bone mineral density (BMD) assessment, but access remains limited. Knee radiographs are obtained at high volume for osteoarthritis evaluation and may offer an opportunity for opportunistic bone-loss screening.
  Objective: To develop and evaluate a multi-task deep learning system for opportunistic bone-loss screening from routine knee radiographs without additional imaging or patient visits.
  Methods: We developed STR-Net, a multi-task framework for single-channel grayscale knee radiographs. The model includes a shared backbone, global average pooling feature aggregation, a shared neck, and a task-aware representation routing module connected to three task-specific heads: binary screening (Normal vs. Bone Loss), severity sub-classification (Osteopenia vs. Osteoporosis), and weakly coupled T-score regression with optional clinical variables. A sensitivity-constrained threshold optimization strategy (minimum sensitivity >= 0.86) was applied. The dataset included 1,570 knee radiographs, split at the patient level into training (n=1,120), validation (n=226), and test (n=224) sets.
  Results: On the held-out test set, STR-Net achieved an AUROC of 0.933, sensitivity of 0.904, specificity of 0.773, and AUPRC of 0.956 for binary screening. Severity sub-classification achieved an AUROC of 0.898. The T-score regression branch showed a Pearson correlation of 0.801 with DXA-measured T-scores in a pilot subset (n=31), with MAE of 0.279 and RMSE of 0.347.
  Conclusions: STR-Net enables single-pass bone-loss screening, severity stratification, and quantitative T-score estimation from routine knee radiographs. Prospective clinical validation is needed before deployment.

AI.tldr: STR-Net is a multi-task deep learning framework that uses routine knee radiographs to opportunistically screen for bone loss, classify severity (osteopenia/osteoporosis), and estimate T-scores, achieving high binary AUROC and strong T-score correlation, though requiring prospective validation.
AI.motivation: DXA-based BMD assessment is the reference but limited in access; knee radiographs are common in OA evaluation. Opportunistic screening could enable earlier identification of osteoporosis without extra imaging or visits.
AI.method: STR-Net uses a single-channel knee radiograph input with a shared backbone, global pooling, a shared neck, and task-aware routing to three heads: (1) binary normal vs. bone loss, (2) severity subclassification (osteopenia vs. osteoporosis), and (3) T-score regression with optional clinical variables. It includes a sensitivity-constrained threshold optimization (min sensitivity ≥ 0.86). Dataset: 1,570 knee radiographs (train 1,120; val 226; test 224).
AI.result: Binary screening AUROC 0.933; sensitivity 0.904; specificity 0.773; AUPRC 0.956. Severity AUROC 0.898. T-score regression Pearson r = 0.801; MAE = 0.279; RMSE = 0.347 (pilot n=31).
AI.conclusion: STR-Net enables a single-pass approach for bone-loss screening, severity stratification, and T-score estimation from routine knee radiographs, with strong performance on held-out data, but requires prospective clinical validation.

title: Scaling Self-Play with Self-Guidance
summary: LLM self-play algorithms are notable in that, in principle, nothing bounds their learning: a Conjecturer model creates problems for a Solver, and both improve together. However, in practice, existing LLM self-play methods do not scale well with large amounts of compute, instead hitting learning plateaus. We argue this is because over long training runs, the Conjecturer learns to hack its reward, collapsing to artificially complex problems that do not help the Solver improve. To overcome this, we introduce Self-Guided Self-Play (SGS), a self-play algorithm in which the language model itself guides the Conjecturer away from degeneracy. In SGS, the model takes on three roles: Solver, Conjecturer, and a Guide that scores synthetic problems by their relevance to unsolved target problems and how clean and natural they are, providing supervision against Conjecturer collapse. Our core hypothesis is that language models can assess whether a subproblem is useful for achieving a goal. We evaluate the scaling properties of SGS by running training for significantly longer than prior works and by fitting scaling laws to cumulative solve rate curves. Applying SGS to formal theorem proving in Lean4, we find that it surpasses the asymptotic solve rate of our strongest RL baseline in fewer than 80 rounds of self-play and enables a 7B parameter model, after 200 rounds of self-play, to solve more problems than a 671B parameter model pass@4.

AI.tldr: SGS adds a Guide role to LLM self-play (Solver, Conjecturer, Guide) to prevent degeneracy from reward hacking, enabling more efficient scaling. In Lean4 theorem proving, a 7B model after 200 rounds matches or exceeds much larger models in key metrics, surpassing an RL baseline in <80 rounds.
AI.motivation: Over long self-play, the Conjecturer tends to generate overly complex subproblems to maximize rewards, causing learning plateaus. SGS aims to keep subproblems relevant and solvable by having the model judge usefulness of subproblems.
AI.method: Extend self-play to three roles (Solver, Conjecturer, Guide). The Guide scores synthetic subproblems by relevance to unsolved goals and quality (clean/natural) to supervise the Conjecturer and avoid degeneracy. Evaluate scaling by long training runs and fit scaling laws to cumulative solve-rate curves. Apply to formal theorem proving in Lean4.
AI.result: SGS surpasses the asymptotic solve rate of the strongest RL baseline in fewer than 80 rounds of self-play. A 7B-parameter model, after 200 rounds, solves more problems than a 671B parameter model on pass@4 in Lean4.
AI.conclusion: Language models can self-assess subproblem usefulness, enabling effective guided self-play that improves scaling and efficiency. SGS demonstrates that adding a supervisory Guide can prevent degeneration and yield strong performance with far smaller models.

title: MedSkillAudit: A Domain-Specific Audit Framework for Medical Research Agent Skills
summary: Background: Agent skills are increasingly deployed as modular, reusable capability units in AI agent systems. Medical research agent skills require safeguards beyond general-purpose evaluation, including scientific integrity, methodological validity, reproducibility, and boundary safety. This study developed and preliminarily evaluated a domain-specific audit framework for medical research agent skills, with a focus on reliability against expert review. Methods: We developed MedSkillAudit (skill-auditor@1.0), a layered framework assessing skill release readiness before deployment. We evaluated 75 skills across five medical research categories (15 per category). Two experts independently assigned a quality score (0-100), an ordinal release disposition (Production Ready / Limited Release / Beta Only / Reject), and a high-risk failure flag. System-expert agreement was quantified using ICC(2,1) and linearly weighted Cohen's kappa, benchmarked against the human inter-rater baseline. Results: The mean consensus quality score was 72.4 (SD = 13.0); 57.3% of skills fell below the Limited Release threshold. MedSkillAudit achieved ICC(2,1) = 0.449 (95% CI: 0.250-0.610), exceeding the human inter-rater ICC of 0.300. System-consensus score divergence (SD = 9.5) was smaller than inter-expert divergence (SD = 12.4), with no directional bias (Wilcoxon p = 0.613). Protocol Design showed the strongest category-level agreement (ICC = 0.551); Academic Writing showed a negative ICC (-0.567), reflecting a structural rubric-expert mismatch. Conclusions: Domain-specific pre-deployment audit may provide a practical foundation for governing medical research agent skills, complementing general-purpose quality checks with structured audit workflows tailored to scientific use cases.

AI.tldr: MedSkillAudit: a domain-specific audit framework for medical-research agent skills; preliminary evaluation shows modest agreement with experts and potential to complement general quality checks, with category-level agreement stronger than inter-expert reliability and a notable mismatch in Academic Writing rubric.
AI.motivation: Medical research agents require safeguards beyond general QA to ensure scientific integrity, methodological validity, reproducibility, and boundary safety; a targeted pre-deployment audit framework aims to improve reliability before deployment.
AI.method: Developed MedSkillAudit v1.0; evaluated 75 skills across five medical categories (15/Category). Two experts provided: quality score (0-100), release disposition (Production Ready / Limited Release / Beta Only / Reject), and high-risk flag. Assessed agreement using ICC(2,1) and linearly weighted Cohen's kappa, benchmarked to human inter-rater baseline.
AI.result: Mean consensus quality 72.4 (SD 13.0). 57.3% of skills below Limited Release. ICC(2,1)=0.449 (95% CI 0.250-0.610), higher than human inter-rater ICC 0.300. System-consensus score divergence SD 9.5 vs inter-expert 12.4; Wilcoxon p=0.613 (no bias). Protocol Design ICC=0.551; Academic Writing ICC=-0.567 (rubric-expert mismatch).
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific pre-deployment audit can provide a practical governance foundation for medical research agent skills, complementing general quality checks with workflow tailored to scientific use cases.

title: Fourier Series Coder: A Novel Perspective on Angle Boundary Discontinuity Problem for Oriented Object Detection
summary: With the rapid advancement of intelligent driving and remote sensing, oriented object detection has gained widespread attention. However, achieving high-precision performance is fundamentally constrained by the Angle Boundary Discontinuity (ABD) and Cyclic Ambiguity (CA) problems, which typically cause significant angle fluctuations near periodic boundaries. Although recent studies propose continuous angle coders to alleviate these issues, our theoretical and empirical analyses reveal that state-of-the-art methods still suffer from substantial cyclic errors. We attribute this instability to the structural noise amplification within their non-orthogonal decoding mechanisms. This mathematical vulnerability significantly exacerbates angular deviations, particularly for square-like objects. To resolve this fundamentally, we propose the Fourier Series Coder (FSC), a lightweight plug-and-play component that establishes a continuous, reversible, and mathematically robust angle encoding-decoding paradigm. By rigorously mapping angles onto a minimal orthogonal Fourier basis and explicitly enforcing a geometric manifold constraint, FSC effectively prevents feature modulus collapse. This structurally stabilized representation ensures highly robust phase unwrapping, intrinsically eliminating the need for heuristic truncations while achieving strict boundary continuity and superior noise immunity. Extensive experiments across three large-scale datasets demonstrate that FSC achieves highly competitive overall performance, yielding substantial improvements in high-precision detection. The code will be available at https://github.com/weiminghong/FSC.

AI.tldr: A novel FSC module encodes angles via a minimal orthogonal Fourier basis with a geometric manifold constraint, yielding continuous, reversible angle representations that resist boundary discontinuities and noise amplification, improving high-precision oriented object detection while remaining lightweight and plug-and-play.
AI.motivation: ABD (Angle Boundary Discontinuity) and CA (Cyclic Ambiguity) cause unstable angle predictions near periodic boundaries in oriented object detection. Existing continuous-angle coders reduce but do not eliminate cyclic errors due to non-orthogonal decoders and structural noise amplification.
AI.method: Map angles onto a minimal orthogonal Fourier basis to obtain a stable, reversible representation. Enforce a geometric manifold constraint to prevent feature modulus collapse and enable robust phase unwrapping without heuristic truncations, yielding a continuous boundary and improved noise immunity. FSC is designed as a lightweight plug-in for existing detectors.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three large-scale datasets shows competitive overall performance with substantial gains in high-precision detection. The authors provide code (GitHub) for reproducibility and integration as a plug-in.
AI.conclusion: FSC delivers a structurally stabilized angle encoding–decoding framework that eliminates the need for heuristic angle truncation, improves boundary continuity and noise robustness, and enhances high-precision detection in oriented object detection tasks.

title: Geometric Layer-wise Approximation Rates for Deep Networks
summary: Depth is widely viewed as a central contributor to the success of deep neural networks, whereas standard neural network approximation theory typically provides guarantees only for the final output and leaves the role of intermediate layers largely unclear. We address this gap by developing a quantitative framework in which depth admits a precise scale-dependent interpretation. Specifically, we design a single shared mixed-activation architecture of fixed width $2dN+d+2$ and any prescribed finite depth such that each intermediate readout $Φ_\ell$ is itself an approximant to the target function $f$. For $f\in L^p([0,1]^d)$ with $p\in [1,\infty)$, the approximation error of $Φ_\ell$ is controlled by $(2d+1)$ times the $L^p$ modulus of continuity at the geometric scale $N^{-\ell}$ for all $\ell$. The estimate reduces to the geometric rate $(2d+1)N^{-\ell}$ if $f$ is $1$-Lipschitz. Our network design is inspired by multigrade deep learning, where depth serves as a progressive refinement mechanism: each new correction targets residual information at a finer scale while the earlier correction terms remain part of the later readouts, yielding a nested architecture that supports adaptive refinement without redesigning the preceding network.

AI.tldr: A unified, fixed-width deep architecture with depth acting as a progressive, scale-refinement mechanism. Each intermediate readout approximates the target function with a scale-dependent error tied to the modulus of continuity at scale N^-ℓ.
AI.motivation: To clarify the role of depth in neural network approximation by providing a quantitative, scale-aware framework where intermediate layers contribute meaningfully to approximation, not just the final output.
AI.method: Propose a single shared mixed-activation network of width 2dN + d + 2 and finite depth; construct readouts Φ_ℓ at each depth ℓ that approximate f. Error per Φ_ℓ is bounded by (2d+1) times the L^p modulus of continuity at scale N^-ℓ. For 1-Lipschitz f this yields a geometric rate (2d+1)N^-ℓ. Architecture mirrors multigrade deep learning with residual-like corrections at progressively finer scales.
AI.result: Establishes a scale-dependent, depth-graded approximation framework where each intermediate readout serves as an independent approximant to f, with explicit error bounds in terms of the modulus of continuity and a fixed-width, depth-flexible network. Demonstrates a nested design where earlier corrections remain in later readouts, enabling adaptive refinement.
AI.conclusion: Depth can be interpreted as a quantitative, scale-based refinement mechanism within a fixed-width network; the proposed architecture achieves controlled, scale-aware approximation with nested corrections, aligning depth with progressively finer-scale residuals.

title: Measuring the Machine: Evaluating Generative AI as Pluralist Sociotechical Systems
comment: PhD Thesis - Author formatted. Original available on the University of Sydney library website
summary:
In measurement theory, instruments do not simply record reality; they help constitute what is observed. The same holds for generative AI evaluation: benchmarks do not just measure, they shape what models appear to be. Functionalist benchmarks treat models as isolated predictors, while prescriptive approaches assess what systems ought to be. Both obscure the sociotechnical processes through which meaning and values are enacted, risking the reification of narrow cultural perspectives in pluralist contexts.
  This thesis advances a descriptive alternative. It argues that generative AI must be evaluated as a pluralist sociotechnical system and develops Machine-Society-Human (MaSH) Loops, a framework for tracing how models, users, and institutions recursively co-construct meaning and values. Evaluation shifts from judging outputs to examining how values are enacted in interaction.
  Three contributions follow. Conceptually, MaSH Loops reframes evaluation as recursive, enactive process. Methodologically, the World Values Benchmark introduces a distributional approach grounded in World Values Survey data, structured prompt sets, and anchor-aware scoring. Empirically, the thesis demonstrates these through two cases: value drift in early GPT-3 and sociotechnical evaluation in real estate. A final chapter draws on participatory realism to argue that prompting and evaluation are constitutive interventions, not neutral observations.
  The thesis argues that static benchmarks are insufficient for generative AI. Responsible evaluation requires pluralist, process-oriented frameworks that make visible whose values are enacted. Evaluation is therefore a site of governance, shaping how AI systems are understood, deployed, and trusted.

AI.tldr: Proposes MaSH Loops as a descriptive, enactive framework for evaluating generative AI as a pluralist sociotechnical system; introduces the World Values Benchmark and demonstrates via GPT-3 value drift and a real estate case; argues that prompting/evaluation are constitutive governance interventions, not neutral measurements.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of functionalist and prescriptive benchmarks by revealing how values and meanings are enacted through interactions among models, users, and institutions; advance governance-centered, pluralist AI evaluation.
AI.method: Develop MaSH Loops as a recursive, enactive framework; the World Values Benchmark uses a distributional approach grounded in World Values Survey data, engineered prompt sets, and anchor-aware scoring; empirically illustrated with two cases (value drift in early GPT-3 and sociotechnical evaluation in real estate); draws on participatory realism to frame evaluation as constitutive intervention.
AI.result: Conceptual shift to recursive, enactive evaluation; practical World Values Benchmark methodology; empirical demonstrations in two domains showing value drift and sociotechnical assessment; framing prompting and evaluation as constitutive interventions rather than neutral observations.
AI.conclusion: Static benchmarks are insufficient for generative AI; responsible evaluation requires pluralist, process-oriented frameworks that reveal whose values are enacted; evaluation becomes a governance site that shapes understanding, deployment, and trust in AI systems.

title: MambaLiteUNet: Cross-Gated Adaptive Feature Fusion for Robust Skin Lesion Segmentation
summary: Recent segmentation models have demonstrated promising efficiency by aggressively reducing parameter counts and computational complexity. However, these models often struggle to accurately delineate fine lesion boundaries and texture patterns essential for early skin cancer diagnosis and treatment planning. In this paper, we propose MambaLiteUNet, a compact yet robust segmentation framework that integrates Mamba state space modeling into a U-Net architecture, along with three key modules: Adaptive Multi-Branch Mamba Feature Fusion (AMF), Local-Global Feature Mixing (LGFM), and Cross-Gated Attention (CGA). These modules are designed to enhance local-global feature interaction, preserve spatial details, and improve the quality of skip connections. MambaLiteUNet achieves an average IoU of 87.12% and average Dice score of 93.09% across ISIC2017, ISIC2018, HAM10000, and PH2 benchmarks, outperforming state-of-the-art models. Compared to U-Net, our model improves average IoU and Dice by 7.72 and 4.61 points, respectively, while reducing parameters by 93.6% and GFLOPs by 97.6%. Additionally, in domain generalization with six unseen lesion categories, MambaLiteUNet achieves 77.61% IoU and 87.23% Dice, performing best among all evaluated models. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that MambaLiteUNet achieves a strong balance between accuracy and efficiency, making it a competitive and practical solution for dermatological image segmentation. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/maklachur/MambaLiteUNet.

AI.tldr: Proposes MambaLiteUNet: a compact segmentation model using Mamba state-space blocks within a U‑Net, with AMF, LGFM, and CGA to boost local–global feature interaction and skip connections; shows strong accuracy with vastly fewer parameters and GFLOPs across ISIC skin-lesion benchmarks and good generalization to unseen categories.
AI.motivation: Need accurate delineation of fine lesion boundaries while reducing model size and computation for practical clinical deployment; standard U‑Nets struggle with boundary detail and texture in dermoscopy images.
AI.method: Integrates Mamba state-space modeling into a U‑Net framework and introduces three modules: Adaptive Multi-Branch Mamba Feature Fusion (AMF) to fuse multi-scale features, Local-Global Feature Mixing (LGFM) to balance local detail and global context, and Cross-Gated Attention (CGA) to improve the quality of skip connections. Evaluated on ISIC2017, ISIC2018, HAM10000, and PH2 with domain-generalization tests on six unseen lesion categories.
AI.result: Average IoU 87.12% and Dice 93.09% across the four datasets; improvements over U‑Net by 7.72 IoU points and 4.61 Dice points; parameters reduced by 93.6% and GFLOPs by 97.6%; domain-generalization IoU 77.61% and Dice 87.23% on six unseen categories, performing best among evaluated models; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: MambaLiteUNet offers a strong accuracy–efficiency balance for dermoscopic segmentation, achieving competitive or state-of-the-art performance with dramatically fewer parameters and operations, and showing promising generalization to unseen lesion categories.

title: Machine Learning for Two-Stage Graph Sparsification for the Travelling Salesman Problem
summary: High-performance TSP solvers like LKH search within a sparsified candidate graph rather than over all possible edges. Graph sparsification is non-trivial: keep too many edges and the solver wastes time; cut too many and it loses edges that belong to the optimal tour. The two leading heuristic methods, $α$-Nearest and POPMUSIC, produce high-quality candidate graphs, but no single heuristic is both sparse and reliable across all instance sizes and distributions. Machine learning methods can potentially learn better sparsification models. However, existing approaches operate on the complete graph, which is expensive and mostly restricted to Euclidean distances. To address this issue, we propose a two-stage graph sparsification approach: Stage~1 takes the union of $α$-Nearest and POPMUSIC to maximise recall; Stage~2 trains a single model to reduce density. We conducted experiments across four TSPLIB distance types, five spatial distributions, and problem sizes from 50 to 500. The two-stage approach substantially reduces candidate-graph density while retaining high coverage, generalises across distance types and distributions, outperforms recent neural sparsification methods that are restricted to Euclidean distances, and becomes increasingly valuable at larger scales where single-stage heuristics degrade.

AI.tldr: A two-stage graph sparsification for TSP: first fuse α-Nearest and POPMUSIC to maximize recall, then train a model to prune density on the union graph; this yields sparse, high-coverage candidate graphs that generalize across distance types, distributions, and scale better than Euclidean-restricted neural sparsification.
AI.motivation: High-performance TSP solvers rely on sparse candidate graphs; existing heuristics are not universally sparse or reliable, and learning-based sparsification on full graphs is costly and often limited to Euclidean distances.
AI.method: Stage 1: take the union of α-Nearest and POPMUSIC to maximize recall. Stage 2: train a single model to reduce graph density on this union. Experiments span four TSPLIB distance types, five spatial distributions, and sizes 50–500.
AI.result: The two-stage approach substantially reduces candidate-graph density while retaining high coverage, generalizes across distance types and distributions, outperforms recent neural sparsification methods restricted to Euclidean distances, and becomes more advantageous at larger scales where single-stage heuristics degrade.
AI.conclusion: A robust, scalable sparsification framework for TSP candidate graphs that leverages a recall-maximizing heuristic union followed by learning-based density reduction, with strong cross-type generalization and improved performance at larger problem sizes.

title: Self-Guided Plan Extraction for Instruction-Following Tasks with Goal-Conditional Reinforcement Learning
summary: We introduce SuperIgor, a framework for instruction-following tasks. Unlike prior methods that rely on predefined subtasks, SuperIgor enables a language model to generate and refine high-level plans through a self-learning mechanism, reducing the need for manual dataset annotation. Our approach involves iterative co-training: an RL agent is trained to follow the generated plans, while the language model adapts and modifies these plans based on RL feedback and preferences. This creates a feedback loop where both the agent and the planner improve jointly. We validate our framework in environments with rich dynamics and stochasticity. Results show that SuperIgor agents adhere to instructions more strictly than baseline methods, while also demonstrating strong generalization to previously unseen instructions.

AI.tldr: A self-learning framework (SuperIgor) couples language-model-generated plans with RL feedback to enable instruction-following without heavy manual annotation, achieving better adherence and generalization.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on predefined subtasks and annotated datasets; enable scalable, instruction-following in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Iterative co-training: LM generates and refines high-level plans; RL agent learns to execute them; LM updates plans based on RL feedback and preferences; mutual improvement loop; evaluation in stochastic, dynamic environments.
AI.result: SuperIgor achieves stricter instruction adherence than baselines and generalizes to unseen instructions; demonstrates robust performance in rich dynamic settings.
AI.conclusion: A self-learning planning-loop can align LM-generated plans with agent behavior, reducing annotation needs and improving generalization; applicable to complex, real-world instruction-following tasks.

title: X-Cache: Cross-Chunk Block Caching for Few-Step Autoregressive World Models Inference
summary: Real-time world simulation is becoming a key infrastructure for scalable evaluation and online reinforcement learning of autonomous driving systems. Recent driving world models built on autoregressive video diffusion achieve high-fidelity, controllable multi-camera generation, but their inference cost remains a bottleneck for interactive deployment. However, existing diffusion caching methods are designed for offline video generation with multiple denoising steps, and do not transfer to this scenario. Few-step distilled models have no inter-step redundancy left for these methods to reuse, and sequence-level parallelization techniques require future conditioning that closed-loop interactive generation does not provide. We present X-Cache, a training-free acceleration method that caches along a different axis: across consecutive generation chunks rather than across denoising steps. X-Cache maintains per-block residual caches that persist across chunks, and applies a dual-metric gating mechanism over a structure- and action-aware block-input fingerprint to independently decide whether each block should recompute or reuse its cached residual. To prevent approximation errors from permanently contaminating the autoregressive KV cache, X-Cache identifies KV update chunks (the forward passes that write clean keys and values into the persistent cache) and unconditionally forces full computation on these chunks, cutting off error propagation. We implement X-Cache on X-world, a production multi-camera action-conditioned driving world model built on multi-block causal DiT with few-step denoising and rolling KV cache. X-Cache achieves 71% block skip rate with 2.6x wall-clock speedup while maintaining minimum degradation.

AI.tldr: Training-free X-Cache accelerates autoregressive diffusion-based driving world models by caching across generation chunks, using per-block residual caches and a dual-metric gate to decide recomputation vs reuse; achieves 2.6x speedup with 71% block skip and minimal degradation.
AI.motivation: Real-time, interactive multi-camera driving world simulation is essential for scalable evaluation and online RL. Diffusion-based world models are accurate but inference-costly; existing offline caching or few-step distillation methods do not translate to interactive, closed-loop generation.
AI.method: X-Cache caches across consecutive generation chunks instead of denoising steps. It maintains per-block residual caches that persist across chunks and uses a dual-metric gating mechanism based on a structure- and action-aware block-input fingerprint to decide whether to recompute or reuse cached residuals. It marks KV-update chunks (forward passes writing clean keys/values) and forces full computation on them to prevent error propagation. Implemented on X-world with multi-block causal DiT, few-step denoising, and rolling KV cache.
AI.result: On X-world, X-Cache achieves ~71% block skip rate and ~2.6× wall-clock speedup with minimal degradation in quality.
AI.conclusion: X-Cache provides a training-free, chunk-level caching solution that enables real-time, interactive driving world generation by reusing computation across chunks while maintaining autoregressive integrity and limiting error propagation.

title: uLEAD-TabPFN: Uncertainty-aware Dependency-based Anomaly Detection with TabPFN
summary: Anomaly detection in tabular data is challenging due to high dimensionality, complex feature dependencies, and heterogeneous noise. Many existing methods rely on proximity-based cues and may miss anomalies caused by violations of complex feature dependencies. Dependency-based anomaly detection provides a principled alternative by identifying anomalies as violations of dependencies among features. However, existing methods often struggle to model such dependencies robustly and to scale to high-dimensional data with complex dependency structures. To address these challenges, we propose uLEAD-TabPFN, a dependency-based anomaly detection framework built on Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs). uLEAD-TabPFN identifies anomalies as violations of conditional dependencies in a learned latent space, leveraging frozen PFNs for dependency estimation. Combined with uncertainty-aware scoring, the proposed framework enables robust and scalable anomaly detection. Experiments on 57 tabular datasets from ADBench show that uLEAD-TabPFN achieves particularly strong performance in medium- and high-dimensional settings, where it attains the top average rank. On high-dimensional datasets, uLEAD-TabPFN improves the average ROC-AUC by nearly 20\% over the average baseline and by approximately 2.8\% over the best-performing baseline, while maintaining overall superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Further analysis shows that uLEAD-TabPFN provides complementary anomaly detection capability, achieving strong performance on datasets where many existing methods struggle.

AI.tldr: Dependency-based anomaly detection for high-dimensional tabular data using uLEAD-TabPFN, leveraging Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs) to model conditional feature dependencies in a learned latent space with uncertainty-aware scoring; achieves strong ROC-AUC gains on 57 ADBench datasets, especially in medium and high dimensions.
AI.motivation: Anomaly detection in tabular data is challenged by high dimensionality, complex feature dependencies, and heterogeneous noise. Proximity-based methods may miss anomalies arising from dependency violations; a principled approach to model dependencies robustly and scalably is needed.
AI.method: uLEAD-TabPFN uses frozen Prior-Data Fitted Networks to estimate conditional dependencies in a latent space. Anomalies are detected as violations of learned conditional dependencies, with uncertainty-aware scoring to improve robustness. Evaluation on 57 tabular datasets from ADBench compares against state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.result: On high-dimensional datasets, the method improves ROC-AUC by ~20% over the average baseline and ~2.8% over the best baseline; achieves top average rank in medium- and high-dimensional settings and demonstrates complementary anomaly detection capabilities on datasets where many methods struggle.
