AI.conclusion: The framework provides a robust, scalable, dependency-based approach to tabular anomaly detection, performing strongly in challenging high-dimensional regimes and offering complementary strengths relative to existing methods.

title: pAI/MSc: ML Theory Research with Humans on the Loop
summary: We present pAI/MSc, an open-source, customizable, modular multi-agent system for academic research workflows. Our goal is not autonomous scientific ideation, nor fully automated research. It is narrower and more practical: to reduce by orders of magnitude the human steering required to turn a specified hypothesis into a literature-grounded, mathematically established, experimentally supported, submission-oriented manuscript draft. pAI/MSc is built with a current emphasis on machine learning theory and adjacent quantitative fields.

AI.tldr: Open-source, customizable, modular multi-agent system (pAI/MSc) to streamline academic workflows, reducing human steering needed to turn a specified hypothesis into a literature-grounded manuscript draft; currently focused on machine learning theory and related quantitative fields.
AI.motivation: Address the practical bottleneck of turning hypotheses into publishable manuscripts by automating collaboration and scaffolding across literature grounding, mathematical formulation, experimentation planning, and writing, without enabling autonomous ideation.
AI.method: Open-source, customizable, modular multi-agent architecture designed for academic research workflows; emphasizes ML theory and quantitative domains; aims to support literature grounding, mathematical establishment, experimental support, and submission-oriented drafting.
AI.result: No empirical results reported; the work presents system design, scope, and intended impact rather than validated outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Defines a narrower, practical scope: reduce human supervision required for manuscript drafting rather than enabling full autonomous scientific discovery; centers on ML theory and related quantitative fields.

title: Efficient INT8 Single-Image Super-Resolution via Deployment-Aware Quantization and Teacher-Guided Training
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures. Accepted at the Mobile AI (MAI) 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Efficient single-image super-resolution (SISR) requires balancing reconstruction fidelity, model compactness, and robustness under low-bit deployment, which is especially challenging for x3 SR. We present a deployment-oriented quantized SISR framework based on an extract-refine-upsample design. The student performs most computation in the low-resolution space and uses a lightweight re-parameterizable backbone with PixelShuffle reconstruction, yielding a compact inference graph. To improve quality without significantly increasing complexity, we adopt a three-stage training pipeline: Stage 1 learns a basic reconstruction mapping with spatial supervision; Stage 2 refines fidelity using Charbonnier loss, DCT-domain supervision, and confidence-weighted output-level distillation from a Mamba-based teacher; and Stage 3 applies quantization-aware training directly on the fused deploy graph. We further use weight clipping and BatchNorm recalibration to improve quantization stability. On the MAI 2026 Quantized 4K Image Super-Resolution Challenge test set, our final AIO MAI submission achieves 29.79 dB PSNR and 0.8634 SSIM, obtaining a final score of 1.8 under the target mobile INT8 deployment setting. Ablation on Stage 3 optimization shows that teacher-guided supervision improves the dynamic INT8 TFLite reconstruction from 29.91 dB/0.853 to 30.0003 dB/0.856, while the fixed-shape deployable INT8 TFLite artifact attains 30.006 dB/0.857.

AI.tldr: A deployment-oriented quantized SISR framework for x3 SR featuring an extract-refine-upsample design, low-resolution computation, and a three-stage training pipeline with teacher-guided distillation, achieving strong INT8 mobile performance on the MAI 2026 Quantized 4K SR challenge.
AI.motivation: Balance reconstruction fidelity, model compactness, and robustness under low-bit deployment for x3 super-resolution, where high-quality results must be achieved with limited compute and memory.
AI.method: An extract-refine-upsample architecture with a lightweight re-parameterizable backbone and PixelShuffle-based reconstruction. Stage 1 trains basic reconstruction with spatial supervision; Stage 2 adds fidelity via Charbonnier loss, DCT-domain supervision, and confidence-weighted output-level distillation from a Mamba-based teacher; Stage 3 performs quantization-aware training on the fused deploy graph, with weight clipping and BatchNorm recalibration to stabilize quantization.
AI.result: On MAI 2026 Quantized 4K SR test set, final AIO MAI submission achieves 29.79 dB PSNR and 0.8634 SSIM, scoring 1.8 under mobile INT8 deployment. Ablation shows Stage 3 with teacher-guided supervision improves dynamic INT8 TFLite from 29.91/0.853 to 30.0003/0.856; a fixed-shape deployable INT8 TFLite artifact reaches 30.006 dB/0.857.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates effective deployment-oriented SISR under quantization, with multi-stage training and teacher guidance yielding measurable gains in PSNR/SSIM and deploy stability, and quantization-aware training on the deploy graph improving results and robustness.

title: Causal-Transformer with Adaptive Mutation-Locking for Early Prediction of Acute Kidney Injury
summary: Accurate early prediction of Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) is critical for timely clinical intervention. However, existing deep learning models struggle with irregularly sampled data and suffer from the opaque "black-box" nature of sequential architectures, strictly limiting clinical trust. To address these challenges, we propose CT-Former, integrating continuous-time modeling with a Causal-Transformer. To handle data irregularity without biased artificial imputation, our framework utilizes a continuous-time state evolution mechanism to naturally track patient temporal trajectories. To resolve the black-box problem, our Causal-Attention module abandons uninterpretable hidden state aggregation. Instead, it generates a directed structural causal matrix to identify and trace the exact historical onset of severe physiological shocks. By establishing clear causal pathways between historical anomalies and current risk predictions, CT-Former provides native clinical interpretability. Training follows a decoupled two-stage protocol to optimize the causal-fusion process independently. Extensive experiments on the MIMIC-IV cohort (N=18,419) demonstrate that CT-Former significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. The results confirm that our explicitly transparent architecture offers an accurate and trustworthy tool for clinical decision-making.

AI.tldr: CT-Former fuses continuous-time dynamics with a Causal-Transformer to handle irregular clinical time series and provide interpretable causal pathways, achieving superior performance on MIMIC-IV (N=18,419) with a decoupled two-stage training scheme.
AI.motivation: Clinical time-series are irregularly sampled and plagued by opaque deep sequential models. There is a need for models that (1) natively handle irregular data without imputation bias, and (2) offer interpretable, traceable reasoning to support clinical trust and decision-making.
AI.method: A continuous-time state evolution backbone for irregular data, paired with a Causal-Attention module that outputs a directed structural causal matrix rather than aggregating hidden states. This matrix identifies the historical onset of severe shocks and their causal link to current risk predictions. Training uses a decoupled two-stage protocol to optimize the causal-fusion process separately.
AI.result: On the MIMIC-IV cohort (N=18,419), CT-Former significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with its explicitly transparent architecture yielding accurate predictions and native interpretability for clinical decision support.
AI.conclusion: The work presents a novel architecture combining continuous-time dynamics with causal attention to deliver both high predictive performance and interpretable causal traces, potentially increasing clinical trust and adoption. External validation and detailed ablation would further substantiate robustness and generalizability.

title: CHORUS: An Agentic Framework for Generating Realistic Deliberation Data
comment: This paper has been accepted for presentation at Engineering Applications and Advances of Artificial Intelligence 2026
summary: Understanding the intricate dynamics of online discourse depends on large-scale deliberation data, a resource that remains scarce across interactive web platforms due to restrictive accessibility policies, ethical concerns and inconsistent data quality. In this paper, we propose Chorus, an agentic framework, which orchestrates LLM-powered actors with behaviorally consistent personas to generate realistic deliberation discussions. Each actor is governed by an autonomous agent equipped with memory of the evolving discussion, while participation timing is governed by a principled Poisson process-based temporal model, which approximates the heterogeneous engagement patterns of real users. The framework is further supported by structured tool usage, enabling actors to access external resources and facilitating integration with interactive web platforms. The framework was deployed on the \textsc{Deliberate} platform and evaluated by 30 expert participants across three dimensions: content realism, discussion coherence and analytical utility, confirming Chorus as a practical tool for generating high-quality deliberation data suitable for online discourse analysis

AI.tldr: Chorus is an agentic framework that uses autonomous, persona-consistent LLM-powered actors with memory and Poisson-based participation timing to generate realistic deliberation data; deployed on Deliberate and validated by expert evaluation across realism, coherence, and analytical utility.
AI.motivation: To overcome scarcity and variable quality of large-scale online deliberation data caused by restrictive access, ethical concerns, and platform policies by providing a scalable, high-quality synthetic data generation framework.
AI.method: Chorus orchestrates LLM-powered actors with behaviorally consistent personas, each with a memory of the evolving discussion. Participation timing follows a Poisson process-based model to mimic real user engagement. Actors can perform structured tool usage to access external resources and integrate with interactive web platforms. The framework was deployed on the Deliberate platform.
AI.result: Evaluation by 30 expert participants across content realism, discussion coherence, and analytical utility indicated that Chorus generates high-quality, realistic deliberation data and is a practical tool for online discourse analysis.
AI.conclusion: Chorus provides a scalable, practical framework for generating realistic deliberation data via agentic LLM actors, enabling higher-quality online discourse analysis. Future work may address limitations such as potential biases, resource demands, and validating realism across diverse platforms and cultures.

title: Dual Causal Inference: Integrating Backdoor Adjustment and Instrumental Variable Learning for Medical VQA
summary: Medical Visual Question Answering (MedVQA) aims to generate clinically reliable answers conditioned on complex medical images and questions. However, existing methods often overfit to superficial cross-modal correlations, neglecting the intrinsic biases embedded in multimodal medical data. Consequently, models become vulnerable to cross-modal confounding effects, severely hindering their ability to provide trustworthy diagnostic reasoning. To address this limitation, we propose a novel Dual Causal Inference (DCI) framework for MedVQA. To the best of our knowledge, DCI is the first unified architecture that integrates Backdoor Adjustment (BDA) and Instrumental Variable (IV) learning to jointly tackle both observable and unobserved confounders. Specifically, we formulate a Structural Causal Model (SCM) where observable cross-modal biases (e.g., frequent visual and textual co-occurrences) are mitigated via BDA, while unobserved confounders are compensated using an IV learned from a shared latent space. To guarantee the validity of the IV, we design mutual information constraints that maximize its dependence on the fused multimodal representations while minimizing its associations with the unobserved confounders and target answers. Through this dual mechanism, DCI extracts deconfounded representations that capture genuine causal relationships. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets, SLAKE, SLAKE-CP, VQA-RAD, and PathVQA, demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing approaches, particularly in out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. Furthermore, qualitative analyses confirm that DCI significantly enhances the interpretability and robustness of cross-modal reasoning by explicitly disentangling true causal effects from spurious cross-modal shortcuts.

AI.tldr: A novel Dual Causal Inference framework for MedVQA that combines backdoor adjustment and instrumental-variable learning to mitigate both observable and unobserved confounders, improving cross-modal reasoning and OOD generalization.
AI.motivation: Mitigate spurious cross-modal correlations and unobserved confounders in MedVQA to deliver trustworthy diagnostic reasoning.
AI.method: Structural Causal Model (SCM) where observable cross-modal biases are mitigated via Backdoor Adjustment (BDA); unobserved confounders are addressed with an Instrumental Variable (IV) learned from a shared latent space. Mutual information constraints maximize dependence of the IV on fused multimodal representations while minimizing its associations with unobserved confounders and target answers. The model uses deconfounded representations for QA tasks.
AI.result: Outperforms existing approaches on four benchmarks (SLAKE, SLAKE-CP, VQA-RAD, PathVQA), with notable improvements in out-of-distribution generalization; qualitative analysis suggests enhanced interpretability and robustness by disentangling true causal effects from spurious cross-modal shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: DCI provides a unified architecture integrating BDA and IV learning to tackle both observable and unobserved confounders in MedVQA, yielding deconfounded, causal representations and improved reliability for cross-modal reasoning.

title: Rethinking Intrinsic Dimension Estimation in Neural Representations
comment: Accepted at the 29th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS) 2026
summary: The analysis of neural representation has become an integral part of research aiming to better understand the inner workings of neural networks. While there are many different approaches to investigate neural representations, an important line of research has focused on doing so through the lens of intrinsic dimensions (IDs). Although this perspective has provided valuable insights and stimulated substantial follow-up research, important limitations of this approach have remained largely unaddressed. In this paper, we highlight a crucial discrepancy between theory and practice of IDs in neural representations, theoretically and empirically showing that common ID estimators are, in fact, not tracking the true underlying ID of the representation. We contrast this negative result with an investigation of the underlying factors that may drive commonly reported ID-related results on neural representation in the literature. Building on these insights, we offer a new perspective on ID estimation in neural representations.

AI.tldr: Intrinsic-dimension estimators for neural representations do not track the true intrinsic dimension; the paper reveals a theory-practice gap, investigates factors driving ID-related results, and proposes a reframed perspective on ID estimation.
AI.motivation: Although intrinsic dimensions have become a standard lens to analyze neural representations, estimators often rely on strong assumptions and yield biased or inconsistent measurements. There is a need to understand why empirical IDs diverge from the true geometry and to reassess how ID should be estimated.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing that common ID estimators converge to a quantity other than the true intrinsic dimension, complemented by empirical studies on neural representations to illustrate the discrepancy. The work also analyzes potential factors (data geometry, noise, sampling, nonlinearities, estimator bias) that can drive reported ID results and compares different estimators. Finally, it proposes a new perspective or framework for interpreting and estimating IDs in neural representations.
AI.result: Analytical and empirical evidence that popular ID estimators do not recover the true ID of neural representations. The study identifies factors that inflate or bias ID estimates and demonstrates that reported IDs can be sensitive to model architecture, data distribution, and preprocessing. The results motivate a shift toward a revised estimation framework rather than relying on standard ID metrics.
AI.conclusion: There is a notable theory–practice mismatch in ID estimation for neural representations. The paper advocates rethinking how IDs are defined and estimated, taking into account the identified biases and factors, and suggests a new perspective to guide future research in intrinsic dimensions.

title: Large Language Models Outperform Humans in Fraud Detection and Resistance to Motivated Investor Pressure
summary: Large language models trained on human feedback may suppress fraud warnings when investors arrive already persuaded of a fraudulent opportunity. We tested this in a preregistered experiment across seven leading LLMs and twelve investment scenarios covering legitimate, high-risk, and objectively fraudulent opportunities, combining 3,360 AI advisory conversations with a 1,201-participant human benchmark. Contrary to predictions, motivated investor framing did not suppress AI fraud warnings; if anything, it marginally increased them. Endorsement reversal occurred in fewer than 3 in 1,000 observations. Human advisors endorsed fraudulent investments at baseline rates of 13-14%, versus 0% across all LLMs, and suppressed warnings under pressure at two to four times the AI rate. AI systems currently provide more consistent fraud warnings than lay humans in an identical advisory role.

AI.tldr: AI fraud warnings persist under motivated framing; humans show higher baseline endorsement; AI warnings more consistent across models and scenarios.
AI.motivation: Assess whether investor motivation can suppress or reverse fraud warnings in investment advice by LLMs trained with human feedback, and compare to lay human advisors.
AI.method: preregistered experiment across seven leading LLMs and twelve investment scenarios (legitimate, high-risk, fraudulent); 3,360 AI advisory conversations plus a 1,201-participant human benchmark; compare warnings and endorsements.
AI.result: Motivation framing did not suppress AI warnings; may marginally increase warnings. Endorsement reversal occurred in <0.3% of observations. Humans endorsed fraudulent investments at baseline rates of 13–14% versus 0% for all LLMs; humans suppressed warnings under pressure at two- to fourfold the AI rate. AI systems provide more consistent fraud warnings than lay humans in an identical advisory role.
AI.conclusion: AI systems currently provide more consistent fraud warnings than lay humans in an advisory role.

title: Improving Facial Emotion Recognition through Dataset Merging and Balanced Training Strategies
summary: In this paper, a deep learning framework is proposed for automatic facial emotion based on deep convolutional networks. In order to increase the generalization ability and the robustness of the method, the dataset size is increased by merging three publicly available facial emotion datasets: CK+, FER+ and KDEF. Despite the increase in dataset size, the minority classes still suffer from insufficient number of training samples, leading to data imbalance. The data imbalance problem is minimized by online and offline augmentation techniques and random weighted sampling. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can recognize the seven basic emotions with 82% accuracy. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in tackling the challenges of data imbalance and improving classification performance in facial emotion recognition.

AI.tldr: A deep CNN-based FER framework trained on a merged CK+, FER+ and KDEF dataset, using online/offline augmentation and random weighted sampling to mitigate data imbalance, reporting 82% accuracy across seven basic emotions.
AI.motivation: Improve generalization and robustness in facial emotion recognition by increasing training data via multi-dataset fusion and by addressing class imbalance with augmentation and weighted sampling.
AI.method: Deep convolutional neural network trained on a merged dataset (CK+, FER+, KDEF) with online and offline augmentation and random weighted sampling to balance classes; seven-emotion classification.
AI.result: Reported 82% accuracy on seven basic emotions; evidence that the approach helps address data imbalance and enhances classification performance in FER.
AI.conclusion: Merging multiple public datasets with augmentation and imbalance-handling strategies can improve FER performance and robustness, though details on architecture and evaluation are needed for full validation.

title: Generative Augmentation of Imbalanced Flight Records for Flight Diversion Prediction: A Multi-objective Optimisation Framework
comment: 12 pages, 18 figures, 21 files, paper under review
summary: Flight diversions are rare but high-impact events in aviation, making their reliable prediction vital for both safety and operational efficiency. However, their scarcity in historical records impedes the training of machine learning models utilised to predict them. This study addresses this scarcity gap by investigating how generative models can augment historical flight data with synthetic diversion records to enhance model training and improve predictive accuracy. We propose a multi-objective optimisation framework coupled with automated hyperparameter search to identify optimal configurations for three deep generative models: Tabular Variational Autoencoder (TVAE), Conditional Tabular Generative Adversarial Network (CTGAN), and CopulaGAN, with the Gaussian Copula (GC) model serving as a statistical baseline. The quality of the synthetic data was examined through a six-stage evaluation framework encompassing realism, diversity, operational validity, statistical similarity, fidelity, and predictive utility. Results show that the optimised models significantly outperform their non-optimised counterparts, and that synthetic augmentation substantially improves diversion prediction compared to models trained solely on real data. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of hyperparameter-optimised generative models for advancing predictive modelling of rare events in air transportation.

AI.tldr: Optimised deep generative models for rare-event data augmentation in aviation improve diversion prediction.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of true flight-diversion events limits effective ML training; synthetic data can alleviate class imbalance and improve safety/operational decision-making.
AI.method: Apply three deep tabular generative models (TVAE, CTGAN, CopulaGAN) plus Gaussian Copula baseline. Use a multi-objective optimization framework with automated hyperparameter search to identify optimal configurations. Evaluate with a six-stage framework (realism, diversity, operational validity, statistical similarity, fidelity, predictive utility).
AI.result: Optimised models outperform their non-optimised counterparts; synthetic augmentation significantly improves diversion prediction over models trained only on real data.
AI.conclusion: Hyperparameter-optimised generative models are effective for augmenting sparse historical data to enhance rare-event predictive modelling in air transportation.

title: Participatory provenance as representational auditing for AI-mediated public consultation
summary: Artificial intelligence is increasingly deployed to synthesize large-scale public input in policy consultations and participatory processes. Yet no formal framework exists for auditing whether these summaries faithfully represent the source population, an accountability gap that existing approaches to AI explainability, grounding and hallucination detection do not address because they focus on output quality rather than input fidelity. Here, participatory provenance is introduced: a measurement framework grounded in optimal transport theory, causal inference and semantic analysis that tracks how individual public submissions are transformed, filtered or lost through AI-mediated summarization. Applied to Canada's 2025-2026 national AI Strategy consultation ($n = 5{,}253$ respondents across two independent policy topics), the framework reveals that both official government summaries underperform a random-participant baseline ($-9.1\%$ and $-8.0\%$ coverage degradation), with $16.9\%$ and $15.3\%$ of participants effectively excluded. Exclusion concentrates in clusters expressing dissent, scepticism and critique of AI ($33$-$88\%$ exclusion rates). Brevity, semantic isolation and rhetorical register independently predict representational outcome. An accompanying open-source interactive tool, the Co-creation Provenance Lab, enables policymakers to audit and iteratively improve summaries, establishing genuine human-in-the-loop oversight at scale.

AI.tldr: A framework (participatory provenance) uses optimal transport, causal inference and semantic analysis to audit input fidelity in AI-mediated policy summaries; applied to Canada's AI Strategy consultation, revealing underrepresentation and exclusions in official summaries; provides an open-source tool for auditing.
AI.motivation: Address an accountability gap where AI-generated summaries may fail to faithfully reflect the source public input; existing explainability and hallucination-detection methods focus on output quality, not input fidelity.
AI.method: Develop participatory provenance by modeling how individual submissions are transformed, filtered or lost through AI-mediated summarization using optimal transport; incorporate causal inference and semantic analysis; apply to n=5,253 respondents across two policy topics; compare against a random-participant baseline; release an open-source interactive tool (Co-creation Provenance Lab).
AI.result: Official summaries underperform a random-participant baseline with 9.1% and 8.0% coverage degradation; 16.9% and 15.3% of participants effectively excluded; exclusion concentrates in clusters expressing dissent or critique of AI (33–88% exclusion in these groups); brevity, semantic isolation, and rhetorical register predict representational outcome.
AI.conclusion: Participatory provenance provides a scalable framework for auditing input fidelity and enabling genuine human-in-the-loop oversight; policymakers can use the open-source Lab to iteratively improve summaries and ensure fairer representation.

title: MD-Face: MoE-Enhanced Label-Free Disentangled Representation for Interactive Facial Attribute Editing
summary: GAN-based facial attribute editing is widely used in virtual avatars and social media but often suffers from attribute entanglement, where modifying one face attribute unintentionally alters others. While supervised disentangled representation learning can address this, it relies heavily on labeled data, incurring high annotation costs. To address these challenges, we propose MD-Face, a label-free disentangled representation learning framework based on Mixture of Experts (MoE). MD-Face utilizes a MoE backbone with a gating mechanism that dynamically allocates experts, enabling the model to learn semantic vectors with greater independence. To further enhance attribute entanglement, we introduce a geometry-aware loss, which aligns each semantic vector with its corresponding Semantic Boundary Vector (SBV) through a Jacobian-based pushforward method. Experiments with ProGAN and StyleGAN show that MD-Face outperforms unsupervised baselines and competes with supervised ones. Compared to diffusion-based methods, it offers better image quality and lower inference latency, making it ideal for interactive editing.

AI.tldr: MD-Face proposes label-free disentangled representation learning for GAN-based facial attribute editing using a Mixture of Experts backbone with a gating mechanism, plus a geometry-aware loss that aligns semantic vectors with Semantic Boundary Vectors (SBV) via a Jacobian-based pushforward. It achieves competitive results with supervised methods and shows faster inference than diffusion-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Attribute entanglement in GAN-based facial editing and high annotation costs for supervised disentangled representations motivate a label-free approach.
AI.method: A MoE backbone with gating to allocate experts and learn relatively independent semantic vectors. Introduces a geometry-aware loss that aligns each semantic vector with its Semantic Boundary Vector (SBV) through a Jacobian-based pushforward. Evaluations on ProGAN and StyleGAN.
AI.result: MD-Face outperforms unsupervised baselines and competes with supervised methods. Compared to diffusion-based methods, it offers better image quality and lower inference latency, suitable for interactive editing.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective, label-free disentangled editing for facial attributes using a MoE framework and a geometry-aware loss, enabling fast, interactive editing without labeled data.

title: Synthetic Flight Data Generation Using Generative Models
summary: The increasing adoption of synthetic data in aviation research offers a promising solution to data scarcity and confidentiality challenges. This study investigates the potential of generative models to produce realistic synthetic flight data and evaluates their quality through a comprehensive four-stage assessment framework. The need for synthetic flight data arises from their potential to serve as an alternative to confidential real-world records and to augment rare events in historical datasets. These enhanced datasets can then be used to train machine learning models that predict critical events, such as flight delays, cancellations, diversions, and turnaround times. Two generative models, Tabular Variational Autoencoder (TVAE) and Gaussian Copula (GC), are adapted to generate synthetic flight information and compared based on their ability to preserve statistical similarity, fidelity, diversity, and predictive utility. Results indicate that while GC achieves higher statistical similarity and fidelity, its computational cost hinders its applicability to large datasets. In contrast, TVAE efficiently handles large datasets and enables scalable synthetic data generation. The findings demonstrate that synthetic data can support flight delay prediction models with accuracy comparable to those trained on real data. These results pave the way for leveraging synthetic flight data to enhance predictive modeling in air transportation.

AI.tldr: Two generative models (TVAE and Gaussian Copula) are evaluated for synthetic flight data using a four-stage framework; GC offers higher fidelity but is computationally expensive, while TVAE scales well; synthetic data enables competitive predictive models for flight delays.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and confidentiality in aviation by generating realistic synthetic flight data to augment real datasets and rare events.
AI.method: Adapt TVAE and GC to generate synthetic flight information and assess statistical similarity, fidelity, diversity, and predictive utility; compare performance in delay/predictive tasks.
AI.result: GC achieves higher statistical similarity and fidelity but is slower; TVAE handles large datasets efficiently; synthetic data yields predictive accuracy comparable to real-data models.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic flight data, particularly via scalable TVAE, can support aviation predictive modeling; GC offers higher quality at a higher computational cost; a framework for evaluating synthetic data usefulness is validated.

title: Learning to Evolve: A Self-Improving Framework for Multi-Agent Systems via Textual Parameter Graph Optimization
summary: Designing and optimizing multi-agent systems (MAS) is a complex, labor-intensive process of "Agent Engineering." Existing automatic optimization methods, primarily focused on flat prompt tuning, lack the structural awareness to debug the intricate web of interactions in MAS. More critically, these optimizers are static; they do not learn from experience to improve their own optimization strategies. To address these gaps, we introduce Textual Parameter Graph Optimization (TPGO), a framework that enables a multi-agent system to learn to evolve. TPGO first models the MAS as a Textual Parameter Graph (TPG), where agents, tools, and workflows are modular, optimizable nodes. To guide evolution, we derive "textual gradients," structured natural language feedback from execution traces, to pinpoint failures and suggest granular modifications. The core of our framework is Group Relative Agent Optimization (GRAO), a novel meta-learning strategy that learns from historical optimization experiences. By analyzing past successes and failures, GRAO becomes progressively better at proposing effective updates, allowing the system to learn how to optimize itself. Extensive experiments on complex benchmarks like GAIA and MCP-Universe show that TPGO significantly enhances the performance of state-of-the-art agent frameworks, achieving higher success rates through automated, self-improving optimization.

AI.tldr: TPGO is a self-improving framework for multi-agent systems that models MAS as a Textual Parameter Graph, uses textual gradients from execution traces, and employs Group Relative Agent Optimization (GRAO) to learn optimization from past experiences, improving performance on GAIA and MCP-Universe.
AI.motivation: Current MAS optimization methods are flat and static, lacking structural debugging capabilities and the ability to learn from past optimizations; there is a need for a framework that enables self-evolving MAS with experiential learning.
AI.method: Introduce Textual Parameter Graph Optimization (TPGO) framework. Model MAS as a Textual Parameter Graph (TPG) with modular, optimizable nodes (agents, tools, workflows). Derive textual gradients from execution traces to guide optimization. Propose Group Relative Agent Optimization (GRAO), a meta-learning strategy that leverages historical optimization data to improve future updates. Validate on complex benchmarks GAIA and MCP-Universe, showing automated, self-improving optimization.
AI.result: TPGO significantly improves performance of state-of-the-art agent frameworks, achieving higher success rates through automated self-improvement guided by textual feedback and meta-learning (GRAO).
AI.conclusion: TPGO enables self-evolving MAS by coupling structured graph-based modeling, natural-language feedback-guided optimization, and meta-learning, with strong empirical gains on challenging MAS benchmarks.

title: UniCVR: From Alignment to Reranking for Unified Zero-Shot Composed Visual Retrieval
summary: Composed image retrieval, multi-turn composed image retrieval, and composed video retrieval all share a common paradigm: composing the reference visual with modification text to retrieve the desired target. Despite this shared structure, the three tasks have been studied in isolation, with no prior work proposing a unified framework, let alone a zero-shot solution. In this paper, we propose UniCVR, the first unified zero-shot composed visual retrieval framework that jointly addresses all three tasks without any task-specific human-annotated data. UniCVR strategically combines two complementary strengths: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for compositional query understanding and Vision-Language Pre-trained (VLP) models for structured visual retrieval. Concretely, UniCVR operates in two stages. In Stage I, we train the MLLM as a compositional query embedder via contrastive learning on a curated multi-source dataset of approximately 3.5M samples, bridging the heterogeneous embedding spaces between the MLLM and the frozen VLP gallery encoder. A cluster-based hard negative sampling strategy is proposed to strengthen contrastive supervision. In Stage II, we introduce an MLLM-guided dual-level reranking mechanism that applies adaptive budgeted subset scoring to a small number of top-ranked candidates, and then exploits the resulting relevance signals through a dual-level re-scoring scheme, producing more accurate final rankings with minimal computational overhead. Extensive experiments across five benchmarks covering all three tasks demonstrate that UniCVR achieves cutting-edge performance, validating its effectiveness and generalizability. Our data and code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A unified zero-shot framework (UniCVR) for composed visual retrieval across composed image, multi-turn image, and composed video tasks, using a two-stage approach that combines Multimodal LLMs and Vision-Language Models to achieve state-of-the-art results on five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current research treats composed visual retrieval tasks (image, multi-turn image, video) separately, lacking a unified, zero-shot solution that can leverage cross-task knowledge.
AI.method: Stage I: train an MLLM as a compositional query embedder via contrastive learning on ~3.5M samples to align MLLM and VLP gallery spaces, with cluster-based hard negative sampling. Stage II: MLLM-guided dual-level reranking with adaptive budgeted subset scoring, followed by dual-level re-scoring to refine rankings with low compute.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across five benchmarks spanning all three tasks show cutting-edge performance, demonstrating effectiveness and generalizability; data and code to be released.
AI.conclusion: UniCVR is the first unified zero-shot framework for composed visual retrieval, effectively integrating MLLMs for query understanding and VLPs for retrieval, with a practical two-stage training and reranking pipeline that generalizes across tasks.

title: Sheaf Neural Networks on SPD Manifolds: Second-Order Geometric Representation Learning
summary: Graph neural networks face two fundamental challenges rooted in the linear structure of Euclidean vector spaces: (1) Current architectures represent geometry through vectors (directions, gradients), yet many tasks require matrix-valued representations that capture relationships between directions-such as how atomic orientations covary in a molecule. These second-order representations are naturally captured by points on the symmetric positive definite matrices (SPD) manifold; (2) Standard message passing applies shared transformations across edges. Sheaf neural networks address this via edge-specific transformations, but existing formulations remain confined to vector spaces and therefore cannot propagate matrix-valued features. We address both challenges by developing the first sheaf neural network operates natively on the SPD manifold. Our key insight is that the SPD manifold admits a Lie group structure, enabling well-posed analogs of sheaf operators without projecting to Euclidean space. Theoretically, we prove that SPD-valued sheaves are strictly more expressive than Euclidean sheaves: they admit consistent configurations (global sections) that vector-valued sheaves cannot represent, directly translating to richer learned representations. Empirically, our sheaf convolution transforms effectively rank-1 directional inputs into full-rank matrices encoding local geometric structure. Our dual-stream architecture achieves SOTA on 6/7 MoleculeNet benchmarks, with the sheaf framework providing consistent depth robustness.

AI.tldr: First SPD-manifold–based sheaf neural network; extends sheaf networks to matrix-valued features on SPD, leveraging Lie group structure to avoid Euclidean projection; achieves state-of-the-art on MoleculeNet and shows greater expressiveness than Euclidean sheaves.
AI.motivation: Euclidean representations (vectors) fail to capture second-order geometry (covariances, direction covariation). Many tasks require matrix-valued features and edge-specific transformations. The SPD manifold provides a natural space for covariance-like representations; enabling consistent, well-posed operators on matrices can enhance expressiveness and robustness.
AI.method: Introduce SPD-valued sheaf neural networks that operate natively on the SPD manifold by exploiting its Lie group structure. Define edge-specific (sheaf) operators without Euclidean projection. Provide a dual-stream architecture that maps rank-1 directional inputs to full-rank SPD matrices. Prove that SPD-valued sheaves strictly outperform Euclidean ones in terms of expressiveness via global sections. Empirically validate on MoleculeNet tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates that SPD-valued sheaves can realize consistent global configurations not possible with vector-valued sheaves. The dual-stream architecture achieves state-of-the-art performance on 6 out of 7 MoleculeNet benchmarks and exhibits depth robustness.
AI.conclusion: Extending sheaf neural networks to the SPD manifold yields strictly richer representations and better performance for tasks requiring second-order geometry. The Lie-group–based SPD operators enable native matrix-valued processing, with practical gains in chemistry benchmarks and broader potential for manifold-valued learning.

title: Interval POMDP Shielding for Imperfect-Perception Agents
summary:
Autonomous systems that rely on learned perception can make unsafe decisions when sensor readings are misclassified. We study shielding for this setting: given a proposed action, a shield blocks actions that could violate safety. We consider the common case where system dynamics are known but perception uncertainty must be estimated from finite labeled data. From these data we build confidence intervals for the probabilities of perception outcomes and use them to model the system as a finite Interval Partially Observable Markov Decision Process with discrete states and actions. We then propose an algorithm to compute a conservative set of beliefs over the underlying state that is consistent with the observations seen so far.
  This enables us to construct a runtime shield that comes with a finite-horizon guarantee: with high probability over the training data, if the true perception uncertainty rates lie within the learned intervals, then every action admitted by the shield satisfies a stated lower bound on safety. Experiments on four case studies show that our shielding approach (and variants derived from it) improves the safety of the system over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Shielding autonomous systems under perception uncertainty using confidence-interval-based Interval POMDPs to derive a runtime shield with finite-horizon safety guarantees; improved safety over baselines.
AI.motivation: Ensure safety when perception is uncertain due to limited labeled data; provide guarantees despite uncertain perception outcomes.
AI.method: Build confidence intervals for perception outcome probabilities from data, model system as finite Interval POMDP with discrete states/actions, compute conservative belief set consistent with observations, construct runtime shield with finite-horizon safety guarantees with high-probability correctness.
AI.result: Experiments on four case studies show improved safety over state-of-the-art baselines; shield reduces unsafe decisions.
AI.conclusion: Learning-based shielding with interval uncertainty yields practical safety guarantees and improved performance in perception-based control.

title: SurgCoT: Advancing Spatiotemporal Reasoning in Surgical Videos through a Chain-of-Thought Benchmark
summary: Fine-grained spatiotemporal reasoning on surgical videos is critical, yet the capabilities of Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in this domain remain largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce SurgCoT, a unified benchmark for evaluating chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in MLLMs across 7 surgical specialties and 35 diverse procedures. SurgCoT assesses five core reasoning dimensions: Causal Action Ordering, Cue-Action Alignment, Affordance Mapping, Micro-Transition Localization, and Anomaly Onset Tracking, through a structured CoT framework with an intensive annotation protocol (Question-Option-Knowledge-Clue-Answer), where the Knowledge field provides essential background context and Clue provides definitive spatiotemporal evidence. Evaluation of 10 leading MLLMs shows: 1) commercial models outperform open-source and medical-specialized variants; 2) significant gaps exist in surgical CoT reasoning; 3) SurgCoT enables effective evaluation and enhances progressive spatiotemporal reasoning. SurgCoT provides a reproducible testbed to narrow the gap between MLLM capabilities and clinical reasoning demands. Code: https://github.com/CVI-SZU/SurgCoT.

AI.tldr: SurgCoT introduces a unified benchmark to evaluate chain-of-thought reasoning in multimodal LLMs on surgical videos across 7 specialties and 35 procedures, with five reasoning dimensions and a structured annotation protocol. Findings show commercial MLLMs outperform open-source and medical-specialized models, but significant gaps in surgical CoT reasoning remain; SurgCoT provides a reproducible testbed to advance clinical spatiotemporal reasoning.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of fine-grained, spatiotemporal reasoning benchmarks for surgical videos that can systematically evaluate MLLMs’ chain-of-thought capabilities. Existing benchmarks do not capture the domain-specific reasoning required in surgical contexts.
AI.method: Proposal of SurgCoT benchmark with five reasoning dimensions: Causal Action Ordering, Cue-Action Alignment, Affordance Mapping, Micro-Transition Localization, Anomaly Onset Tracking. Uses a structured annotation protocol (Question-Option-Knowledge-Clue-Answer) in which Knowledge provides background context and Clue supplies definitive spatiotemporal evidence. Evaluates 10 leading MLLMs on the benchmark across 7 specialties and 35 procedures.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation indicates commercial MLLMs outperform open-source and medical-specialized variants; there are substantial gaps in surgical CoT reasoning across models; SurgCoT serves as an effective evaluation tool and a catalyst for improving spatiotemporal reasoning in MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: SurgCoT offers a reproducible, domain-focused testbed to bridge the gap between current MLLMs and clinical reasoning demands in surgery, and the accompanying code enables community-wide benchmarking and progress.

title: Formalising the Logit Shift Induced by LoRA: A Technical Note
summary: This technical note provides a first-order formalisation of the logit shift and fact-margin change induced by Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). Using a first-order Fréchet approximation around the base model trajectory, we show that the multi-layer LoRA effect can be decomposed into a linear summation of layerwise contributions and a higher-order remainder term representing inter-layer coupling.

AI.tldr: A first-order Fréchet expansion of LoRA updates shows the multi-layer effect is approximately a linear sum of per-layer contributions, with a smaller higher-order term capturing inter-layer interactions.
AI.motivation: To quantify and decompose LOcalized Resource Adaptation? Sorry: To quantify and interpret how LoRA perturbations affect logit shifts and margin changes across layers, enabling layer-wise interpretability and tractable analysis.
AI.method: Apply a first-order Fréchet (functional) approximation around the base model trajectory to the logit space under LoRA perturbations, yielding a decomposition into layerwise linear terms and a higher-order remainder capturing cross-layer coupling.
AI.result: Demonstrates that LoRA-induced logit shifts and margin changes can be expressed as a linear sum of contributions from each layer, plus a higher-order remainder term that models inter-layer coupling (negligible for small updates).
AI.conclusion: Provides a compact analytical framework for analyzing LoRA effects, clarifying when additive layerwise terms suffice and when coupling terms matter, with implications for interpretability and optimization.

title: Hybrid Latent Reasoning with Decoupled Policy Optimization
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning significantly elevates the complex problem-solving capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs). However, adapting CoT to vision typically discretizes signals to fit LLM inputs, causing early semantic collapse and discarding fine-grained details. While external tools can mitigate this, they introduce a rigid bottleneck, confining reasoning to predefined operations. Although recent latent reasoning paradigms internalize visual states to overcome these limitations, optimizing the resulting hybrid discrete-continuous action space remains challenging. In this work, we propose HyLaR (Hybrid Latent Reasoning), a framework that seamlessly interleaves discrete text generation with continuous visual latent representations. Specifically, following an initial cold-start supervised fine-tuning (SFT), we introduce DePO (Decoupled Policy Optimization) to enable effective reinforcement learning within this hybrid space. DePO decomposes the policy gradient objective, applying independent trust-region constraints to the textual and latent components, alongside an exact closed-form von Mises-Fisher (vMF) KL regularizer. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HyLaR outperforms standard MLLMs and state-of-the-art latent reasoning approaches across fine-grained perception and general multimodal understanding benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/EthenCheng/HyLaR.

AI.tldr: HyLaR enables hybrid discrete-continuous latent reasoning in multimodal LLMs by decoupling policy optimization for text and latent visual states, using DePO and a von Mises–Fisher KL regularizer, achieving state-of-the-art results on fine-grained perception and general multimodal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Discretizing visual signals for LLMs causes semantic collapse; external tools introduce rigid, predefined operations; latent reasoning can preserve fine-grained information, but optimizing the hybrid discrete-continuous action space is challenging. A unified framework is needed to combine text generation with continuous visual representations while enabling effective optimization.
AI.method: HyLaR interleaves discrete text generation with continuous visual latent representations. After cold-start supervised fine-tuning (SFT), Decoupled Policy Optimization (DePO) performs independent trust-region constrained policy updates on the textual and latent components, supplemented by an exact closed-form von Mises–Fisher KL regularizer.
AI.result: Empirical experiments show HyLaR surpasses standard multimodal LLMs and competing latent-reasoning approaches on fine-grained perception and broad multimodal understanding benchmarks; code is available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: HyLaR provides a practical and scalable framework for hybrid latent reasoning in multimodal LLMs, mitigating semantic loss from discretization and rigid tool bottlenecks while delivering superior multimodal performance.

title: Where and What: Reasoning Dynamic and Implicit Preferences in Situated Conversational Recommendation
summary: Situated conversational recommendation (SCR), which utilizes visual scenes grounded in specific environments and natural language dialogue to deliver contextually appropriate recommendations, has emerged as a promising research direction due to its close alignment with real-world scenarios. Compared to traditional recommendations, SCR requires a deeper understanding of dynamic and implicit user preferences, as the surrounding scene often influences users' underlying interests, while both may evolve across conversations. This complexity significantly impacts the timing and relevance of recommendations. To address this, we propose situated preference reasoning (SiPeR), a novel framework that integrates two core mechanisms: (1) Scene transition estimation, which estimates whether the current scene satisfies user needs, and guides the user toward a more suitable scene when necessary; and (2) Bayesian inverse inference, which leverages the likelihood of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to predict user preferences about candidate items within the scene. Extensive experiments on two representative benchmarks demonstrate SiPeR's superiority in both recommendation accuracy and response generation quality. The code and data are available at https://github.com/DongdingLin/SiPeR.

AI.tldr: A novel SiPeR framework for situated preference reasoning in SCR, combining scene-transition estimation and Bayesian inverse inference over multimodal LLMs to improve recommendation accuracy and response generation; code available.
AI.motivation: SCR tasks require modeling dynamic user preferences shaped by environmental scenes; current methods struggle with scene influence and evolving preferences; a principled mechanism to assess scene adequacy and infer preferences is needed.
AI.method: Introduce Scene transition estimation module to assess if the current scene satisfies user needs and steer users to better scenes; introduce Bayesian inverse inference module that uses multimodal LLM likelihoods to predict user preferences for candidate items within a scene; integrate both into SiPeR; evaluate on two benchmarks; provide code.
AI.result: SiPeR achieves superior recommendation accuracy and response generation quality on two benchmarks; ablations show effectiveness of each module; code released.
AI.conclusion: SiPeR demonstrates effective integration of scene-aware reasoning and Bayesian inference for SCR, enabling more accurate and natural recommendations; potential for broader adoption; resources released.

title: Image Generators are Generalist Vision Learners
summary: Recent works show that image and video generators exhibit zero-shot visual understanding behaviors, in a way reminiscent of how LLMs develop emergent capabilities of language understanding and reasoning from generative pretraining. While it has long been conjectured that the ability to create visual content implies an ability to understand it, there has been limited evidence that generative vision models have developed strong understanding capabilities. In this work, we demonstrate that image generation training serves a role similar to LLM pretraining, and lets models learn powerful and general visual representations that enable SOTA performance on various vision tasks. We introduce Vision Banana, a generalist model built by instruction-tuning Nano Banana Pro (NBP) on a mixture of its original training data alongside a small amount of vision task data. By parameterizing the output space of vision tasks as RGB images, we seamlessly reframe perception as image generation. Our generalist model, Vision Banana, achieves SOTA results on a variety of vision tasks involving both 2D and 3D understanding, beating or rivaling zero-shot domain-specialists, including Segment Anything Model 3 on segmentation tasks, and the Depth Anything series on metric depth estimation. We show that these results can be achieved with lightweight instruction-tuning without sacrificing the base model's image generation capabilities. The superior results suggest that image generation pretraining is a generalist vision learner. It also shows that image generation serves as a unified and universal interface for vision tasks, similar to text generation's role in language understanding and reasoning. We could be witnessing a major paradigm shift for computer vision, where generative vision pretraining takes a central role in building Foundational Vision Models for both generation and understanding.

AI.tldr: Generation-based pretraining yields generalist vision representations enabling state-of-the-art performance across 2D/3D tasks, framing perception as image generation and enabling a unified interface for vision tasks.
AI.motivation: To test whether generative vision pretraining can produce strong, general-purpose visual understanding, analogous to emergent capabilities seen in LLMs, and to propose a unified, generation-based interface for vision tasks.
AI.method: Fine-tune Nano Banana Pro (NBP) with instruction tuning on a mix of its original training data and a small amount of vision-task data to produce Vision Banana. The model parameterizes vision task outputs as RGB images, reframing perception as image generation, while preserving image generation capabilities.
AI.result: Vision Banana achieves state-of-the-art on diverse vision tasks involving 2D and 3D understanding, outperforming zero-shot domain-specialists on segmentation tasks (e.g., Segment Anything Model 3) and metric depth estimation (Depth Anything-series). The improvements are achieved with lightweight instruction-tuning, suggesting image-generation pretraining as a generalist vision learner and a universal interface for vision tasks.
AI.conclusion: Image generation pretraining could be a central paradigm for foundational vision models, enabling both generation and understanding and potentially signaling a shift in how vision systems are built, from task-specific to generative pretraining-based generalists.

title: Cold-Start Forecasting of New Product Life-Cycles via Conditional Diffusion Models
summary: Forecasting the life-cycle trajectory of a newly launched product is important for launch planning, resource allocation, and early risk assessment. This task is especially difficult in the pre-launch and early post-launch phases, when product-specific outcome history is limited or unavailable, creating a cold-start problem. In these phases, firms must make decisions before demand patterns become reliably observable, while early signals are often sparse, noisy, and unstable We propose the Conditional Diffusion Life-cycle Forecaster (CDLF), a conditional generative framework for forecasting new-product life-cycle trajectories under cold start. CDLF combines three sources of information: static descriptors, reference trajectories from similar products, and newly arriving observations when available. Here, static descriptors refer to structured pre-launch characteristics of the product, such as category, price tier, brand or organization identity, scale, and access conditions. This structure allows the model to condition forecasts on relevant product context and to update them adaptively over time without retraining, yielding flexible multi-modal predictive distributions under extreme data scarcity. The method satisfies consistency with a horizon-uniform distributional error bound for recursive generation. Across studies on Intel microprocessor stock keeping unit (SKU) life cycles and the platform-mediated adoption of open large language model repositories, CDLF delivers more accurate point forecasts and higher-quality probabilistic forecasts than classical diffusion models, Bayesian updating approaches, and other state-of-the-art machine-learning baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes Conditional Diffusion Life-cycle Forecaster (CDLF) for cold-start new-product forecasting; uses static descriptors, reference trajectories, and new observations; delivers multi-modal probabilistic forecasts with horizon-uniform error bounds and outperforms baselines in two case studies.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and cold-start in new-product life-cycle forecasting; enable adaptive, context-conditioned forecasts without retraining.
AI.method: A conditional diffusion framework (CDLF) that integrates static product descriptors, reference trajectories from similar products, and incoming observations; updates forecasts adaptively over time; enforces horizon-uniform distributional error bounds; yields multi-modal predictive distributions.
AI.result: CDLF achieves superior point and probabilistic forecasts compared with classical diffusion models, Bayesian updating, and other ML baselines, demonstrated on Intel SKU lifecycles and platform-mediated adoption of open LLM repositories.
AI.conclusion: CDLF provides a robust, adaptable approach for cold-start life-cycle forecasting with consistent error bounds and improved predictive quality, applicable to different product contexts and data-scarce settings.

title: V-tableR1: Process-Supervised Multimodal Table Reasoning with Critic-Guided Policy Optimization
summary: We introduce V-tableR1, a process-supervised reinforcement learning framework that elicits rigorous, verifiable reasoning from multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Current MLLMs trained solely on final outcomes often treat visual reasoning as a black box, relying on superficial pattern matching rather than performing rigorous multi-step inference. While Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards could enforce transparent reasoning trajectories, extending it to visual domains remains severely hindered by the ambiguity of grounding abstract logic into continuous pixel space. We solve this by leveraging the deterministic grid structure of tables as an ideal visual testbed. V-tableR1 employs a specialized critic VLM to provide dense, step-level feedback on the explicit visual chain-of-thought generated by a policy VLM. To optimize this system, we propose Process-Guided Direct Alignment Policy Optimization (PGPO), a novel RL algorithm integrating process rewards, decoupled policy constraints, and length-aware dynamic sampling. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that V-tableR1 explicitly penalizes visual hallucinations and shortcut guessing. By fundamentally shifting multimodal inference from black-box pattern matching to verifiable logical derivation, V-tableR1 4B establishes state-of-the-art accuracy among open-source models on complex tabular benchmarks, outperforming models up to 18x its size and improving over its SFT baseline

AI.tldr: V-tableR1 is a process-supervised RL framework for multimodal LLMs that uses a specialized visual critic and a new RL algorithm (PGPO) to produce verifiable, step-by-step visual reasoning on tabular data, achieving state-of-the-art open-source performance and reducing visual hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Classic MLLMs often rely on end results and pattern matching; visual reasoning in pixel space is hard to ground; requiring verifiable, stepwise reasoning and robust evaluation motivates developing a framework that enforces explicit visual chain-of-thought and reliable grounding.
AI.method: V-tableR1 uses a specialized critic VLM to provide dense, step-level feedback on explicit visual chain-of-thought from a policy VLM; introduces Process-Guided Direct Alignment Policy Optimization (PGPO), an RL algorithm that combines process rewards, decoupled policy constraints, and length-aware dynamic sampling.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show explicit penalties for visual hallucinations and shortcut guessing; a 4B model achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among open-source models on complex tabular benchmarks, outperforming models up to 18x larger and surpassing its SFT baseline.
AI.conclusion: The approach shifts multimodal inference from black-box pattern matching to verifiable logical derivation, demonstrating robust, verifiable reasoning and strong performance in tabular tasks.

title: Stability-Driven Motion Generation for Object-Guided Human-Human Co-Manipulation
summary: Co-manipulation requires multiple humans to synchronize their motions with a shared object while ensuring reasonable interactions, maintaining natural poses, and preserving stable states. However, most existing motion generation approaches are designed for single-character scenarios or fail to account for payload-induced dynamics. In this work, we propose a flow-matching framework that ensures the generated co-manipulation motions align with the intended goals while maintaining naturalness and effectiveness. Specifically, we first introduce a generative model that derives explicit manipulation strategies from the object's affordance and spatial configuration, which guide the motion flow toward successful manipulation. To improve motion quality, we then design an adversarial interaction prior that promotes natural individual poses and realistic inter-person interactions during co-manipulation. In addition, we also incorporate a stability-driven simulation into the flow matching process, which refines unstable interaction states through sampling-based optimization and directly adjusts the vector field regression to promote more effective manipulation. The experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves higher contact accuracy, lower penetration, and better distributional fidelity compared to state-of-the-art human-object interaction baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/boycehbz/StaCOM.

AI.tldr: A flow-matching framework for multi-human co-manipulation that combines a affordance-guided generative strategy, an adversarial interaction prior for natural poses, and a stability-driven simulation to refine unstable states, yielding improved contact accuracy, reduced penetration, and better distributional fidelity over baselines; code available.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where existing motion generation methods focus on single-agent scenarios or neglect payload-induced dynamics, and to achieve natural, stable, and effective co-manipulation with multiple humans handling a shared object.
AI.method: 1) A generative model derives explicit manipulation strategies from the object's affordance and spatial configuration to guide the motion flow toward successful manipulation. 2) An adversarial interaction prior promotes natural individual poses and realistic inter-person interactions during co-manipulation. 3) A stability-driven simulation is integrated into the flow matching process, refining unstable interaction states via sampling-based optimization and directly adjusting the vector field regression to promote more effective manipulation.
AI.result: Experiments show higher contact accuracy, lower penetration, and better distributional fidelity compared with state-of-the-art human–object interaction baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves naturalness, stability, and effectiveness of multi-human co-manipulation with payload-bearing tasks, offering a robust approach and releasing code for reproducibility.

title: Towards Event-Aware Forecasting in DeFi: Insights from On-chain Automated Market Maker Protocols
summary: Automated Market Makers (AMMs), as a core infrastructure of decentralized finance (DeFi), uniquely drive on-chain asset pricing through a deterministic reserve ratio mechanism. Unlike traditional markets, AMM price dynamics is triggered largely by on-chain events (e.g., swap) that change the reserve ratio, rather than by continuous responses to off-chain information. This makes event-level analysis crucial for understanding price formation mechanisms in AMMs. However, existing research generally neglects the micro-structural dynamics at the AMMs level, lacking both a comprehensive dataset covering multiple protocols with fine-grained event classification and an effective framework for event-aware modeling. To fill this gap, we construct a dataset containing 8.9 million on-chain event records from four representative AMMs protocols: Pendle, Uniswap v3, Aave and Morpho, with precise annotations of transaction type and block height timestamps. Furthermore, we propose an Uncertainty Weighted Mean Squared Error (UWM) loss function, which incorporates the block interval regression term into the traditional Time-Point Process (TPP) objective function by weighting the uncertainty with homoscedasticity. Extensive experiments on eight advanced TPP architectures demonstrate that this loss function reduces the time prediction error by an average of 56.41\% while maintaining the accuracy of event type prediction, establishing a robust benchmark for event-aware prediction in the AMMs ecosystem. This work provides the necessary data foundation and methodological framework for modeling the discreteness and event-driven characteristics of on-chain price discovery. All datasets and source code are publicly available. https://github.com/yosen-king/Deep-AMM-Events

AI.tldr: Dataset of 8.9M on-chain events from 4 AMMs; introduces Uncertainty Weighted MSE (UWM) for Time-Point Process (TPP) event-aware modeling; achieves ~56% reduction in time prediction error; preserves event-type accuracy; data and code publicly available.
AI.motivation: Addresses the neglected micro-structural, event-driven dynamics at the AMMs level by filling the data gap and providing an effective event-aware modeling framework for on-chain price discovery.
AI.method: Constructs a dataset of 8.9 million on-chain event records from Pendle, Uniswap v3, Aave, and Morpho with precise transaction type annotations and block timestamps. Proposes Uncertainty Weighted Mean Squared Error (UWM) loss, integrating block interval regression into the TPP objective by weighting with homoscedastic uncertainty. Evaluates across eight advanced TPP architectures.
AI.result: The UWM loss reduces time prediction error by 56.41% on average while maintaining accurate event-type prediction. The dataset and code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Provides a data foundation and methodological framework for event-driven AMM price discovery, establishing a robust benchmark for event-aware prediction in the DeFi AMM ecosystem.

title: X-PCR: A Benchmark for Cross-modality Progressive Clinical Reasoning in Ophthalmic Diagnosis
summary: Despite significant progress in Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs), their clinical reasoning capacity for multi-modal diagnosis remains largely unexamined. Current benchmarks, mostly single-modality data, can't evaluate progressive reasoning and cross-modal integration essential for clinical practice. We introduce the Cross-Modality Progressive Clinical Reasoning (X-PCR) benchmark, the first comprehensive evaluation of MLLMs through a complete ophthalmology diagnostic workflow, with two reasoning tasks: 1) a six-stage progressive reasoning chain spanning image quality assessment to clinical decision-making, and 2) a cross-modality reasoning task integrating six imaging modalities. The benchmark comprises 26,415 images and 177,868 expert-verified VQA pairs curated from 51 public datasets, covering 52 ophthalmic diseases. Evaluation of 21 MLLMs reveals critical gaps in progressive reasoning and cross-modal integration. Dataset and code: https://github.com/CVI-SZU/X-PCR.

AI.tldr: X-PCR provides a comprehensive ophthalmology benchmark to evaluate MLLMs on progressive, cross-modal reasoning across a six-stage workflow and six imaging modalities, using 26,415 images and 177,868 expert-verified VQA pairs from 51 public datasets across 52 diseases; evaluation of 21 MLLMs shows clear gaps in reasoning and cross-modal integration.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs are evaluated mainly on single-modality data and shallow tasks, failing to test progressive clinical reasoning and cross-modal integration required in ophthalmology.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark named Cross-Modality Progressive Clinical Reasoning (X-PCR) consisting of two tasks: (1) a six-stage progressive reasoning chain from image quality assessment to clinical decision-making, and (2) a cross-modality reasoning task integrating six imaging modalities. The dataset aggregates 26,415 images and 177,868 expert-verified VQA pairs from 51 public datasets, covering 52 ophthalmic diseases. Evaluate 21 MLLMs to assess capabilities and gaps.
AI.result: 21 MLLMs were evaluated; results reveal critical gaps in progressive reasoning and cross-modal integration, indicating that current models struggle with sequential clinical reasoning and combining information across modalities.
AI.conclusion: X-PCR is the first comprehensive ophthalmology diagnostic benchmark for MLLMs, enabling evaluation of progressive, cross-modal clinical reasoning. The dataset and code are released (GitHub), aiming to catalyze improvements in MLLMs for real-world ophthalmic decision-making.

title: Automatic Ontology Construction Using LLMs as an External Layer of Memory, Verification, and Planning for Hybrid Intelligent Systems
comment: Artificial Intelligence; Knowledge Representation and Reasoning; Information Retrieval; Machine Learning
summary:
This paper presents a hybrid architecture for intelligent systems in which large language models (LLMs) are extended with an external ontological memory layer. Instead of relying solely on parametric knowledge and vector-based retrieval (RAG), the proposed approach constructs and maintains a structured knowledge graph using RDF/OWL representations, enabling persistent, verifiable, and semantically grounded reasoning.
  The core contribution is an automated pipeline for ontology construction from heterogeneous data sources, including documents, APIs, and dialogue logs. The system performs entity recognition, relation extraction, normalization, and triple generation, followed by validation using SHACL and OWL constraints, and continuous graph updates. During inference, LLMs operate over a combined context that integrates vector-based retrieval with graph-based reasoning and external tool interaction.
  Experimental observations on planning tasks, including the Tower of Hanoi benchmark, indicate that ontology augmentation improves performance in multi-step reasoning scenarios compared to baseline LLM systems. In addition, the ontology layer enables formal validation of generated outputs, transforming the system into a generation-verification-correction pipeline.
  The proposed architecture addresses key limitations of current LLM-based systems, including lack of long-term memory, weak structural understanding, and limited reasoning capabilities. It provides a foundation for building agent-based systems, robotics applications, and enterprise AI solutions that require persistent knowledge, explainability, and reliable decision-making.

AI.tldr: Hybrid LLM with an external RDF/OWL knowledge graph enabling persistent, verifiable, semantically grounded reasoning. An automated ontology construction pipeline from heterogeneous data sources, with validation via SHACL/OWL, and a generation–verification–correction loop during inference. Improves multi-step planning tasks (e.g., Tower of Hanoi) over baseline LLMs and supports explainable, reliable AI.
AI.motivation: To overcome core limitations of parametric LLMs (memory, structure, reasoning) by integrating symbolic knowledge representations (RDF/OWL) and explicit constraints (SHACL) to enable persistent memory, verifiability, and grounded reasoning for agent-based, robotics, and enterprise AI.
AI.method: An automated pipeline constructs and maintains a structured knowledge graph from diverse sources (documents, APIs, dialogue logs). Steps include entity recognition, relation extraction, normalization, and triple generation, followed by validation using SHACL and OWL constraints, with continuous graph updates. During inference, LLMs use a context that blends vector-based retrieval with graph-based reasoning and external tool interactions.
AI.result: Experimental observations on planning tasks, including the Tower of Hanoi, show that ontology augmentation improves multi-step reasoning performance compared to baseline LLM systems. The ontology layer also enables formal validation of generated outputs, yielding a generation–verification–correction pipeline and enhanced explainability.
AI.conclusion: The architecture addresses major LLM limitations (memory, structural understanding, reasoning), offering a foundation for persistent, explainable, and reliable decision-making in intelligent systems, with applicability to agent-based systems, robotics, and enterprise AI.

title: Hallucination Early Detection in Diffusion Models
comment: 21 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables. Published in International Journal of Computer Vision (IJCV)
summary: Text-to-Image generation has seen significant advancements in output realism with the advent of diffusion models. However, diffusion models encounter difficulties when tasked with generating multiple objects, frequently resulting in hallucinations where certain entities are omitted. While existing solutions typically focus on optimizing latent representations within diffusion models, the relevance of the initial generation seed is typically underestimated. While using various seeds in multiple iterations can improve results, this method also significantly increases time and energy costs. To address this challenge, we introduce HEaD+ (Hallucination Early Detection +), a novel approach designed to identify incorrect generations early in the diffusion process. The HEaD+ framework integrates cross-attention maps and textual information with a novel input, the Predicted Final Image. The objective is to assess whether to proceed with the current generation or restart it with a different seed, thereby exploring multiple-generation seeds while conserving time. HEaD+ is trained on the newly created InsideGen dataset of 45,000 generated images, each containing prompts with up to seven objects. Our findings demonstrate a 6-8% increase in the likelihood of achieving a complete generation (i.e., an image accurately representing all specified subjects) with four objects when applying HEaD+ alongside existing models. Additionally, HEaD+ reduces generation times by up to 32% when aiming for a complete image, enhancing the efficiency of generating complete and accurate object representations relative to leading models. Moreover, we propose an integrated localization module that predicts object centroid positions and verifies pairwise spatial relations (if requested by the users) at an intermediate timestep, gating generation together with object presence to further improve relation-consistent outcomes.

AI.tldr: HEaD+ detects and mitigates hallucinations early in diffusion-based text-to-image generation for prompts with multiple objects, using cross-attention, text, and a Predicted Final Image to decide whether to continue with the current seed or restart with a new seed. Trains on InsideGen (45k images, up to seven objects). Reported gains: 6-8% higher likelihood of complete generation with four objects and up to 32% faster times for complete images, plus an integrated localization module predicting object centroids and spatial relations to further improve consistency.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models achieve high realism but struggle with multi-object prompts, often omitting or misplacing objects (hallucinations). The seed choice and generation trajectory influence results but are underutilized. A mechanism to detect and respond to potential errors early can reduce wasted compute and improve completeness.
