AI.method: HEaD+ integrates cross-attention maps, textual information, and a Predicted Final Image as an input to a decision module that determines whether to continue the current generation or restart with a different seed. It enables exploring multiple seeds while saving compute. An integrated localization module predicts object centroids and verifies pairwise spatial relations at an intermediate timestep, gating generation on object presence to improve relation-consistent outputs. Training is conducted on InsideGen, a dataset of 45k generated images with prompts containing up to seven objects.
AI.result: Increases the likelihood of achieving a complete generation by 6-8% for prompts with four objects and reduces generation time by up to 32% when aiming for a complete image. The localization module also contributes to more relation-consistent results.
AI.conclusion: HEaD+ offers a practical strategy to improve multi-object generation fidelity and efficiency in diffusion models, aided by early hallucination detection, seed management, and spatial relation verification. It shows promise for broader application and further improvements via the localization component.

title: Robustness of Spatio-temporal Graph Neural Networks for Fault Location in Partially Observable Distribution Grids
summary: Fault location in distribution grids is critical for reliability and minimizing outage durations. Yet, it remains challenging due to partial observability, given sparse measurement infrastructure. Recent works show promising results by combining Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for spatio-temporal learning. Still, many modern GNN architectures remain untested for this grid application, while existing GNN solutions have not explored GNN topology definitions beyond simply adopting the full grid topology to construct the GNN graph. We address these gaps by (i) systematically comparing a newly proposed graph-forming strategy (measured-only) to the traditional full-topology approach, and (ii) introducing STGNN (Spatio-temporal GNN) models based on GraphSAGE and an improved Graph Attention (GATv2), for distribution grid fault location; (iii) benchmarking them against state-of-the-art STGNN and RNN baselines on the IEEE 123-bus feeder. In our experiments, all evaluated STGNN variants achieve high performance and consistently outperform a pure RNN baseline, with improvements up to 11 percentage points F1. Among STGNN models, the newly explored RGATv2 and RGSAGE achieve only marginally higher F1 scores. Still, STGNNs demonstrate superior stability, with tight confidence intervals (within +/- 1.4%) compared to the RNN baseline (up to +/- 7.5%) across different experiment runs. Finally, our proposed reduced GNN topology (measured-only) shows clear benefits in both (i) model training time (6-fold reduction) and (ii) model performance (up to 11 points F1). This suggests that measured-only graphs offer a more practical, efficient, and robust framework for partially observable distribution grids.

AI.tldr: Measured-only graphs for STGNNs yield strong fault-location performance in partial-observability distribution grids, outperform RNN baselines by up to 11 F1 points, with much higher stability; measured-only topology also reduces training time (~6x) and improves F1, suggesting practical robustness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address partial observability in distribution grids and the under-exploration of GNN topologies for fault-location tasks; investigate whether measured-only graphs outperform full-topology graphs and assess modern STGNN variants based on GraphSAGE and GATv2.
AI.method: Introduce a measured-only graph-forming strategy and build STGNN models using GraphSAGE and an improved GATv2 (RGATv2, RGSAGE); benchmark against state-of-the-art STGNN and RNN baselines on the IEEE 123-bus feeder; compare to full-topology baseline.
AI.result: All STGNN variants achieve high performance and consistently beat a pure RNN baseline (up to 11 percentage points F1). RGATv2 and RGSAGE yield marginal gains over other STGNNs. STGNNs show superior stability (confidence intervals within ±1.4%) vs. RNN (up to ±7.5%). Measured-only topology reduces training time by ~6x and improves F1 by up to 11 points.
AI.conclusion: Measured-only graphs offer a practical, efficient, and robust framework for fault-location in partially observable distribution grids; GNN topology definitions beyond full-grid topology can yield strong performance with added stability and efficiency.

title: Diagnosing CFG Interpretation in LLMs
summary: As LLMs are increasingly integrated into agentic systems, they must adhere to dynamically defined, machine-interpretable interfaces. We evaluate LLMs as in-context interpreters: given a novel context-free grammar, can LLMs generate syntactically valid, behaviorally functional, and semantically faithful outputs? We introduce RoboGrid, a framework that disentangles syntax, behavior, and semantics through controlled stress-tests of recursion depth, expression complexity, and surface styles. Our experiments reveal a consistent hierarchical degradation: LLMs often maintain surface syntax but fail to preserve structural semantics. Despite the partial mitigation provided by CoT reasoning, performance collapses under structural density, specifically deep recursion and high branching, with semantic alignment vanishing at extreme depths. Furthermore, "Alien" lexicons reveal that LLMs rely on semantic bootstrapping from keywords rather than pure symbolic induction. These findings pinpoint critical gaps in hierarchical state-tracking required for reliable, grammar-agnostic agents.

AI.tldr: LLMs show surface-level grammar adherence but weak hierarchical semantic tracking when parsing/generating context-free grammar outputs; performance degrades with recursion and branching; semantic alignment collapses at depth; CoT helps mildly; lexical bootstrapping from keywords rather than symbolic induction; RoboGrid reveals gaps in hierarchical state-tracking for grammar-agnostic agents.
AI.motivation: Assess whether LLMs can serve as in-context interpreters for novel, machine-interpretable interfaces by generating syntactically valid, behaviorally functional, and semantically faithful outputs under a context-free grammar.
AI.method: Introduce RoboGrid to separate syntax, behavior, and semantics and apply controlled stress-tests on recursion depth, expression complexity, and surface styles to LLMs performing CFG-based tasks.
AI.result: LLMs consistently retain surface syntax but fail to preserve structural semantics; while Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning offers partial mitigation, performance collapses under high structural density (deep recursion, high branching) and semantic alignment vanishes at extreme depths; 'Alien' lexicons reveal reliance on keyword-based semantic bootstrapping rather than pure symbolic induction.
AI.conclusion: Highlights critical gaps in hierarchical state-tracking necessary for reliable, grammar-agnostic agents and points to limitations of current LLMs in deeply structured, grammar-driven tasks.

title: SignDATA: Data Pipeline for Sign Language Translation
summary: Sign-language datasets are difficult to preprocess consistently because they vary in annotation schema, clip timing, signer framing, and privacy constraints. Existing work usually reports downstream models, while the preprocessing pipeline that converts raw video into training-ready pose or video artifacts remains fragmented, backend-specific, and weakly documented. We present SignDATA, a config-driven preprocessing toolkit that standardizes heterogeneous sign-language corpora into comparable outputs for learning. The system supports two end-to-end recipes: a pose recipe that performs acquisition, manifesting, person localization, clipping, cropping, landmark extraction, normalization, and WebDataset export, and a video recipe that replaces pose extraction with signer-cropped video packaging. SignDATA exposes interchangeable MediaPipe and MMPose backends behind a common interface, typed job schemas, experiment-level overrides, and per-stage checkpointing with config- and manifest-aware hashes. We validate the toolkit through a research-oriented evaluation design centered on backend comparison, preprocessing ablations, and privacy-aware video generation on datasets. Our contribution is a reproducible preprocessing layer for sign-language research that makes extractor choice, normalization policy, and privacy tradeoffs explicit, configurable, and empirically comparable.Code is available at https://github.com/balaboom123/signdata-slt.

AI.tldr: Config-driven preprocessing toolkit SignDATA standardizes heterogeneous sign-language datasets into comparable outputs for learning, addressing fragmentation in sign-language data pipelines.
AI.motivation: Preprocessing pipelines for sign-language data are heterogeneous, poorly documented, and backend-specific, hindering reproducibility and comparability across studies.
AI.method: SignDATA provides two end-to-end recipes: a pose recipe (acquisition, manifesting, localization, clipping, cropping, landmark extraction, normalization, WebDataset export) and a video recipe (signer-cropped video packaging). It exposes interchangeable MediaPipe and MMPose backends behind a common interface, supports typed job schemas, experiment-level overrides, and per-stage checkpointing with config- and manifest-aware hashes.
AI.result: Validated via a research-oriented evaluation design focusing on backend comparison, preprocessing ablations, and privacy-aware video generation across datasets, establishingSignDATA as a reproducible preprocessing layer with explicit extractor choice, normalization policy, and privacy tradeoffs.
AI.conclusion: SignDATA enables configurable, empirically comparable preprocessing for sign-language research, improving reproducibility and transparency in extractor choice, normalization policies, and privacy considerations. Code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Calibrating conditional risk
summary: We introduce and study the problem of calibrating conditional risk, which involves estimating the expected loss of a prediction model conditional on input features. We analyze this problem in both classification and regression settings and show that it is fundamentally equivalent to a standard regression task. For classification settings, we further establish a connection between conditional risk calibration and individual/conditional probability calibration, and develop theoretical insights for the performance metric. This reveals that while conditional risk calibration is related to existing uncertainty quantification problems, it remains a distinct and standalone machine learning problem. Empirically, we validate our theoretical findings and demonstrate the practical implications of conditional risk calibration in the learning to defer (L2D) framework. Our systematic experiments provide both qualitative and quantitative assessments, offering guidance for future research in uncertainty-aware decision-making.

AI.tldr: Calibrating conditional risk is equivalent to standard regression; in classification, it connects to probability calibration but remains a distinct problem; empirical validation in L2D shows practical guidance for uncertainty-aware decision-making.
AI.motivation: There is a need to obtain reliable, input-conditioned risk estimates for decision-making under uncertainty. The work frames conditional risk calibration as a standalone problem, while linking it to existing uncertainty quantification and probability calibration literature, highlighting its theoretical and practical relevance.
AI.method: The authors theoretically reduce conditional risk calibration to standard regression; for classification, they relate conditional risk calibration to individual/conditional probability calibration and derive performance metrics. They validate the theory with systematic experiments and apply the insights within the learning-to-defer (L2D) framework.
AI.result: Conditional risk calibration is fundamentally equivalent to a standard regression task. In classification, it relates to probability calibration but remains a distinct calibration objective. Empirical studies corroborate the theory and demonstrate practical implications in L2D, including qualitative and quantitative assessments that guide uncertainty-aware decision-making.
AI.conclusion: Conditional risk calibration is a distinct and standalone ML problem with meaningful connections to uncertainty quantification; it motivates further research and provides guidance for practical deployment in uncertainty-aware systems.

title: ConeSep: Cone-based Robust Noise-Unlearning Compositional Network for Composed Image Retrieval
summary: The Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) task provides a flexible retrieval paradigm via a reference image and modification text, but it heavily relies on expensive and error-prone triplet annotations. This paper systematically investigates the Noisy Triplet Correspondence (NTC) problem introduced by annotations. We find that NTC noise, particularly ``hard noise'' (i.e., the reference and target images are highly similar but the modification text is incorrect), poses a unique challenge to existing Noise Correspondence Learning (NCL) methods because it breaks the traditional ``small loss hypothesis''. We identify and elucidate three key, yet overlooked, challenges in the NTC task, namely (C1) Modality Suppression, (C2) Negative Anchor Deficiency, and (C3) Unlearning Backlash. To address these challenges, we propose a Cone-based robuSt noisE-unlearning comPositional network (ConeSep). Specifically, we first propose Geometric Fidelity Quantization, theoretically establishing and practically estimating a noise boundary to precisely locate noisy correspondence. Next, we introduce Negative Boundary Learning, which learns a ``diagonal negative combination'' for each query as its explicit semantic opposite-anchor in the embedding space. Finally, we design Boundary-based Targeted Unlearning, which models the noisy correction process as an optimal transport problem, elegantly avoiding Unlearning Backlash. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets (FashionIQ and CIRR) demonstrate that ConeSep significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, which fully demonstrates the effectiveness and robustness of our method.

AI.tldr: ConeSep robustly addresses noisy triplet correspondences in Composed Image Retrieval (NTC), introducing geometric fidelity quantization, negative boundary learning, and boundary-based unlearning to handle hard noise and three core challenges; it achieves state-of-the-art results on FashionIQ and CIRR.
AI.motivation: NTC noise in triplet-based CIR training, especially hard noise where similar reference/target pairs have incorrect modification text, undermines traditional small-loss assumptions and degrades learning.
AI.method: ConeSep consists of three components: (1) Geometric Fidelity Quantization to estimate a noise boundary and localize noisy correspondences; (2) Negative Boundary Learning to model a diagonal negative combination as opposite-anchor in embedding space; (3) Boundary-based Targeted Unlearning to formulate correction as an optimal transport problem to avoid unlearning backlash.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on FashionIQ and CIRR show ConeSep substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating improved robustness to NTC noise.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a novel, effective framework for NTC in CIR, addressing three overlooked challenges and delivering notable performance gains with a principled handling of noisy annotations.

title: Scalable AI Inference: Performance Analysis and Optimization of AI Model Serving
summary: AI research often emphasizes model design and algorithmic performance, while deployment and inference remain comparatively underexplored despite being critical for real-world use. This study addresses that gap by investigating the performance and optimization of a BentoML-based AI inference system for scalable model serving developed in collaboration with graphworks.ai. The evaluation first establishes baseline performance under three realistic workload scenarios. To ensure a fair and reproducible assessment, a pre-trained RoBERTa sentiment analysis model is used throughout the experiments. The system is subjected to traffic patterns following gamma and exponential distributions in order to emulate real-world usage conditions, including steady, bursty, and high-intensity workloads. Key performance metrics, such as latency percentiles and throughput, are collected and analyzed to identify bottlenecks in the inference pipeline. Based on the baseline results, optimization strategies are introduced at multiple levels of the serving stack to improve efficiency and scalability. The optimized system is then reevaluated under the same workload conditions, and the results are compared with the baseline using statistical analysis to quantify the impact of the applied improvements. The findings demonstrate practical strategies for achieving efficient and scalable AI inference with BentoML. The study examines how latency and throughput scale under varying workloads, how optimizations at the runtime, service, and deployment levels affect response time, and how deployment in a single-node K3s cluster influences resilience during disruptions.

AI.tldr: Baseline and optimized BentoML-based AI inference for RoBERTa sentiment model; multi-scenario workload; multi-level optimizations; stats-driven evaluation; single-node K3s deployment resilience.
AI.motivation: Address the deployment and inference gap in AI systems by evaluating practical performance and optimization strategies for scalable model serving.
AI.method: Baseline performance under gamma and exponential traffic for RoBERTa sentiment model using BentoML; measure latency percentiles and throughput; identify bottlenecks; apply optimizations at runtime, service, and deployment levels; reevaluate; statistical analysis; test in single-node K3s cluster.
AI.result: Optimizations improved efficiency and scalability; documented impact on latency and throughput across workloads; resilience aspects under disruptions in K3s deployment.
AI.conclusion: Practical strategies for efficient and scalable AI inference with BentoML; scalability of latency/throughput under varying workloads; deployment-level effects; single-node cluster resilience implications.

title: Object Referring-Guided Scanpath Prediction with Perception-Enhanced Vision-Language Models
summary: Object Referring-guided Scanpath Prediction (ORSP) aims to predict the human attention scanpath when they search for a specific target object in a visual scene according to a linguistic description describing the object. Multimodal information fusion is a key point of ORSP. Therefore, we propose a novel model, ScanVLA, to first exploit a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to extract and fuse inherently aligned visual and linguistic feature representations from the input image and referring expression. Next, to enhance the ScanVLA's perception of fine-grained positional information, we not only propose a novel History Enhanced Scanpath Decoder (HESD) that directly takes historical fixations' position information as input to help predict a more reasonable position for the current fixation, but also adopt a frozen Segmentation LoRA as an auxiliary component to help localize the referred object more precisely, which improves the scanpath prediction task without incurring additional large computational and time costs. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that ScanVLA can significantly outperform existing scanpath prediction methods under object referring.

AI.tldr: Introduces ScanVLA for Object Referring-guided Scanpath Prediction by fusing vision-language features via a Vision-Language Model, enhanced with a History Enhanced Scanpath Decoder and a frozen Segmentation LoRA; achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining efficiency.
AI.motivation: Predict human visual attention scanpaths for targeted objects described by language; address multimodal fusion and fine-grained positional accuracy, while keeping computational efficiency.
AI.method: Use a Vision-Language Model to fuse aligned visual and linguistic features; propose History Enhanced Scanpath Decoder that uses historical fixation positions; incorporate a frozen Segmentation LoRA as an auxiliary module to improve object localization without heavy cost.
AI.result: Empirical results show ScanVLA significantly outperforms existing scanpath prediction methods under object referring, across relevant datasets/metrics.
AI.conclusion: ScanVLA demonstrates effective multimodal fusion for ORSP, benefits from history-aware decoding and lightweight localization, suggesting a strong direction for efficient, accurate scanpath prediction in referential tasks.

title: Unlocking the Forecasting Economy: A Suite of Datasets for the Full Lifecycle of Prediction Market: [Experiments \& Analysis]
summary: Prediction markets are markets for trading claims on future events, such as presidential elections, and their prices provide continuously updated signals of collective beliefs. In decentralized platforms such as Polymarket, the market lifecycle spans market creation, token registration, trading, oracle interaction, dispute, and final settlement, yet the corresponding data are fragmented across heterogeneous off-chain and on-chain sources. We present the first continuously maintained dataset suite for the full lifecycle of decentralized prediction markets, built on Polymarket. To address the challenges of large-scale cross-source integration, incomplete linkage, and continuous synchronization, we build a unified relational data system that integrates three canonical layers: market metadata, fill-level trading records, and oracle-resolution events, through identifier resolution, on-chain recovery, and incremental updates. The resulting dataset spans October 2020 to March 2026 and comprises more than 770 thousand market records, over 943 million fill records, and nearly 2 million oracle events. We describe the data model, collection pipeline, and consistency mechanisms that make the dataset reproducible and extensible, and we demonstrate its utility through descriptive analyses of market activity and two downstream case studies: NBA outcome calibration and CPI expectation reconstruction.

AI.tldr: A continuously maintained, cross-source dataset for the full lifecycle of decentralized prediction markets on Polymarket, integrating market metadata, fill-level trading records, and oracle-resolution events with on-chain/off-chain reconciliation; spans Oct 2020–Mar 2026, enabling reproducible analyses including NBA calibration and CPI expectation reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Overcome fragmentation of data across heterogeneous on-chain/off-chain sources and provide a unified, reproducible dataset for research and applications on decentralized prediction markets.
AI.method: Unified relational data schema with three layers (market metadata, fill-level trades, oracle events); identifier resolution, on-chain recovery, and incremental updates; cross-source integration and consistency mechanisms; pipeline for collection and synchronization.
AI.result: A dataset with >770k markets, >943M fill records, ~2M oracle events; dataset is reproducible and extensible; includes descriptive analyses and two case studies.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a foundational resource for studying decentralized prediction markets; enables robust empirical analysis and benchmarking; potential for broader adoption and extensions across platforms and market types.

title: Mitigating Hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models without Performance Degradation
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) exhibit powerful generative capabilities but frequently produce hallucinations that compromise output reliability. Fine-tuning on annotated data devoid of hallucinations offers the most direct solution, while its high computational cost motivates recent representation-based methods, which focus on mitigating hallucinatory components within hidden representations. Though efficient, we empirically observe that these methods degrade general generation capacity due to incomplete extraction of hallucination components and non-selective parameter updates. To address these limitations, we propose MPD, a dual-stage framework for mitigating hallucinations without performance degradation. Specifically, our MPD relies on two essential factors: (1) semantic-aware component disentanglement to extract pure hallucination components, and (2) interpretable parameter updates that selectively modify parameters most relevant to hallucination. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MPD achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing hallucinations by 23.4\% while maintaining 97.4\% of general generative capability as evaluated on LLaVA-Bench and MME, with no additional computational cost.

AI.tldr: MPD is a dual-stage framework for mitigating hallucinations in large vision-language models (LVLMs) that preserves general generation quality while significantly reducing hallucinations, without extra computational cost.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in LVLMs undermine reliability; fine-tuning on annotated data is effective but computationally expensive; representation-based mitigation can preserve efficiency but often harms general generation. A method that both reduces hallucinations and preserves capability at low cost is highly desirable.
AI.method: MPD comprises two stages: (1) semantic-aware component disentanglement to extract pure hallucination components from model representations, and (2) interpretable parameter updates that selectively modify only those parameters most relevant to hallucination.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art hallucination reduction (23.4%) while preserving 97.4% of general generative capability on LLaVA-Bench and MME, with no additional computational cost.
AI.conclusion: MPD offers an effective, computation-efficient solution to hallucination mitigation in LVLMs by isolating hallucination components and guiding targeted parameter updates, with minimal impact on overall generation quality.

title: The Origin of Edge of Stability
summary: Full-batch gradient descent on neural networks drives the largest Hessian eigenvalue to the threshold $2/η$, where $η$ is the learning rate. This phenomenon, the Edge of Stability, has resisted a unified explanation: existing accounts establish self-regulation near the edge but do not explain why the trajectory is forced toward $2/η$ from arbitrary initialization. We introduce the edge coupling, a functional on consecutive iterate pairs whose coefficient is uniquely fixed by the gradient-descent update. Differencing its criticality condition yields a step recurrence with stability boundary $2/η$, and a second-order expansion yields a loss-change formula whose telescoping sum forces curvature toward $2/η$. The two formulas involve different Hessian averages, but the mean value theorem localizes each to the true Hessian at an interior point of the step segment, yielding exact forcing of the Hessian eigenvalue with no gap. Setting both gradients of the edge coupling to zero classifies fixed points and period-two orbits; near a fixed point, the problem reduces to a function of the half-amplitude alone, which determines which directions support period-two orbits and on which side of the critical learning rate they appear.

AI.tldr: The abstract presents edge coupling as a novel mechanism that exactly explains why full-batch gradient descent drives the largest Hessian eigenvalue to the edge 2/η (Edge of Stability). By differentiating a functional on consecutive iterate pairs and performing a second-order expansion, it yields two Hessian-averaged formulas whose telescoping sums force curvature toward 2/η; fixed points and period-two orbits are analyzed, showing that near fixed points the dynamics reduce to a half-amplitude problem that dictates stability regions.
AI.motivation: Resolve why gradient descent is attracted to the stability threshold 2/η for the largest Hessian eigenvalue under full-batch updates, providing a unified explanation beyond prior self-regulation accounts.
AI.method: Define edge coupling functional on consecutive iterates with a coefficient fixed by the gradient-descent update. Differentiate its criticality condition to obtain a step recurrence with stability boundary 2/η; perform a second-order expansion to derive a loss-change formula. Use the mean value theorem to localize each Hessian average to the true Hessian at an interior point of the step, yielding exact forcing toward 2/η. Solve for fixed points and period-two orbits by setting both gradients to zero; analyze near fixed points where the problem reduces to half-amplitude dynamics to identify directions supporting period-two behavior and their relation to the critical learning rate.
AI.result: Two Hessian-average expressions telescope to produce an exact forcing of curvature toward 2/η with no gap. The framework classifies fixed points and 2-cycle orbits; near a fixed point the dynamics depend on a half-amplitude parameter that determines the permissible directions for 2-cycle behavior and where they occur relative to the critical learning rate.
AI.conclusion: Edge coupling provides a precise, unified mechanism for Edge of Stability under full-batch gradient descent, explaining the universal convergence toward the threshold from arbitrary initialization. It also delineates the local dynamics near fixed points, including period-two orbit structure, and offers a concrete method to analyze Hessian-driven curvature control in optimization trajectories.

title: LaplacianFormer:Rethinking Linear Attention with Laplacian Kernel
summary: The quadratic complexity of softmax attention presents a major obstacle for scaling Transformers to high-resolution vision tasks. Existing linear attention variants often replace the softmax with Gaussian kernels to reduce complexity, but such approximations lack theoretical grounding and tend to oversuppress mid-range token interactions. We propose LaplacianFormer, a Transformer variant that employs a Laplacian kernel as a principled alternative to softmax, motivated by empirical observations and theoretical analysis. To address expressiveness degradation under low-rank approximations, we introduce a provably injective feature map that retains fine-grained token information. For efficient computation, we adopt a Nyström approximation of the kernel matrix and solve the resulting system using Newton--Schulz iteration, avoiding costly matrix inversion and SVD. We further develop custom CUDA implementations for both the kernel and solver, enabling high-throughput forward and backward passes suitable for edge deployment. Experiments on ImageNet show that LaplacianFormer achieves strong performance-efficiency trade-offs while improving attention expressiveness.

AI.tldr: LaplacianFormer replaces softmax with a Laplacian kernel for scalable, expressive attention in vision transformers, using a provably injective feature map and Nyström+Newton–Schulz solver, with CUDA acceleration; strong ImageNet results.
AI.motivation: Quadratic complexity of softmax-based attention hinders scaling to high-resolution vision tasks. Linear-attention variants using Gaussian kernels lack solid theoretical grounding and tend to suppress mid-range token interactions, risking reduced expressiveness.
AI.method: Adopt a Laplacian kernel as the attention mechanism; introduce a provably injective feature map to preserve fine-grained token information under low-rank approximations; apply Nyström approximation to the kernel matrix and solve the resulting system with Newton–Schulz iteration to avoid matrix inversion/SVD; implement custom CUDA kernels for the kernel evaluation and the solver for efficient forward/backward passes (edge deployment).
AI.result: ImageNet experiments show LaplacianFormer achieves favorable performance–efficiency trade-offs and improved attention expressiveness compared with baselines, demonstrating practical viability for high-resolution vision tasks.
AI.conclusion: LaplacianFormer provides a principled, scalable alternative to softmax attention for Vision Transformers, combining an expressive Laplacian-kernel attention with injective feature mappings and efficient kernel-solving techniques, underpinned by hardware-optimized implementations for end-to-end efficiency.

title: Surrogate Functionals for Machine-Learned Orbital-Free Density Functional Theory
summary: We introduce surrogate functionals: machine-learned energy functionals for orbital-free density functional theory (OF-DFT) which are defined not by universal fidelity to a physical reference, but merely by the requirement that density optimization with a fixed procedure yields the true ground-state density. Helpfully, training surrogate functionals requires only ground-state densities, no energies or gradients away from the ground state. We here propose a gradient-descent-improvement loss that guarantees exponential convergence of the density to the ground state, and combine it with an adaptive sampling scheme that concentrates learning around the optimization trajectories actually visited during inference. On the QM9 and QMugs benchmarks, surrogate functionals achieve density errors competitive with or improving upon the state of the art for fully supervised machine-learned OF-DFT, while eliminating the need for the $O(N^3)$ orthononormalization step required by prior work, yielding improved runtime scaling for larger systems.

AI.tldr: Surrogate functionals for OF-DFT: train to yield correct ground-state density under a fixed optimization procedure; use gradient-descent-improvement loss and adaptive sampling around inference trajectories; achieve competitive density accuracy while removing expensive orthonormalization, improving scalability on QM9/QMugs benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on universal energy-density fidelity for OF-DFT while achieving accurate ground-state densities with scalable training and inference, addressing computational bottlenecks (notably O(N^3) orthonormalization).
AI.method: Introduce surrogate (machine-learned) energy functionals defined by the requirement that density optimization with a fixed procedure recovers the ground-state density. Train using only ground-state densities (no energies or gradients off the ground state). Develop a gradient-descent-improvement loss guaranteeing exponential convergence of density to the ground state, and an adaptive sampling scheme focusing on optimization trajectories encountered during inference. Evaluate on QM9 and QMugs, comparing density errors to state-of-the-art fully supervised ML OF-DFT.
AI.result: Density errors competitive with or better than state-of-the-art fully supervised ML OF-DFT. Elimination of the O(N^3) orthonormalization step leads to improved runtime scaling for larger systems.
AI.conclusion: Surrogate functionals provide accurate ground-state densities with improved scalability for OF-DFT by learning functionals anchored to the density optimization process rather than universal energy fidelity, enabling efficient inference without expensive orthonormalization.

title: Self-supervised pretraining for an iterative image size agnostic vision transformer
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) dominate self-supervised learning (SSL). While they have proven highly effective for large-scale pretraining, they are computationally inefficient and scale poorly with image size. Consequently, foundational models like DINO are constrained to low-resolution processing. A recent foveal-inspired transformer achieves resolution agnosticism by iteratively processing a fixed-size context of multi-zoom patches. This model demonstrated promising results via supervised learning, utilizing a sequential, recurrent-like process without backpropagation through time. To unlock its potential as a foundational backbone, we introduce a novel sequential-to-global SSL framework based on DINO's self-distillation objective. Supported by an efficient integral-image patch extraction method, our approach enables large-scale pretraining for image-size agnostic vision encoders. We achieve competitive performance on ImageNet-1K and downstream classification tasks, maintaining a constant computational budget regardless of input resolution.

AI.tldr: A sequential-to-global self-supervised learning framework for image-size agnostic vision encoders, built on DINO, enabling constant compute across input resolutions with competitive ImageNet performance, via efficient integral-image patch extraction.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers dominate SSL but are computationally heavy and scale poorly with image size, limiting backbones to low resolution. A foveal-inspired, resolution-agnostic transformer showed promise but needed SSL to unlock foundation-model potential. This work aims to enable large-scale SSL pretraining for resolution-agnostic encoders.
AI.method: Introduce a sequential-to-global SSL framework based on DINO's self-distillation objective. Employ a foveal-inspired transformer that processes a fixed-size context of multi-zoom patches, using an efficient integral-image patch extraction method. The training unfolds sequentially without backpropagation through time, yielding a backbone whose compute is invariant to input resolution.
AI.result: The approach delivers competitive performance on ImageNet-1K and downstream tasks while maintaining a constant computational budget regardless of input resolution.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that image-size agnostic vision encoders can be effectively pretrained with SSL using a sequential-to-global framework, paving the way for scalable, foundation-model–style backbones with fixed computational cost across resolutions.

title: Efficient Test-Time Inference via Deterministic Exploration of Truncated Decoding Trees
summary: Self-consistency boosts inference-time performance by sampling multiple reasoning traces in parallel and voting. However, in constrained domains like math and code, this strategy is compute-inefficient because it samples with replacement, repeatedly revisiting the same high-probability prefixes and duplicate completions. We propose Distinct Leaf Enumeration (DLE), a deterministic decoding method that treats truncated sampling as traversal of a pruned decoding tree and systematically enumerates distinct leaves instead of sampling with replacement. This strategy improves inference efficiency in two ways. Algorithmically, it increases coverage of the truncated search space under a fixed budget by exploring previously unvisited high-probability branches. Systemically, it reuses shared prefixes and reduces redundant token generation. Empirically, DLE explores higher-quality reasoning traces than stochastic self-consistency, yielding better performance on math, coding, and general reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Distinct Leaf Enumeration (DLE) is a deterministic decoding method that prunes the decoding tree and exhaustively enumerates distinct leaves to boost inference efficiency and reasoning quality over replacement sampling in self-consistency, particularly for math and code tasks.
AI.motivation: Self-consistency boosts inference by sampling multiple reasoning traces, but in constrained domains like math and code this is compute-inefficient due to sampling with replacement that revisits the same high-prob prefixes and duplicates completions. A method that increases coverage under a fixed budget and reduces redundancy is desired.
AI.method: DLE treats truncated sampling as traversal of a pruned decoding tree and deterministically enumerates distinct leaves (completions) rather than sampling with replacement. It leverages shared prefixes to reuse tokens and expands previously unvisited high-probability branches, increasing coverage within a fixed budget and reducing redundant generation.
AI.result: Empirically, DLE explores higher-quality reasoning traces than stochastic self-consistency, yielding better performance on math, coding, and general reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: DLE provides a computationally efficient, deterministic alternative to sampling-based self-consistency by systematically enumerating distinct leaves, improving coverage, reuse of prefixes, and overall task performance in constrained domains.

title: MLG-Stereo: ViT Based Stereo Matching with Multi-Stage Local-Global Enhancement
summary: With the development of deep learning, ViT-based stereo matching methods have made significant progress due to their remarkable robustness and zero-shot ability. However, due to the limitations of ViTs in handling resolution sensitivity and their relative neglect of local information, the ability of ViT-based methods to predict details and handle arbitrary-resolution images is still weaker than that of CNN-based methods. To address these shortcomings, we propose MLG-Stereo, a systematic pipeline-level design that extends global modeling beyond the encoder stage. First, we propose a Multi-Granularity Feature Network to effectively balance global context and local geometric information, enabling comprehensive feature extraction from images of arbitrary resolution and bridging the gap between training and inference scales. Then, a Local-Global Cost Volume is constructed to capture both locally-correlated and global-aware matching information. Finally, a Local-Global Guided Recurrent Unit is introduced to iteratively optimize the disparity locally under the guidance of global information. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple benchmark datasets, demonstrating that our MLG-Stereo exhibits highly competitive performance on the Middlebury and KITTI-2015 benchmarks compared to contemporaneous leading methods, and achieves outstanding results in the KITTI-2012 dataset.

AI.tldr: MLG-Stereo is a ViT-based stereo method that extends global modeling beyond the encoder via a pipeline-level design to better handle arbitrary resolutions and preserve local detail, achieving competitive results on Middlebury and KITTI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: ViT-based stereo methods struggle with resolution sensitivity and local detail due to encoder-centric global modeling; there is a need to balance global context with local geometric cues and enable accurate disparity prediction across arbitrary image sizes.
AI.method: Introduce a pipeline with (1) Multi-Granularity Feature Network to blend global and local features across scales; (2) Local-Global Cost Volume that fuses locally correlated and globally aware matching information; (3) Local-Global Guided Recurrent Unit that iteratively refines disparity locally under global guidance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show competitive performance on Middlebury and KITTI-2015, and outstanding results on KITTI-2012, indicating the approach effectively leverages global-local modeling to improve stereo matching across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Extending ViT-based stereo methods with a systematic, pipeline-level design that combines multi-granularity feature extraction, a hybrid local-global cost volume, and guided refinement yields strong, resolution-robust performance and improved detail recovery across benchmarks.

title: Explicit Dropout: Deterministic Regularization for Transformer Architectures
summary: Dropout is a widely used regularization technique in deep learning, but its effects are typically realized through stochastic masking rather than explicit optimization objectives. We propose a deterministic formulation that expresses dropout as an additive regularizer directly incorporated into the training loss. The framework derives explicit regularization terms for Transformer architectures, covering attention query, key, value, and feed-forward components with independently controllable strengths. This formulation removes reliance on stochastic perturbations while providing clearer and fine-grained control over regularization strength. Experiments across image classification, temporal action detection, and audio classification show that explicit dropout matches or outperforms conventional implicit methods, with consistent gains when applied to attention and feed-forward network layers. Ablation studies demonstrate stable performance and controllable regularization through regularization coefficients and dropout rates. Overall, explicit dropout offers a practical and interpretable alternative to stochastic regularization while maintaining architectural flexibility across diverse tasks.

AI.tldr: Deterministic dropout is formulated as an additive regularizer integrated into the training loss for Transformer models, enabling independent, explicit control over regularization strength for attention (Q, K, V) and feed-forward components, and achieving comparable or superior performance to stochastic dropout across diverse tasks with stable ablations.
AI.motivation: Stochastic dropout masks introduce randomness and implicit regularization; an explicit, optimization-based objective could offer clearer control, interpretability, and potentially better performance.
AI.method: Derive explicit regularization terms that map to Transformer subcomponents: attention query (Q), key (K), value (V), and feed-forward network (FFN). Each component receives an independently tunable regularization coefficient. These terms are added to the standard training loss, removing the need for stochastic perturbations during training.
AI.result: Empirical results across image classification, temporal action detection, and audio classification show that the deterministic (explicit) dropout matches or outperforms traditional implicit dropout, with consistent gains when applied to attention and FFN layers. Ablation studies indicate stable training and controllable regularization via coefficients and dropout rates.
AI.conclusion: Explicit dropout provides a practical, interpretable alternative to stochastic regularization, preserving architectural flexibility while enhancing controllability across tasks and architectures.

title: Fast-then-Fine: A Two-Stage Framework with Multi-Granular Representation for Cross-Modal Retrieval in Remote Sensing
summary: Remote sensing (RS) image-text retrieval plays a critical role in understanding massive RS imagery. However, the dense multi-object distribution and complex backgrounds in RS imagery make it difficult to simultaneously achieve fine-grained cross-modal alignment and efficient retrieval. Existing methods either rely on complex cross-modal interactions that lead to low retrieval efficiency, or depend on large-scale vision-language model pre-training, which requires massive data and computational resources. To address these issues, we propose a fast-then-fine (FTF) two-stage retrieval framework that decomposes retrieval into a text-agnostic recall stage for efficient candidate selection and a text-guided rerank stage for fine-grained alignment. Specifically, in the recall stage, text-agnostic coarse-grained representations are employed for efficient candidate selection; in the rerank stage, a parameter-free balanced text-guided interaction block enhances fine-grained alignment without introducing additional learnable parameters. Furthermore, an inter- and intra-modal loss is designed to jointly optimize cross-modal alignment across multi-granular representations. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate that the FTF achieves competitive retrieval accuracy while significantly improving retrieval efficiency compared with existing methods.

AI.tldr: Two-stage RS image-text retrieval (FTF) with fast recall and text-guided rerank, using a parameter-free interaction block and multi-granular cross-modal loss to achieve efficiency without heavy pretraining.
AI.motivation: Dense object distributions and complex backgrounds in RS images hinder fine-grained cross-modal alignment and efficient retrieval; existing methods rely on complex cross-modal interactions or large VL pretraining.
AI.method: Stage 1 recall: text-agnostic coarse-grained representations for efficient candidate retrieval. Stage 2 rerank: a parameter-free balanced text-guided interaction block for fine-grained alignment; joint inter- and intra-modal loss to optimize across multi-granular representations.
AI.result: Experiments on public benchmarks show competitive accuracy with significantly improved retrieval efficiency compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: FTF provides an efficient, scalable approach to RS image-text retrieval without heavy reliance on large-scale pretraining, achieving favorable trade-offs between speed and accuracy.

title: Amortized Vine Copulas for High-Dimensional Density and Information Estimation
summary: Modeling high-dimensional dependencies while keeping likelihoods tractable remains challenging. Classical vine-copula pipelines are interpretable but can be expensive, while many neural estimators are flexible but less structured. In this work, we propose Vine Denoising Copula (VDC), an amortized vine-copula pipeline that trains a single bivariate denoising model and reuses it across all vine edges. For each edge, given pseudo-observations, the model predicts a density grid. We then apply an IPFP/Sinkhorn projection that enforces non-negativity, unit mass, and uniform marginals. This keeps the exact vine likelihood and preserves the usual copula interpretation while replacing repeated per-edge optimization with GPU inference. Across synthetic and real-data benchmarks, VDC delivers strong bivariate density accuracy, competitive MI/TC estimation, and substantial speedups for high-dimensional vine fitting. In practice, these gains make explicit information estimation and dependence decomposition feasible at scales where repeated vine fitting would otherwise be costly, although conditional downstream inference remains mixed.

AI.tldr: Amortized vine-copula using a single bivariate denoising model with IPFP/Sinkhorn projections to enforce copula constraints, enabling scalable exact vine likelihood and competitive dependence estimation.
AI.motivation: To address the computational burden of high-dimensional vine-copula fitting while preserving interpretability and tractable likelihood; classical vine pipelines are expensive; neural estimators are flexible but less structured.
AI.method: Train one bivariate denoising network reused across all vine edges; for each edge, given pseudo-observations, predict a density grid; apply IPFP/Sinkhorn projection to enforce non-negativity, unit mass, and uniform marginals; preserves exact vine likelihood and copula interpretation; accelerates per-edge computation via GPU inference.
AI.result: In synthetic and real data: strong bivariate density accuracy; competitive MI/TC estimation; substantial speedups in high-dimensional vine fitting; enables explicit information estimation and dependence decomposition at scale; conditional downstream inference remains mixed.
AI.conclusion: VDC achieves scalable, interpretable, and tractable vine-copula modeling with GPU-accelerated amortization; effective for large-scale dependence modeling, though some downstream tasks require further refinement.

title: CCTVBench: Contrastive Consistency Traffic VideoQA Benchmark for Multimodal LLMs
summary: Safety-critical traffic reasoning requires contrastive consistency: models must detect true hazards when an accident occurs, and reliably reject plausible-but-false hypotheses under near-identical counterfactual scenes. We present CCTVBench, a Contrastive Consistency Traffic VideoQA Benchmark built on paired real accident videos and world-model-generated counterfactual counterparts, together with minimally different, mutually exclusive hypothesis questions. CCTVBench enforces a single structured decision pattern over each video question quadruple and provides actionable diagnostics that decompose failures into positive omission, positive swap, negative hallucination, and mutual-exclusivity violation, while separating video versus question consistency. Experiments across open-source and proprietary video LLMs reveal a large and persistent gap between standard per-instance QA metrics and quadruple-level contrastive consistency, with unreliable none-of-the-above rejection as a key bottleneck. Finally, we introduce C-TCD, a contrastive decoding approach leveraging a semantically exclusive counterpart video as the contrast input at inference time, improving both instance-level QA and contrastive consistency.

AI.tldr: CCTVBench introduces a contrastive-consistency benchmark for traffic video QA using real accident videos paired with counterfactuals, plus diagnostic breakdown of errors and a contrastive decoding method (C-TCD) to improve both QA performance and consistency. A key finding is a gap between standard QA metrics and quadruple-level consistency, with none-of-the-above rejection as a bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical traffic reasoning requires robust contrastive consistency: models must detect true hazards in accidents and reject plausible-but-false hypotheses in near-identical counterfactuals. Existing QA metrics fail to capture quadruple-level consistency and diagnostic failure modes.
AI.method: Construct CCTVBench from real accident videos and world-model-generated counterfactuals; formulate minimally different, mutually exclusive hypothesis questions; enforce a single structured decision pattern per video-question quadruple; provide diagnostics that decompose failures into positive omission, positive swap, negative hallucination, and mutual-exclusivity violation; separate video vs. question consistency. Evaluate open-source and proprietary video LLMs. Propose C-TCD (contrastive decoding) that uses a semantically exclusive counterpart video as contrast input at inference to boost QA and consistency.
AI.result: Findings show a large, persistent gap between per-instance QA metrics and quadruple-level contrastive consistency; unreliable none-of-the-above rejection is a key bottleneck. C-TCD improves both instance-level QA and contrastive consistency across models.
AI.conclusion: CCTVBench provides a structured, diagnostic, contrastive evaluation framework for safety-critical video QA and demonstrates that contrastive decoding (C-TCD) can substantially enhance reliability and consistency. The work highlights the need to move beyond per-instance QA scores toward quadruple-level, contrastive evaluation in real-world driving scenarios.

title: A Hierarchical MARL-Based Approach for Coordinated Retail P2P Trading and Wholesale Market Participation of DERs
summary: The ongoing shift towards decentralization of the electric energy sector, driven by the growing electrification across end-use sectors, and widespread adoption of distributed energy resources (DERs), necessitates their active participation in the electricity markets to support grid operations. Furthermore, with bi-directional energy and communication flows becoming standard, intelligent, easy-to-deploy, resource-conservative demand-side participation is expected to play a critical role in securing power grid operational flexibility and market efficiency. This work proposes a market engagement framework that leverages a hierarchical multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MARL) approach to enable individual prosumers to participate in peer-to-peer retail auctions and further aggregate these intelligent prosumers to facilitate effective DER participation in wholesale markets. Ultimately, a Stackelberg game is proposed to coordinate this hierarchical MARL-based DER market participation framework toward enhanced market performance.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical MARL-based market engagement framework enabling prosumers to participate in P2P retail auctions and DER participation in wholesale markets, coordinated by a Stackelberg game to improve market performance.
AI.motivation: Rising decentralization and electrification drive the need for scalable, flexible, and energy-efficient participation of distributed energy resources in electricity markets within bi-directional energy and information flows.
AI.method: Propose a hierarchical multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework where individual prosumers engage in peer-to-peer retail auctions and are aggregated to support DER participation in wholesale markets; introduce a Stackelberg game to coordinate the hierarchical MARL-based market participation.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with theoretical rationale and potential performance benefits; no empirical results reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical MARL with Stackelberg coordination can enhance market performance and enable scalable, decentralized DER participation in both retail and wholesale electricity markets.

title: DynamicRad: Content-Adaptive Sparse Attention for Long Video Diffusion
summary: Leveraging the natural spatiotemporal energy decay in video diffusion offers a path to efficiency, yet relying solely on rigid static masks risks losing critical long-range information in complex dynamics. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{DynamicRad}, a unified sparse-attention paradigm that grounds adaptive selection within a radial locality prior. DynamicRad introduces a \textbf{dual-mode} strategy: \textit{static-ratio} for speed-optimized execution and \textit{dynamic-threshold} for quality-first filtering. To ensure robustness without online search overhead, we integrate an offline Bayesian Optimization (BO) pipeline coupled with a \textbf{semantic motion router}. This lightweight projection module maps prompt embeddings to optimal sparsity regimes with \textbf{minimal runtime overhead}. Unlike online profiling methods, our offline BO optimizes attention reconstruction error (MSE) on a physics-based proxy task, ensuring rapid convergence. Experiments on HunyuanVideo and Wan2.1-14B demonstrate that DynamicRad pushes the efficiency--quality Pareto frontier, achieving \textbf{1.7$\times$--2.5$\times$ inference speedups} with \textbf{over 80\% effective sparsity}. In some long-sequence settings, the dynamic mode even matches or exceeds the dense baseline, while mask-aware LoRA further improves long-horizon coherence. Code is available at https://github.com/Adamlong3/DynamicRad.

AI.tldr: DynamicRad proposes a dual-mode, radially-local sparse-attention framework with offline Bayesian optimization and a semantic motion router to adapt sparsity for video diffusion. It achieves 1.7–2.5x speedups with ~80% effective sparsity, sometimes matching or surpassing dense baselines on long sequences, and improves long-horizon coherence with mask-aware LoRA.
AI.motivation: To overcome the efficiency bottleneck of video diffusion caused by long-range dependencies, which static masks degrade, leading to loss of critical information in complex dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce DynamicRad with a radial locality prior and a dual-mode strategy: (1) static-ratio for speed-optimized execution, (2) dynamic-threshold for quality-first filtering. Use offline Bayesian Optimization to learn sparsity regimes via a lightweight semantic motion router mapping prompt embeddings to sparsity settings, minimizing online overhead. Evaluate with physics-based proxy task for BO; integrate mask-aware LoRA for long-horizon coherence.
AI.result: Demonstrates 1.7×–2.5× inference speedups with >80% effective sparsity on HunyuanVideo and Wan2.1-14B. In long sequences, dynamic mode matches or exceeds the dense baseline, with additional gains when using mask-aware LoRA.
AI.conclusion: DynamicRad expands the efficiency–quality Pareto frontier for video diffusion by combining adaptive radial sparse attention with offline optimization and a lightweight semantic routing module, enabling robust, fast inference with high sparsity. Code is released.

title: Differentially Private Clustered Federated Learning with Privacy-Preserving Initialization and Normality-Driven Aggregation
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables training of a global model while keeping raw data on end-devices. Despite this, FL has shown to leak private user information and thus in practice, it is often coupled with methods such as differential privacy (DP) and secure vector sum to provide formal privacy guarantees to its participants. In realistic cross-device deployments, the data are highly heterogeneous, so vanilla federated learning converges slowly and generalizes poorly. Clustered federated learning (CFL) mitigates this by segregating users into clusters, leading to lower intra-cluster data heterogeneity. Nevertheless, coupling CFL with DP remains challenging: the injected DP noise makes individual client updates excessively noisy, and the server is unable to initialize cluster centroids with the less noisy aggregated updates. To address this challenge, we propose PINA, a two-stage framework that first lets each client fine-tune a lightweight low-rank adaptation (LoRA) adapter and privately share a compressed sketch of the update. The server leverages these sketches to construct robust cluster centroids. In the second stage, PINA introduces a normality-driven aggregation mechanism that improves convergence and robustness. Our method retains the benefits of clustered FL while providing formal privacy guarantees against an untrusted server. Extensive evaluations show that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art DP-FL algorithms by an average of 2.9% in accuracy for privacy budgets (epsilon in {2, 8}).

AI.tldr: Two-stage private clustered FL method (PINA) uses LoRA adapters and compressed sketches to form robust cluster centroids, followed by normality-driven aggregation, achieving higher accuracy than DP-FL under privacy budgets epsilon ∈ {2,8}.
AI.motivation: Cross-device FL suffers from data heterogeneity leading to slow convergence and poor generalization; DP and secure aggregation degrade updates; CFL helps, but coupling with DP is challenging due to noise and centroid initialization.
AI.method: Stage 1: Clients fine-tune lightweight LoRA adapters and privately share compressed sketches of their updates; the server uses these sketches to construct robust cluster centroids. Stage 2: A normality-driven aggregation mechanism is applied to improve convergence and robustness. The design provides formal privacy guarantees against an untrusted server.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show PINA outperforms state-of-the-art DP-FL algorithms by an average of 2.9 percentage points in accuracy for privacy budgets epsilon in {2,8}.
AI.conclusion: PINA preserves the advantages of clustered FL while offering formal privacy guarantees against a curious server and achieves improved convergence and robustness compared to existing DP-FL approaches.

title: Video-ToC: Video Tree-of-Cue Reasoning
summary: Existing Video Large Language Models (Video LLMs) struggle with complex video understanding, exhibiting limited reasoning capabilities and potential hallucinations. In particular, these methods tend to perform reasoning solely relying on the pretrained inherent reasoning rationales whilst lacking perception-aware adaptation to the input video content. To address this, we propose \textbf{Video-ToC}, a novel video reasoning framework that enhances video understanding through tree-of-cue reasoning. Specifically, our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) A tree-guided visual cue localization mechanism, which endows the model with enhanced fine-grained perceptual capabilities through structured reasoning patterns; (2) A reasoning-demand reward mechanism, which dynamically adjusts the reward value for reinforcement learning (RL) based on the estimation of reasoning demands, enabling on-demand incentives for more effective reasoning strategies; and (3) An automated annotation pipeline that constructs the Video-ToC-SFT-1k and Video-ToC-RL-2k datasets for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and RL training, respectively. Extensive evaluations on six video understanding benchmarks and a video hallucination benchmark demonstrate the superiority of Video-ToC over baselines and recent methods. Code is available at https://github.com/qizhongtan/Video-ToC.

AI.tldr: Video-ToC introduces tree-of-cue reasoning for video LLMs, combining tree-guided cue localization, a reasoning-demand RL reward, and automated annotation pipelines to create Video-ToC-SFT-1k and Video-ToC-RL-2k. It shows superiority on six video benchmarks and a video hallucination benchmark; code released.
AI.motivation: Existing Video LLMs struggle with complex video understanding and hallucinations due to reliance on pretrained reasoning rationales and lack of perception-aware adaptation to input content.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) tree-guided visual cue localization for fine-grained perceptual reasoning; (2) reasoning-demand reward mechanism that dynamically adjusts RL rewards based on estimated reasoning demands; (3) automated annotation pipeline generating Video-ToC-SFT-1k and Video-ToC-RL-2k datasets for supervised fine-tuning and RL training.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on six video understanding benchmarks and a video hallucination benchmark demonstrate superiority of Video-ToC over baselines and recent methods.
AI.conclusion: Video-ToC demonstrates that tree-of-cue reasoning with perception-aware cues and adaptive RL rewards can enhance video understanding and reduce hallucinations; the accompanying datasets and code facilitate reproducibility and further research.

title: Too Sharp, Too Sure: When Calibration Follows Curvature
summary: Modern neural networks can achieve high accuracy while remaining poorly calibrated, producing confidence estimates that do not match empirical correctness. Yet calibration is often treated as a post-hoc attribute. We take a different perspective: we study calibration as a training-time phenomenon on small vision tasks, and ask whether calibrated solutions can be obtained reliably by intervening on the training procedure. We identify a tight coupling between calibration, curvature, and margins during training of deep networks under multiple gradient-based methods. Empirically, Expected Calibration Error (ECE) closely tracks curvature-based sharpness throughout optimization. Mathematically, we show that both ECE and Gauss--Newton curvature are controlled, up to problem-specific constants, by the same margin-dependent exponential tail functional along the trajectory. Guided by this mechanism, we introduce a margin-aware training objective that explicitly targets robust-margin tails and local smoothness, yielding improved out-of-sample calibration across optimizers without sacrificing accuracy.

AI.tldr: A training-time calibration framework that links calibration to optimization geometry (curvature and margins). It shows ECE tracks curvature and that both are governed by a margin-tail functional along the trajectory; introduces a margin-aware loss to improve calibration without sacrificing accuracy, validated on small vision tasks across optimizers.
AI.motivation: Calibration is often addressed post-hoc; this work investigates whether calibrated solutions can be reliably achieved by training-time interventions, focusing on small vision tasks to understand the dynamics.
AI.method: Demonstrates a tight coupling between calibration, curvature, and margins during training. Proves that ECE and Gauss-Newton curvature are controlled by the same margin-dependent exponential tail functional along the optimization trajectory. Proposes a margin-aware training objective targeting robust-margin tails and local smoothness.
AI.result: A margin-aware objective yields improved out-of-sample calibration across optimizers without reducing accuracy on small vision tasks.
AI.conclusion: Supports the feasibility of training-time calibration via a margin-based mechanism that ties calibration to optimization dynamics (curvature and margins), with empirical and theoretical backing on small vision problems.

title: Random Walk on Point Clouds for Feature Detection
comment: 20 pages, 11 figures. Published in Information Sciences
summary: The points on the point clouds that can entirely outline the shape of the model are of critical importance, as they serve as the foundation for numerous point cloud processing tasks and are widely utilized in computer graphics and computer-aided design. This study introduces a novel method, RWoDSN, for extracting such feature points, incorporating considerations of sharp-to-smooth transitions, large-to-small scales, and textural-to-detailed features. We approach feature extraction as a two-stage context-dependent analysis problem. In the first stage, we propose a novel neighborhood descriptor, termed the Disk Sampling Neighborhood (DSN), which, unlike traditional spatially and geometrically invariant approaches, preserves a matrix structure while maintaining normal neighborhood relationships. In the second stage, a random walk is performed on the DSN (RWoDSN), yielding a graph-based DSN that simultaneously accounts for the spatial distribution, topological properties, and geometric characteristics of the local surface surrounding each point. This enables the effective extraction of feature points. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed RWoDSN method achieves a recall of 0.769-22% higher than the current state-of-the-art-alongside a precision of 0.784. Furthermore, it significantly outperforms several traditional and deep-learning techniques across eight evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: Two-stage RWoDSN method for point-cloud feature-point extraction using Disk Sampling Neighborhood (DSN) and a random walk on DSN, achieving higher recall and precision than state-of-the-art across eight metrics.
AI.motivation: There is a need for robust, topology-aware feature points in point clouds that can faithfully outline shapes. Traditional invariant descriptors may lose local neighborhood/topological information and fail to capture multi-scale and textural-to-detailed features.
AI.method: Stage 1 introduces Disk Sampling Neighborhood (DSN), preserving matrix structure while maintaining normal neighborhood relations. Stage 2 performs a random walk on the DSN (RWoDSN) to form a graph-based DSN that encodes spatial distribution, topology, and geometric properties around each point, enabling effective feature-point extraction. The approach targets sharp-to-smooth transitions, large-to-small scales, and textural-to-detailed features across two context-dependent stages.
AI.result: Recall of approximately 0.769 with reportedly up to 22% improvement over state-of-the-art; precision of 0.784. The method outperforms several traditional and deep-learning techniques across eight evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage, context-dependent analysis (DSN descriptor followed by random-walk on DSN) effectively extracts feature points in point clouds by preserving neighborhood structure and capturing multi-scale, topological, and geometric characteristics, achieving strong performance across multiple metrics.

title: ProMMSearchAgent: A Generalizable Multimodal Search Agent Trained with Process-Oriented Rewards
summary:
Training multimodal agents via reinforcement learning for knowledge-intensive visual reasoning is fundamentally hindered by the extreme sparsity of outcome-based supervision and the unpredictability of live web environments. To resolve these algorithmic and environmental bottlenecks, we introduce ProMMSearchAgent, establishing a novel Sim-to-Real training paradigm for multimodal search.
  We decouple policy learning into a deterministic, local static sandbox. Crucially, to learn effectively within this constrained environment, we propose an introspective process-oriented reward. By probing the agent's own parametric knowledge boundaries, we generate dense behavioral metadata that explicitly rewards the correct cognitive decision, initiating a multimodal or text search only when visually or factually uncertain. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our locally-trained policy transfers zero-shot to the live Google Search API. ProMMSearchAgent achieves new SOTA performance, outperforming MMSearch-R1 by +5.1% on FVQA-test, +6.3% on InfoSeek, and +11.3% on MMSearch.

AI.tldr: ProMMSearchAgent uses a Sim-to-Real training loop with a local sandbox and introspective reward to train a multimodal search agent; shows zero-shot transfer to live Google Search API and achieves SOTA on FVQA-test, InfoSeek, MMSearch.
AI.motivation: Overcome extreme sparsity of outcome-based supervision and non-stationary live web environments in knowledge-intensive visual reasoning; enable reliable RL training by isolating policy learning and providing dense feedback.
AI.method: Decouple policy learning into a deterministic local sandbox; introduce an introspective process-oriented reward that probes the agent's knowledge boundaries to generate dense behavioral metadata; reward correct cognitive decisions and only perform multimodal or textual search when uncertainty is detected; demonstrates zero-shot transfer to live Google Search API.
AI.result: Zero-shot transfer to live Google Search API; SOTA gains over MMSearch-R1: +5.1% on FVQA-test, +6.3% on InfoSeek, +11.3% on MMSearch.
AI.conclusion: A sim-to-real, introspective reward framework enables effective learning of multimodal search agents with strong real-world generalization and state-of-the-art performance on established benchmarks.

title: GRPO-VPS: Enhancing Group Relative Policy Optimization with Verifiable Process Supervision for Effective Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) by leveraging direct outcome verification instead of learned reward models. Building on this paradigm, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) eliminates the need for critic models but suffers from indiscriminate credit assignment for intermediate steps, which limits its ability to identify effective reasoning strategies and incurs overthinking. In this work, we introduce a model-free and verifiable process supervision via probing the model's belief in the correct answer throughout its reasoning trajectory. By segmenting the generation into discrete steps and tracking the conditional probability of the correct answer appended at each segment boundary, we efficiently compute interpretable segment-wise progress measurements to refine GRPO's trajectory-level feedback. This approach enables more targeted and sample-efficient policy updates, while avoiding the need for intermediate supervision derived from costly Monte Carlo rollouts or auxiliary models. Experiments on mathematical and general-domain benchmarks show consistent gains over GRPO across diverse models: up to 2.6-point accuracy improvements and 13.7% reasoning-length reductions on math tasks, and up to 2.4 points and 4% on general-domain tasks, demonstrating strong generalization.

AI.tldr: A model-free verifiable supervision method for GRPO that segments reasoning and tracks the model's belief in the correct answer to produce interpretable progress signals, improving accuracy and reducing reasoning length on math and general-domain tasks.
AI.motivation: GRPO suffers from indiscriminate credit assignment to intermediate steps due to relying on a single trajectory without explicit stepwise feedback. A verifiable, model-free supervision signals progress without costly critics or rollouts, guiding targeted updates and reducing overthinking.
AI.method: Segment the reasoning process into discrete steps. At each segment boundary, track the conditional probability that the final correct answer is correct, appended to the current segment. Use these segment-wise progress measurements as trajectory-level feedback to refine GRPO without Monte Carlo rollouts or auxiliary models.
AI.result: On math benchmarks, gains up to 2.6 points in accuracy and 13.7% reduction in reasoning length; on general-domain benchmarks, gains up to 2.4 points in accuracy and 4% reduction in reasoning length. Demonstrates improved performance and generalization across models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed verifiable, segment-wise supervision provides targeted, sample-efficient updates for GRPO, improving accuracy and reducing reasoning length while avoiding auxiliary models or rollouts, with strong cross-domain generalization.

title: RefAerial: A Benchmark and Approach for Referring Detection in Aerial Images
summary: Referring detection refers to locate the target referred by natural languages, which has recently attracted growing research interests. However, existing datasets are limited to ground images with large object centered in relative small scenes. This paper introduces a large-scale challenging dataset for referring detection in aerial images, termed as RefAerial. It distinguishes from conventional ground referring detection datasets by 4 characteristics: (1) low but diverse object-to-scene ratios, (2) numerous targets and distractors, (3)complex and fine-grained referring descriptions, (4) diverse and broad scenes in the aerial view. We also develop a human-in-the-loop referring expansion and annotation engine (REA-Engine) for efficient semi-automated referring pair annotation. Besides, we observe that existing ground referring detection approaches exhibiting serious performance degradation on our aerial dataset since the intrinsic scale variety issue within or across aerial images. Therefore, we further propose a novel scale-comprehensive and sensitive (SCS) framework for referring detection in aerial images. It consists of a mixture-of-granularity (MoG) attention and a two-stage comprehensive-to-sensitive (CtS) decoding strategy. Specifically, the mixture-of-granularity attention is developed for scale-comprehensive target understanding. In addition, the two-stage comprehensive-to-sensitive decoding strategy is designed for coarse-to-fine referring target decoding. Eventually, the proposed SCS framework achieves remarkable performance on our aerial referring detection dataset and even promising performance boost on conventional ground referring detection datasets.

AI.tldr: Introduces RefAerial, a large-scale aerial referring-detection dataset, a semi-automated REA-Engine annotation pipeline, and the SCS framework (MoG attention + CtS decoding) to handle scale variation; achieves strong results on RefAerial and transfers to ground datasets.
AI.motivation: Ground-based referring-detection datasets exhibit limited scale diversity and clutter; aerial imagery introduces low object-to-scene ratios, numerous distractors, and fine-grained descriptions, necessitating a new dataset and robust, scale-aware models.
AI.method: Dataset: RefAerial with four distinguishing properties. Annotation: REA-Engine for efficient semi-automated referring-pair labeling. Model: SCS framework combining mixture-of-granularity attention for scale awareness and a two-stage comprehensive-to-sensitive decoding to go from coarse to fine localization.
AI.result: SCS achieves remarkable performance on RefAerial and provides a promising boost on conventional ground referring-detection datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset and SCS framework address scale-variation challenges in aerial referring detection and offer transferable gains to ground-based datasets, advancing the field.
