title: Improving clinical interpretability of linear neuroimaging models through feature whitening
summary: Linear models are widely used in computational neuroimaging to identify biomarkers associated with brain pathologies. However, interpreting the learned weights remains challenging, as they do not always yield clinically meaningful insights. This difficulty arises in part from the inherent correlation between brain regions, which causes linear weights to reflect shared rather than region-specific contributions. In particular, some groups of regions, including homologous structures in the left and right hemispheres, are known to exhibit strong anatomical correlations. In this work, we leverage this prior neuroanatomical knowledge to introduce a whitening approach applied to groups of regions with known shared variance, designed to disentangle overlapping information across correlated brain measures. We additionally propose a regularized variant that allows controlled tuning of the degree of decorrelation. We evaluate this method using region-of-interest features in two psychiatric classification tasks, distinguishing individuals with bipolar disorder or schizophrenia from healthy controls. Importantly, unlike PCA or ICA which use whitening as a dimensionality reduction step, our approach decorrelates anatomically informed pairs of neuroanatomical regions while retaining the full input signal, making it specifically suited for feature interpretation rather than feature selection. Our findings demonstrate that whitening improves the interpretability of model weights while preserving predictive performance, providing a robust framework for linking linear model outputs to neurobiological mechanisms.

AI.tldr: An anatomically informed whitening approach for correlated ROI groups improves interpretability of linear model weights without sacrificing predictive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Interpretability of linear models in neuroimaging is hindered by inter-regional correlations. Exploiting neuroanatomical priors (e.g., homologous left–right regions) to decorrelate shared variance can clarify region-specific contributions.
AI.method: Apply whitening to predefined groups of brain regions with known shared variance; introduce a regularized variant to tune the degree of decorrelation; retain the full input signal (no feature reduction); evaluate on ROI-based features in two psychiatric classification tasks (bipolar disorder vs controls; schizophrenia vs controls).
AI.result: Whitening enhances interpretability of model weights while preserving predictive performance; decorrelates anatomically informed pairs of regions without discarding features, aligning weights with neurobiological structure.
AI.conclusion: An anatomically informed decorrelation framework improves interpretability of linear model outputs in neuroimaging, distinct from PCA/ICA, and is particularly suited for interpretation rather than feature selection.

title: Evian: Towards Explainable Visual Instruction-tuning Data Auditing
summary: The efficacy of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) is critically dependent on the quality of their training data, requiring a precise balance between visual fidelity and instruction-following capability. Existing datasets, however, are plagued by inconsistent quality, and current data filtering methods rely on coarse-grained scores that lack the granularity to identify nuanced semantic flaws like logical fallacies or factual errors. This creates a fundamental bottleneck in developing more reliable models. To address this, we make three core contributions. First, we construct a large-scale, 300K-sample benchmark by systematically injecting diverse, subtle defects to provide a challenging testbed for data auditing. Second, we introduce a novel "Decomposition-then-Evaluation" paradigm that breaks model responses into constituent cognitive components: visual description, subjective inference, and factual claim, enabling targeted analysis. Third, we instantiate this paradigm via EVIAN (Explainable Visual Instruction-tuning Data AuditiNg), an automated framework that evaluates these components along the orthogonal axes of Image-Text Consistency, Logical Coherence, and Factual Accuracy. Our empirical findings challenge the prevailing scale-centric paradigm: a model fine-tuned on a compact, high-quality subset curated by EVIAN consistently surpassed models trained on orders-of-magnitude larger datasets. We also reveal that dividing complex auditing into verifiable subtasks enables robust curation, and that Logical Coherence is the most critical factor in data quality evaluation.

AI.tldr: Introduces EVIAN for auditing LVLM training data by decomposing model responses into visual description, subjective inference, and factual claim, and evaluating them along Image-Text Consistency, Logical Coherence, and Factual Accuracy. A compact, high-quality subset curated by EVIAN outperforms much larger datasets, highlighting the importance of logical coherence in data quality.
AI.motivation: Training data quality critically impacts LVLM efficacy. Existing datasets suffer from inconsistent quality and coarse-grained filters that miss nuanced semantic flaws (e.g., logical fallacies, factual errors). A fine-grained auditing framework is needed to improve reliability and reduce reliance on scale.
AI.method: 1) Build a large 300K-sample benchmark with systematically injected diverse, subtle defects. 2) Propose Decomposition-then-Evaluation, breaking responses into visual description, subjective inference, and factual claim. 3) Implement EVIAN to automatically audit these components across three axes: Image-Text Consistency, Logical Coherence, Factual Accuracy.
AI.result: Empirical results show that a model fine-tuned on a compact, high-quality subset curated by EVIAN can outperform models trained on orders of magnitude larger datasets. Decomposed auditing enables robust data curation, and Logical Coherence emerges as the most critical factor for data quality evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Shifts emphasis from sheer dataset scale to high-quality, auditable data. EVIAN provides an automated, fine-grained auditing framework; decomposing responses clarifies error sources and guides targeted data curation for LVLMs.

title: Variance Is Not Importance: Structural Analysis of Transformer Compressibility Across Model Scales
summary:
We present a systematic empirical study of transformer compression through over 40 experiments on GPT-2 (124M parameters) and Mistral 7B (7.24B parameters). Our analysis covers spectral compression, block-level function replacement, rotation-based quantization, activation geometry, and adaptive early exit.
  We identify five structural properties relevant to compression. (1) Variance is not importance: high-variance activation directions are approximately 96 percent uncorrelated with predictive directions (measured via CCA), and projecting onto these subspaces preserves over 90 percent of variance while degrading perplexity. (2) Block linearity is conditional: transformer blocks are approximately linear (R^2 ~ 0.95 on GPT-2, 0.93 on Mistral block 31) only under the correct upstream distribution; modifying earlier blocks induces distribution shift that degrades downstream approximations. (3) The reconstruction wall: approaches that factor weights into quantized components amplify errors through cross-terms, making direct quantization strictly superior. (4) Linearity increases with depth: Mistral 7B exhibits a progression from R^2 = 0.17 (block 0) to R^2 = 0.93 (block 31), indicating a division between nonlinear feature construction and linear refinement. (5) Approximately 30 percent of tokens are computationally easy, confirmed via exit heads and KL divergence sensitivity.
  We demonstrate that single-block linear replacement achieves 34x compression with a 1.71 perplexity increase on the final block of Mistral 7B, while multi-block replacement fails due to residual error accumulation and distribution shift. These findings suggest fundamental limits to static post-training compression and motivate adaptive, per-token computation as a more effective direction.

AI.tldr: Empirical study of transformer compression across GPT-2 and Mistral 7B showing limited gains from static post-training methods; single-block linear replacement yields large compression with modest perplexity cost, while multi-block approaches fail due to error accumulation and distribution shift; linearity grows with depth; ~30% of tokens are easy to compute; results favor adaptive per-token computation.
AI.motivation: Quantify what makes transformers compressible and identify structural properties that govern the success or failure of post-training compression methods.
AI.method: Systematic study with 40+ experiments on GPT-2 (124M) and Mistral 7B (7.24B). Analyses include spectral compression, block-level function replacement, rotation-based quantization, activation geometry, and adaptive early exit. Measurements include variance directions vs predictive directions (via CCA), block linearity (R^2), reconstruction errors, depth-related linearity progression, exit head KL sensitivity, and perplexity changes. Comparisons of single-block vs multi-block weight replacement.
AI.result: Identifies five structural properties relevant to compression: (1) high-variance directions are ~96% uncorrelated with predictive directions; projection preserves >90% of variance but degrades perplexity. (2) Block linearity is distribution-dependent; blocks are near-linear only under correct upstream distribution; shifting earlier blocks degrades downstream approximations. (3) Reconstruction wall: factorized weight quantization amplifies cross-term errors; direct quantization is superior. (4) Linearity increases with depth; Mistral 7B shows R^2 rising from 0.17 (block 0) to 0.93 (block 31). (5) ~30% of tokens are computationally easy as shown by exit heads and KL divergence sensitivity. Demonstrates 34x compression with 1.71 perplexity increase on the final block of Mistral 7B with single-block linear replacement; multi-block replacement fails due to cumulative error and distribution shift. Concludes static post-training compression has fundamental limits and advocates adaptive per-token computation.
AI.conclusion: Static post-training compression has fundamental limits. Adaptive, per-token computation—such as selective early exits or dynamic recomputation—offers a more effective path to practical compression in transformers.

title: Exploring Spatial Intelligence from a Generative Perspective
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. Project page: https://aim-uofa.github.io/GSI-Bench/
summary: Spatial intelligence is essential for multimodal large language models, yet current benchmarks largely assess it only from an understanding perspective. We ask whether modern generative or unified multimodal models also possess generative spatial intelligence (GSI), the ability to respect and manipulate 3D spatial constraints during image generation, and whether such capability can be measured or improved. We introduce GSI-Bench, the first benchmark designed to quantify GSI through spatially grounded image editing. It consists of two complementary components: GSI-Real, a high-quality real-world dataset built via a 3D-prior-guided generation and filtering pipeline, and GSI-Syn, a large-scale synthetic benchmark with controllable spatial operations and fully automated labeling. Together with a unified evaluation protocol, GSI-Bench enables scalable, model-agnostic assessment of spatial compliance and editing fidelity. Experiments show that fine-tuning unified multimodal models on GSI-Syn yields substantial gains on both synthetic and real tasks and, strikingly, also improves downstream spatial understanding. This provides the first clear evidence that generative training can tangibly strengthen spatial reasoning, establishing a new pathway for advancing spatial intelligence in multimodal models.

AI.tldr: GSI-Bench introduces a generative spatial intelligence benchmark for multimodal models using real and synthetic datasets for spatially grounded image editing; fine-tuning on GSI-Syn improves both synthetic/real tasks and downstream spatial understanding.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal benchmarks largely evaluate understanding but not the ability to generate and manipulate 3D spatial constraints; there is a need to quantify generative spatial intelligence (GSI) and its improvement.
AI.method: Two-part benchmark: GSI-Real (real-world data via a 3D-prior-guided generation and filtering pipeline) and GSI-Syn (large-scale synthetic with controllable spatial operations and automated labeling); unified evaluation protocol; evaluation of spatial compliance and editing fidelity; experiments show fine-tuning UNIMODAL? unified multimodal models on GSI-Syn.
AI.result: Fine-tuning on GSI-Syn yields substantial gains on both synthetic and real tasks and improves downstream spatial understanding; provides first evidence that generative training strengthens spatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: GSI-Bench enables scalable, model-agnostic assessment of spatial compliance and editing fidelity; demonstrates generative training as a viable pathway to enhance spatial intelligence in multimodal models.

title: MGDA-Decoupled: Geometry-Aware Multi-Objective Optimisation for DPO-based LLM Alignment
comment: Accepted to the Algorithmic Fairness Across Alignment Procedures and Agentic Systems Workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) to desirable human values requires balancing multiple, potentially conflicting objectives such as helpfulness, truthfulness, and harmlessness, which presents a multi-objective optimisation challenge. Most alignment pipelines rely on a fixed scalarisation of these objectives, which can introduce procedural unfairness by systematically under-weighting harder-to-optimise or minority objectives. To promote more equitable trade-offs, we introduce MGDA-Decoupled, a geometry-based multi-objective optimisation algorithm that finds a shared descent direction while explicitly accounting for each objective's convergence dynamics. In contrast to prior methods that depend on reinforcement learning (e.g., GAPO) or explicit reward models (e.g., MODPO), our approach operates entirely within the lightweight Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) paradigm. Experiments on the UltraFeedback dataset show that geometry-aware methods -- and MGDA-Decoupled in particular -- achieve the highest win rates against golden responses, both overall and per objective.

AI.tldr: Introduces MGDA-Decoupled, a geometry-based multi-objective optimization method for LLM alignment that balances conflicting objectives within Direct Preference Optimization, achieving superior performance over RL/reward-model baselines on UltraFeedback.
AI.motivation: Address procedural unfairness from fixed scalarisation of conflicting objectives (helpfulness, truthfulness, harmlessness) and ensure equitable trade-offs by considering each objective's convergence dynamics.
AI.method: MGDA-Decoupled algorithm: geometry-based multi-objective optimization that finds a shared descent direction by accounting for convergence dynamics of each objective; operates within DPO, avoiding reinforcement learning or explicit reward models.
AI.result: On UltraFeedback dataset, geometry-aware methods, particularly MGDA-Decoupled, achieve the highest win rates against golden responses overall and per objective.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that incorporating geometry-aware, decoupled MGDA into DPO yields improved alignment performance and fairness without RL or explicit reward models; suggests favorable trade-offs across multiple objectives.

title: Where are they looking in the operating room?
summary: Purpose: Gaze-following, the task of inferring where individuals are looking, has been widely studied in computer vision, advancing research in visual attention modeling, social scene understanding, and human-robot interaction. However, gaze-following has never been explored in the operating room (OR), a complex, high-stakes environment where visual attention plays an important role in surgical workflow analysis. In this work, we introduce the concept of gaze-following to the surgical domain, and demonstrate its great potential for understanding clinical roles, surgical phases, and team communications in the OR. Methods: We extend the 4D-OR dataset with gaze-following annotations, and extend the Team-OR dataset with gaze-following and a new team communication activity annotations. Then, we propose novel approaches to address clinical role prediction, surgical phase recognition, and team communication detection using a gaze-following model. For role and phase recognition, we propose a gaze heatmap-based approach that uses gaze predictions solely; for team communication detection, we train a spatial-temporal model in a self-supervised way that encodes gaze-based clip features, and then feed the features into a temporal activity detection model. Results: Experimental results on the 4D-OR and Team-OR datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on all downstream tasks. Quantitatively, our approach obtains F1 scores of 0.92 for clinical role prediction and 0.95 for surgical phase recognition. Furthermore, it significantly outperforms existing baselines in team communication detection, improving previous best performances by over 30%. Conclusion: We introduce gaze-following in the OR as a novel research direction in surgical data science, highlighting its great potential to advance surgical workflow analysis in computer-assisted interventions.

AI.tldr: This work introduces gaze-following in the operating room, extending 4D-OR and Team-OR datasets and proposing gaze-based methods for clinical role prediction, surgical phase recognition, and team communication detection, achieving state-of-the-art results (e.g., F1 0.92/0.95; >30% improvement).
AI.motivation: Gaze behavior reflects visual attention and teamwork, which are critical in the high-stakes OR. Gaze-following has been explored in computer vision but not in surgical settings; applying it could enhance surgical workflow analysis, role understanding, and communication detection.
AI.method: Extend the 4D-OR dataset with gaze-following annotations and the Team-OR dataset with gaze-following and new team communication annotations. Propose a gaze heatmap-based approach for role and phase recognition using gaze predictions alone; and a self-supervised spatial-temporal model encoding gaze-based clip features for team communication detection, followed by a temporal activity detector.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on 4D-OR and Team-OR. Quantitatively, F1 scores of 0.92 for clinical role prediction and 0.95 for surgical phase recognition. Team communication detection shows >30% improvement over prior best baselines.
AI.conclusion: Gaze-following in the OR is a promising new direction in surgical data science, with potential to advance surgical workflow analysis in computer-assisted interventions.

title: Storm Surge Modeling, Bias Correction, Graph Neural Networks, Graph Convolution Networks
summary: Storm surge forecasting remains a critical challenge in mitigating the impacts of tropical cyclones on coastal regions, particularly given recent trends of rapid intensification and increasing nearshore storm activity. Traditional high fidelity numerical models such as ADCIRC, while robust, are often hindered by inevitable uncertainties arising from various sources. To address these challenges, this study introduces StormNet, a spatio-temporal graph neural network (GNN) designed for bias correction of storm surge forecasts. StormNet integrates graph convolutional (GCN) and graph attention (GAT) mechanisms with long short-term memory (LSTM) components to capture complex spatial and temporal dependencies among water-level gauge stations. The model was trained using historical hurricane data from the U.S. Gulf Coast and evaluated on Hurricane Idalia (2023). Results demonstrate that StormNet can effectively reduce the root mean square error (RMSE) in water-level predictions by more than 70\% for 48-hour forecasts and above 50\% for 72-hour forecasts, as well as outperform a sequential LSTM baseline, particularly for longer prediction horizons. The model also exhibits low training time, enhancing its applicability in real-time operational forecasting systems. Overall, StormNet provides a computationally efficient and physically meaningful framework for improving storm surge prediction accuracy and reliability during extreme weather events.

AI.tldr: StormNet—a spatio-temporal graph neural network for bias correcting ADCIRC storm surge forecasts, achieving substantial RMSE reductions for 48- and 72-hour predictions on Gulf Coast data (Idalia 2023) with efficient training.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity but uncertain surge forecasts require data-driven bias correction to improve accuracy and lead times for coastal risk management.
AI.method: Hybrid GC/LSTM architecture: combine graph convolutional networks and graph attention networks to model spatial relations among water-level gauges, with LSTM for temporal dynamics; trained on historical hurricane data; evaluated against a sequential LSTM baseline; focus on 48- and 72-hour forecasts; assesses computational efficiency.
AI.result: RMSE reductions >70% at 48h and >50% at 72h; outperforms LSTM baseline, particularly at longer horizons; low training time; improved accuracy and operational viability.
AI.conclusion: StormNet offers an efficient, physically meaningful bias-correction framework that enhances storm surge forecasting reliability, with potential for real-time deployment.

title: On the Impact of Face Segmentation-Based Background Removal on Recognition and Morphing Attack Detection
summary: This study investigates the impact of face image background correction through segmentation on face recognition and morphing attack detection performance in realistic, unconstrained image capture scenarios. The motivation is driven by operational biometric systems such as the European Entry/Exit System (EES), which require facial enrolment at airports and other border crossing points where controlled backgrounds usually required for such captures cannot always be guaranteed, as well as by accessibility needs that may necessitate image capture outside traditional office environments. By analyzing how such preprocessing steps influence both recognition accuracy and security mechanisms, this work addresses a critical gap between usability-driven image normalization and the reliability requirements of large-scale biometric identification systems. Our study evaluates a comprehensive range of segmentation techniques, three families of morphing attack detection methods, and four distinct face recognition models, using databases that include both controlled and in-the-wild image captures. The results reveal consistent patterns linking segmentation to both recognition performance and face image quality. Additionally, segmentation is shown to systematically influence morphing attack detection performance. These findings highlight the need for careful consideration when deploying such preprocessing techniques in operational biometric systems.

AI.tldr: Segmentation-based background correction affects both face recognition and morphing attack detection in unconstrained images; results stress careful preprocessing choice for deployment in large-scale biometric systems.
AI.motivation: Operational biometric systems (e.g., EES) require enrollment under variable backgrounds; controlled settings not always feasible; addressing usability needs vs reliability gaps in real-world identification.
AI.method: Evaluate a comprehensive set of segmentation techniques, three families of morphing attack detection methods, and four face recognition models, across databases with controlled and in-the-wild captures.
AI.result: Segmentation systematically influences both face image quality/recognition performance and morphing attack detection performance; consistent patterns observed across setups.
AI.conclusion: Background segmentation preprocessing can materially impact system performance; deployment must consider these effects and possibly calibrate or constrain segmentation to balance usability and security.

title: Generative Flow Networks for Model Adaptation in Digital Twins of Natural Systems
summary: Digital twins of natural systems must remain aligned with physical systems that evolve over time, are only partially observed, and are typically modeled by mechanistic simulators whose parameters cannot be measured directly. In such settings, model adaptation is naturally posed as a simulation-based inference problem. However, sparse and indirect observations often fail to identify a unique and optimal calibration, leaving several simulator parameterizations compatible with the available evidence. This article presents a GFlowNet-based approach to model adaptation for digital twins of natural systems. We formulate adaptation as a generative modeling problem over complete simulator configurations, so that plausible parameterizations can be sampled with probability proportional to a reward derived from agreement between simulated and observed behavior. Using a controlled environment agriculture case study based on a mechanistic tomato model, we show that the learned policy recovers dominant regions of the adaptation landscape, retrieves strong calibration hypotheses, and preserves multiple plausible configurations under uncertainty.

AI.tldr: A GFlowNet-driven framework to sample complete simulator configurations for digital twin adaptation under partial observations; demonstrates multi-modal calibration in a mechanistic tomato growth model.
AI.motivation: Digital twins of natural systems evolve over time, are partially observed, and rely on mechanistic simulators with unmeasurable parameters. Sparse/indirect data often yields multiple compatible calibrations. The work seeks to explore and preserve diverse plausible configurations.
AI.method: Formulate adaptation as a generative modeling problem over full simulator configurations. Use a GFlowNet to sample parameterizations with probability proportional to a reward based on agreement between simulated and observed behavior. Validate on a controlled environment agriculture case study using a mechanistic tomato model.
AI.result: The learned policy recovers dominant regions of the adaptation landscape, retrieves strong calibration hypotheses, and preserves multiple plausible configurations under uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: GFlowNet-based adaptation yields diverse, high-reward calibrations for digital twins under sparse observations, enabling robust maintenance of alignment between models and real systems; approach may generalize to other natural systems.

title: Structure-Augmented Standard Plane Detection with Temporal Aggregation in Blind-Sweep Fetal Ultrasound
summary: In low-resource settings, blind-sweep ultrasound provides a practical and accessible method for identifying fetal growth restriction. However, unlike freehand ultrasound which is subjectively controlled, detection of biometry plane in blind-sweep ultrasound is more challenging due to the uncontrolled fetal structure to be observed and the variaties of oblique planes in the scan. In this work, we propose a structure-augmented system to detect fetal abdomen plane, where the abdominal structure is highlighted using a segmentation prior. Since standard planes are emerging gradually, the decision boundary of the keyframes is unstable to predict. We thus aggregated the structure-augmented planes with a temporal sliding window to help stabilise keyframe localisation. Extensive results indicate that the structure-augmented temporal sliding strategy significantly improves and stabilises the detection of anatomically meaningful planes, which enables more reliable biometric measurements in blind-sweep ultrasound.

AI.tldr: A structure-augmented approach detects fetal abdomen planes in blind-sweep ultrasound by using segmentation priors to highlight abdominal anatomy and a temporal sliding window to stabilize keyframe localization, improving reliability of biometric measurements in low-resource settings.
AI.motivation: In blind-sweep ultrasound, uncontrolled fetal orientation and oblique planes make standard plane detection challenging, risking unreliable biometric measurements in low-resource contexts.
AI.method: Incorporates a segmentation-driven prior to emphasize abdominal structures and aggregates candidate keyframes over a temporal sliding window to stabilize plane localization across frames.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that the structure-augmented temporal sliding strategy significantly improves and stabilizes detection of anatomically meaningful planes, enabling more reliable biometric measurements in blind-sweep ultrasound.
AI.conclusion: Structure-augmented segmentation priors combined with temporal smoothing provide more stable and reliable plane detection for blind-sweep ultrasound, supporting more dependable biometric assessments in resource-limited settings.

title: COMPASS: COntinual Multilingual PEFT with Adaptive Semantic Sampling
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often exhibit performance disparities across languages, with naive multilingual fine-tuning frequently degrading performance due to negative cross-lingual interference. To address this, we introduce COMPASS (COntinual Multilingual PEFT with Adaptive Semantic Sampling), a novel data-centric framework for adapting LLMs to target languages. COMPASS leverages parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) by training lightweight, language-specific adapters on a judiciously selected subset of auxiliary multilingual data. The core of our method is a distribution-aware sampling strategy that uses multilingual embeddings and clustering to identify semantic gaps between existing training data and a target usage distribution. By prioritizing auxiliary data from under-represented semantic clusters, COMPASS maximizes positive cross-lingual transfer while minimizing interference. We extend this into a continual learning framework, COMPASS-ECDA, which monitors for data distribution shifts in production and dynamically updates adapters to prevent model staleness, balancing adaptation to new data with the preservation of existing knowledge. Across three different model architectures (Phi-4-Mini, Llama-3.1-8B, and Qwen2.5-7B) and multiple challenging multilingual benchmarks (Global-MMLU, MMLU-ProX), including unseen long-context tasks (OneRuler), we demonstrate that COMPASS consistently outperforms baseline methods guided by linguistic similarity, providing an effective, efficient, and sustainable solution for developing and maintaining high-performing multilingual models in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: COMPASS proposes a data-centric, continual multilingual PEFT framework that uses adaptive semantic sampling via multilingual embeddings and clustering to select auxiliary data for language-specific adapters, improving cross-lingual transfer while mitigating interference, and extending to continual learning (COMPASS-ECDA) to adapt to distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Address negative cross-lingual interference and model staleness in multilingual LLM fine-tuning by focusing data selection and continual adaptation on semantic coverage and distribution shifts.
AI.method: Distribution-aware sampling: cluster multilingual embeddings to identify semantic gaps between training data and target usage; select under-represented semantic clusters for auxiliary data to train lightweight adapters (PEFT). Extend to continual learning (COMPASS-ECDA) that detects distribution shifts in production and updates adapters dynamically.
AI.result: Across Phi-4-Mini, Llama-3.1-8B, and Qwen2.5-7B, and benchmarks Global-MMLU, MMLU-ProX, including unseen long-context tasks (OneRuler), COMPASS consistently outperforms baselines guided by linguistic similarity, with improved multilingual performance and data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, efficient data-centric framework for building and maintaining high-performing multilingual LLMs in dynamic environments, balancing cross-lingual transfer, data efficiency, and continual adaptation.

title: Physics-Informed Conditional Diffusion for Motion-Robust Retinal Temporal Laser Speckle Contrast Imaging
summary: Retinal laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI) is a noninvasive optical modality for monitoring retinal blood flow dynamics. However, conventional temporal LSCI (tLSCI) reconstruction relies on sufficiently long speckle sequences to obtain stable temporal statistics, which makes it vulnerable to acquisition disturbances and limits effective temporal resolution. A physically informed reconstruction framework, termed RetinaDiff (Retinal Diffusion Model), is proposed for retinal tLSCI that is robust to motion and requires only a few frames. In RetinaDiff, registration based on phase correlation is first applied to stabilize the raw speckle sequence before contrast computation, reducing interframe misalignment so that fluctuations at each pixel primarily reflect true flow dynamics. This step provides a physics prior corrected for motion and a high quality multiframe tLSCI reference. Next, guided by the physics prior, a conditional diffusion model performs inverse reconstruction by jointly conditioning on the registered speckle sequence and the corrected prior. Experiments on data acquired with a retinal LSCI system developed in house show improved structural continuity and statistical stability compared with direct reconstruction from few frames and representative baselines. The framework also remains effective in a small number of extremely challenging cases, where both the direct 5-frame input and the conventional multiframe reconstruction are severely degraded. Overall, this work provides a practical and physically grounded route for reliable retinal tLSCI reconstruction from extremely limited frames. The source code and model weights will be publicly available at https://github.com/QianChen113/RetinaDiff.

AI.tldr: RetinaDiff uses phase-correlation based registration to stabilize retinal speckle sequences, then employs a conditional diffusion model guided by a physics-driven prior to reconstruct retinal tLSCI from very few frames, improving stability and robustness against motion.
AI.motivation: Conventional temporal LSCI requires long speckle sequences for stable statistics and is sensitive to motion disturbances, limiting temporal resolution. A physically informed reconstruction from few frames could enable reliable retinal blood flow imaging with higher robustness.
AI.method: 1) Apply phase-correlation registration to stabilize the raw speckle sequence before contrast computation. 2) Use a conditional diffusion model conditioned on the registered sequence and a physics-based prior to perform inverse reconstruction from few frames, enabling joint leveraging of data and prior.
AI.result: Experiments on in-house retinal LSCI data show improved structural continuity and statistical stability compared with direct reconstruction from few frames and representative baselines; the method remains effective in challenging cases where both 5-frame input and standard multiframe reconstructions are degraded.
AI.conclusion: RetinaDiff offers a practical,physically grounded route for reliable retinal tLSCI reconstruction from extremely limited frames; source code and model weights will be released at the provided GitHub link.

title: Tokenised Flow Matching for Hierarchical Simulation Based Inference
summary: The cost of simulator evaluations is a key practical bottleneck for Simulation Based Inference (SBI). In hierarchical settings with shared global parameters and exchangeable site-level parameters and observations, this structure can be exploited to improve simulation efficiency. Existing hierarchical SBI approaches factorise the posterior yet still simulate across multiple sites per training sample; We instead explore likelihood factorisation (LF) to train from single-site simulations. In LF sampling we learn a per-site neural surrogate of the simulator and then assemble synthetic multi-site observations to amortise inference for the full hierarchical posterior. Building on this, we propose Tokenised Flow Matching for Posterior Estimation (TFMPE), a tokenised flow matching approach that supports function-valued observations through likelihood factorisation. To enable systematic evaluation, we introduce a benchmark for hierarchical SBI. We validate TFMPE on this benchmark and on realistic infectious disease and computational fluid dynamics models, finding well-calibrated posteriors while reducing computational cost.

AI.tldr: LF-based hierarchical SBI with per-site neural surrogates and Tokenised Flow Matching (TFMPE); achieves calibration with reduced simulator cost via single-site training and likelihood factorisation.
AI.motivation: Simulation-based inference (SBI) is hampered by expensive simulator evaluations. In hierarchical models with shared global parameters and exchangeable site-level parameters, exploiting structure can boost efficiency. Prior hierarchical SBI methods still simulate across multiple sites per training sample; there is a need for single-site training via likelihood factorisation (LF).
AI.method: Introduce likelihood factorisation (LF) to train from single-site simulations by learning per-site neural surrogates of the simulator and assembling synthetic multi-site observations to approximate the full hierarchical posterior. Propose Tokenised Flow Matching for Posterior Estimation (TFMPE), a tokenised flow matching framework that handles function-valued observations through LF. Establish a benchmark for hierarchical SBI for systematic evaluation.
AI.result: TFMPE yields well-calibrated posteriors while reducing computational cost; validated on the benchmark and on realistic infectious disease and computational fluid dynamics models.
AI.conclusion: LF-based training with per-site surrogates and TFMPE provides efficient, scalable hierarchical SBI capable of handling function-valued data; the introduced benchmark enables systematic evaluation and the approach shows practical gains in accuracy-cost trade-offs.

title: Supplement Generation Training for Enhancing Agentic Task Performance
comment: Accepted to the Findings of ACL 2026
summary: Training large foundation models for agentic tasks is increasingly impractical due to the high computational costs, long iteration cycles, and rapid obsolescence as new models are continuously released. Instead of post-training massive models for every new task or domain, we propose Supplement Generation Training (SGT), a more efficient and sustainable strategy. SGT trains a smaller LLM to generate useful supplemental text that, when appended to the original input, helps the larger LLM solve the task more effectively. These lightweight models can dynamically adapt supplements to task requirements, improving performance without modifying the underlying large models. This approach decouples task-specific optimization from large foundation models and enables more flexible, cost-effective deployment of LLM-powered agents in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: SGT trains a small model to generate supplements that augment inputs to a large LLM, improving task performance without modifying the base model.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational burden and rapid obsolescence of large foundation models when adapting to new tasks, enabling efficient, flexible deployment.
AI.method: A smaller LLM is trained to produce contextual supplementary text that, when appended to the original input, guides the large LLM to better solve the task. Supplements can be task-specific and dynamically adapt to requirements, decoupling optimization from the base model.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with anticipated performance gains and efficiency; no empirical results provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: SGT offers a decoupled, cost-effective approach to deploying LLM-powered agents by leveraging lightweight supplement generators instead of retraining or fine-tuning large foundation models.

title: RSRCC: A Remote Sensing Regional Change Comprehension Benchmark Constructed via Retrieval-Augmented Best-of-N Ranking
summary: Traditional change detection identifies where changes occur, but does not explain what changed in natural language. Existing remote sensing change captioning datasets typically describe overall image-level differences, leaving fine-grained localized semantic reasoning largely unexplored. To close this gap, we present RSRCC, a new benchmark for remote sensing change question-answering containing 126k questions, split into 87k training, 17.1k validation, and 22k test instances. Unlike prior datasets, RSRCC is built around localized, change-specific questions that require reasoning about a particular semantic change. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first remote sensing change question-answering benchmark designed explicitly for such fine-grained reasoning-based supervision. To construct RSRCC, we introduce a hierarchical semi-supervised curation pipeline that uses Best-of-N ranking as a critical final ambiguity-resolution stage. First, candidate change regions are extracted from semantic segmentation masks, then initially screened using an image-text embedding model, and finally validated through retrieval-augmented vision-language curation with Best-of-N ranking. This process enables scalable filtering of noisy and ambiguous candidates while preserving semantically meaningful changes. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/google/RSRCC.

AI.tldr: RSRCC is a large remote-sensing change-question-answering benchmark (126k questions) focused on localized, change-specific reasoning, built via a hierarchical semi-supervised pipeline using Best-of-N ranking.
AI.motivation: Existing RS change datasets describe only image-level differences, lacking fine-grained, localized semantic reasoning about what exactly changed.
AI.method: Pipeline: (1) extract candidate change regions from semantic segmentation masks; (2) filter via image-text embedding; (3) validate with retrieval-augmented vision-language curation using Best-of-N ranking; enables scalable, noise-tolerant curation of meaningful changes.
AI.result: RSRCC comprises 126k questions across 87k train, 17.1k val, 22k test; first RS change QA benchmark designed for fine-grained reasoning; dataset available on HuggingFace.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a scalable, semi-supervised data-collection approach yielding a novel benchmark for fine-grained, localized RS change reasoning and a generalizable curation pipeline.

title: Near-Future Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a core post-training recipe. Introducing suitable off-policy trajectories into on-policy exploration accelerates RLVR convergence and raises the performance ceiling, yet finding a source of such trajectories remains the key challenge. Existing mixed-policy methods either import trajectories from external teachers (high-quality but distributionally far) or replay past training trajectories (close but capped in quality), and neither simultaneously satisfies the strong enough (higher $Q$ , more new knowledge to learn) and close enough (lower $V$ , more readily absorbed) conditions required to maximize the effective learning signal $\mathcal{S} = Q/V$. We propose \textbf{N}ear-Future \textbf{P}olicy \textbf{O}ptimization (\textbf{NPO}), a simple mixed-policy scheme that learns from a policy's own near-future self: a later checkpoint from the same training run is a natural source of auxiliary trajectories that is both stronger than the current policy and closer than any external source, directly balancing trajectory quality against variance cost. We validate NPO through two manual interventions, early-stage bootstrapping and late-stage plateau breakthrough, and further propose \textbf{AutoNPO},an adaptive variant that automatically triggers interventions from online training signals and selects the guide checkpoint that maximizes $S$. On Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct with GRPO, NPO improves average performance from 57.88 to 62.84, and AutoNPO pushes it to 63.15, raising the final performance ceiling while accelerating convergence.

AI.tldr: Proposes Near-Future Policy Optimization (NPO) for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to leverage near-future self-trajectories as intermediate-quality, low-variance auxiliary data, and its adaptive variant AutoNPO; demonstrates improved performance and faster convergence on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct with GRPO.
AI.motivation: To address the trade-off in mixed-policy RLVR between high-quality but distributionally distant external trajectories and close-but-lower-quality replayed trajectories. The goal is to obtain stronger-than-current policies while remaining close enough to be easily absorbed, maximizing the effective learning signal S = Q/V.
AI.method: Introduce Near-Future Policy Optimization (NPO), a mixed-policy scheme that uses a later checkpoint from the same training run as auxiliary trajectories—stronger than the current policy and closer than external sources. Validate via two manual interventions (early-stage bootstrapping and late-stage plateau breakthrough) and an adaptive AutoNPO that selects guide checkpoints online to maximize S.
AI.result: Empirical results on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct with GRPO show average performance improving from 57.88 to 62.84 with NPO, and AutoNPO further improving to 63.15, indicating higher final performance ceilings and faster convergence.
AI.conclusion: NPO provides a simple yet effective mechanism to balance trajectory quality and absorbability by leveraging near-future self-trajectories, with AutoNPO offering an online, adaptive improvement. The approach enhances RLVR performance and convergence and may generalize beyond the tested setup.

title: MAPRPose: Mask-Aware Proposal and Amodal Refinement for Multi-Object 6D Pose Estimation
summary: 6D object pose estimation in cluttered scenes remains challenging due to severe occlusion and sensor noise. We propose MAPRPose, a two-stage framework that leverages mask-aware correspondences for pose proposal and amodal-driven Region-of-Interest (ROI) prediction for robust refinement. In the Mask-Aware Pose Proposal (MAPP) stage, we lift 2D correspondences into 3D space to establish reliable keypoint matches and generate geometrically consistent pose hypotheses based on correspondence-level scoring, from which the top-$K$ candidates are selected. In the refinement stage, we introduce a tensorized render-and-compare pipeline integrated with an Amodal Mask Prediction and ROI Re-Alignment (AMPR) module. By reconstructing complete object geometry and dynamically adjusting the ROI, AMPR mitigates localization errors and spatial misalignment under heavy occlusion. Furthermore, our GPU-accelerated RGB-XYZ reprojection enables simultaneous refinement of all $N \times B$ pose hypotheses in a single forward pass. Evaluated on the BOP benchmark, MAPRPose achieves a state-of-the-art Average Recall (AR) of 76.5%, outperforming FoundationPose by 3.1% AR while delivering a 43x speedup in multi-object inference.

AI.tldr: MAPRPose is a two-stage 6D pose estimation method for cluttered scenes: (1) MAPP lifts 2D→3D correspondences to generate top-K pose hypotheses; (2) AMPR uses amodal masks and ROI re-alignment in a tensorized render-and-compare pipeline with GPU-accelerated reprojection to refine all hypotheses in parallel. It achieves 76.5% AR on BOP, outperforming FoundationPose by 3.1% AR and offering 43x speedup for multi-object inference.
AI.motivation: Address severe occlusion and sensor noise in 6D pose estimation by combining robust proposal generation with occlusion-aware refinement and parallelized refinement.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: Mask-Aware Pose Proposal (MAPP) lifts 2D correspondences to 3D for reliable keypoint matches and geometrically consistent pose proposals; select top-K candidates. Refinement via tensorized render-and-compare with Amodal Mask Prediction and ROI Re-Alignment (AMPR). Reconstructs complete geometry, dynamically adjusts ROI to mitigate localization errors under occlusion. GPU-accelerated RGB-XYZ reprojection enables simultaneous refinement of all N×B hypotheses in one forward pass.
AI.result: On BOP benchmark, AR = 76.5%, 3.1 percentage points higher than FoundationPose; about 43x speedup in multi-object inference.
AI.conclusion: MAPRPose delivers state-of-the-art AR on BOP while offering significant acceleration and robustness to occlusion through mask-aware proposals and amodal ROI refinement.

title: Fast Bayesian equipment condition monitoring via simulation based inference: applications to heat exchanger health
comment: Submitted, 15 pages, 9 figures, code available on github
summary: Accurate condition monitoring of industrial equipment requires inferring latent degradation parameters from indirect sensor measurements under uncertainty. While traditional Bayesian methods like Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) provide rigorous uncertainty quantification, their heavy computational bottlenecks render them impractical for real-time process control. To overcome this limitation, we propose an AI-driven framework utilizing Simulation-Based Inference (SBI) powered by amortized neural posterior estimation to diagnose complex failure modes in heat exchangers. By training neural density estimators on a simulated dataset, our approach learns a direct, likelihood-free mapping from thermal-fluid observations to the full posterior distribution of degradation parameters. We benchmark this framework against an MCMC baseline across various synthetic fouling and leakage scenarios, including challenging low-probability, sparse-event failures. The results show that SBI achieves comparable diagnostic accuracy and reliable uncertainty quantification, while accelerating inference time by a factor of82$\times$ compared to traditional sampling. The amortized nature of the neural network enables near-instantaneous inference, establishing SBI as a highly scalable, real-time alternative for probabilistic fault diagnosis and digital twin realization in complex engineering systems.

AI.tldr: Amortized neural SBI enables real-time, likelihood-free inference of degradation parameters for heat exchangers, matching MCMC accuracy with 82x faster inference.
AI.motivation: Traditional MCMC is accurate but computationally prohibitive for online condition monitoring; need real-time uncertainty quantification in industrial systems.
AI.method: Train neural density estimators to learn the posterior over degradation parameters from simulated thermal-fluid observations; use Simulation-Based Inference with amortized posterior estimation; benchmark against MCMC on synthetic fouling/leakage scenarios including rare failures.
AI.result: SBI yields comparable diagnostic accuracy and reliable uncertainty quantification, with ~82× faster inference; amortization enables near-instantaneous inference, suitable for real-time digital twin applications.
AI.conclusion: SBI is a scalable, real-time probabilistic fault diagnosis approach for complex engineering systems, offering substantial speedups without sacrificing uncertainty quantification.

title: The Expense of Seeing: Attaining Trustworthy Multimodal Reasoning Within the Monolithic Paradigm
summary: The rapid proliferation of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is widely celebrated as the dawn of unified multimodal knowledge discovery but its foundation operates on a dangerous, unquestioned axiom: that current VLMs faithfully synthesise multimodal data. We argue they do not. Instead, a profound crisis of trustworthiness underlies the dominant Vision Encoder-Projector-LLM paradigm. Rather than extracting grounded knowledge from visual inputs, state-of-the-art models frequently exhibit functional blindness, i.e., exploiting strong language priors to bypass severe visual representation bottlenecks. In this work, we challenge the conventional methodology of multimodal evaluation, which relies on data ablation or new dataset creation and therefore fatally conflates dataset biases with architectural incapacity. We propose a radical, information-theoretic departure: the Modality Translation Protocol, designed to quantifiably unmask the Expense of Seeing. By translating semantic payloads rather than ablating them, we formulate three novel metrics -- the Toll (ToS), Curse (CoS), and Fallacy (FoS) of Seeing -- culminating in the Semantic Sufficiency Criterion (SSC). Furthermore, we posit a provocative Divergence Law of Multimodal Scaling, hypothesising that as the underlying language engines scale to unprecedented reasoning capabilities, the mathematical penalty of the visual knowledge bottleneck paradoxically increases. We challenge the KDD community to abandon the illusory pursuit of "multimodal gain". By elevating the SSC from a passive diagnostic constraint to an active architectural blueprint, we provide the rigorous, trustworthy foundation required to force the next generation of AI systems to truly see the data, achieving true multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Radical information-theoretic critique of Vision-Language Models; introduces the Modality Translation Protocol and Semantic Sufficiency Criterion to quantify and expose the visual bottleneck; defines three metrics (Toll, Curse, Fallacy of Seeing) and posits a Divergence Law of multimodal scaling; argues for architectural redesign toward true multimodal seeing rather than relying on language priors.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs suffer from a trustworthiness crisis: they often rely on strong language priors and exhibit visual representation bottlenecks, making they fail to truly ground multimodal data. Conventional evaluation conflates dataset bias with model incapacity, hindering genuine progress. An information-theoretic diagnostic is proposed to quantify the expense of seeing and ground multimodal capabilities.
AI.method: Proposes Modality Translation Protocol (MTP) that translates semantic payloads instead of ablating them; defines three metrics—Toll of Seeing (ToS), Curse of Seeing (CoS), and Fallacy of Seeing (FoS)—leading to the Semantic Sufficiency Criterion (SSC); introduces a Divergence Law of Multimodal Scaling suggesting that larger language engines increase the penalty of the visual bottleneck.
AI.result: Presents a theoretical framework and diagnostic toolkit (SSC) to assess whether VLMs truly leverage visual inputs; emphasizes a move away from data-ablation studies toward principled, information-theoretic evaluation; no empirical results reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Advocates abandoning the pursuit of presumed multimodal gains and adopting SSC as an architectural blueprint to enable true visual grounding and multimodal reasoning, thereby fostering trustworthy AI systems.

title: F\textsuperscript{2}LP-AP: Fast \& Flexible Label Propagation with Adaptive Propagation Kernel
summary: Semi-supervised node classification is a foundational task in graph machine learning, yet state-of-the-art Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are hindered by significant computational overhead and reliance on strong homophily assumptions. Traditional GNNs require expensive iterative training and multi-layer message passing, while existing training-free methods, such as Label Propagation, lack adaptability to heterophilo\-us graph structures. This paper presents \textbf{F$^2$LP-AP} (Fast and Flexible Label Propagation with Adaptive Propagation Kernel), a training-free, computationally efficient framework that adapts to local graph topology. Our method constructs robust class prototypes via the geometric median and dynamically adjusts propagation parameters based on the Local Clustering Coefficient (LCC), enabling effective modeling of both homophilous and heterophilous graphs without gradient-based training. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmark datasets demonstrate that \textbf{F$^2$LP-AP} achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to trained GNNs, while significantly outperforming existing baselines in computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: A training-free, adaptive label propagation framework (F^2LP-AP) that uses geometric-median class prototypes and Local Clustering Coefficient-based parameterization to handle both homophily and heterophily with competitive accuracy and superior efficiency.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational burden and rigidity of traditional GNNs by eliminating gradient-based training and improving applicability to heterophilous graphs.
AI.method: Construct robust class prototypes via geometric median; dynamically adjust propagation parameters using Local Clustering Coefficient; training-free, no gradient updates; suitable for both homogeneous and heterogeneous graph structures.
AI.result: Experiments show competitive or superior accuracy to trained GNNs and significantly better computational efficiency than existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: F^2LP-AP provides a fast, flexible, training-free approach to semi-supervised node classification that adapts to local topology and remains effective across diverse graph types.

title: R-CoV: Region-Aware Chain-of-Verification for Alleviating Object Hallucinations in LVLMs
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance in various multimodal understanding and reasoning tasks. However, they still struggle with object hallucinations, i.e., the claim of nonexistent objects in the visual input. To address this challenge, we propose Region-aware Chain-of-Verification (R-CoV), a visual chain-of-verification method to alleviate object hallucinations in LVLMs in a post-hoc manner. Motivated by how humans comprehend intricate visual information -- often focusing on specific image regions or details within a given sample -- we elicit such region-level processing from LVLMs themselves and use it as a chaining cue to detect and alleviate their own object hallucinations. Specifically, our R-CoV consists of six steps: initial response generation, entity extraction, coordinate generation, region description, verification execution, and final response generation. As a simple yet effective method, R-CoV can be seamlessly integrated into various LVLMs in a training-free manner and without relying on external detection models. Extensive experiments on several widely used hallucination benchmarks across multiple LVLMs demonstrate that R-CoV can significantly alleviate object hallucinations in LVLMs. Project page: https://github.com/Jiahao000/R-CoV.

AI.tldr: Region-aware Chain-of-Verification (R-CoV) is a post-hoc, training-free method to reduce object hallucinations in LVLMs by leveraging region-focused processing as a verification chain, using a six-step pipeline and compatible with multiple LVLMs; experiments report significant mitigation on hallucination benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Object hallucinations undermine LVLM reliability. A detector-free, post-hoc approach that exploits the model's own region-level reasoning can mitigate hallucinations without additional training or external detectors.
AI.method: A six-step pipeline: (1) initial response generation, (2) entity extraction, (3) coordinate generation, (4) region description, (5) verification execution, (6) final response generation. It elicits region-level processing from the LVLM itself to serve as a chaining cue for verification and correction, and is designed to be training-free and training-detector-free, modular, and easily integrated with various LVLMs.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple hallucination benchmarks across several LVLMs show that R-CoV significantly alleviates object hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: R-CoV provides a simple, effective, and broadly applicable post-hoc mechanism to reduce object hallucinations in LVLMs without additional detectors or training, with a straightforward integration path and demonstrated empirical benefits.

title: Lifecycle-Aware Federated Continual Learning in Mobile Autonomous Systems
summary: Federated continual learning (FCL) allows distributed autonomous fleets to adapt collaboratively to evolving terrain types across extended mission lifecycles. However, current approaches face several key challenges: 1) they use uniform protection strategies that do not account for the varying sensitivities to forgetting on different network layers; 2) they focus primarily on preventing forgetting during training, without addressing the long-term effects of cumulative drift; and 3) they often depend on idealized simulations that fail to capture the real-world heterogeneity present in distributed fleets. In this paper, we propose a lifecycle-aware dual-timescale FCL framework that incorporates training-time (pre-forgetting) prevention and (post-forgetting) recovery. Under this framework, we design a layer-selective rehearsal strategy that mitigates immediate forgetting during local training, and a rapid knowledge recovery strategy that restores degraded models after long-term cumulative drift. We present a theoretical analysis that characterizes heterogeneous forgetting dynamics and establishes the inevitability of long-term degradation. Our experimental results show that this framework achieves up to 8.3\% mIoU improvement over the strongest federated baseline and up to 31.7\% over conventional fine-tuning. We also deploy the FCL framework on a real-world rover testbed to assess system-level robustness under realistic constraints; the testing results further confirm the effectiveness of our FCL design.

AI.tldr: A lifecycle-aware, dual-timescale FCL framework with layer-selective rehearsal and rapid knowledge recovery; theoretical analysis of forgetting dynamics; validated with simulated benchmarks and a real rover deployment, showing improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Current FCL approaches use uniform protection across layers, ignore long-term cumulative drift, and rely on idealized simulations that miss real-world fleet heterogeneity. There is a need to address both training-time forgetting and post-forgetting recovery in realistic, heterogeneous environments.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-timescale FCL framework: (1) training-time pre-forgetting prevention via layer-selective rehearsal to mitigate immediate forgetting during local training; (2) post-forgetting rapid knowledge recovery to restore degraded models after long-term drift. Provides theoretical analysis of heterogeneous forgetting dynamics and inevitability of long-term degradation. Validates on simulated benchmarks and a real-world rover testbed.
AI.result: Achieves up to 8.3% mIoU improvement over the strongest federated baseline and up to 31.7% over conventional fine-tuning. Real rover deployment confirms system-level robustness under realistic constraints.
AI.conclusion: Lifecycle-aware dual-timescale FCL effectively tackles both immediate forgetting and long-term degradation, with evidence from theory and real-world deployment. The framework shifts focus from training-only protection to continuous recovery, highlighting remaining challenges in heterogeneity, scalability, and long-horizon evaluation.

title: SSL-R1: Self-Supervised Visual Reinforcement Post-Training for Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated the great potential of enhancing the reasoning abilities in multimodal large language models (MLLMs). However, the reliance on language-centric priors and expensive manual annotations prevents MLLMs' intrinsic visual understanding and scalable reward designs. In this work, we introduce SSL-R1, a generic self-supervised RL framework that derives verifiable rewards directly from images. To this end, we revisit self-supervised learning (SSL) in visual domains and reformulate widely-used SSL tasks into a set of verifiable visual puzzles for RL post-training, requiring neither human nor external model supervision. Training MLLMs on these tasks substantially improves their performance on multimodal understanding and reasoning benchmarks, highlighting the potential of leveraging vision-centric self-supervised tasks for MLLM post-training. We think this work will provide useful experience in devising effective self-supervised verifiable rewards to enable RL at scale. Project page: https://github.com/Jiahao000/SSL-R1.

AI.tldr: SSL-R1 introduces a self-supervised RL framework that generates verifiable rewards from images by converting visual SSL tasks into verifiable puzzles, enabling RL training without human supervision, improving MLLM multimodal understanding.
AI.motivation: Overcome dependence on language priors and costly annotations in RLVR for MLLMs; seek scalable, vision-based reward signals derived from SSL.
AI.method: Revisit widely-used visual SSL tasks and reformulate them into verifiable visual puzzles for RL post-training; design image-verifiable rewards derived from puzzle solvability/consistency; no human or external supervision required; train MLLMs on these tasks during RL post-training.
AI.result: Training on these vision-centric SSL puzzles yields substantial improvements on multimodal understanding and reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating the potential of vision-centric self-supervised rewards for scalable RL in MLLMs; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: Vision-centric self-supervised rewards can enable scalable RL post-training for MLLMs, reducing reliance on language priors and labeled data; the SSL-R1 framework offers a generic approach to derive verifiable rewards from SSL tasks.

title: Relative Entropy Estimation in Function Space: Theory and Applications to Trajectory Inference
summary: Trajectory Inference (TI) seeks to recover latent dynamical processes from snapshot data, where only independent samples from time-indexed marginals are observed. In applications such as single-cell genomics, destructive measurements make path-space laws non-identifiable from finitely many marginals, leaving held-out marginal prediction as the dominant but limited evaluation protocol. We introduce a general framework for estimating the Kullback-Leibler divergence (KL) divergence between probability measures on function space, yielding a tractable, data-driven estimator that is scalable to realistic snapshot datasets. We validate the accuracy of our estimator on a benchmark suite, where the estimated functional KL closely matches the analytic KL. Applying this framework to synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets, we show that current evaluation metrics often give inconsistent assessments, whereas path-space KL enables a coherent comparison of trajectory inference methods and exposes discrepancies in inferred dynamics, especially in regions with sparse or missing data. These results support functional KL as a principled criterion for evaluating trajectory inference under partial observability.

AI.tldr: A data-driven estimator of the KL divergence between path-space distributions enables principled evaluation of trajectory inference under partial observability.
AI.motivation: Path-space laws are non-identifiable from finite marginals in trajectory inference, particularly with destructive single-cell measurements; existing metrics can be unreliable.
AI.method: Proposes a scalable framework to estimate KL between probability measures on function space from snapshot data; validates against analytic KL on benchmarks and applies to synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets.
AI.result: The functional KL estimator closely matches the analytic KL; reveals inconsistencies in existing evaluation metrics; supports coherent ranking of TI methods and highlights discrepancies in sparse data regions.
AI.conclusion: Functional KL provides a principled criterion for evaluating trajectory inference under partial observability.

title: GeoRelight: Learning Joint Geometrical Relighting and Reconstruction with Flexible Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformers
summary: Relighting a person from a single photo is an attractive but ill-posed task, as a 2D image ambiguously entangles 3D geometry, intrinsic appearance, and illumination. Current methods either use sequential pipelines that suffer from error accumulation, or they do not explicitly leverage 3D geometry during relighting, which limits physical consistency. Since relighting and estimation of 3D geometry are mutually beneficial tasks, we propose a unified Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer (DiT) that jointly solves for both: GeoRelight. We make this possible through two key technical contributions: isotropic NDC-Orthographic Depth (iNOD), a distortion-free 3D representation compatible with latent diffusion models; and a strategic mixed-data training method that combines synthetic and auto-labeled real data. By solving geometry and relighting jointly, GeoRelight achieves better performance than both sequential models and previous systems that ignored geometry.

AI.tldr: Jointly estimate 3D geometry and relighting from a single photo using GeoRelight, a unification via a Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer with iNOD depth and mixed synthetic/auto-labeled data to improve physical consistency.
AI.motivation: Relighting a person from a single image is highly ill-posed due to entangled geometry, appearance, and illumination. 2D-only pipelines suffer from error accumulation and miss explicit 3D geometry, while geometry and relighting can mutually reinforce each other.
AI.method: Proposes GeoRelight, a unified Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer (DiT) that jointly solves geometry and relighting. Key components: isotropic NDC-Orthographic Depth (iNOD), a distortion-free 3D representation compatible with latent diffusion models, and a mixed-data training scheme combining synthetic and auto-labeled real data.
AI.result: Jointly solving geometry and relighting yields superior performance compared to sequential models and previous geometry-agnostic systems, indicating improved physical consistency and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the benefit of integrating 3D geometry estimation with relighting via iNOD and mixed-data training within a diffusion-Transformer framework, enabling more physically consistent and reliable face relighting from a single image.

title: Efficient Multi-Cohort Inference for Long-Term Effects and Lifetime Value in A/B Testing with User Learning
summary:
In streaming platforms churn is extremely costly, yet A/B tests are typically evaluated using outcomes observed within a limited experimental horizon. Even when both short- and predicted long-term engagement metrics are considered, they may fail to capture how a treatment affects users' retention. Consequently, an intervention may appear beneficial in the short term and neutral in the long term while still generating lower total value than the control due to users churn.
  To address this limitation, we introduce a method that estimates long-term treatment effects (LTE) and residual lifetime value change ($ΔERLV$) in short multi-cohort A/B tests under user learning. To estimate time-varying treatment effects efficiently, we introduce an inverse-variance weighted estimator that combines multiple cohorts estimates, reducing variance relative to standard approaches in the literature. The estimated treatment trajectory is then modeled as a parametric decay to recover both the asymptotic treatment effect and the cumulative value generated over time.
  Our framework enables simultaneous evaluation of steady-state impact and residual user value within a single experiment. Empirical results show improved precision in estimating LTE and $ΔERLV$ and identify scenarios in which relying on either short-term or long-term metrics alone would lead to incorrect product decisions.

AI.tldr: Proposes LTE and ΔERLV estimation for short multi-cohort A/B tests with user learning in streaming platforms; uses inverse-variance weighting and a decaying treatment-effect model to capture long-run impact and value.
AI.motivation: Churn in streaming platforms makes long-term effects critical; standard A/B metrics on short horizons miss retention and lifetime value effects, potentially misinforming decisions.
AI.method: Inverse-variance weighted estimator aggregating cross-cohort estimates of time-varying treatment effects; parametric decay model of the treatment trajectory to recover asymptotic effects and cumulative value, enabling estimation of LTE and ΔERLV.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved precision in LTE and ΔERLV estimates and identify scenarios where relying on only short-term or only long-term metrics could lead to incorrect product decisions.
AI.conclusion: Framework enables simultaneous evaluation of steady-state impact and residual user value within a single experiment, improving decision quality for interventions under user learning.

title: Render-in-the-Loop: Vector Graphics Generation via Visual Self-Feedback
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promising capabilities in generating Scalable Vector Graphics (SVG) via direct code synthesis. However, existing paradigms typically adopt an open-loop "blind drawing" approach, where models generate symbolic code sequences without perceiving intermediate visual outcomes. This methodology severely underutilizes the powerful visual priors embedded in MLLMs vision encoders, treating SVG generation as a disjointed textual sequence modeling task rather than an integrated visuo-spatial one. Consequently, models struggle to reason about partial canvas states and implicit occlusion relationships, which are visually explicit but textually ambiguous. To bridge this gap, we propose Render-in-the-Loop, a novel generation paradigm that reformulates SVG synthesis as a step-wise, visual-context-aware process. By rendering intermediate code states into a cumulative canvas, the model explicitly observes the evolving visual context at each step, leveraging on-the-fly feedback to guide subsequent generation. However, we demonstrate that applying this visual loop naively to off-the-shelf models is suboptimal due to their inability to leverage incremental visual-code mappings. To address this, we first utilize fine-grained path decomposition to construct dense multi-step visual trajectories, and then introduce a Visual Self-Feedback (VSF) training strategy to condition the next primitive generation on intermediate visual states. Furthermore, a Render-and-Verify (RaV) inference mechanism is proposed to effectively filter degenerate and redundant primitives. Our framework, instantiated on a multimodal foundation model, outperforms strong open-weight baselines on the standard MMSVGBench. This result highlights the remarkable data efficiency and generalization capability of our Render-in-the-Loop paradigm for both Text-to-SVG and Image-to-SVG tasks.

AI.tldr: Render-in-the-Loop reframes SVG generation as a stepwise, visual-context-aware process that renders intermediate code to a canvas, enabling feedback-based generation. It uses fine-grained path decomposition for dense multi-step visual trajectories, Visual Self-Feedback (VSF) to condition the next primitive on visual states, and Render-and-Verify (RaV) to filter degenerates. Instantiated on a multimodal foundation model, it outperforms open-weight baselines on MMSVGBench for Text-to-SVG and Image-to-SVG, showing data efficiency and generalization.
AI.motivation: Open-loop text-only SVG synthesis fails to leverage rich vision priors and struggles with partial canvas reasoning and occlusion, treating visuo-spatial generation as disjointed symbol generation and hindering integration with trajectory-aware drawing.
AI.method: 1) Render intermediate code into a cumulative canvas to observe evolving visuals. 2) Use fine-grained path decomposition to create dense multi-step visual trajectories. 3) Introduce Visual Self-Feedback (VSF) to condition the next primitive on intermediate visuals. 4) Propose Render-and-Verify (RaV) during inference to filter degenerate/redundant primitives. 5) Instantiate on a multimodal foundation model and apply to Text-to-SVG and Image-to-SVG tasks.
AI.result: Outperforms strong open-weight baselines on MMSVGBench, demonstrating superior data efficiency and generalization for both Text-to-SVG and Image-to-SVG tasks.
AI.conclusion: Render-in-the-Loop effectively integrates visuo-spatial reasoning into SVG synthesis, leveraging intermediate visual states for guided generation. VSF and RaV are key components enabling improved performance and robustness, with strong potential for broader visuo-conditioned generation.

title: Physics-Conditioned Synthesis of Internal Ice-Layer Thickness for Incomplete Layer Traces
comment: Accepted for 2026 IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium (IGARSS 2026)
summary: Internal ice layers imaged by radar provide key evidence of snow accumulation and ice dynamics, but radar-derived layer boundary observations are often incomplete, with discontinuous traces and sometimes entirely missing layers, due to limited resolution, sensor noise, and signal loss. Existing graph-based models for ice stratigraphy generally assume sufficiently complete layer profiles and focus on predicting deeper-layer thickness from reliably traced shallow layers. In this work, we address the layer-completion problem itself by synthesizing complete ice-layer thickness annotations from incomplete radar-derived layer traces by conditioning on colocated physical features synchronized from physical climate models. The proposed network combines geometric learning to aggregate within-layer spatial context with a transformer-based temporal module that propagates information across layers to encourage coherent stratigraphy and consistent thickness evolution. To learn from incomplete supervision, we optimize a mask-aware robust regression objective that evaluates errors only at observed thickness values and normalizes by the number of valid entries, enabling stable training under varying sparsity without imputation and steering completions toward physically plausible values. The model preserves observed thickness where available and infers only missing regions, recovering fragmented segments and even fully absent layers while remaining consistent with measured traces. As an additional benefit, the synthesized thickness stacks provide effective pretraining supervision for a downstream deep-layer predictor, improving fine-tuned accuracy over training from scratch on the same fully traced data.

AI.tldr: A method to complete incomplete radar-derived ice-layer data by fusing geometric learning with transformer-based temporal reasoning, using a mask-aware robust regression that learns from sparse supervision and leverages colocated climate features. It enables coherent stratigraphy and yields useful pretraining data for downstream deep-layer prediction.
AI.motivation: Radar-derived ice-layer boundaries are often incomplete due to resolution, noise, and signal loss. Existing models assume complete profiles and mainly predict deeper-layer thickness from reliable shallow traces, motivating a method that can recover missing layers.
AI.method: A network that combines geometric learning to aggregate within-layer spatial context with a transformer-based temporal module to propagate information across layers.
