It uses a mask-aware robust regression objective, evaluating errors only on observed thickness values and normalizing by the number of valid entries. Colocated physical features from climate models are used to condition the synthesis. The model preserves observed thickness and infers missing regions without imputation, producing coherent, physically plausible thickness stacks. It also provides pretraining data for a downstream deep-layer predictor.
AI.result: The approach recovers fragmented segments and fully missing layers, yielding thickness stacks consistent with measured traces. It improves accuracy for a downstream deep-layer predictor when used as pretraining data, compared to training from scratch on fully traced data.
AI.conclusion: Combining geometric context with temporal transformers and a mask-aware training objective enables robust completion of sparse ice-layer traces, producing physically plausible stratigraphy and beneficial pretraining data for deeper layer prediction.

title: Amodal SAM: A Unified Amodal Segmentation Framework with Generalization
summary: Amodal segmentation is a challenging task that aims to predict the complete geometric shape of objects, including their occluded regions. Although existing methods primarily focus on amodal segmentation within the training domain, these approaches often lack the generalization capacity to extend effectively to novel object categories and unseen contexts. This paper introduces Amodal SAM, a unified framework that leverages SAM (Segment Anything Model) for both amodal image and amodal video segmentation. Amodal SAM preserves the powerful generalization ability of SAM while extending its inherent capabilities to the amodal segmentation task. The improvements lie in three aspects: (1) a lightweight Spatial Completion Adapter that enables occluded region reconstruction, (2) a Target-Aware Occlusion Synthesis (TAOS) pipeline that addresses the scarcity of amodal annotations by generating diverse synthetic training data, and (3) novel learning objectives that enforce regional consistency and topological regularization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Amodal SAM achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks, while simultaneously exhibiting robust generalization to novel scenarios. We anticipate that this research will advance the field toward practical amodal segmentation systems capable of operating effectively in unconstrained real-world environments.

AI.tldr: Amodal SAM unifies amodal image/video segmentation by leveraging SAM with a Spatial Completion Adapter, TAOS data synthesis, and regional/topological losses, achieving state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization.
AI.motivation: Amodal segmentation remains challenging due to occlusions and poor generalization to unseen object categories and contexts. Existing methods rely on domain-specific training and struggle with unconstrained real-world scenarios.
AI.method: Integrates SAM as backbone for amodal segmentation. Introduces a lightweight Spatial Completion Adapter to reconstruct occluded regions. Employs Target-Aware Occlusion Synthesis (TAOS) to generate diverse synthetic amodal training data. Proposes learning objectives enforcing regional consistency and topological regularization. Applicable to both image and video segmentation.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on standard amodal benchmarks and strong generalization to novel scenarios across image and video data. Extensive experiments validate effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Amodal SAM advances practical amodal segmentation by preserving SAM's generalization while extending it to occluded shapes, bringing closer deployment in unconstrained real-world environments.

title: ParetoSlider: Diffusion Models Post-Training for Continuous Reward Control
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training has become the standard for aligning generative models with human preferences, yet most methods rely on a single scalar reward. When multiple criteria matter, the prevailing practice of ``early scalarization'' collapses rewards into a fixed weighted sum. This commits the model to a single trade-off point at training time, providing no inference-time control over inherently conflicting goals -- such as prompt adherence versus source fidelity in image editing. We introduce ParetoSlider, a multi-objective RL (MORL) framework that trains a single diffusion model to approximate the entire Pareto front. By training the model with continuously varying preference weights as a conditioning signal, we enable users to navigate optimal trade-offs at inference time without retraining or maintaining multiple checkpoints. We evaluate ParetoSlider across three state-of-the-art flow-matching backbones: SD3.5, FluxKontext, and LTX-2. Our single preference-conditioned model matches or exceeds the performance of baselines trained separately for fixed reward trade-offs, while uniquely providing fine-grained control over competing generative goals.

AI.tldr: ParetoSlider trains a single diffusion model conditioned on continuous preference weights to approximate the full Pareto front for multi-objective RL in generative modeling, enabling inference-time trade-off control without retraining; validated on three diffusion backbones, matching or surpassing fixed-trade-off baselines and enabling fine-grained control.
AI.motivation: Early scalarization in MORL fixes a single trade-off and eliminates inference-time control, which is problematic for generative tasks needing to balance conflicting goals (e.g., prompt adherence vs. source fidelity). A single model that can navigate the Pareto front at inference would provide flexible, user-driven trade-offs.
AI.method: Train a diffusion model with continuously varying preference weights as a conditioning signal to approximate the entire Pareto front, allowing users to adjust trade-offs at inference time without retraining or maintaining multiple checkpoints. Evaluate on three backbones (SD3.5, FluxKontext, LTX-2).
AI.result: A single preference-conditioned model matches or exceeds baselines trained separately for fixed reward trade-offs and uniquely provides fine-grained control over competing generative goals across three backbones.
AI.conclusion: ParetoSlider enables multi-objective RL with inference-time control using a single, conditioned model, achieving Pareto-front approximation across multiple diffusion backbones without retraining.

title: Exploring High-Order Self-Similarity for Video Understanding
summary: Space-time self-similarity (STSS), which captures visual correspondences across frames, provides an effective way to represent temporal dynamics for video understanding. In this work, we explore higher-order STSS and demonstrate how STSSs at different orders reveal distinct aspects of these dynamics. We then introduce the Multi-Order Self-Similarity (MOSS) module, a lightweight neural module designed to learn and integrate multi-order STSS features. It can be applied to diverse video tasks to enhance motion modeling capabilities while consuming only marginal computational cost and memory usage. Extensive experiments on video action recognition, motion-centric video VQA, and real-world robotic tasks consistently demonstrate substantial improvements, validating the broad applicability of MOSS as a general temporal modeling module. The source code and checkpoints will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces Multi-Order Self-Similarity (MOSS) to model higher-order space-time self-similarity for videos, achieving improved motion modeling with low overhead across multiple tasks.
AI.motivation: STSS captures cross-frame correspondences, but higher-order STSS potentially encodes richer temporal dynamics. There is a need for a lightweight, general module that can learn and fuse multi-order STSS features for diverse video tasks.
AI.method: Proposes MOSS, a lightweight neural module that learns and integrates multi-order STSS representations. It is designed as a plug-in temporal modeling block applicable to various video tasks (action recognition, motion-centric VQA, robotics) with minimal additional computation and memory.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show substantial performance gains across action recognition, motion-centric VQA, and robotic tasks, validating MOSS as a broadly applicable temporal modeling module with modest resource overhead. Source code/checkpoints to be released.
AI.conclusion: MOSS provides a general, efficient approach to temporal modeling by leveraging higher-order STSS, enabling improved motion understanding across domains with limited computational impact. It has broad applicability and potential for integration into existing video architectures.

title: Stream-CQSA: Avoiding Out-of-Memory in Attention Computation via Flexible Workload Scheduling
summary: The scalability of long-context large language models is fundamentally limited by the quadratic memory cost of exact self-attention, which often leads to out-of-memory (OOM) failures on modern hardware. Existing methods improve memory efficiency to near-linear complexity, while assuming that the full query, key, and value tensors fit in device memory. In this work, we remove this assumption by introducing CQS Divide, an operation derived from cyclic quorum sets (CQS) theory that decomposes attention into a set of independent subsequence computations whose recomposition yields exactly the same result as full-sequence attention. Exploiting this decomposition, we introduce Stream-CQSA, a memory-adaptive scheduling framework that partitions attention into subproblems that fit within arbitrary memory budgets. This recasts attention from a logically monolithic operation into a collection of schedulable tasks, enabling flexible execution across devices without inter-device communication. Experiments demonstrate predictable memory scaling and show that exact attention over billion-token sequences can be executed on a single GPU via streaming, without changing the underlying mathematical definition of attention or introducing approximation error.

AI.tldr: Introduces CQS Divide and Stream-CQSA to enable exact, memory-adaptive self-attention on long sequences by decomposing attention into manageable subproblems without approximation.
AI.motivation: Quadratic memory cost of exact self-attention for long contexts, with prior methods assuming full Q/K/V reside in device memory. The work aims to enable exact attention under limited memory and scale to billion-token sequences on a single GPU.
AI.method: Proposes CQS Divide, an operation from cyclic quorum sets theory that decomposes attention into independent subsequences whose recomposition yields the exact full-sequence result. Builds Stream-CQSA, a memory-adaptive scheduling framework that partitions attention into subproblems fitting a given memory budget, treating attention as a collection of schedulable tasks with no inter-device communication.
AI.result: Demonstrates predictable memory scaling; shows exact attention over billion-token sequences can be executed on a single GPU via streaming without changing the attention’s mathematical definition or introducing approximation error.
AI.conclusion: Validates the feasibility of exact long-context attention under practical memory constraints and provides a scheduling-based framework (Stream-CQSA) to scale exact attention without approximation, enabling new long-sequence workflows; potential considerations include scheduling overhead and hardware-specific performance.

title: GeoRect4D: Geometry-Compatible Generative Rectification for Dynamic Sparse-View 3D Reconstruction
summary: Reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes from sparse multi-view videos is highly ill-posed, often leading to geometric collapse, trajectory drift, and floating artifacts. Recent attempts introduce generative priors to hallucinate missing content, yet naive integration frequently causes structural drift and temporal inconsistency due to the mismatch between stochastic 2D generation and deterministic 3D geometry. In this paper, we propose GeoRect4D, a novel unified framework for sparse-view dynamic reconstruction that couples explicit 3D consistency with generative refinement via a closed-loop optimization process. Specifically, GeoRect4D introduces a degradation-aware feedback mechanism that incorporates a robust anchor-based dynamic 3DGS substrate with a single-step diffusion rectifier to hallucinate high-fidelity details. This rectifier utilizes a structural locking mechanism and spatiotemporal coordinated attention, effectively preserving physical plausibility while restoring missing content. Furthermore, we present a progressive optimization strategy that employs stochastic geometric purification to eliminate floaters and generative distillation to infuse texture details into the explicit representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GeoRect4D achieves state-of-the-art performance in reconstruction fidelity, perceptual quality, and spatiotemporal consistency across multiple datasets.

AI.tldr: GeoRect4D unifies explicit 3D consistency with a single-step diffusion rectifier and progressive optimization to reconstruct dynamic scenes from sparse views, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity and spatiotemporal consistency.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes from sparse multi-view videos is highly ill-posed, often suffering from geometric collapse, trajectory drift, and floating artifacts. While generative priors can hallucinate missing content, naive integration causes structural drift and temporal inconsistency due to the mismatch between stochastic 2D generation and deterministic 3D geometry. A method that enforces 3D consistency while refining details via generative priors in a closed loop is needed.
AI.method: GeoRect4D introduces a degradation-aware feedback loop that couples an anchor-based dynamic 3DGS substrate with a single-step diffusion rectifier to hallucinate high-fidelity details. The rectifier employs a structural locking mechanism and spatiotemporal coordinated attention to preserve physical plausibility while restoring content. A progressive optimization strategy is used, featuring stochastic geometric purification to remove floaters and generative distillation to infuse texture details into the explicit representation, all within a closed-loop optimization that ties explicit 3D reconstruction with generative refinement.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments, GeoRect4D achieves state-of-the-art performance in reconstruction fidelity, perceptual quality, and spatiotemporal consistency across multiple datasets for sparse-view dynamic reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: GeoRect4D demonstrates that integrating explicit 3D consistency with diffusion-based refinement in a closed-loop framework, augmented by progressive purification and distillation, yields robust, high-fidelity, temporally coherent dynamic 3D reconstructions from sparse views.

title: Closing the Domain Gap in Biomedical Imaging by In-Context Control Samples
summary: The central problem in biomedical imaging are batch effects: systematic technical variations unrelated to the biological signal of interest. These batch effects critically undermine experimental reproducibility and are the primary cause of failure of deep learning systems on new experimental batches, preventing their practical use in the real world. Despite years of research, no method has succeeded in closing this performance gap for deep learning models. We propose Control-Stabilized Adaptive Risk Minimization via Batch Normalization (CS-ARM-BN), a meta-learning adaptation method that exploits negative control samples. Such unperturbed reference images are present in every experimental batch by design and serve as stable context for adaptation. We validate our novel method on Mechanism-of-Action (MoA) classification, a crucial task for drug discovery, on the large-scale JUMP-CP dataset. The accuracy of standard ResNets drops from 0.939 $\pm$ 0.005, on the training domain, to 0.862 $\pm$ 0.060 on data from new experimental batches. Foundation models, even after Typical Variation Normalization, fail to close this gap. We are the first to show that meta-learning approaches close the domain gap by achieving 0.935 $\pm$ 0.018. If the new experimental batches exhibit strong domain shifts, such as being generated in a different lab, meta-learning approaches can be stabilized with control samples, which are always available in biomedical experiments. Our work shows that batch effects in bioimaging data can be effectively neutralized through principled in-context adaptation, which also makes them practically usable and efficient.

AI.tldr: CS-ARM-BN uses control-stabilized meta-learning to adapt to batch effects in bioimaging, leveraging negative control samples to perform in-context adaptation; achieves near-baseline MoA classification across new experimental batches, outperforming standard ResNets and foundation models.
AI.motivation: Batch effects undermine reproducibility and domain transfer in bioimaging; existing deep learning models lose performance on new experimental batches; negative controls are readily available but underused for adaptation.
AI.method: Propose CS-ARM-BN, a meta-learning adaptation framework that uses negative control samples within each batch to stabilize Adaptive Risk Minimization via Batch Normalization; leverages in-context adaptation to neutralize batch effects, enabling robust MoA classification on JUMP-CP.
AI.result: ResNet performance drops from 0.939 to 0.862 when tested on new batches; foundation models underperform even after Typical Variation Normalization; CS-ARM-BN/meta-learning with control samples achieves 0.935 ± 0.018, effectively closing the gap.
AI.conclusion: Batch effects in bioimaging can be effectively neutralized by principled in-context meta-learning using stable control samples, enabling practical, efficient deployment of deep learning models across experimental batches.

title: LLaDA2.0-Uni: Unifying Multimodal Understanding and Generation with Diffusion Large Language Model
summary: We present LLaDA2.0-Uni, a unified discrete diffusion large language model (dLLM) that supports multimodal understanding and generation within a natively integrated framework. Its architecture combines a fully semantic discrete tokenizer, a MoE-based dLLM backbone, and a diffusion decoder. By discretizing continuous visual inputs via SigLIP-VQ, the model enables block-level masked diffusion for both text and vision inputs within the backbone, while the decoder reconstructs visual tokens into high-fidelity images. Inference efficiency is enhanced beyond parallel decoding through prefix-aware optimizations in the backbone and few-step distillation in the decoder. Supported by carefully curated large-scale data and a tailored multi-stage training pipeline, LLaDA2.0-Uni matches specialized VLMs in multimodal understanding while delivering strong performance in image generation and editing. Its native support for interleaved generation and reasoning establishes a promising and scalable paradigm for next-generation unified foundation models. Codes and models are available at https://github.com/inclusionAI/LLaDA2.0-Uni.

AI.tldr: A unified discrete diffusion LLM (dLLM) called LLaDA2.0-Uni integrates a semantic discrete tokenizer, MoE-based backbone, and a diffusion decoder to achieve multimodal understanding and generation within a single framework, using SigLIP-VQ for visual discretization and diffusion-based image reconstruction, with efficiency gains via prefix-aware backbones and few-step distillation.
AI.motivation: Address the need for a single, scalable foundation model that can jointly understand and generate across text and vision, enabling interleaved generation and reasoning with improved inference efficiency.
AI.method: Architecture combines: (1) a fully semantic discrete tokenizer; (2) a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLM backbone; (3) a diffusion decoder. Visual inputs are discretized with SigLIP-VQ for block-level masked diffusion in the backbone; the decoder reconstructs high-fidelity images from discrete visual tokens. Inference is sped up with prefix-aware optimizations and few-step distillation. Training uses a tailored multi-stage pipeline on large-scale data.
AI.result: Claims that LLaDA2.0-Uni matches specialized vision-language models (VLMs) in multimodal understanding and delivers strong image generation/editing capabilities, with native support for interleaved reasoning and generation. The approach reportedly improves inference efficiency beyond parallel decoding.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a promising, scalable paradigm for next-generation unified foundation models, demonstrating that discreteness, an MoE backbone, and diffusion decoding can jointly handle multimodal understanding and generation within a single framework; code and models are publicly available.

title: FedSIR: Spectral Client Identification and Relabeling for Federated Learning with Noisy Labels
comment: Accepted at the 5th Workshop on Federated Learning for Computer Vision (FedVision), CVPR 2026. Sina Gholami and Abdulmoneam Ali contributed equally
summary:
Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data; however, the presence of noisy labels across distributed clients can severely degrade the learning performance. In this paper, we propose FedSIR, a multi-stage framework for robust FL under noisy labels. Different from existing approaches that mainly rely on designing noise-tolerant loss functions or exploiting loss dynamics during training, our method leverages the spectral structure of client feature representations to identify and mitigate label noise.
  Our framework consists of three key components. First, we identify clean and noisy clients by analyzing the spectral consistency of class-wise feature subspaces with minimal communication overhead. Second, clean clients provide spectral references that enable noisy clients to relabel potentially corrupted samples using both dominant class directions and residual subspaces. Third, we employ a noise-aware training strategy that integrates logit-adjusted loss, knowledge distillation, and distance-aware aggregation to further stabilize federated optimization. Extensive experiments on standard FL benchmarks demonstrate that FedSIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods for FL with noisy labels. The code is available at https://github.com/sinagh72/FedSIR.

AI.tldr: FedSIR is a three-stage spectral framework for robust Federated Learning under noisy labels: (i) identify clean vs. noisy clients via spectral consistency of class-wise feature subspaces with low communication, (ii) clean clients provide spectral references to enable relabeling on noisy clients using dominant directions and residual subspaces, and (iii) a noise-aware training strategy combining logit-adjusted loss, knowledge distillation, and distance-aware aggregation to stabilize federated optimization; it achieves superior performance on standard FL benchmarks and has open-source code.
AI.motivation: Noisy labels across distributed FL clients significantly harm model performance. Existing solutions focus on robust loss functions or training loss dynamics but may ignore the geometric structure of feature representations across clients. Exploiting spectral subspace structure offers a way to identify trustworthy clients and relabel noisy data with minimal communication.
AI.method: Three-stage framework: (1) spectral-based client selection by evaluating the consistency of class-wise feature subspaces to separate clean and noisy clients with minimal communication; (2) clean clients supply spectral references enabling noisy clients to relabel corrupted samples via dominant class directions and residual subspaces; (3) noise-aware training that fuses logit-adjusted loss, knowledge distillation from clean spectral references, and distance-aware aggregation during federated optimization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on standard FL benchmarks shows FedSIR consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods for FL with noisy labels, demonstrating robust performance and generalization, with code available at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: Spectral structure of client feature representations can effectively identify clean clients and enable reliable relabeling of noisy data in FL. Integrating this with a noise-aware training strategy yields improved robustness to label noise while maintaining efficient communication, making FedSIR competitive with or superior to existing approaches.

title: LEXIS: LatEnt ProXimal Interaction Signatures for 3D HOI from an Image
comment: 26 pages, 11 figures, 4 tables. Project page: https://anticdimi.github.io/lexis
summary: Reconstructing 3D Human-Object Interaction from an RGB image is essential for perceptive systems. Yet, this remains challenging as it requires capturing the subtle physical coupling between the body and objects. While current methods rely on sparse, binary contact cues, these fail to model the continuous proximity and dense spatial relationships that characterize natural interactions. We address this limitation via InterFields, a representation that encodes dense, continuous proximity across the entire body and object surfaces. However, inferring these fields from single images is inherently ill-posed. To tackle this, our intuition is that interaction patterns are characteristically structured by the action and object geometry. We capture this structure in LEXIS, a novel discrete manifold of interaction signatures learned via a VQ-VAE. We then develop LEXIS-Flow, a diffusion framework that leverages LEXIS signatures to estimate human and object meshes alongside their InterFields. Notably, these InterFields help in a guided refinement that ensures physically-plausible, proximity-aware reconstructions without requiring post-hoc optimization. Evaluation on Open3DHOI and BEHAVE shows that LEXIS-Flow significantly outperforms existing SotA baselines in reconstruction, contact, and proximity quality. Our approach not only improves generalization but also yields reconstructions perceived as more realistic, moving us closer to holistic 3D scene understanding. Code & models will be public at https://anticdimi.github.io/lexis.

AI.tldr: Dense InterFields encode continuous body-object proximity; LEXIS learns a discrete interaction-signature manifold via VQ-VAE; LEXIS-Flow uses diffusion to estimate human/object meshes and InterFields with guided refinement; outperforms baselines on Open3DHOI/BEHAVE.
AI.motivation: Current HOI reconstruction from single RGB images relies on sparse, binary contact cues, failing to capture continuous proximity and dense spatial relations between body and objects.
AI.method: Introduce InterFields for dense proximity; learn LEXIS as a discrete manifold of interaction signatures with a VQ-VAE; apply LEXIS-Flow, a diffusion-based pipeline that uses LEXIS to estimate meshes and InterFields; then refine with InterFields to ensure physically plausible, proximity-aware reconstructions without post-hoc optimization.
AI.result: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in reconstruction quality, contact accuracy, and proximity fidelity on Open3DHOI and BEHAVE; better generalization and more realistic reconstructions.
AI.conclusion: Advances holistic 3D scene understanding by leveraging dense proximity fields guided by learned interaction signatures; code and models will be released.

title: OMIBench: Benchmarking Olympiad-Level Multi-Image Reasoning in Large Vision-Language Model
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have made substantial advances in reasoning tasks at the Olympiad level. Nevertheless, current Olympiad-level multimodal reasoning benchmarks for these models often emphasize single-image analysis and fail to exploit contextual information across multiple images. We present OMIBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate Olympiad-level reasoning when the required evidence is distributed over multiple images. It contains problems from biology, chemistry, mathematics, and physics Olympiads, together with manually annotated rationales and evaluation protocols for both exact and semantic answer matching. Across extensive experiments on OMIBench, we observe meaningful performance gaps in existing models. Even the strongest LVLMs, such as Gemini-3-Pro, attain only about 50% on the benchmark. These results position OMIBench as a focused resources for studying and improving multi-image reasoning in LVLMs.

AI.tldr: OMIBench is a multi-image reasoning benchmark for LVLMs, targeting Olympiad-style problems across biology, chemistry, math, and physics. It addresses the gap in existing benchmarks that focus on single images and requires consistent evidence across multiple images with annotated rationales and exact/semantic evaluation protocols. Top LVLMs still lag (~50%), highlighting substantial room for improvement in cross-image reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current Olympiad-level benchmarks in multimodal vision-language models emphasize single-image analysis and fail to test reasoning that aggregates evidence from multiple images. There is a need to evaluate cross-image reasoning capabilities of LVLMs on problems requiring distributed evidence.
AI.method: Introduce OMIBench consisting of Olympiad-style problems across biology, chemistry, mathematics, and physics. Provide manually annotated rationales and evaluation protocols for both exact and semantic answer matching. Evaluate state-of-the-art LVLMs (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro) on the benchmark with multi-image reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Significant performance gaps observed: even the strongest LVLMs achieve only around 50% accuracy on OMIBench, indicating that multi-image reasoning remains a bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: OMIBench fills a specialized niche for evaluating and advancing multi-image reasoning in LVLMs, enabling targeted research to improve cross-image information integration and reasoning capabilities.

title: Adapting TrOCR for Printed Tigrinya Text Recognition: Word-Aware Loss Weighting for Cross-Script Transfer Learning
comment: Code and models available at https://github.com/YoHa2024NKU/Tigrinya_TrOCR_Printed Pre-trained models: https://huggingface.co/Yonatanhaile2026/tigrinya-trocrprinted, https://huggingface.co/Yonatanhaile2026/tigrinya-trocrhandwritten
summary: Transformer-based OCR models have shown strong performance on Latin and CJK scripts, but their application to African syllabic writing systems remains limited. We present the first adaptation of TrOCR for printed Tigrinya using the Ge'ez script. Starting from a pre-trained model, we extend the byte-level BPE tokenizer to cover 230 Ge'ez characters and introduce Word-Aware Loss Weighting to resolve systematic word-boundary failures that arise when applying Latin-centric BPE conventions to a new script. The unmodified model produces no usable output on Ge'ez text. After adaptation, the TrOCR-Printed variant achieves 0.22% Character Error Rate and 97.20% exact match accuracy on a held-out test set of 5,000 synthetic images from the GLOCR dataset. An ablation study confirms that Word-Aware Loss Weighting is the critical component, reducing CER by two orders of magnitude compared to vocabulary extension alone. The full pipeline trains in under three hours on a single 8 GB consumer GPU. All code, model weights, and evaluation scripts are publicly released.

AI.tldr: First adaptation of TrOCR to printed Ge'ez (Tigrinya) OCR; extend BPE to 230 Ge'ez chars; Word-Aware Loss Weighting crucial; CER 0.22%, exact 97.20%; training under 3h on 8 GB GPU; code released.
AI.motivation: African syllabic writing systems are underrepresented in OCR. The work addresses word-boundary errors from Latin-centric BPE adaptations and demonstrates practical OCR for Ge'ez-script text.
AI.method: Fine-tune a pre-trained TrOCR model. Extend the byte-level BPE tokenizer to cover 230 Ge'ez characters. Introduce Word-Aware Loss Weighting to mitigate word-boundary failures. Evaluate on 5,000 synthetic Ge'ez images from the GLOCR dataset. Conduct ablation to compare vocabulary extension alone vs with Word-Aware Loss Weighting.
AI.result: The unmodified model produced no usable Ge'ez output. After adaptation, TrOCR-Printed achieves 0.22% CER and 97.20% exact-match on held-out test data. Ablation shows Word-Aware Loss Weighting reduces CER by about two orders of magnitude compared to vocabulary extension alone. Training completes in under three hours on a single 8 GB GPU. All code, weights, and evaluation scripts are publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Word-Aware Loss Weighting is the critical component enabling accurate OCR for Ge'ez-script text. The work demonstrates feasibility and efficiency of adapting transformer-based OCR to African syllabic scripts and supports reproducibility through released resources.

title: Global Offshore Wind Infrastructure: Deployment and Operational Dynamics from Dense Sentinel-1 Time Series
summary: The offshore wind energy sector is expanding rapidly, increasing the need for independent, high-temporal-resolution monitoring of infrastructure deployment and operation at global scale. While Earth Observation based offshore wind infrastructure mapping has matured for spatial localization, existing open datasets lack temporally dense and semantically fine-grained information on construction and operational dynamics. We introduce a global Sentinel-1 synthetic aperture radar (SAR) time series data corpus that resolves deployment and operational phases of offshore wind infrastructure from 2016Q1 to 2025Q1. Building on an updated object detection workflow, we compile 15,606 time series at detected infrastructure locations, with overall 14,840,637 events as analysis-ready 1D SAR backscatter profiles, one profile per Sentinel-1 acquisition and location. To enable direct use and benchmarking, we release (i) the analysis ready 1D SAR profiles, (ii) event-level baseline semantic labels generated by a rule-based classifier, and (iii) an expert-annotated benchmark dataset of 553 time series with 328,657 event labels. The baseline classifier achieves a macro F1 score of 0.84 in event-wise evaluation and an area under the collapsed edit similarity-quality threshold curve (AUC) of 0.785, indicating temporal coherence. We demonstrate that the resulting corpus supports global-scale analyses of deployment dynamics, the identification of differences in regional deployment patterns, vessel interactions, and operational events, and provides a reference for developing and comparing time series classification methods for offshore wind infrastructure monitoring.

AI.tldr: Global Sentinel-1 SAR time-series corpus for offshore wind infrastructure enabling detection of deployment and operational phases; large-scale, temporally dense dataset with event labels and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in temporally dense, semantically fine-grained offshore wind monitoring at global scale; current datasets give spatial localization but lack temporal dynamics and event-level information; enable benchmarking of time-series classification methods and analysis of deployment patterns.
AI.method: Updated object detection workflow to identify offshore wind infrastructure; generate 15,606 time series with 14,840,637 events of 1D SAR backscatter profiles; release: (i) analysis-ready 1D SAR profiles, (ii) rule-based baseline semantic labels, (iii) expert-annotated benchmark with 553 time series and 328,657 event labels.
AI.result: A comprehensive, globally distributed SAR time-series corpus for offshore wind, with extensive event annotations and a baseline classifier achieving macro F1 = 0.84 and AUC = 0.785; enables global deployment-dynamics analyses and benchmarking of time-series classification methods.
AI.conclusion: The dataset provides a reference resource for studying deployment dynamics, regional deployment differences, vessel interactions, and operational events in offshore wind, and establishes a benchmark for future time-series analysis methods.

title: DeVI: Physics-based Dexterous Human-Object Interaction via Synthetic Video Imitation
summary: Recent advances in video generative models enable the synthesis of realistic human-object interaction videos across a wide range of scenarios and object categories, including complex dexterous manipulations that are difficult to capture with motion capture systems. While the rich interaction knowledge embedded in these synthetic videos holds strong potential for motion planning in dexterous robotic manipulation, their limited physical fidelity and purely 2D nature make them difficult to use directly as imitation targets in physics-based character control. We present DeVI (Dexterous Video Imitation), a novel framework that leverages text-conditioned synthetic videos to enable physically plausible dexterous agent control for interacting with unseen target objects. To overcome the imprecision of generative 2D cues, we introduce a hybrid tracking reward that integrates 3D human tracking with robust 2D object tracking. Unlike methods relying on high-quality 3D kinematic demonstrations, DeVI requires only the generated video, enabling zero-shot generalization across diverse objects and interaction types. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeVI outperforms existing approaches that imitate 3D human-object interaction demonstrations, particularly in modeling dexterous hand-object interactions. We further validate the effectiveness of DeVI in multi-object scenes and text-driven action diversity, showcasing the advantage of using video as an HOI-aware motion planner.

AI.tldr: DeVI is a video-based imitation framework that uses text-conditioned synthetic videos and a hybrid 3D+2D tracking reward to enable physically plausible dexterous manipulation and zero-shot generalization to unseen objects, outperforming methods relying on 3D demonstrations.
AI.motivation: To overcome the 2D limitations and limited physics fidelity of synthetic videos for dexterous manipulation, and to achieve cross-object generalization without needing high-quality 3D demonstrations by leveraging HOI-aware video as planning signals.
AI.method: Introduce DeVI, which utilizes text-conditioned synthetic videos and a hybrid tracking reward that fuses 3D human tracking with robust 2D object tracking. It requires only generated video, enabling zero-shot generalization across objects and interaction types.
AI.result: Experiments show DeVI surpasses approaches imitating 3D HOI demonstrations, particularly for dexterous hand–object interactions, and scales to multi-object scenes and diverse, text-driven actions, validating video as an HOI-aware motion planner.
AI.conclusion: Video-based imitation augmented by 3D–2D tracking is effective for dexterous manipulation, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen objects and illustrating the potential of video as a planning signal for HOI-aware motion control.
